 BOOK I. 



 CHAPTER I. 

  in which the history open with a description of the social manner , 
  habit , and amusement of the English People , as exhibit in an 
  immemorial National Festivity . - character to be commemorate in the 
  history , introduce and graphically portray , with a nasological 
  illustration . - original suggestion as to the idiosyncrasy 
  engender by trade and calling , with other matter worthy of 
  note , convey in artless dialogue after the manner of Herodotus , 
  Father of History   mother unknown . 

 it be a summer fair in one of the prettiest village in Surrey . the 
 main street be line with booth , abound in toy , gleam crockery , 
 gay ribbon , and gild ginger bread . far on , where the street 
 widen into the ample village - green , rise the more pretending fabric 
 which lodge the attractive form of the Mermaid , the Norfolk Giant ; the 
 Pig - faced Lady , the Spotted Boy , and the Calf with two head ; while 
 high over even these edifice , and occupy the most conspicuous 
 vantage - ground , a lofty stage promise to rural playgoer the " Grand 
 Melodramatic Performance of the Remorseless Baron and the Bandit 's 
 child . " music , lively if artless , resound on every side , - drum , 
 fife , penny - whistle , cat - call , and a hand - organ play by a dark 
 foreigner , from the height of whose shoulder a cynical but observant 
 monkey eye the hubbub and crack his nut . 

 it be now sunset , - the throng at the full , - an animate , joyous 
 scene . the , day have be sultry ; no cloud be to be see , except low 
 on the western horizon , where they stretch , in lengthen ridge of 
 gold and purple , like the border - land between earth and sky . the tall 
 elm on the green be still , save , near the great stage , one or two , 
 upon which have climb young urchin , whose laugh face peer 
 forth , here and there , from the foliage tremble under their restless 
 movement . 

 amidst the crowd , as it stream saunteringly along , be two 
 spectator ; stranger to the place , as be notably prove by the 
 attention they excite , and the broad joke their dress and appearance 
 provoke from the rustic wit , - joke which they take with amused 
 good - humour , and sometimes retaliate with a zest which have already make 
 they very popular personage . indeed , there be that about they which 
 propitiate liking . they be young ; and the freshness of enjoyment be 
 so visible in their face , that it beget a sympathy , and wherever they 
 go , other face brighten round they . 

 one of the two whom we have thus individualized be of that enviable 
 age , range from five - and - twenty to seven - and - twenty , in which , if a 
 man can not contrive to make life very pleasant , - pitiable indeed must be 
 the state of his digestive organ . but you might see by this gentleman 's 
 countenance that if there be many like he , it would be a bad world 
 for the doctor . his cheek , though not highly colour , be yet ruddy 
 and clear ; his hazel eye be lively and keen ; his hair , which escape 
 in loose cluster from a jean shooting - cap set jauntily on a well - shape 
 head , be of that deep sunny auburn rarely see but in person of 
 vigorous and hardy temperament . he be good - look on the whole , and 
 would have deserve the more flattering epithet of handsome , but for his 
 nose , which be what the french call " a nose in the air , " - not a nose 
 supercilious , not a nose provocative , as such nose mostly be , but a 
 nose decidedly in earnest to make the good of itself and of thing in 
 general , - a nose that would push its way up in life , but so pleasantly 
 that the most irritable finger would never itch to lay hold of it . with 
 such a nose a man might play the violoncello , marry for love , or even 
 write poetry , and yet not go to the dog . 

 never would he stick in the mud so long as he follow that nose in the 
 air . 

 by the help of that nose this gentleman wear a black velveteen jacket 
 of foreign cut ; a mustache and imperial   then much rarer in England 
 than they have be since the Siege of Sebastopol ; and yet leave you 
 perfectly convinced that he be an honest Englishman , who have not only 
 no design on your pocket , but would not be easily dupe by any design 
 upon his own . 

 the companion of the personage thus sketch might be somewhere about 
 seventeen ; but his gait , his air , his lithe , vigorous frame , show a 
 manliness at variance with the boyish bloom of his face . he strike 
 the eye much more than his eld comrade . not that he be regularly 
 handsome , - far from it ; yet it be no paradox to say that he be 
 beautiful , at least , few indeed be the woman who would not have call 
 he so . his hair , long like his friend 's , be of a dark chestnut , with 
 gold gleam through it where the sun fall , incline to curl , and 
 singularly soft and silken in its texture . his large , clear , dark - blue , 
 happy eye be fringe with long ebon lash , and set under brow which 
 already wear the expression of intellectual power , and , well still , 
 of frank courage and open loyalty . his complexion be fair , and somewhat 
 pale , and his lip in laugh show tooth exquisitely white and even . 
 but though his profile be clearly cut , it be far from the greek 
 ideal ; and he want the height of stature which be usually consider 
 essential to the personal pretension of the male sex . without be 
 positively short , he be still under middle height , and from the compact 
 development of his proportion , seem already to have attain his full 
 growth . his dress , though not foreign , like his comrade 's , be peculiar : 
 a broad - brim straw hat , with a wide blue ribbon ; shirt collar turn 
 down , leave the throat bare ; a dark - green jacket of thin material 
 than cloth ; white trouser and waistcoat complete his costume . he 
 look like a mother 's darling , - perhaps he be one . 

 scratch across his back go one of those ingenious mechanical 
 contrivance familiarly in vogue at fair , which be design to impress 
 upon the victim to whom they be apply , the pleasing conviction that 
 his garment be rent in twain . 

 the boy turn round so quickly that he catch the arm of the 
 offender , - a pretty village - girl , a year or two young than himself . 
 " find in the act , sentence , punish , " cry he , snatch a kiss , and 
 receive a gentle slap . " and now , good for evil , here be a ribbon for 
 you ; choose . " 

 the girl slunk back shyly , but her companion push she forward , and 
 she end by select a cherry - colour ribbon , for which the boy pay 
 carelessly , while his elder and wise friend look at he with grave , 
 compassionate rebuke , and grumble out , - " Dr. Franklin tell we that 
 once in his life he pay too dear for a whistle ; but then he be only 
 seven year old , and a whistle have its us . but to pay such a price for 
 a scratch - back ! - prodigal ! come along . " 

 as the friend stroll on , naturally enough all the young girl who 
 wish for ribbon , and be possess of scratch - back , follow in 
 their wake . Scratch go the instrument , but in vain . 

 " lass , " say the elder , turn sharply upon they his nose in the air , 
 " ribbon be plentiful , - shilling scarce ; and kiss , though pleasant 
 in private , be insipid in public . what , still ! beware ! know that , 
 innocent as we seem , we be woman - eater ; and if you follow we far , 
 you be devour ! " so say , he expand his jaw to a width so 
 preternaturally large , and exhibit a row of grinder so formidable , 
 that the girl fall back in consternation . the friend turn down 
 a narrow alley between the booth , and though still pursue by some 
 adventurous and mercenary spirit , be comparatively undisturbed as 
 they thread their way along the back of the booth , and arrive at 
 last on the village - green , and in front of the Great Stage . 

 " Oho , Lionel ! " quoth the eld friend ; " thespian and classical , - worth 
 seeing , no doubt . " then turn to a grave cobbler in leathern apron , 
 who be regard with saturnine interest the motley figure range 
 in front of the curtain as the Drumatis Persona , he say , " you seem 
 attract , sir ; you have probably already witness the performance . " 
 " yes , " return the Cobbler ; " this be the third day , and to - morrow 's 
 the last . I be not   miss once yet , and I sha ' not   miss ; but it be not 
 what it be a while back . " 

 " ' that be sad ; but then the same thing be say of everything by 
 everybody who have reach your respectable age , friend . summer , and 
 sun , stupid old watering - place , and pretty young woman , ` be not   what 
 they be a while back . ' if man and thing go on degenerate in this 
 way , our grandchild will have a dull time of it . " 

 the Cobbler eye the young man , and nod approvingly . he have sense 
 enough to comprehend the ironical philosophy of the reply ; and our 
 Cobbler love talk out of the common way . " you speak truly and 
 cleverly , sir . but if old folk do always say that thing be bad than 
 they be , ben't   there always summat in what be always say ? I be for the 
 old time ; my neighbour , Joe Spruce , be for the new , and say we be all 
 a - progressing . but he be a pink ; I ' m a blue . " 

 " you be a blue ? " say the boy Lionel ; " I do not   understand . " 

 " Young ' un , I be a Tory , - that be blue ; and Spruce be a Rad , - that be pink ! 
 and , what be more to the purpose , he be a tailor , and I be a cobbler . " 

 " Aha ! " say the elder , with much interest ; " more to the purpose be it ? 
 how so ? " 

 the Cobbler put the forefinger of the right hand on the forefinger 
 of the left ; it be the gesture of a man about to ratiocinate or 
 demonstrate , as Quintilian , in his remark on the oratory of finger , 
 probably observe ; or if he have fail to do so , it be a blot in his 
 essay . 

 " you see , sir , " quoth the Cobbler , " that a man 's business have a deal 
 to do with his manner of thinking . every trade , I take it , have idea as 
 belong to it . butcher do not   see life as baker do ; and if you talk to 
 a dozen tallow - chandler , then to a dozen blacksmith , you will see 
 tallow - chandler be peculiar , and blacksmith too . " 

 " you be a keen observer , " say he of the jean cap , admiringly ; " your 
 remark be new to I ; I dare say it be true . " 

 " course it be ; and the star have summat to do with it ; for if they 
 order a man 's calling , it stand to reason that they order a man 's mind 
 to fit it . now , a tailor sit on his board with other , and be always 
 a - talk with them , and a - read the news ; therefore he think , as his 
 fellow do , smart and sharp , bang up to the day , but nothing ' riginal 
 and all his own , like . but a cobbler , " continue the man of leather , 
 with a majestic air , ' sit by hisself , and talk with hisself ; and what 
 he think get into his head without be put there by another man 's 
 tongue . " 

 " you enlighten I more and more , " say our friend with the nose in the 
 air , bow respectfully , - " a tailor be gregarious , a cobbler solitary . 
 the gregarious go with the future , the solitary stick by the past . I 
 understand why you be a Tory and perhaps a poet . " 

 " well , a bit of one , " say the Cobbler , with an iron smile . " and many 
 be the cobbler who be a poet , - or discover marvellous thing in a 
 crystal , - whereas a tailor , sir "   speak with great contempt , " only 
 see the upper leather of the world 's sole in a newspaper . " 

 here the conversation be interrupt by a sudden pressure of the crowd 
 towards the theatre . the two young friend look up , and see that the 
 new object of attraction be a little girl , who seem scarcely ten 
 year old , though in truth she be about two year old . she have just 
 emerge from behind the curtain , make her obeisance to the crowd , and 
 be now walk in front of the stage with the prettiest possible air 
 of infantine solemnity . " poor little thing ! " say Lionel . " poor little 
 thing ! " say the Cobbler . and have you be there , my reader , ten to 
 one but you would have say the same . and yet she be attire in white 
 satin , with spangled flounce and a tinsel jacket ; and she wear a wreath 
 of flower   to be sure , the flower be not real   on her long fair 
 curl , with gaudy bracelet   to be sure , the stone be mock   on her 
 slender arm . still there be something in she that all this finery 
 could not vulgarize ; and since it could not vulgarize , you pity she 
 for it . she have one of those charming face that look straight into 
 the heart of we all , young and old . and though she seem quite 
 self - possess , there be no effrontery in her air , but the ease of 
 a little lady , with a simple child 's unconsciousness that there be 
 anything in her situation to induce you to sigh , " poor thing ! " 

 " you should see her act , young gent , " say the Cobbler : ' she play 
 uncommon . but if you have see he as teach she , - see he a year ago . " 

 " who be he ? " 

 " Waife , sir ; mayhap you have hear speak of Waife ? " 

 " I blush to say , no . " 

 " why , he might have make his fortune at Common Garden ; but that be a long 
 story . poor fellow ! he be break down now , anyhow . but she take care of 
 he , little darling : God bless thee ! " and the Cobbler here exchange a 
 smile and a nod with the little girl , whose face brighten when she see 
 he amidst the crowd . 

 " by the brush and pallet of Raphael ! " cry the elder of the young man , 
 " before I be many hour old I must have that child 's head ! " 

 " her head , man ! " cry the Cobbler , aghast . 

 " in my sketch - book . you be a poet , - I a painter . you know the little 
 girl ? " 

 " do not   I ! she and her grandfather lodge with I ; her 
 grandfather , - that be Waife , - marvellous man ! but they ill - use he ; and 
 if it warn't   for she , he would starve . he feed they all once : he can feed 
 they no long ; he would starve . that be the world : they use up a genus , 
 and when it fall on the road , push on ; that be what Joe Spruce call 
 a - progressing . but there be the drum ! they be a - go to act ; will not   you 
 look in , gent ? " 

 " of course , " cry Lionel , - " of course . and , hark ye , Vance , we will toss 
 up which shall be the first to take that little girl 's head . " 

 " murderer in either sense of the word ! " say Vance , with a smile that 
 would have become Correggio if a tyro have offer to toss up which 
 should be the first to paint a cherub . 




 CHAPTER II . 

  the historian take a view of the british stage as represent by 
  the irregular drama , the regular have   ere the date of the event 
  to which this narrative be restrict   disappear from the vestige 
  of creation . 

 they enter the little theatre , and the Cobbler with they ; but the 
 last retire modestly to the threepenny row . the young gentleman be 
 favour with reserved seat , price one shilling . " very dear , " murmur 
 Vance , as he carefully button the pocket to which he restore a 
 purse weave from link of steel , after the fashion of chain mail . ah , 
 Messieurs and Confreres the Dramatic Authors , do not flatter yourself 
 that we be about to give you a complacent triumph over the Grand 
 Melodrame of " the Remorseless Baron and the Bandit 's child . " we grant it 
 be horrible rubbish , regard in an aesthetic point of view , but it be 
 mighty effective in the theatrical . nobody yawn ; you do not even hear 
 a cough , nor the cry of that omnipresent baby , who be always sure to set 
 up an unappeasable wail in the midmost interest of a classical five - act 
 piece , represent for the first time on the metropolitan board . here 
 the story rush on , per fas aut nefas , and the audience go with it . 
 certe , some man who understand the stage must have put the incident 
 together , and then leave it to each illiterate histrio to find the 
 word , - word , my dear confrere , signify so little in an act play . 
 the movement be the thing . grand secret ! Analyze , practise it , and 
 restore to grateful star that lose Pleiad the British Acting Drama . 

 of course the Bandit be an ill - use and most estimable man . he have some 
 mysterious right to the Estate and Castle of the Remorseless Baron . 
 that title usurper , therefore , do all in his power to hunt the Bandit 
 out in his fastness and bring he to a bloody end . here the interest 
 centre itself in the Bandit 's child , who , we need not say , be the 
 little girl in the wreath and spangle , style in the playbill " Miss 
 Juliet Araminta Wife , " and the incident consist in her various 
 device to foil the pursuit of the Baron and save her father . some of 
 these incident be indebted to the Comic Muse , and keep the audience 
 in a broad laugh . her arch playfulness here be exquisite . with what 
 vivacity she dupe the High Sheriff , who have the command of his king 
 to take the Bandit alive or dead , into the belief that the very Lawyer 
 employ by the Baron be the criminal in disguise , and what pearly 
 tooth she show when the Lawyer be seize and gag ! how dexterously 
 she ascertain the weak point in the character of the " King 's 
 Lieutenant "   jeune premier , who be depute by his royal master to aid 
 the Remorseless Baron in trounce the Bandit ! how cunningly she learn 
 that he be in love with the Baron 's ward   jeune amoureuse , whom that 
 unworthy noble intend to force into a marriage with himself on account 
 of her fortune ! how prettily she pass note to and fro , the Lieutenant 
 never suspect that she be the Bandit 's child , and at last get the 
 king 's soldier on her side , as the event prove ! and oh , how gayly , and 
 with what mimic art , she steal into the Baron 's castle , disguise as 
 a witch , startle his conscience with revelation and prediction , 
 frighten all the vassal with blue light and chemical illusion , and 
 venture even into the usurper 's own private chamber , while the tyrant 
 be toss restless on the couch , over which hang his terrible sword , 
 abstract from his coffer the deed that prove the well right of 
 the persecute Bandit ! then , when he wake before she could escape 
 with her treasure , and pursue she with his sword , with what glee she 
 apparently set herself on fire , and skip out of the casement in an 
 explosion of cracker ! and when the drama approach its denouement , 
 when the Baron 's man , and the royal officer of justice , have , despite 
 all her art , track the Bandit to the cave , in which , after various 
 retreat , he lie hide , wound by shot , and bruise by a fall from a 
 precipice , - with what admirable byplay she hover around the spot , with 
 what pathos she seek to decoy away the pursuer ! it be the skylark 
 playing round the nest . and when all be vain , - when , no long to be 
 deceive , the enemy seek to seize she , how mockingly she elude 
 they , bound up the rock , and shake her slight finger at they in scorn ! 
 surely she will save that estimable Bandit still ! now , hitherto , though 
 the Bandit be the nominal hero of the piece , though you be always 
 hearing of he , - his wrong , virtue , hairbreadth escape , - he have never 
 be see . not Mrs. Harris , in the immortal narrative , be more quote 
 and more mythical . but in the last scene there be the Bandit , there in 
 his cavern , helpless with bruise and wound , lie on a rock . in rush 
 the enemy , Baron , High Sheriff , and all , to seize he . not a word 
 speak the Bandit , but his attitude be sublime , - even Vance cry 
 " bravo ; " and just as he be seize , halter round his neck , and about 
 to be hang , down from the chasm above leap his child , hold 
 the title - deed , filch from the Baron , and by her side the King 's 
 Lieutenant , who proclaim the Bandit 's pardon , with due restoration 
 to his honour and estate , and consign to the astounded Sheriff the 
 august person of the Remorseless Baron . then the affect scene , father 
 and child in each other 's arm ; and then an exclamation , which have be 
 long hover about the lip of many of the audience , break out , " Waife , 
 Waife ! " yes , the Bandit , who appear but in the last scene , and even 
 then utter not a word , be the once great actor on that itinerant 
 thespian stage , know through many a fair for his exuberant humour , his 
 impromptu joke , his arch eye , his redundant life of drollery , and the 
 strange pathos or dignity with which he could suddenly exalt a jester 's 
 part , and call forth tear in the startled hush of laughter ; he whom the 
 Cobbler have rightly say , " might have make a fortune at Covent Garden . " 
 there be the remnant of the old popular mime ! - all his attribute of 
 eloquence reduce to dumb show ! masterly touch of nature and of art 
 in this representation of he , - touch which all who have ever in former 
 year see and hear he on that stage feel simultaneously . he come in 
 for his personal portion of dramatic tear . " Waife , Waife ! " cry many a 
 village voice , as the little girl lead he to the front of the stage . 

 he hobble ; there be a bandage round his eye . the plot , in describe 
 the accident that have befall the Bandit , idealize the genuine 
 infirmity of the man , - infirmity that have befall he since last 
 see in that village . he be blind of one eye ; he have become crippled ; 
 some malady of the trachea or larynx have seemingly break up the once 
 joyous key of the old pleasant voice . he do not trust himself to speak , 
 even on that stage , but silently bend his head to the rustic audience ; 
 and Vance , who be an habitual playgoer , see in that simple salutation 
 that the man be an artistic actor . all be over , the audience stream 
 out , much affected , and talk one to the other . it have not be at all 
 like the ordinary stage exhibition at a village fair . Vance and Lionel 
 exchange look of surprise , and then , by a common impulse , move 
 towards the stage , push aside the curtain , which have fall , and be 
 in that strange world which have so many reduplication , fragment of 
 one break mirror , whether in the proud theatre or the lowliest 
 barn , - nay , whether in the palace of king , the cabinet of statesman , 
 the home of domestic life , - the world we call " behind the Scenes . " 




 CHAPTER III . 

  strike illustration of lawless tyranny and infant avarice

  Superstitions of the dark age still in force amongst the trading 
  community , furnish valuable hint to certain american 
  journalist , and highly suggestive of reflection humiliating to the 
  national vanity . 

 the Remorseless Baron , who be no other than the managerial proprietor

 people . " 

 " I beg pardon respectfully : I be an artist , - a pupil of the Royal

 " sketch ! nonsense . " 

 " Sir , " say Lionel , with the seasonable extravagance of early youth , " my 
 friend would , I be sure , pay for the sitting - handsomely ! " 

 " ha ! " say the manager , soften , " you speak like a gentleman , sir : but ,

 " precocious villain ! how do you know that she be greedy and mercenary ? "

 call to - morrow , - imply that he be to be pay for allow she to

 those word hiss in my ear , and come from that fairy mouth , which 
 ought to drop pearl and diamond , I leave she , " add Lionel , as gravely 
 as if he be sixty , " and lose an illusion ! " 

 " three pound ! " cry Vance , raise his eyebrow to the high arch

 moon , - " three pound ! - a fabulous sum ! who have three pound to throw 
 away ? duke , with a hundred thousand a year in acre , have not three 
 pound to draw out of their pocket in that reckless , profligate manner . 
 three pound ! - what could I not buy for three pound ? I could buy the

 a month for three pound ! and a jade in tinsel , just enter on her 
 teen , to ask three pound for what ? for become immortal on the canvas 
 of Francis Vance ? - bother ! " 

 here Vance feel a touch on his shoulder . he turn round quickly , as a

 " well , master , do not she act fine ? - how d'ye like she ? " 

 " not much in her natural character ; but she set a mighty high value on 
 herself . " 

 " Anan , I do not   take you . " 

 " she will not catch I take she ! three pound ! - three kingdom ! stay , "

 they be now in the main road . the Cobbler seat himself on a lonely

 " young master , " he say , " it be now just four year ago , when Mr. Rugge , 
 come here , as he and his troop have do at fair - time ever sin ' I can

 " cos of Saturn ! " 

 " Satan ? " 

 " Saturn , - dead agin his second and Tenth House , I will swear . Lord of 
 Ascendant , mayhap ; in combustion of the Sun , - who know ? " 

 " you be not an astrologer ? " say Vance , suspiciously , edge off . 

 " bit of it ; no offence . " 

 " what do it signify ? " say Lionel , impatiently ; " go on . so you call 
 Mr. Waife ' Gentleman Waife ; ' and if you have not be an astrologer you 
 would have be puzzle to see he in such a calling . " 

 " ay , that be it ; for he warn't   like any as we ever see on these board 
 hereabout ; and yet he warn't   exactly like a Lunnon actor , as I have 
 see them in Lunnon , either , but more like a clever fellow who act for 
 the spree of the thing . he have sich droll jest , and look so comical , 
 yet not commonlike , but always what I call a gentleman , - just as if 
 one o ' ye two be do a bit of sport to please your friend . well , 
 he draw hugely , and so he do , every time he come , so that the great 
 family in the neighbourhood would go to hear he ; and he lodge in my 
 house , and have pleasant way with he , and be what I call a scollard . 
 but still I do not   want to deceive ye , and I should judge he to have 
 be a wild dog in his day . Mercury ill - aspecte , - not a doubt of it . 
 last year it so happen that one of the great gent who belong to a 
 Lunnon theatre be here at fair - time . whether he have hear of Waife 
 chanceway , and come express to judge for hisself , I can not   say ; like 
 eno ' . and when he have see Gentleman Waife act , he send for he to the 
 inn - Red Lion - and offer he a power o ' money to go to Lunnon , - Common 
 Garden . well , sir , Waife do not take to it all at once , but hem and 
 haw , and be at last quite coax into it , and so he go . but 
 bad luck come on it ; and I know there would , for I see it all in my 
 crystal . " 

 " oh , " exclaim Vance , " a crystal , too ; really it be get late , and 
 if you have your crystal about you , you might see that we want to sup . " 

 " what happen ? " ask Lionel , more blandly , for he see the Cobbler , who 
 have mean to make a great effect by the introduction of the crystal , be 
 offend . 

 " what happen ? why , just what I foreseed . there be an accident in the 
 railway ' tween this and Lunnon , and poor Waife lose an eye , and be a

 what be bad , he be a long time atwixt life and death , and get summat 
 bad on his chest wi ' catch cold , and lose his voice , and become the

 " but he get some compensation from the railway , I suppose ? " say Vance , 
 with the unfeeling equanimity of a stoical demon . 

 " he do , and spend it . I suppose the gentleman break out in he as soon

 and have even think of take the huge theatre at York , and bring

 think himself ill - use , and so at first he would have nothing more to 
 say to Waife . and truth be , what could the poor man do for Rugge ? but 
 then Waife produce little Sophy . "

 " Same - in private life she be Sophy . and Waife teach she to act , 
 and put together the play for she . and Rugge catch at she ; and 
 she support Waife with what she get ; for Rugge only give he four 
 shilling a week , and that go on ' baccy and such like . "

 " no - he do not   drink . but he do smoke , and he have little genteel way 
 with he , and four shilling go on them . and they have be about the

 behave shock hard to both on them : and I do not   believe he have any 
 right to she in law , as he pretend , - only a sort of understanding which 
 she and her grandfather could break if they please ; and that be what 
 they wish to do , and that be why little Sophy want the three pound . " 

 " how ? " cry Lionel , eagerly . " if they have three pound could they get 
 away ? and if they do , how could they live ? where could they go ? " 

 " that be their secret . but I hear Waife say - the first night they come 
 here - I that if he could get three pound , he have hit on a plan to 
 be independent like . I tell you what put his back up : it be Rugge 
 insist on his come on the stage agin , for he do not like to be 
 see such a wreck . but he be force to give in ; and so he contrive

 speak . " 

 " my good friend , " cry young Lionel , " we be greatly oblige to you 
 for your story ; and we should much like to see little Sophy and her 
 grandfather at your house to - morrow , - can we ? " 

 " certain sure you can , after the play be over ; to - night , if you like . " 

 " no , to - morrow : you see my friend be impatient to get back now ; we will 
 call to - morrow . " 

 " ' t be the last day of their stay , " say the Cobbler . " but you can not   be 
 sure to see they safely at my house afore ten o'clock at night ; and not 
 a word to Rugge ! mum ! " 

 " not a word to Rugge , " return Lionel ; " good - night to you . " 

 the young man leave the Cobbler still seat on the milestone , gaze on 
 the star and ruminating . they walk briskly down the road . 

 " it be I who have have the talk now , " say Lionel , in his soft tone . 
 he be bent on coax three pound out of his rich friend , and that 
 might require some management . for amongst the wild youngster in Mr. 
 Vance 's profession , there run many a joke at the skill with which he 
 parry irregular assault on his purse ; and that gentleman , with his 
 nose more than usually in the air , have once observe to such scoffer 
 " that they be quite welcome to any joke at his expense , " a wag 
 have exclaim , " at your expense ! do not   fear ; if a joke be worth a 
 farthing , you would never give that permission . " 

 so when Lionel make that innocent remark , the softness of his tone

 silent . Lionel , in a voice still sweet , repeat , - " it be I who have 
 all the talk now ! " 

 " naturally , " then return Vance , " naturally you have , for it be you , 
 I suspect , who alone have the intention to pay for it , and three pound 
 appear to be the price . Dearish , eh ? " 

 " ah , Vance , if I have three pound ! " 

 " tush ; and say no more till we have sup . I have the hunger of a 
 wolf . " 

 just in sight of the next milestone the young traveller turn a few

 cover with honeysuckle , between the porch and the river , - a couple of 
 pleasure - boat moor to the bank ; and now all the wave ripple under 
 the moonlight . 

 " supper and light in the arbour , " cry Vance to the waiting - maid , 
 " hey , presto , quick ! while we turn in to wash our hand . and hark ! a 
 quart jug of that capital whiskey - toddy . " 




 CHAPTER IV . 

  be a chapter that link the past to the future by the gradual 
  elucidation of antecedent . 

 o wayside inn and pedestrian ramble ! o summer night , under 
 honeysuckle arbour , on the bank of starry wave ! o Youth , Youth ! 

 Vance ladle out the toddy and light his cigar ; then , lean his head 
 on his hand and his elbow on the table , he look with an artist 's eye 
 along the glance river . 

 " after all , " say he , " I be glad I be a painter ; and I hope I may live 
 to be a great one . " 

 " no doubt , if you live , you will be a great one , " cry Lionel , with 
 cordial sincerity . " and if I , who can only just paint well enough to 
 please myself , find that it give a new charm to nature - " 

 " cut sentiment , " quoth Vance , " and go on . " 

 " what , " continue Lionel , unchille by the admonitory interruption , 
 " must you feel who can fix a fade sunshine - a fleeting face - on a 
 scrap of canvas , and say ' Sunshine and Beauty , live there forever ! ' " 

 VANCE . - " forever ! no ! colour perish , canvas rot . what remain to we 
 of Zeuxis ? still it be prettily say on behalf of the poetic side of the 
 profession ; there be a prosaic one ; - we will blink it . yes ; I be glad to 
 be a painter . but you must not catch the fever of my calling . your poor 
 mother would never forgive I if she think I have make you a dauber by 
 my example . " 

 lionel   gloomily . - " no . I shall not be a painter ! but what can I be ? 
 how shall I ever build on the earth one of the castle I have build in 
 the air ? fame look so far , - Fortune so impossible . but one thing I be 
 bent upon "   speak with knit brow and clench tooth , " I will gain an 
 independence somehow , and support my mother . " 

 VANCE . - " your mother be support : she have the pension - " 

 lionel . - " of a captain 's widow ; and "   he add with a flushed cheek   " a 
 first floor that she let to lodger . " 

 VANCE . - " no shame in that ! peer let house ; and on the continent , 
 prince let not only first floor , but fifth and sixth floor , to say 
 nothing of attic and cellar . in begin the world , friend Lionel , 
 if you do not   wish to get chafe at every turn , fold up your pride 
 carefully , put it under lock and key , and only let it out to air upon 
 grand occasion . Pride be a garment all stiff brocade outside , all 
 grate sackcloth on the side next to the skin . even king do not   wear 
 the dalmaticum except at a coronation . independence you desire ; good . 
 but be you dependent now ? your mother have give you an excellent 
 education , and you have already put it to profit . my dear boy , " add 
 Vance , with unusual warmth , " I honour you ; at your age , on leave 
 school , to have shut yourself up , translate Greek and latin per sheet 
 for a bookseller , at less than a valet 's wage , and all for the purpose 
 of buy comfort for your mother ; and have a few pound in your own 
 pocket , to rove your little holiday with I and pay your share of the 
 cost ! ah , there be energy and spirit and life in all that , Lionel , 
 which will find upon rock some castle as fine as any you have build in 
 air . your hand , my boy . " 

 this burst be so unlike the practical dryness , or even the more 
 unctuous humour , of Frank Vance , that it take Lionel by surprise , and 
 his voice falter as he press the hand hold out to he . he answer , 
 " I do not   deserve your praise , Vance , and I fear the pride you tell I to 
 put under lock and key have the large share of the merit you ascribe to 
 well motif . independent ? no ! I have never be so . " 

 VANCE . - " well , you depend on a parent : who , at seventeen do not ? " 

 lionel . - " I do not mean my mother ; of course , I could not be too proud 
 to take benefit from she . but the truth be simply this - , my father 
 have a relation , not very near , indeed , - a cousin , at about as distant 
 a remove , I fancy , as a cousin well can be . to this gentleman my mother 
 write when my poor father die ; and he be generous , for it be he who 
 pay for my schooling . I do not know this till very lately . I have a 
 vague impression , indeed , that I have a powerful and wealthy kinsman who 
 take an interest in I , but whom I have never see . " 

 VANCE . - " never see ? " 

 lionel . - " no . and here come the sting . on leave school last 
 Christmas , my mother , for the first time , tell I the extent of my 
 obligation to this benefactor , and inform I that he wish to know 
 my own choice as to a profession , - that if I prefer Church or Bar , he 
 would maintain I at college . " 

 VANCE . - " body o ' I ! where be the sting in that ? help yourself to toddy , 
 my boy , and take more genial view of life . " 

 LIONEL . - " you have not hear I out . I then ask to see my benefactor 's 
 letter ; and my mother , unconscious of the pain she be about to 
 inflict , show I not only the last one , but all she have receive from 
 he . oh , Vance , they be terrible , those letter ! the first begin by 
 a dry acquiescence in the claim of kindred , a curt proposal to pay my 
 schooling ; but not one word of kindness , and a stern proviso that the 
 writer be never to see nor hear from I . he want no gratitude ; he 
 disbelieve in all profession of it . his favour would cease if I 
 molest he . ' molest ' be the word ; it be bread throw to a dog . " 

 VANCE . - " Tut ! only a rich man 's eccentricity . a bachelor , I presume ? " 

 lionel . - " my mother say he have be marry , and be a widower . " 

 VANCE . - " any child ? " 

 lionel . - " my mother say none live ; but I know little or nothing about 
 his family . " 

 Vance look with keen scrutiny into the face of his boyfriend , and , 
 after a pause , say , drily , - " Plain as a pikestaff . your relation be one 
 of those man who , have no child , suspect and dread the attention 
 of an heir presumptive ; and what have make this sting , as you call it , 
 keener to you be - pardon I - be in some silly word of your mother , who , 
 in show you the letter , have hint to you that that heir you might 
 be , if you be sufficiently pliant and subservient . be I not right ? " 

 Lionel hang his head , without reply . 

 VANCE   cheeringly . - " so , so ; no great harm as yet . enough of the first 
 letter . what be the last ? " 

 lionel . - " still more offensive . he , this kinsman , this patron , desire 
 my mother to spare he those reference to her son 's ability and 
 promise , which , though natural to herself , have slight interest to 
 he , - he , the condescend benefactor ! as to his opinion , what could 
 I care for the opinion of one I have never see ? all that could sensibly 
 affect my - oh , but I can not go on with those cutting phrase , which 
 imply but this , ' all I can care for be the money of a man who insult I 
 while he give it . ' " 

 VANCE   emphatically . - " without be a wizard , I should say your 
 relative be rather a disagreeable person , - not what be call urbane 
 and amiable , - in fact , a brute . " 

 LIONEL . - " you will not blame I , then , when I tell you that I resolve 
 not to accept the offer to maintain I at college , with which the letter 
 close . luckily Dr. Wallis   the head master of my school , who have 
 always be very kind to I , have just undertake to supervise a popular 
 translation of the classic . he recommend I , at my request , to the 
 publisher engage in the undertaking , as not incapable of translate 
 some of the less difficult latin author , - subject to his correction . 
 when I have finish the first instalment of the work thus intrust to 
 I , my mother grow alarmed for my health , and insist on my take some 
 recreation . you be about to set out on a pedestrian tour . I have , as 
 you say , some pound in my pocket ; and thus I have pass with you the 
 merry day of my life . " 

 VANCE . - " what say your civil cousin when your refusal to go to college 
 be convey to he ? " 

 lionel . - " he do not answer my mother 's communication to that effect 
 till just before I leave home , and then , - no , it be not his last letter 
 from which I repeat that wither extract , - no , the last be more 
 galling still , for in it he say that if , in spite of the ability and 
 promise that have be so vaunted , the dulness of a college and the 
 labour of learn profession be so distasteful to I , he have no 
 desire to dictate to my choice , but that as he do not wish one who be , 
 however remotely , of his blood , and bear the name of Haughton , to turn 
 shoeblack or pickpocket - Vance - Vance ! " 

 VANCE . - " lock up your pride - the sackcloth fret you - and go on ; and 
 that therefore he - " 

 lionel . - " would buy I a commission in the army , or get I an 
 appointment in India . " 

 VANCE . - " which do you take ? " 

 lionel   passionately . " which ! so offer , - which ? - of course neither ! 
 but distrust the tone of my mother 's reply , I sit down , the evening 
 before I leave home , and write myself to this cruel man . I do not show 
 any letter to my mother , - do not tell she of it . I write shortly , - that 
 if he would not accept my gratitude , I would not accept his benefit ; 
 that shoeblack I might be , - pickpocket , no ! that he need not fear I 
 should disgrace his blood or my name ; and that I would not rest till , 
 soon or later , I have pay he back all that I have cost he , and feel 
 relieve from the burden of an obligation which - which - " the boy 
 pause , cover his face with his hand , and sob . 

 Vance , though much move , pretend to scold his friend , but find 
 that ineffectual , fairly rise , wound his arm brother - like round he , and 
 draw he from the arbour to the shelve margin of the river . " Comfort , " 
 then say the Artist , almost solemnly , as here , from the inner depth 
 of his character , the true genius of the man come forth and 
 speak , - " comfort , and look round ; see where the islet interrupt the 
 tide , and how smilingly the stream flow on . see , just where we stand , 
 how the slight pebble be fret the wave would the wave if not 
 fretted make that pleasant music ? a few mile far on , and the river 
 be span by a bridge , which busy foot now be cross : by the side 
 of that bridge now be rise a palace ; all the man who rule England have 
 room in that palace . at the rear of the palace soar up the old Abbey 
 where king have their tomb in right of the name they inherit ; man , 
 lowly as we , have find tomb there , in right of the name which they 
 make . think , now , that you stand on that bridge with a boy 's lofty hope , 
 with a man 's steadfast courage ; then turn again to that stream , calm 
 with starlight , flow on towards the bridge , - spite of islet and 
 pebble . " 

 Lionel make no audible answer , though his lip murmur , but he press 
 close and close to his friend 's side ; and the tear be already dry 
 on his cheek , though their dew still glisten in his eye . 




 CHAPTER V. 

  Speculations on the moral quality of the Bandit . - Mr. Vance , with 
  mingle emotion , foresee that the acquisition of the Bandit 's 
  acquaintance may be attend with pecuniary loss . 

 Vance loosen the boat from its mooring , step in , and take up the 
 oar . Lionel follow , and sit by the stern . the Artist row on slowly , 
 whistle melodiously in time to the dash of the oar . they soon come to 
 the bank of garden - ground surround with turf on which fairy might 
 have dance one of those villa never see out of England . from the 
 window of the villa the light gleam steadily ; over the bank , 
 dip into the water , hang large willow breathlessly ; the boat gently 
 brush aside their pendent bough , and Vance rest in a grassy cove . 

 " and faith , " say the Artist , gayly , - " faith , " say he , light his 
 third cigar , " it be time we should bestow a few word more on the 
 Remorseless Baron and the Bandit 's child ! what a cock - and - a - bull story 
 the Cobbler tell we ! he must have think we precious green . " 

 LIONEL   rouse . - " Nay , I see nothing so wonderful in the story , though 
 much that be sad . you must allow that Waife may have be a good actor : 
 you become quite excited merely at his attitude and bow . natural , 
 therefore , that he should have be invite to try his chance on the 
 London stage ; not improbable that he may have meet with an accident by 
 the train , and so lose his chance forever ; natural , then , that he should 
 press into service his poor little grandchild , natural , also , that , 
 hardly treat and his pride hurt , he should wish to escape . " 

 VANCE . - " and more natural than all that he should want to extract from 
 our pocket three pound , the Bandit ! no , Lionel , I tell you what be 
 not probable , that he should have dispose of that clever child to a 
 vagabond like Rugge : she play admirably . the manager who be to have 
 engage he would have engage she if he have see she . I be puzzle . " 

 LIONEL . - " true , she be an extraordinary child . I can not say how she have 
 interested I . " he take out his purse , and begin count its content . 
 " I have nearly three pound leave , " he cry joyously . " L2 . 18 . if 
 I give up the thought of a long excursion with you , and go quietly 
 home - " 

 VANCE . - " and not pay your share of the bill yonder ? " 

 lionel . - " ah , I forget that ! but come , I be not too proud to borrow from 
 you : it be not for a selfish purpose . " 

 VANCE . - " borrow from I , Cato ! that come of fall in with bandit and 
 their child . no ; but let we look at the thing like man of sense . 
 one story be good till another be tell . I will call by myself on Rugge 
 to - morrow , and hear what he say ; and then , if we judge favourably of 
 the Cobbler 's version , we will go at night and talk with the Cobbler 's 
 lodger ; and I dare say , " add Vance , kindly , but with a sigh , - " I 
 daresay the three pound will be coax out of I ! after all , her head 
 be worth it . I want an idea for Titania . " 

 lionel   joyously . - " my dear Vance , you be the good fellow in the 
 world . " 

 VANCE . - " small compliment to humankind ! take the oar : it be your turn 
 now . " 

 Lionel obey ; the boat once more dance along the tide - thoro ' 
 reed , - -thoro ' wave , skirt the grassy islet - out into pale 
 moonlight . they talk but by fit and start . what of ? - a thousand 
 thing ! bright young heart , eloquent young tongue ! no sin in the 
 past ; hope gleam through the future . o summer night , on the glass 
 of starry wave ! o Youth , Youth ! 




 CHAPTER VI . 

  wherein the historian track the public character that fret their 
  hour on the stage , into the bosom of private life . - the reader be 
  invite to arrive at a conclusion which may often , in period of 
  perplexity , restore ease to his mind ; namely , that if man will 
  reflect on all the hope he have nourish , all the fear he have 
  admit , all the project he have form , the wise thing he can 
  do , nine time out of ten , with hope , fear , and project , be to let 
  they end with the chapter - in smoke . 

 it be past nine o'clock in the evening of the following day . the 
 exhibition at Mr. Rugge 's theatre have close for the season in that 
 village , for it be the conclusion of the fair . the final performance 
 have be begin and end somewhat early than on former night . the 
 theatre be to be clear from the ground by daybreak , and the whole 
 company to proceed onward betime in the morning . another fair await 
 they in an adjoining county , and they have a long journey before they . 

 Gentleman Waife and his Juliet Araminta have go to their lodging over 
 the Cobbler 's stall . their room be homely enough , but have an air not 
 only of the comfortable , but the picturesque . the little sit - room 
 be very old - fashioned , - panel in wood that have once be paint 
 blue , with a quaint chimney - piece that reach to the ceiling . that 
 part of the house speak of the time of Charles I. , it might have be 
 tenant by a religious Roundhead ; and , frame - in over the low door , 
 there be a grim , faded portrait of a pinch - faced saturnine man , with 
 long lank hair , starch band , and a length of upper lip that betoken 
 relentless obstinacy of character , and might have curl in sullen glee 
 at the monarch 's scaffold , or preach an interminable sermon to the 
 stout Protector . on a table , under the deep - sink window , be neatly 
 array a few sober - looking old book ; you would find amongst they 
 Colley 's " astrology , " Owen Feltham 's " resolve , " Glanville " on Witches , " 
 the " Pilgrim 's progress , " an early edition of " Paradise Lost , " and 
 an old Bible ; also two flower - pot of clay brightly redden , and 
 contain stock ; also two small worsted rug , on one of which rest a 
 carve cocoa - nut , on the other an egg - shape ball of crystal , - that last 
 the pride and joy of the cobbler 's visionary soul . a door leave wide open 
 communicate with an inner room   very low be its ceiling , in which the 
 Bandit sleep , if the severity of his persecutor permit he to sleep . 
 in the corner of the sitting - room , near that door , be a small horsehair 
 sofa , which , by the aid of sheet and a needlework coverlid , do duty 
 for a bed , and be consign to the Bandit 's child . here the tenderness 
 of the Cobbler 's heart be visible , for over the coverlid be strew 
 sprig of lavender and leaf of vervain ; the last , be it say , to 
 induce happy dream , and scare away witchcraft and evil spirit . on 
 another table , near the fireplace , the child be busy in set out 
 the tea - thing for her grandfather . she have leave in the property - room 
 of the theatre her robe of spangle and tinsel , and appear now in 
 a simple frock . she have no long the look of Titania , but that of a 
 lively , active , affectionate human child ; nothing theatrical about she 
 now , yet still , in her graceful movement , so nimble but so noiseless , 
 in her slight fair hand , in her transparent colouring , there be 
 Nature 's own lady , - that something which strike we all as well - bear and 
 high - breed : not that it necessarily be so ; the semblance of aristocracy , 
 in female childhood more especially , be often delusive . the 
  souvenance flower , work into the collar of prince , spring up 
 wild on field and fall . 

 Gentleman Waife , wrap negligently in a gray dressing - gown and seat 
 in an old leathern easy - chair , be evidently out of sort . he do not 
 seem to heed the little preparation for his comfort , but , rest his 
 cheek on his right hand , his left droop on his crossed knee , - an 
 attitude rarely see in a man when his heart be light and his spirit 
 high . his lip move : he be talk to himself . though he have lay 
 aside his theatrical bandage over both eye , he wear a black patch 
 over one , or rather where one have be ; the eye expose be of singular 
 beauty , dark and brilliant . for the rest , the man have a striking 
 countenance , rugge , and rather ugly than otherwise , but by no mean 
 unprepossessing ; full of line and wrinkle and strong muscle , with 
 large lip of wondrous pliancy , and an aspect of wistful sagacity , that , 
 no doubt , on occasion could become exquisitely comic , - dry comedy , - the 
 comedy that make other roar when the comedian himself be as grave as a 
 judge . 

 you might see in his countenance , when quite in its natural repose , that 
 Sorrow have pass by there ; yet the instant the countenance break into 
 play , you would think that Sorrow must have be send about her business 
 as soon as the respect due to that visitor , so accustomed to have she 
 own way , would permit . though the man be old , you could not call 
 he aged . one - eyed and crippled , still , mark the muscular arm , the 
 expansive chest , you would have scarcely call he break or infirm . 
 and hence there be a certain indescribable pathos in his whole 
 appearance , as if Fate have brand , on face and form , character in 
 which might be read her agency on career and mind , - pluck an eye 
 from intelligence , shorten one limb for life 's progress , yet leave whim 
 sparkle out in the eye she have spar , and a light heart 's wild spring 
 in the limb she have maim not . 

 " come , Grandy , come , " say the little girl , coaxingly ; " your tea will 
 get quite cold ; your toast be ready , and here be such a nice egg ; Mr. 
 Merle say you may be sure it be new lay . come , do not   let that hateful 
 man fret you : smile on your own Sophy ; come . " 

 " if , " say Mr. Waife , in a hollow undertone , if I be alone in the 
 world - " 

 " oh , Grandy ! " 

  " ' I know a spot on which a bed - post grow , 
  and do remember where a roper live . ' 

 delightful prospect , not to be indulge ; for if I be in peace at one 
 end of the rope , what would chance to my Sophy , leave forlorn at the 
 other ? " 

 " do not   talk so , or I shall think you be sorry to have take care of 
 I . " 

 " care of thee , oh , child ! and what care ? it be thou who takest care of 
 I . put thy hand from thy mouth ; sit down , darling , there , opposite , 
 and let we talk . now , Sophy , thou hast often say that thou wouldst be 
 glad to be out of this mode of life , even for one humbler and hard : 
 think well , be it so ? " 

 " oh , yes , indeed , grandfather . " 

 " no more tinsel dress and flowery wreath ; no more applause ; no more 
 of the dear divine stage excitement ; the heroine and fairy vanish ; 
 only a little commonplace child in dingy gingham , with a purblind 
 cripple for thy sole charge and playmate ; Juliet Araminta evaporate 
 evermore into little Sophy ! " 

 " it would be so nice ! " answer little Sophy , laugh merrily . 

 " what would make it nice ? " ask the Comedian , turn on her his 
 solitary pierce eye , with curious interest in his gaze . 

 Sophy leave her seat , and place herself on a stool at her grandfather 's 
 knee ; on that knee she clasp her tiny hand , and shake aside her 
 curl , look into his face with confident fondness . evidently these two 
 be much more than grandfather and grandchild : they be friend , they 
 be equals , they be in the habit of consult and prattle with 
 each other . she get at his meaning , however covert his humour ; and he to 
 the core of her heart , through its careless babble . between you and I , 
 Reader , I suspect that , in spite of the Comedian 's sagacious wrinkle , 
 the one be as much a child as the other . 

 " well , " say Sophy , " I will tell you , Grandy , what would make it nice : 
 no one would vex and affront you , - we should be all by ourselves ; and 
 then , instead of those nasty lamp and those dreadful paint creature , 
 we could go out and play in the field and gather daisy ; and I could 
 run after butterfly , and when I be tired I should come here , where I 
 be now , any time of the day , and you would tell I story and pretty 
 verse , and teach I to write a little well than I do now , and make 
 such a wise little woman of I ; and if I wear gingham - but it need not

 she hang on his neck caressingly , kiss his furrowed brow , and leave

 Mr. Waife draw a long whiff , and take a more serene view of affair .

 " what , soft than woman ? " whisper the young reader . young reader , 
 woman tease as well as console . woman make half the sorrow which she 
 boast the privilege to soothe . woman console we , it be true , while we 
 be young and handsome ! when we be old and ugly , woman snub and scold 
 we . on the whole , then , woman in this scale , the weed in that , Jupiter , 
 hang out thy balance , and weigh they both ; and if thou give the 
 preference to woman , all I can say be , the next time Juno ruffle 
 thee , - o Jupiter , try the weed .

 CHAPTER IX . 

  the historian show that , notwithstanding the progressive spirit of 
  the time , a Briton be not permit , without an effort , " to 
  progress " accord to his own inclination . 

 Sophy could not sleep . at first she be too happy . without be 
 conscious of any degradation in her lot amongst the itinerant artist 
 of Mr. Rugge 's exhibition , - how could she , when her beloved and revere 
 protector have be one of those artist for year ? - yet instinctively 
 she shrink from their contact . Doubtless , while absorb in some 
 stir part , she forget companion , audience , all , and enjoy 
 what she perform , - necessarily enjoy , for she acting be really 
 excellent , and where no enjoyment there no excellence ; but when the 
 histrionic enthusiasm be not positively at work , she creep to she 
 grandfather with something between loathing and terror of the " paint 
 creature " and her own borrow tinsel . 

 but , more than all , she feel acutely every indignity or affront offer 
 to Gentleman Waife . Heaven know , these be not few ; and to escape from 
 such a life - to be with her grandfather alone , have he all to herself 
 to tend and to pet , to listen to and to prattle with - seem to her the 
 consummation of human felicity . ah , but should she be all alone ? just 
 as she be lull herself into a doze , that question seize and rouse 
 she . and then it be not happiness that keep she wake : it be what be 
 less rare in the female breast , curiosity . who be to be the mysterious 
 third , to whose acquisition the three pound be evidently to be 
 devote ? what new face have she purchase by the loan of her own ? not the 
 Pig - faced Lady nor the Spotted boy . could it be the Norfolk Giant or the 
 Calf with two head ? horrible idea ! monstrous phantasmagoria begin to 
 stalk before her eye ; and to charm they away , with great fervour she 
 fall to say her prayer , - an act of devotion which she have forget , 
 in her excitement , to perform before rest her head on the pillow , - an 
 omission , let we humbly hope , not note down in very dark character by 
 the record angel . 

 that act over , her thought take a more comely aspect than have be wear 
 by the precede phantasy , reflect Lionel 's kind look and repeat 
 his gentle word . " Heaven bless he ! " she say with emphasis , as a 
 supplement to the habitual prayer ; and then tear gather to her 
 grateful eyelid , for she be one of those being whose tear come 
 slow from sorrow , quick from affection . and so the gray dawn find she 
 still - wakeful , and she rise , bath her cheek in the cold fresh water , 
 and draw they forth with a glow like Hebe 's . dress herself with the 
 quiet activity which characterize all her movement , she then open 
 the casement and inhale the air . all be still in the narrow lane ; the 
 shop yet unclose . but on the still tree behind the shop the bird 
 be begin to stir and chirp . Chanticleer , from some neighbouring 
 yard , ring out his brisk rereillee . pleasant English summer dawn in the 
 pleasant english country village . she stretch her graceful neck far 
 from the casement , try to catch a glimpse of the blue river . she 
 have see its majestic flow on the day they have arrive at the fair , and 
 long to gain its bank ; then her servitude to the stage forbid she . 
 now she be to be free ! o joy ! now she might have her careless hour of 
 holiday ; and , forgetful of Waife 's warning that their vocation must 
 be ply in town , she let her fancy run riot amidst vision of green 
 field and laugh water , and in fond delusion gather the daisy 
 and chase the butterfly . Changeling transfer into that low 
 world of art from the cradle of civil Nature , her human child 's heart 
 yearn for the human childlike delight . all child love the country , 
 the flower , the sward , the bird , the butterfly ; or if some do not , 
 despair , o Philanthropy , of their afterlive ! 

 she close the window , smile to herself , steal through the adjoining 
 doorway , and see that her grandfather be still asleep . then she busy 
 herself in put the little sitting - room to right , reset the table 
 for the morning meal , water the stock , and finally take up the 
 crystal and look into it with awe , wonder why the Cobbler could 
 see so much , and she only the distorted reflection of her own face . so 
 interested , however , for once , do she become in the inspection of this 
 mystic globe , that she do not notice the dawn pass into broad daylight ,

 the external world , till a heavy tread shake the floor , and then , 
 start , she behold the Remorseless Baron , with a face black enough to 
 have darken the crystal of Dr. Dee himself .

 come ? where be your grandfather , baggage ? "

 on the Baron . 

 " your vile scamp of a grandfather ? " 

 SOPHY   with spirit . - " he be not vile . you ought to be ashamed of

 here simultaneously , Mr. Waife , hastily indue in his gray 
 dressing - gown , present himself at the aperture of the bedroom door ,

 CHAPTER X. 

  show the cause why man and nation , when one man or nation 
  wish to get for its own arbitrary purpose what the other man or 
  nation do not desire to part with , be apt to ignore the mild 
  precept of Christianity , shock the sentiment and upset the 
  theory of Peace Societies . 

 " be I to understand , " say Mr. Rugge , in a whisper , when Waife have draw

  progress of the Fine Arts . - biographical anecdote . - fluctuation in

 Vance , with surprising alacrity , slip the sovereign into the old

 CHAPTER XII . 

  in which it be show that a man do this or decline to do that for 
  reason well know to himself , - a reserve which be extremely 
  conducive to the social interest of a community , since the 
  conjecture into the origin and nature of those reason stimulate 
  the inquire faculty , and furnish the staple of modern 
  conversation . and as it be not to be deny that , if their 
  neighbour leave they nothing to guess at , three - fourth of civilized 
  humankind , male or female , would have nothing to talk about ; so we 
  can not too gratefully encourage that needful curiosity term by the 
  inconsiderate tittle - tattle or scandal , which save the vast 
  majority of our specie from be reduce to the degraded condition 
  of dumb animal . 

 the next day the sitting be renew : but Waife do not go out , and

 that which most puzzle Vance be that which have most puzzle the

 now , in what the french term the beau monde , there be little trait

 CHAPTER XIV . 

  the historian take advantage of the summer hour vouchsafe to the 
  present life of Mr. Waife 's grandchild , in order to throw a few 
  gleam of light on her past . - he lead she into the palace of our 
  king , and moralize thereon ; and , enter the Royal Gardens , show 
  the uncertainty of human event , and the insecurity of british law , 
  by the abrupt seizure and constrain deportation of an innocent and 
  unforebode Englishman . 

 such a glorious afternoon ! the capricious english summer be so kind 
 that day to the child and her new friend ! when Sophy 's small foot once 
 trod the sward , have she be really queen of the Green People , sward 
 and footstep could not more joyously have meet together . the grasshopper 
 bound in fearless trust upon the hem of her frock ; she throw herself 
 down on the grass and catch he , but , oh , so tenderly ! and the gay 
 insect , dear to poet and fairy , seem to look at she from that quaint 
 sharp face of his with sagacious recognition , rest calmly on the

 the Misses Burton ; and they keep a day - school , and teach Sophy to read ,

 and now they near that stately palace , rich in association of storm

 startle the Epicurean , " insanientis sapientiae consultus , " and strike

 CHAPTER XV . 

  the historian record the attachment to public business which 
  distinguish the british legislator . - touch instance of the 
  regret which ever in patriotic bosom attend the neglect of a 
  public duty .

 and garter : my existence shall pass from day to day in honeysuckle

 CHAPTER XVI . 

  sign of an impending revolution , which , like all revolution , seem 
  to come of a sudden , though its cause have long be at work ; and 
  to go off in a tantrum , though its effect must run on to the end of 
  a history . 

 Lionel could not find in the toy - shop of the village a doll good enough 
 to satisfy his liberal inclination , but he buy one which amply 
 content the humbler aspiration of Sophy . he then stroll to the

 soothe aid to his undeveloped philosophy . 

 he break the seal slowly ; another letter be enclose within . at

 exclamation , read on eagerly ; then , before conclude his mother 's 
 epistle , hastily tear open that which it have contain , run his eye over 
 its content , and , drop both letter on the turf below , rest his 
 face on his hand in agitated thought . thus run his mother 's letter : 

 MY DEAR BOY , - how could you ! do it slyly ! ! unknown to your own mother ! ! 
 I could not believe it of you ! ! ! ! take advantage of my confidence 
 in show you the letter of your father 's cousin , to write to 
 himself - clandestinely ! - you , who I think have such an open character , 
 and who ought to appreciate mine . every one who know I say I be a 
 woman in ten thousand , - not for beauty and talent   though I have have my 
 admirer for they too , but for GOODNESS I as a wife and mother , I may 
 say I have be exemplary . I have sore trial with the dear captain - and 
 IMMENSE temptation . but he say on his death - bed , " Jessica , you be 
 an angel . " and I have have offer since , - IMMENSE offer , - but I devote 
 myself to my child , as you know . and what I have put up with , let 
 the first floor , nobody can tell ; and only a widow 's pension , - go 
 before a magistrate to get it pay ! and to think my own child , for whom 
 I have bear so much , should behave so cruelly to I ! clandestine ! that 
 be that which stab I . Mrs. Inman find I cry , and say , " what be 
 the matter ? - you who be such an angel , cry like a baby ! " and I could 
 not help say , " ' t be the serpent 's tooth , Mrs. L " what you write 
 to your benefactor   and I have hop patron   I do not   care to guess ; 
 something very rude and imprudent it must be , judge by the few line 
 he address to I . I do not   mind copy they for you to read . all my 
 act be aboveboard , as often and often Captain H. use to say , " your 
 heart be in a glass case , Jessica ; " and so it be ! but my son keep his 
 under lock and key . 

 " Madam   this be what he write to I , your son have think fit to 
 infringe the condition upon which I agree to assist you on his behalf . 
 I enclose a reply to himself , which I beg you will give to his own hand 
 without break the seal . since it do not seem to you indiscreet to 
 communicate to a boy of his year letter write solely to yourself , 
 you can not blame I if I take your imply estimate of his capacity to 
 judge for himself of the nature of a correspondence , and of the view 
 and temper of , madam , your very obedient servant . " and that be all to I . 

 I send his letter to you , - seal unbroken . I conclude he have do with 
 you forever , and your career be lose ! but if it be so , oh , my poor , poor 
 child I at that thought I have not the heart to scold you far . if it 
 be so , come home to I , and I will work and slave for you , and you shall 
 keep up your head and be a gentleman still , as you be , every inch of 
 you . do not   mind what I have   say at the beginning , dear : do not   you 
 know I be hasty ; and I be hurt . but you could not mean to be sly and 
 underhand : ' twa only your high spirit , and it be my fault ; I should 
 not have show you the letter . I hope you be well , and have quite lose 
 that nasty cough , and that Mr. Vance treat you with proper respect . I 
 think he rather too push and familiar , though a pleasant young man 
 on the whole . but , after all , he be only a painter Bless you , my child , 
 and do not   have secret again from your poor mother . 

  JESSICA HAUGHTON . 


 the enclose letter be as follow : - 

  LIONEL HAUGHTON , - some man might be displease at receive such a 
  letter as you have address to I ; I be not . at your year , and

  GUY HAUGHTON DARRELL . 

  MANOR HOUSE , FAWLEY . 

 Sophy pick up the fallen letter , place they on Lionel 's lap , and 
 look into his face wistfully . he smile , resume his mother 's epistle , 
 and read the concluding passage , which he have before omit . their 
 sudden turn from reproof to tenderness melt he . he begin to feel that 
 his mother have a right to blame he for an act of concealment . still she 
 never would have consent to his writing such a letter ; and have that 
 letter be attend with so ill a result ? again he read Mr. Darrell 's 
 blunt but not offensive line . his pride be soothe : why should he not 
 now love his father 's friend ? he rise briskly , pay for the fruit , and 
 go his way back to the boat with Sophy . as his oar cut the wave he 
 talk gayly , but he cease to interrogate Sophy on her past . energetic , 
 sanguine , ambitious , his own future enter now into his thought . 
 still , when the sun sink as the inn come partially into view from the 
 winding of the bank and the fringe of the willow , his mind again 
 settle on the patient , quiet little girl , who have not venture to ask 
 he one question in return for all he have put so unceremoniously to she . 
 indeed , she be silently muse over word he have inconsiderately let 
 fall , - " what I hate to think you have ever stoop to perform . " little 
 could Lionel guess the unquiet thought which those word might 
 hereafter call forth from the brood deepening meditation of lonely 
 childhood ! at length say the boy abruptly , as he have say once before , 

 " I wish , Sophy , you be my sister . " he add in a sadden tone , " I 
 never have a sister : I have so long for one ! however , surely we shall 
 meet again . you go to - morrow so must I. " 

 Sophy 's tear flow softly , noiselessly . 

 " cheer up , lady - bird , I wish you like I half as much as I like you ! " 

 " I do like you : oh , so much ! " cry Soppy , passionately . " well , then ,

 " a little . " 

 " you shall write to I now and then , and I to you . I will talk to your 
 grandfather about it . ah , there he be , surely ! " the boat now run into

 " you be late , " say the actor , as they land , and Sophy spring into

 have not catch cold , child ? "

 LIONEL . - " she be the good of child . pray , come into the inn , Mr. 
 Waife ; no toddy , but some refreshment . " 

 WAIFE . - " I thank you , - no , sir ; I wish to get home at once . I walk 
 slowly ; it will be dark soon . " 

 Lionel try in vain to detain he . there be a certain change in Mr. 
 Waife 's manner to he : it be much more distant ; it be even pettish ,

 " I see you wish to have your grandchild to yourself now . may I call 
 early to - morrow ? Sophy will tell you that I hope we may not altogether 
 lose sight of each other . I will give you my address when I call . " 

 " what time to - morrow , sir ? " 

 " about nine . " 

 Waife bow his head and walk on , but Sophy look back towards her 
 boy friend , sorrowfully , gratefully ; twilight in the sky that have be 
 so sunny , - twilight in her face that have be so glad ! she look back 
 once , twice , thrice , as Lionel halt on the road and kiss his hand . 
 the third time Waife say with unwonted crossness , - 

 " enough of that , Sophy ; look after young man be not proper ! what do 
 he mean about ' see each other , and give I his address ' ? " 

 " he wish I to write to he sometimes and he would write to I . " 

 Waife 's brow contract ; but if , in the excess of grandfatherly 
 caution , he could have suppose that the bright - hearted boy of seventeen 
 meditate ulterior ill to that fairy child in such a scheme for 
 correspondence , he must have be in his dotage , and he have not hitherto 
 evince any sign of that . 

 Farewell , pretty Sophy ! the evening star shin upon yon elm - tree that 
 hide thee from view . fading - fading grow the summer landscape ; fade 
 already from the landscape thy gentle image ! so end a holiday in life . 
 Hallow it , Sophy ; hallow it , Lionel ! Life 's holiday be not too many ! 




 CHAPTER XVII . 

  by this chapter it appeareth that he who set out on a career can 
  scarcely expect to walk in perfect comfort , if he exchange his own 
  thick - sole shoe for dress - boot which be make for another man 's 
  measure , and that the say boot may not the less pinch for be 
  brilliantly varnish . - it also showeth , for the instruction of Men 
  and States , the connection between democratic opinion and wound 
  self - love ; so that , if some liberal statesman desire to rouse 
  against an aristocracy the class just below it , he have only to 
  persuade a fine lady to be exceedingly civil " to that sort of 
  people . " 

 Vance , return late at night , find his friend still up in the little 
 parlour , the window open , pace the floor with restless stride , 
 stop now and then to look at the moon upon the river .

  ' to find my welcome at an inn . '

 lionel . - " no , I find I can get across the country , - two hour by the

 " you never tell I your cousin 's name be Darrell : not that I should

 lionel . - " Famous ! - nonsense . I suppose he be a good lawyer , for I have

 talk of Mr. Darrell ? - they would not have know his name if you have

 lionel . - " certainly not . "

 Landseer , of Turner , Maclise ? I speak of painter . "

 VANCE   soliloquize . - " and yet her serene sublimity - ship , Lady Selina 
 Vipont , say to I with divine compassion , ' not in the way of your

 jacket , ' what matter how you dress ? every one know who you be ! ' 
 would she have say that to the earl of Dunder , or even to Sir Gregory 
 Stollhead ? no . I be the painter Frank Vance , - nothing more nor less ; and 
 if I stand on my head in a check shirt and a sky - colour apron , Lady 
 Selina Vipont would kindly murmur , ' only Frank Vance the painter : what 
 do it signify ? ' aha ! - and they think to put I to use , puppet and lay

 become of their set , by right of your famous Mr. Darrell ; if ever you 
 hear an artist , musician , scribbler , no matter what , ridicule as a 
 tuft - hunter , - seek the great , and so forth , - before you join in the 
 laugh , ask some great man 's son , with a pedigree that date from the 
 Ark , ' be you not a toad - eater too ? do you want political influence ; do 
 you stand contested election ; do you curry and fawn upon greasy Sam

 wine . nothing so treacherous ! they inflame the brain like fire , while

 be quarrelsome and democratic . 

 lionel   astounded . - " no one , I be sure , could have mean to call you a

 CHAPTER XVIII . 

  be devote exclusively to a reflection , not inapposite to the 
  event in this history nor to those in any other which chronicle 
  the life of man . 

 there be one warning lesson in life which few of we have not receive , 
 and no book that I can call to memory have note down with an adequate 
 emphasis . it be this : " beware of part ! " the true sadness be not in 
 the pain of the parting , it be in the when and the how you be to meet 
 again with the face about to vanish from your view ! from the passionate 
 farewell to the woman who have your heart in her keeping , to the cordial 
 good - by exchange with pleasant companion at a watering - place , a 
 country - house , or the close of a festive day 's blithe and careless 
 excursion , - a cord , strong or weak , be snap asunder in every 
 parting , and Time 's busy finger be not practise in re - splicing break 
 tie . meet again you may ; will it be in the same way ? - with the same 
 sympathy ? - with the same sentiment ? will the soul , hurry on in 
 diverse path , unite once more , as if the interval have be a dream ? 
 rarely , rarely ! have you not , after even a year , even a month 's absence , 
 return to the same place , find the same group reassemble , and yet 
 sigh to yourself , " but where be the charm that once breathe from the 
 spot , and once smile from the face ? " a poet have say , " eternity itself 
 can not restore the loss strike from the minute . " be you happy in the 
 spot on which you tarry with the person whose voice be now melodious 
 to your ear ? beware of parting ; or , if part you must , say not in 
 insolent defiance to Time and Destiny , " what matter ! - we shall soon 
 meet again . " 

 Alas , and alas ! when we think of the lip which murmur , " soon meet 
 again , " and remember how in heart , soul , and think , we stand forever 
 divide the one from the other , when , once more face to face , we each 
 inly exclaim , " Met again ! " 

 the air that we breathe make the medium through which sound be 
 convey ; be the instrument unchanged , be the force which be apply to 
 it the same , still the air that thou se not , the air to thy ear give 
 the music . 

 ring a bell underneath an exhausted receiver , thou wilt scarce hear the 
 sound ; give the bell due vibration by free air in warm daylight , or sink 
 it down to the heart of the ocean , where the air , all compress , fill 
 the vessel around it , ' and the chime , hear afar , start thy soul , 
 check thy footstep , unto deep call the deep , - a voice from the ocean 
 be bear to thy soul . 

 where then the change , when thou sayest , " Lo , the same metal , - why so 
 faint - hear the ringing ? " ask the air that thou se not , or above 
 thee in sky , or below thee in ocean . art thou sure that the bell , so 
 faint - heard , be not strike underneath an exhausted receiver ?

  the wandering inclination of nomad tribe not to be account for

  the pocket of a nomad , neither lawful art nor occult science can , 
  with certainty , discover what he will do with it . - Mr. Vance 
  narrowly escape well - merit punishment from the nail of the 
  British Fair - Lionel Haughton , in the temerity of youth , brave the 
  danger of a British Railway .

 within the gate of the golden Carduel an equal in the list with the

 first floor in the back street of Pimlico ? truth to say , as he stride

 penniless gentleman , who must work his way up like a man , than as one

 soil honour of man . " therefore take into account the poor cousin 's 
 vigilant pride on the qui vive for offence , and the rich cousin 's 
 temper   as judge by his letter   rude enough to resent it , we must own
