 chance , " the question as yet be not , what be that chance ? but , what 
 will he do with it ? and as the reader advance in this history , he will 
 acknowledge that there be few question in this world so frequently 
 agitated , to which the solution be more important to each puzzle mortal 
 than that upon which start every sage 's discovery , every novelist 's 
 plot , - that which apply to MAN 'S LIFE , from its first sleep in the 
 cradle , " what will he do with it ? " 




 BOOK II . 




 CHAPTER I. 

  Primitive character of the country in certain district of Great 
  Britain . - connection between the feature of surround scenery and 
  the mental and moral inclination of man , after the fashion of all 
  sound ethnological historian . - a charioteer , to whom an experience 
  of british law suggest an ingenious mode of arrest the progress 
  of Roman Papacy , carry Lionel Haughton and his fortune to a place 
  which allow of description and invite repose . 

 in safety , but with naught else rare enough , in a railway train , to 
 deserve commemoration , Lionel reach the station to which he be bind . 
 he there inquire the distance to Fawley Manor House ; it be five mile . 
 he order a fly , and be soon wheel briskly along a rough parish 
 road , through a country strongly contrast the gay river scenery he 
 have so lately quit , - quite as English , but rather the England of a 
 former race than that which spread round our own generation like one 
 vast suburb of garden - ground and villa . here , nor village nor spire , 
 nor porter 's lodge come in sight . Rare even be the cornfield ; wide 
 space of unenclosed common open , solitary and primitive , on the road , 
 border by large wood , chiefly of beech , close the horizon with 
 ridge of undulate green . in such an England , Knights Templars might 
 have wend their way to scatter monastery , or fugitive partisan in 
 the bloody war of the Roses have find shelter under leafy covert . 

 the scene have its romance , its beauty - half savage , half gentle - lead 
 perforce the mind of any cultivate and imaginative gazer far back from 
 the present day , wake up long - forget passage from old poet . the 
 stillness of such waste of sward , such deep of woodland , induce the 
 nurture of revery , gravely soft and lulling . there , ambition might give 
 rest to the wheel of Ixion , Avarice to the sieve of the Danaids ; there , 
 disappoint Love might muse on the brevity of all human passion , and 
 count over the torture heart that have find peace in holy meditation , 
 or be now still under grassy knoll . see where , at the crossing of 
 three road upon the waste , the landscape suddenly unfold , an upland 
 in the distance , and on the upland a building , the first sign of social 
 man . what be the building ? only a silence windmill , the sail dark and 
 sharp against the dull leaden sky . 

 Lionel touch the driver , - " be we yet on Mr. Darrell 's property ? " of 
 the extent of that property he have involuntarily conceive a vast idea . 

 " Lord , sir , no ; we be two mile from Squire Darrell 's . he han't   much 
 property to speak of hereabout . but he buy a good bit o ' land , too , 
 some year ago , ten or twelve mile t ' other side o ' the county . first 
 time you be go to Fawley , sir ? " 

 " yes . " 

 " ah ! I do not   mind see you afore ; and I should have know you if I 
 have , for it be seldom indeed I have a fare to Fawley old Manor House . it 
 must be , I take it , four or five year ago sin ' I wor there with a gent , 
 and he go away while I wor feed the horse ; do I out o ' my back 
 fare . what bisness have he to walk when he come in my fly ? Shabby . " 

 " Mr. Darrell live very retire , then ? see few person ? " " s'pose so . I 
 never seed he as I know on ; see'd two o ' his hosse though , - rare good 
 un ; " and the driver whip on his own horse , take to whistling , and 
 Lionel ask no more . 

 at length the chaise stop at a carriage gate , recede from the 
 road , and deeply shadow by venerable tree , - no lodge . the driver , 
 dismounting , open the gate . 

 " be this the place ? " 

 the driver nod assent , remount , and drive on rapidly through what 
 night by courtesy he call a park . the enclosure be indeed little 
 beyond that of a good - sized paddock ; its boundary be visible on 
 every side : but swell upland cover with massy foliage slop down 
 to its wild , irregular turf soil , - soil poor for pasturage , but pleasant 
 to the eye ; with dell and dingle , bosk of fantastic pollard ; dot 
 oak of vast growth ; here and there a weird hollow thorn - tree ; patch 
 of fern and gorse . Hoarse and loud caw the rook ; and deep , deep as 
 from the innermost core of the lovely woodland come the mellow note of 
 the cuckoo . a few moment more a wind of the road bring the house in 
 sight . at its rear lay a piece of water , scarcely large enough to be 
 style a lake ; too wind in its shaggy bank , its end too conceal 
 by tree and islet , to be call by the dull name of pond . such as it be 
 it arrest the eye before the gaze turn towards the house : it have an 
 air of tranquillity so sequester , so solemn . a lively man of the world 
 would have be seize with spleen at the first glimpse of it ; but he 
 who have know some great grief , some anxious care , would have drink 
 the calm into his weary soul like an anodyne . the house , - small , low , 
 ancient , about the date of Edward VI . , before the stately architecture 
 of Elizabeth . few house in England so old , indeed , as Fawley Manor 
 House . a vast weight of roof , with high gable ; window on the upper 
 story project far over the low part ; a cover porch with a coat 
 of half - obliterate arm deep panel over the oak door . nothing grand , 
 yet all how venerable ! but what be this ? close beside the old , quiet , 
 unassume Manor House rise the skeleton of a superb and costly 
 pile , - a palace uncomplete , and the work evidently suspend , - perhaps 
 long since , perhaps now forever . no busy workman nor animate 
 scaffold . the perforated battlement roof over with visible 
 haste , - here with slate , there with tile ; the Elizabethan mullion 
 casement unglazed ; some roughly board across , - some with star 
 forlorn aperture , that show floorless chamber , for wind to whistle 
 through and rat to tenant . weed and long grass be grow over 
 block of stone that lie at hand . a wallflower have force itself into 
 root on the sill of a giant oriel . the effect be startling . a fabric 
 which he who conceive it must have found for posterity , - so solid 
 its masonry , so thick its wall , - and thus abruptly leave to moulder ; 
 a palace construct for the reception of crowd guest , the pomp 
 of stately revel , abandon to owl and bat . and the homely old house 
 beside it , which that lordly hall be doubtless design to replace , 
 look so safe and tranquil at the baffled presumption of its spectral 
 neighbour . 

 the driver have ring the bell , and now turn back to the chaise meet 
 Lionel 's inquire eye , and say , " yes ; Squire Darrell begin to build 
 that - many year ago - when I be a boy . I heerd say it be to be the

 the driver have thus just disposed of the Papacy , when an elderly servant

 Haughton . I come here on Mr. Darrell 's invitation . " 

 the servant 's face change in a moment ; he bow respectfully . " I beg 
 pardon , sir . I will look for my master ; he be somewhere on the ground . " 
 the servant then approach the fly , take out the knapsack , and , 
 observe Lionel have his purse in his hand , say , " allow I to save you 
 that trouble , sir . driver , round to the stable - yard . " step back into 
 the house , the servant throw open a door to the left , on entrance , and 
 advance a chair . " if you will wait here a moment , sir , I will seek for 
 my master . " 




 CHAPTER II . 

 Guy Darrell - and Stilled Life . 

 the room in which Lionel now find himself be singularly quaint . an 
 antiquarian or architect would have discover at a glance that at some 
 period it have form part of the entrance - hall ; and when , in Elizabeth 's 
 or James the First 's day , the refinement in manner begin to penetrate 
 from baronial mansion to the home of the gentry , and the entrance - hall 
 cease to be the common refectory of the owner and his dependant , this 
 apartment have be screen off by perforated panel , which for the 
 sake of warmth and comfort have be fill up into solid wainscot by a 
 succeed generation . thus one side of the room be richly carve with 
 geometrical design and arabesque pilaster , while the other three side 
 be in small simple panel , with a deep fantastic frieze in plaster , 
 depict a deer - chase in relief and running be tween woodwork and 
 ceiling . the ceiling itself be relieve by long pendant without any 
 apparent meaning , and by the crest of the Darrells , - a heron , wreathe 
 round with the family motto , " Ardua petit Ardea . " it be a dining - room , 
 as be show by the character of the furniture . but there be no attempt 
 on the part of the present owner , and there have clearly be none on 
 the part of his predecessor , to suit the furniture to the room . the 
 furniture , indeed , be of the heavy , graceless taste of George the 
 first , - cumbrous chair in walnut - tree , with a worm - eat mosaic of the 
 heron on their homely back , and a fade blue worst on their seat ; 
 a marvellously ugly sideboard to match , and on it a couple of black 
 shagreen case , the lid of which be fling open , and discover the 
 pistol - shape handle of silver knife . the mantelpiece reach to the 
 ceiling , in panel compartment , with heraldic shield , and support 
 by rude stone caryatide . on the wall be several picture , - family 
 portrait , for the name be inscribe on the frame . they vary in 
 date from the reign of Elizabeth to that of George I. a strong 
 family likeness pervade they all , - high feature , dark hair , grave 
 aspect , - save indeed one , a Sir Ralph Haughton Darrell , in a dress 
 that speak he of the holiday date of Charles II . , - all knot , lace , and 
 ribbon ; evidently the beau of the race ; and he have blue eye , a 
 blonde peruke , a careless profligate smile , and look altogether as 
 devil - I - care , rakehelly , handsome , good - for - nought , as ever swear at a 
 drawer , beat a watchman , charm a lady , terrify a husband , and hum

 CHAPTER III . 

  in our happy country every man 's house be his castle . but however 
  stoutly he fortify it , Care enter , as surely as she do in Horace 's 
  time , through the portico of a Roman 's villa . nor , whether 
  ceiling be fret with gold and ivory , or whether only colour 
  with whitewash , do it matter to care any more than it do to a 
  house - fly . but every tree , be it cedar or blackthorn , can harbour 
  its singing - bird ; and few be the home in which , from nooks least 
  suspect , there start not a music . be it quite true that , " non 
  avium citharaeque cantus somnum reducent " ? would not even Damocles 
  himself have forget the sword , if the lute - player have chance on 
  the note that lull ? 

 the dinner be simple enough , but well dressed and well serve . one 
 footman , in plain livery , assist Mr. Mills . Darrell eat sparingly , and 
 drank only water , which be place by his side ice , with a single glass 
 of wine at the close of the repast , which he drink on bend his head 
 to Lionel , with a certain knightly grace , and the prefatory word of 
 " welcome here to a Haughton . " Mr. Fairthorn be less abstemious ; taste 
 of every dish , after examine it long through a pair of tortoise - shell 
 spectacle , and drink leisurely through a bottle of port , hold up 
 every glass to the light . Darrell talk with his usual cold but not 
 uncourteous indifference . a remark of Lionel on the portrait in the 
 room turn the conversation chiefly upon picture , and the host show 
 himself thoroughly accomplish in the attribute of the various school 
 and master . Lionel , who be very fond of the art , and indeed paint 
 well for a youthful amateur , listen with great delight . 

 " surely , sir , " say he , strike much with a very subtile observation 
 upon the cause why the italian master admit of copyist with great 
 facility than the Flemish , - ' surely , sir , you yourself must have 
 practise the art of painting ? " 

 " not I ; but I instruct myself as a judge of picture , because at one 
 time I be a collector . " 

 Fairthorn , speak for the first time : " the rare collection , - such 
 Albert Durers ! such holbein ! and that head by Leonardo da Vinci ! " 
 he stop ; look extremely frightened ; help himself to the port , 
 turn his back upon his host , to hold , as usual , the glass to the 
 light . 

 " be they here , sir ? " ask Lionel . 

 Darrell 's face darken , and he make no answer ; but his head sink on his 
 breast , and he seem suddenly absorb in gloomy thought . Lionel 
 feel that he have touch a wrong chord , and glance timidly towards 
 Fairthorn ; but that gentleman cautiously hold up his finger , and then 
 rapidly put it to his lip , and as rapidly draw it away . after that 
 signal the boy do not dare to break the silence , which now last 
 uninterruptedly till Darrell rise , and with the formal and superfluous 
 question , " any more wine ? " lead the way back to the library . there he 
 ensconce himself in an easy - chair , and say , " will you find a book 
 for yourself , Lionel ? " take a volume at random from the near shelf , 
 and soon seem absorb in its content . the room , make irregular by 
 baywindow , and shelf that project as in public library , abound 
 with nook and recess . to one of these Fairthorn sidle himself , and 
 become invisible . Lionel look round the shelf . no belles lettre of 
 our immediate generation be find there ; none of those author most 
 in request in circulate library and literary institute . the shelf 
 disclose no poet , no essayist , no novelist , more recent than the 
 Johnsonian age . neither in the lawyer 's library be to be find any law 
 book ; no , nor the pamphlet and parliamentary volume that should have 
 speak of the once eager politician . but there be superb copy of the 
 ancient classic . french and italian author be not want , nor such 
 of the English as have withstand the test of time . the large portion 
 of the shelf seem , however , devote to philosophical work . here 
 alone be novelty admit , the new essay on science , or the good 
 edition of old work thereon . Lionel at length make his choice , - a 
 volume of the " Faerie Queene . " Coffee be serve ; at a late hour tea . 
 the clock strike ten . Darrell lay down his book . 

 " Mr. Fairthorn , the flute ! " 

 from the recess a mutter ; and presently - the musician remain still 
 hidden - there come forth the sweetest note , - so dulcet , so plaintive ! 
 Lionel 's ear be ravish . the music suit well with the enchanted page 
 through which his fancy have be wander dreamlike , - the flute with 
 the " Faerie Queene . " as the air flow liquid on , Lionel 's eye fill 
 with tear . he do not observe that Darrell be intently watch he . 
 when the music stop , he turn aside to wipe the tear from his eye . 
 somehow or other , what with the poem , what with the flute , his thought 
 have wander far , far hence to the green bank and blue wave of the 
 Thames , - to Sophy 's charming face , to her part childish gift ! and 
 where be she now ? whither pass away , after so brief a holiday , into 
 the shadow of forlorn life ? Darrell 's bell - like voice smite his ear . 

 " Spenser ; you love he ! do you write poetry ? " " no , sir : I only feel it ! " 

 " do neither ! " say the host , abruptly . then , turn away , he light 
 his candle , murmur a quick good - night , and disappear through a 
 side - door which lead to his own room . 

 Lionel look round for Fairthorn , who now emerge ab anqulo from his 
 nook . 

 " oh , Mr. Fairthorn , how you have enchant I ! I never believe the 
 flute could have be capable of such effect ! " 

 Mr. Fairthorn 's grotesque face light up . he take off his spectacle , 
 as if the well to contemplate the face of his eulogist . " so you be 
 pleased ! really ? " he say , chuckle a strange , grim chuckle , deep in 
 his inmost self . 

 " please ! it be a cold word ! who would not be more than pleased ? " 

 " you should hear I in the open air . " 

 " let I do so - to - morrow . " 

 " my dear young sir , with all my heart . hist ! " - gaze round as if 
 haunt , - " I like you . I wish he to like you . answer all his question 
 as if you do not care how he turn you inside out . never ask he a 
 question , as if you seek to know what he do not himself confide . so 
 there be some thing , you think , in a flute , after all ? there be people 
 who prefer the fiddle . " 

 " then they never hear your flute , Mr. Fairthorn . " the musician again 
 emit his discordant chuckle , and , nod his head nervously and

 CHAPTER IV . 

  the old world and the new . 

 it be long before Lionel could sleep . what with the strange house 
 and the strange master , what with the magic flute and the musician 's 
 admonitory caution , what with tender and regretful reminiscence of 
 Sophy , his brain have enough to work on . when he sleep at last , his 
 slumber be deep and heavy , and he do not wake till gently shake by 
 the well - breed arm of Mr. Mills . " I humbly beg pardon : nine o'clock , sir , 
 and the breakfast - bell go to ring . " Lionel 's toilet be soon hurry 
 over ; Mr. Darrell and Fairthorn be talk together as he enter the 
 breakfast - room , - the same room as that in which they have din . 

 " good morning , Lionel , " say the host . " no leave - taking to - day , as you 
 threaten . I find you have make an appointment with Mr. Fairthorn , 
 and I shall place you under his care . you may like to look over the 
 old house , and make yourself " - Darrell pause " at home , " jerk out Mr. 
 Fairthorn , fill up the hiatus . Darrell turn his eye towards the 
 speaker , who evidently become much frightened , and , after look in 
 vain for a corner , sidle away to the window and poke himself behind 
 the curtain . " Mr. Fairthorn , in the capacity of my secretary , have 
 learn to find I thought , and put they in his own word , " say 
 Darrell , with a coldness almost icy . he then seat himself at the 
 breakfast - table ; Lionel follow his example , and Mr. Fairthorn , 
 courageously emerge , also take a chair and a roll . " you be a true 
 diviner , Mr. Darrell , " say Lionel ; " it be a glorious day . " 

 " but there will be shower later . the fish be at play on the surface of 
 the lake , " Darrell add , with a soften glance towards Fairthorn , who 
 be look the picture of misery . " after twelve , it will be just the 
 weather for trout to rise ; and if you fish , Mr. Fairthorn will lend you 
 a rod . he be a worthy successor of Izaak Walton , and love a companion 
 as Izaak do , but more rarely get one . " 

 " be there trout in your lake , sir ? " 

 " the lake ! you must not dream of invade that sacred water . the 
 inhabitant of rivulet and brook not within my boundary be beyond the 
 pale of Fawley civilization , to be snare and slaughter like caifre , 
 red man , or any other savage , for whom we bait with a missionary 
 and whom we impale on a bayonet . but I regard my lake as a politic 
 community , under the protection of the law , and leave its denizen 
 to devour each other , as Europeans , fish , and other cold - blooded 
 creature wisely do , in order to check the overgrowth of population . to 
 fatten one pike it take a great many minnow . naturally I support the 
 vest right of pike . I have be a lawyer . " 

 it would be in vain to describe the manner in which Mr. Darrell vent 
 this or similar remark of mock irony or sarcastic spleen . it be 
 not bitter nor sneering , but in his usual mellifluous level tone and 
 passionless tranquillity . 

 the breakfast be just over as a groom pass in front of the window 
 with a led horse . " I be go to leave you , Lionel , " say the host , " to 
 make - friend with Mr. Fairthorn , and I thus complete , accord to 
 my own original intention , the sentence which he divert astray . " he 
 pass across the hall to the open house - door , and stand by the horse , 
 stroke its neck and give some direction to the groom . Lionel 
 and Fairthorn follow to the threshold , and the beauty of the horse 
 provoke the boy 's admiration : it be a dark muzzled brown , of that 
 fine old - fashioned breed of english roadster which be now so seldom 
 see , - showy , bownecke , long - tail , stumble , reedy hybrid , bear 
 of bad barb , ill - mated , have mainly supply their place . this be , 
 indeed , a horse of great power , immense girth of loin , high shoulder , 
 broad hoof ; and such a head ! the ear , the frontal , the nostril ! you 
 seldom see a human physiognomy half so intelligent , half so expressive 
 of that high spirit and sweet generous temper , which , when unite , 
 constitute the ideal of thorough - breeding , whether in horse or man . the 
 english rider be in harmony with the english steed . darrell at this 
 moment be rest his arm lightly on the animal 's shoulder , and his 
 head still uncover . it have be say before that he be , of impose 
 presence ; the striking attribute of his person , indeed , be that of 
 unconscious grandeur ; yet , though above the ordinary height , he be not 
 very tall - five foot eleven at the utmost - and far from be very erect . 
 on the contrary , there be that habitual bend in his proud neck which 
 man who meditate much and live alone almost invariably contract . but 
 there be , to use an expression common with our old writer , that 
 " great air " about he which fill the eye , and give he the dignity 
 of elevated stature , the commanding aspect that accompany the upright 
 carriage . his figure be incline to be slender , though broad of 
 shoulder and deep of chest ; it be the figure of a young man and 
 probably little change from what it might have be at five - and - twenty . 
 a certain youthfulness still linger even on the countenance , - strange , 
 for sorrow be suppose to expedite the work of age ; and Darrell have 
 know sorrow of a kind most adapt to harrow his peculiar nature , 
 as great in its degree as ever leave man 's heart in ruin . no gray be 
 visible in the dark brown hair , that , wear short behind , still retain 
 in front the large jove - like curl . no wrinkle , save at the corner of the 
 eye , mar the pale bronze of the firm cheek ; the forehead be 
 smooth as marble , and as massive . it be that forehead which chiefly 
 contribute to the superb expression of his whole aspect . it be high 
 to a fault ; the perceptive organ , over a dark , strongly - mark , arched 
 eyebrow , powerfully develop , as they be with most eminent lawyer ; it 
 do not want for breadth at the temple ; yet , on the whole , it bespoke 
 more of intellectual vigour and dauntless will than of serene philosophy 
 or all - embrace benevolence . it be the forehead of a man form to 
 command and awe the passion and intellect of other by the strength 
 of passion in himself , rather concentre than chastise , and by an 
 intellect forceful from the weight of its mass rather than the niceness 
 of its balance . the other feature harmonize with that brow ; they be 
 of the noble order of aquiline , at once high and delicate . the lip have 
 a rare combination of exquisite refinement and inflexible resolve . the 
 eye , in repose , be cold , bright , unrevealing , with a certain absent , 
 musing , self - absorb expression , that often make the man 's word appear 
 as if speak mechanically , and assist towards that seem of listless 
 indifference to those whom he address , by which he wound vanity 
 without , perhaps , any malice prepense . but it be an eye in which the 
 pupil could suddenly expand , the hue change from gray to dark , and the 
 cold still brightness flash into vivid fire . it could not have occur 
 to any one , even to the most commonplace woman , to have describe 
 Darrell 's as a handsome face ; the expression would have seem trivial 
 and derogatory ; the word that would have occur to all , would have 
 be somewhat to this effect : " what a magnificent countenance ! what a 
 noble head ! " yet an experienced physiognomist might have note that the 
 same lineament which bespoke a virtue bespoke also its neighbouring 
 vice ; that with so much will there go stubborn obstinacy ; that with 
 that power of grasp there would be the tenacity in adherence which 
 narrow , in astringe , the intellect ; that a prejudice once conceive , 
 a passion once cherish , would resist all rational argument for 
 relinquishment . when man of this mould do relinquish prejudice or 
 passion , it be by their own impulse , their own sure conviction that what 
 they hold be worthless : then they do not yield it graciously ; they fle 
 it from they in scorn , but not a scorn that console . that which they 
 thus wrench away have " grow a live part of themselves ; " their own 
 flesh bleed ; the wound seldom or never heal . such man rarely fail 
 in the achievement of what they covet , if the god be neutral ; but , 
 adamant against the world , they be vulnerable through their affection . 
 their love be intense , but undemonstrative ; their hatred implacable , but 
 unrevengeful , - too proud to revenge , too gall to pardon . 

 there stand Guy Darrell , to whom the bar have destine its high 
 honour , to whom the senate have accord its most rapturous cheer ; and 
 the more you gaze on he as he there stand , the more perplexed became 
 the enigma , - how with a career seek with such energy , advance with 
 such success , the man have abruptly subside into a listless recluse , and 
 the career have be voluntarily resign for a home without neighbour , 
 a hearth without child . 

 " I have no idea , " say Lionel , as Darrell ride slowly away , soon lose 
 from sight amidst the thick foliage of summer tree , - " I have no idea 
 that my cousin be so young ! " 

 " oh , yes , " say Mr. Fairthorn ; " he be only a year old than I be ! " 

 " old than you ! " exclaim Lionel , star in blunt amaze at the 
 elderly - look personage beside he ; " yet true , he tell I so himself . " 

 " and I be fifty - one last birthday . " " Mr. Darrell fifty - two ! incredible ! " 

 " I do not   know why we should ever grow old , the life we lead , " observe 
 Mr. Fairthorn , readjust his spectacle . " time stand so still ! 
 Fishing , too , be very conducive to longevity . if you will follow I , we 
 will get the rod ; and the flute , - you be quite sure you would like the 
 flute ? yes ! thank you , my dear young sir . and yet there be folk who 
 prefer the fiddle ! " 

 " be not the sun a little too bright for the fly at present ; and will you 
 not , in the meanwhile , show I over the house ? " 

 " very well ; not that this house have much worth see . the other indeed 
 would have have a music - room ! but , after all , nothing like the open air 
 for the flute . this way . " 

 I spare thee , gentle reader , the minute inventory of Fawley Manor House . 
 it have nothing but its antiquity to recommend it . it have a great many 
 room , all , except those use as the dining - room and library , very 
 small , and very low , - innumerable closet , nook , - unexpected cavity , 
 as if make on purpose for the venerable game of hide - and - seek . save 
 a stately old kitchen , the office be sadly defective even for Mr. 
 Darrell 's domestic establishment , which consist but of two man and 
 four maid   the stableman not lodge in the house . drawing - room 
 properly speak that primitive mansion have none . at some remote 
 period a sort of gallery under the gable roof   above the first floor , 
 stretch from end to end of the house , might have serve for the 
 reception of guest on grand occasion ; for fragment of moulder 
 tapestry still here and there clung to the wall ; and a high 
 chimney - piece , whereon , in plaster relief , be commemorate the 
 memorable fishing party of Antony and Cleopatra , retain patch of 
 colour and gilding , which must when fresh have make the egyptian queen 
 still more appallingly hideous , and the fish at the end of Antony 's hook 
 still less resemble any creature know to ichthyologist . 

 the library have be arrange into shelf from floor to roof by Mr. 
 Darrell 's father , and subsequently , for the mere purpose of hold 
 as many volume as possible , bring out into project wing 
  college - like   by Darrell himself , without any pretension to mediaeval 
 character . with this room communicate a small reading - closet , which 
 the host reserve to himself ; and this , by a circular stair cut into the 
 massive wall , ascend first into Mr. Darrell 's sleeping - chamber , and 
 thence into a gable recess that adjoin the gallery , and which the host 
 have fit up for the purpose of scientific experiment in chemistry or 
 other branch of practical philosophy . these more private room Lionel 
 be not permit to enter . altogether the house be one of those cruel 
 tenement which it would be a sin to pull down , or even materially to 
 alter , but which it would be an hourly inconvenience for a modern 
 family to inhabit . it be out of all character with Mr. Darrell 's former 
 position in life , or with the fortune which Lionel vaguely suppose he 
 to possess , and considerably underrated . like Sir Nicholas Bacon , the 
 man have grow too large for his habitation . 

 " I do not   wonder , " say Lionel , as , their wandering over , he and 
 Fairthorn find themselves in the library , " that Mr. Darrell begin to 
 build a new house . but it would have be a great pity to pull down this 
 for it . " 

 " pull down this ! do not   hint at such an idea to Mr. Darrell . he would as 
 soon have pull down the British Monarchy ! Nay , I suspect , soon . " 

 " but the new building must surely have swallow up the old one ? " 

 " oh , no ; Mr. Darrell have a plan by which he would have enclose this 
 separately in a kind of court , with an open screen - work or cloister ; 
 and it be his intention to appropriate it entirely to mediaeval 
 antiquity , of which he have a wonderful collection . he have a notion 
 of illustrate every early reign in which his ancestor 
 flourish , - different apartment in correspondence with different 
 date . it would have be a chronicle of national manner . " 

 " but , if it be not an impertinent question , where be this collection ? in 
 London ? " 

 " hush ! hush ! I will give you a peep of some of the treasure , only do not 
 betray I . " 

 Fairthorn here , with singular rapidity , consider that he never move 
 in a straightforward direction , undulate into the open air in front 
 of the house , describe a rhomboid towards a side - buttress in the new 
 building , near to which be a postern - door ; unlock that door from a 
 key in his pocket , and , motion Lionel to follow he , enter 
 within the rib of the stony skeleton . Lionel follow in a sort of 
 supernatural awe , and beheld , with more substantial alarm , Mr. Fairthorn 
 wind up an inclined plank which lie embrace with both arm , and by 
 which he ultimately ascend to a timber joist in what should have be 
 an upper floor , only floor there be none . perch there , Fairthorn 
 glare down on Lionel through his spectacle . " dangerous , " he say 
 whisperingly ; " but one get use to everything ! if you feel afraid , 
 do not   venture ! " 

 Lionel , animate by that doubt of his courage , spring up the plank , 
 balance himself schoolboy fashion , with outstretched arm , and gain 
 the side of his guide . 

 " do not   touch I ! " exclaim Mr. Fairthorn , shrinking , " or we shall both 
 be over . now observe and imitate . " drop himself , then , carefully 
 and gradually , till he drop on the timber joist as if it be a 
 velocipede , his long leg dangle down , he with thigh and hand impel 
 himself onward till he gain the ridge of a wall , on which he deliver 
 his person , and wipe his spectacle . 

 Lionel be not long before he stand in the same place . " here we be , " 
 say Fairthorn . 

 " I do not   see the collection , " answer Lionel , first peer down 
 athwart the joist upon the rugged ground overspread with stone and 
 rubbish , then glance up through similar interstice above to the gaunt 
 rafter . 

 " here be some , - most precious , " answer Fairthorn , tap behind he . 
 " wall up , except where these board , case in iron , be nail 
 across , with a little door just big enough to creep through ; but that be 
 locked , - Chubb 's lock , and Mr. Darrell keep the key ! - treasure for 
 a palace ! no , you can not   peep through here - not a chink ; but come on a 
 little far , - mind your footing . " 

 skirt the wall , and still on the perilous ridge , Fairthorn creep on , 
 form an angle , and stop short , clap his eye to the crevice 
 of some plank nail rudely across a yawning aperture . Lionel find 
 another crevice for himself , and see , pile up in admire disorder , 
 picture , with their back turn to a desolate wall , rare cabinet , and 
 article of curious furniture , chest , box , crate , - heap pell - mell . 
 this receptacle have be roughly floor in deal , in order to support 
 its miscellaneous content , and be light from a large window   not 
 visible in front of the house , glaze in dull rough glass , with 
 ventilator . 

 " these be the heavy thing , and least costly thing , that no one could 
 well rob . the picture here be merely curious as early specimen , 
 intend for the old house , all spoil and rot ; Mr. Darrell wish 
 they to do so , I believe ! what he wish must be do ! my dear young 
 sir : a prodigious mind ; it be of granite ! " 

 " I can not understand it , " say Lionel , aghast . " the last man I should 
 have think capriciously whimsical . " 

 " Whimsical ! bless my soul ! do not   say such a word , do not , pray ! or the 
 roof will fall down upon we ! come away . you have see all you can see . 
 you must go first now ; mind that loose stone there ! " 

 nothing far be say till they be out of the building ; and Lionel 
 feel like a knight of old who have be lead into sepulchral hall by a 
 wizard . 




 CHAPTER V. 

  the annal of empire be briefly chronicle in family record 
  bring down to the present day , show that the race of man be

 it be mid - day . the boy and his new friend be stand apart , as

 Mr. Fairthorn screw his flute , an exceedingly handsome one . he

 out of an art , a man may be so trivial you would mistake he for an

 in his art , Fairthorn be certainly a master , and the air he now play

 quiet sky , with the lone but summer landscape , with Lionel 's melancholic

 darrell , then apparently drop , now it be bear again by my cousin . "

 " the Darrells be suppose to have get their name from a knight in the

 all comer , and be call , or call himself , John the Dare - all ; or , in

 your father Charlie become a gay young man about town , and very much the

 CHAPTER VI . 

  show how sinful it be in a man who do not care for his honour 
  to beget child . 

 when Lionel see Mr. Fairthorn devote his intellectual being to the 
 content of a cold chicken - pie , he silently step out of the room and 
 slunk away into a thick copse at the farth end of the paddock . he 
 long to be alone . the rain descend , not heavily , but in penetrate 
 drizzle ; he do not feel it , or rather he feel glad that there be no 
 gaudy mocking sunlight . he sit down forlorn in the hollow of a glen 
 which the copse cover , and bury his face in his clasp hand . 

 Lionel Haughton , as the reader may have notice , be no premature 
 man , - a manly boy , but still a habitant of the twilight , dreamy , 
 shadow - land of boyhood . noble element be stir fitfully within 
 he , but their agency be crude and undeveloped . sometimes , through 
 the native acuteness of his intellect , he apprehend truth quickly 
 and truly as a man ; then , again , through the warm haze of undisciplined 
 tenderness , or the raw mist of that sensitive pride in which object , 
 small in themselves , loom large with undetected outline , he fall back 
 into the passionate dimness of a child 's reasoning . he be intensely 
 ambitious ; quixotic in the point of honour ; dauntless in peril : but 
 morbidly tremble at the very shadow of disgrace , as a foal , destine 
 to be the war - horse and trample down level steel , start in its 
 tranquil pasture at the rustling of a leaf . glowingly romantic , but not 
 inclined to vent romance in literary creation , his feeling be the 
 more high - wrought and enthusiastic because they have no outlet in poetic 
 channel . Most boy of great ability and strong passion write verse - it 
 be Nature 's relief to brain and heart at the critical turning age . Most 
 boy thus gifted do so ; a few do not , and out of those few Fate select 
 the great man of action , - those large luminous character that stamp 
 poetry on the world 's prosaic surface . Lionel have in he the pith 
 and substance of Fortune 's grand nobody , who become Fame 's abrupt 
 somebody when the chance of life throw suddenly in their way a noble 
 something , to be ardently covet and boldly win . but I repeat , as yet 
 he be a boy ; so he sit there , his hand before his face , an unreasone 
 self - torturer . he know now why this haughty Darrell have write with so 
 little tenderness and respect to his beloved mother . Darrell look 
 on she as the cause of his ignoble kinsman 's ' sale of name ; " nay , most 
 probably ascribe to she not the fond girlish love which level all 
 disparity of rank , but the vulgar cold - blooded design to exchange her 
 father 's bank - note for a marriage beyond her station . and he be the 
 debtor to this supercilious creditor , as his father have be before he . 
 his father ! till then he have be so proud of that relationship ! Mrs. 
 Haughton have not be happy with her captain ; his confirm habit of 
 wild dissipation have embitter her union , and at last wear away her 
 wifely affection . but she have tend and nurse he in his last illness 
 as the lover of her youth ; and though occasionally she hint at his 
 fault , she ever speak of he as the ornament of all society , - poor , 
 it be true , harass by unfeele creditor , but the fine of fine 
 gentleman . Lionel have never hear from she of the ancestral estate sell 
 for a gambling debt ; never from she of the county jail nor the mercenary 
 misalliance . in boyhood , before we have any cause to be proud of 
 ourselves , we be so proud of our father , if we have a decent excuse 
 for it . of his father could Lionel Haughton be proud now ? and darrell 
 be cognizant of his paternal disgrace , have taunt his father in yonder 
 old hall - for what ? - the marriage from which Lionel sprang ! the hand 
 grow tight and tight before that burning face . he do not weep , as 
 he have do in Vance 's presence at a thought much less galling . not that 
 tear would have misbecome he . shallow judge of human nature be they 
 who think that tear in themselves ever misbecome boy or even man . well 
 do the stern of roman writer place the arch distinction of humanity 
 aloft from all meaner of Heaven 's creature , in the prerogative of 
 tear ! soon mayst thou trust thy purse to a professional pickpocket 
 than give loyal friendship to the man who boast of eye to which the 
 heart never mount in dew ! only , when man weep he should be alone , - not 
 because tear be weak , but because they should be sacred . tear be 
 akin to prayer . Pharisees parade prayer ! impostor parade tear . o 
 Pegasus , Pegasus , - softly , softly , - thou hast hurry I off amidst the 
 cloud : drop I gently down - there , by the side of the motionless boy in 
 the shadowy glen . 




 CHAPTER VII . 

  Lionel Haughton , have hitherto much improve his chance of 
  fortune , decide the question , " what will he do with it ? " 

 " I have be seek you everywhere , " say a well - know voice ; and a 
 hand rest lightly on Lionel 's shoulder . the boy look up , startle , 
 but yet heavily , and see Guy Darrell , the last man on earth he could 
 have desire to see . " will you come in for a few minute ? you be 
 want . " 

 " what for ? I would rather stay here . who can want I ? " 

 Darrell , strike by the word and the sullen tone in which they be 
 utter , survey Lionel 's face for an instant , and reply in a voice 
 involuntarily more kind than usual , - 

 " some one very commonplace , but since the Picts go out of fashion , 
 very necessary to mortal the most sublime . I ought to apologize for his 
 come . you threaten to leave I yesterday because of a defect in your 
 wardrobe . Mr. Fairthorn write to my tailor to hasten hither and repair 
 it . he be here . I commend he to your custom ! do not   despise he because 
 he make for a man of my remote generation . tailor be keen observer 
 and do not grow out of date so quickly as politician . " 

 the word be say with a playful good - humour very uncommon to Mr. 
 Darrell . the intention be obviously kind and kinsmanlike . Lionel sprang 
 to his foot ; his lip curl , his eye flash , and his crest rise . 

 " no , sir ; I will not stoop to this ! I will not be clothe by your 
 charity , - your ! I will not submit to an imply taunt upon my poor 
 mother 's ignorance of the manner of a rank to which she be not bear ! 
 you say we might not like each other , and , if so , we should part 
 forever . I do not like you , and I will go ! " he turn abruptly , and 
 walk to the house - magnanimous . if Mr. Darrell have not be the most 
 singular of man , he might well have be offend . as it be , though few 
 be less accessible to surprise , he be surprised . but offend ? judge 
 for yourself . " I declare , " mutter Guy Darrell , gaze on the boy 's 
 recede figure , " I declare that I almost feel as if I could once again 
 be capable of an emotion ! I hope I be not go to like that boy ! the 
 old Darrell blood in his vein , surely . I might have speak as he do at 
 his age , but I must have have some well reason for it . what do I say 
 to justify such an explosion ? 

 " quid feci ? - ubi lapsus ? Gone , no doubt , to pack up his knapsack , and 
 take the Road to Ruin ! shall I let he go ? well for I , if I be really 
 in danger of like he ; and so be at his mercy to sting - what ? my 
 heart ! I defy he ; it be dead . no ; he shall not go thus . I be the head 
 of our joint house . house ! I wish he have a house , poor boy ! and his 
 grandfather love I . let he go ? I will beg his pardon first ; and he 
 may dine in his drawer if that will settle the matter . " 

 thus , no less magnanimous than Lionel , do this misanthropical man 
 follow his ungracious cousin . " ha ! " cry Darrell , suddenly , as , 
 approach the threshold , he see Mr. Fairthorn at the dining - room 
 window occupy in nib a pen upon an ivory thumb - stall - " I have hit 
 it ! that abominable Fairthorn have be shed its prickle ! how could 
 I trust flesh and blood to such a bramble ? I will know what it be this 
 instant ! " vain menace ! no soon do Mr. Fairthorn catch glimpse 
 of Darrell 's countenance within ten yard of the porch , than , his 
 conscience take alarm , he rush incontinent from the window , the 
 apartment , and , ere Darrell could fling open the door , be lose in some 
 lair - " nullis penetrabilis astris " - in that sponge - like and cavernous 
 abode wherewith benignant Providence have suit the locality to the 
 creature . 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

  new imbroglio in that ever - recur , never - to - be - settle question , 
  " what will he do with it ? " 

 with a disappointed glare and a baffled shrug of the shoulder , Mr. 
 Darrell turn from the dining - room , and pass up the stair to 
 Lionel 's chamber , open the door quickly , and extend his hand say , 
 in that tone which have disarm the wrath of ambitious faction , and 
 even   if fame lie not   once seduce from the hostile Treasury - bench a 
 placeman 's vote , " I must have hurt your feeling , and I come to beg your 
 pardon ! " 

 but before this time Lionel 's proud heart , in which ungrateful anger 
 could not long find room , have smite he for so ill a return to 
 well - mean and not indelicate kindness . and , his wounded egotism 
 appease by its very outburst , he have call to mind Fairthorn 's 
 allusion to Darrell 's secret grief , - grief that must have be indeed 
 stormy so to have revulse the current of a life . and , despite those 
 grief , the great man have speak playfully to he , - playfully in order 
 to make light of obligation . so when Guy Darrell now extend that 
 hand , and stoop to that apology , Lionel be fairly overcome . tear , 
 before refuse , now find irresistible way . the hand he could not take , 
 but , yield to his yearning impulse , he throw his arm fairly round 
 his host 's neck , lean his young cheek upon that granite breast , and 
 sob out incoherent word of passionate repentance , honest , venerate 
 affection . Darrell 's face change , look for a moment wondrous soft ; 
 and then , as by an effort of supreme self - control , it become severely 
 placid . he do not return that embrace , but certainly he in no way 
 repel it ; nor do he trust himself to speak till the boy have 
 exhaust the force of his first feeling , and have turn to dry his 
 tear . 

 then he say , with a soothing sweetness : " Lionel Haughton , you have 
 the heart of a gentleman that can never listen to a frank apology for 
 unintentional wrong but what it spring forth to take the blame to 
 itself and return apology tenfold . enough ! a mistake no doubt , on both 
 side . More time must elapse before either can truly say that he do 
 not like the other . meanwhile , " add Darrell , with almost a laugh , - and 
 that conclude query show that even on trifle the man be bent upon 
 either force or steal his own will upon other , - " meanwhile must I 
 send away the tailor ? " I need not repeat Lionel 's answer . 




 CHAPTER IX . 

  DARRELL - mystery in his past life - what have he do with it ? 

 some day pass , each day vary little from the other . it be the 
 habit of Darrell if he go late to rest to rise early . he never allow 
 himself more than five hour sleep . a man great than Guy Darrell - Sir 
 Walter Raleigh - carve from the solid day no large a slice for 
 Morpheus . and it be this habit perhaps , yet more than temperance in 
 diet , which preserve to Darrell his remarkable youthfulness of aspect 
 and frame , so that at fifty - two he look , and really be , young than 
 many a strong man of thirty - five . for , certain it be , that on enter 
 middle life , he who would keep his brain clear , his step elastic , his 
 muscle from fleshiness , his nerve from tremor , - in a word , retain his 
 youth in spite of the register , - should beware of long slumber . nothing 
 age like laziness . the hour before breakfast Darrell devote first to 
 exercise , whatever the weather ; next to his calm scientific pursuit . at 
 ten o'clock punctually he ride out alone and seldom return till late 
 in the afternoon . then he would stroll forth with Lionel into devious 
 woodland , or lounge with he along the margin of the lake , or lie down 
 on the ted grass , call the boy 's attention to the insect populace 
 which sport out its happy life in the summer month , and treat of the 
 way and habit of each vary specie , with a quaint learning , half 
 humorous , half grave . he be a minute observer and an accomplished 
 naturalist . his range of knowledge be , indeed , amazingly large for a 
 man who have have to pass his good year in a dry and absorbing study : 
 necessarily not so profound in each section as that of a special 
 professor ; but if the science be often on the surface , the thought he 
 deduce from what he know be as often original and deep . a maxim of 
 his , which he drop out one day to Lionel in his careless manner , but 
 pointed diction , may perhaps illustrate his own practice and its result 
 " never think it enough to have solve the problem start by another 
 mind till you have deduce from it a corollary of your own . " 

 after dinner , which be not over till past eight o'clock , they always 
 adjourn to the library , Fairthorn vanish into a recess , Darrell and 
 Lionel each with his several book , then an air on the flute , and each 
 to his own room before eleven . no life could be more methodical ; yet 
 to Lionel it have an animating charm , for his interest in his host daily 
 increase , and vary his thought with perpetual occupation . Darrell , 
 on the contrary , while more kind and cordial , more cautiously on his 
 guard not to wound his young guest 's susceptibility than he have be 
 before the quarrel and its reconciliation , do not seem to feel for 
 Lionel the active interest which Lionel feel for he . he do not , as 
 most clever man be apt to do in their intercourse with youth , attempt 
 to draw he out , plumb his intellect , or guide his taste . if he be 
 at time instructive , it be because talk fall on subject on which 
 it please himself to touch , and in which he could not speak without 
 involuntarily instruct . nor do he ever allure the boy to talk of 
 his school - day , of his friend , of his predilection , his hope , his 
 future . in short , have you observe they together , you would have never 
 suppose they be connection , that one could and ought to influence 
 and direct the career of the other . you would have say the host 
 certainly like the guest , as any man would like a promising , 
 warm - hearted , high - spirit , graceful boy , under his own roof for a 
 short time , but who feel that that boy be nothing to he ; would soon 
 pass from his eye ; form friend , pursuit , aim , with which he could be 
 in no way commingle , for which he should be wholly irresponsible . there 
 be also this peculiarity in Darrell 's conversation ; if he never speak 
 of his guest 's past and future , neither do he ever do more than advert 
 in the most general term to his own . of that grand stage on which he 
 have be so brilliant an actor he impart no reminiscence ; of those 
 great man , the leader of his age , with whom he have mingle familiarly , 
 he tell no anecdote . equally silent be he as to the early step in 
 his career , the mode by which he have study , the accident of which he 
 have seize advantage , - silent there as upon the cause he have gain , or 
 the debate he have adorn . never could you have suppose that this man , 
 still in the prime of public life , have be the theme of journal and 
 the boast of party . neither do he ever , as man who talk easily at 
 their own hearth be prone to do , speak of project in the future , 
 even though the project be no vaster than the planting of a tree or the 
 alteration of a parterre , - project with which rural life so copiously 
 and so innocently teem . the past seem as if it have leave to he no 
 memory , the future as if it store for he no desire . but do the past 
 leave no memory ? why then at interval would the book slide from his 
 eye , the head sink upon the breast , and a shade of unutterable dejection 
 darken over the grand beauty of that strong stern countenance ? still 
 that dejection be not morbidly feed and encourage , for he would fling 
 it from he with a quick impatient gesture of the head , resume the 
 book resolutely , or change it for another which induce fresh train of 
 thought , or look over Lionel 's shoulder , and make some subtile comment 
 on his choice , or call on Fairthorn for the flute ; and in a few minute 
 the face be severely serene again . and be it here say , that it be only 
 in the poetry of young gentleman , or the prose of lady novelist , that a 
 man in good health and of sound intellect wear the livery of unvarying 
 gloom . however great his cause of sorrow , he do not forever parade 
 its ostentatious mourning , nor follow the hearse of his hope with 
 the long face of an undertaker . he will still have his gleam of 
 cheerfulness , his moment of good humour . the old smile will sometimes 
 light the eye , and awake the old playfulness of the lip . but what a 
 great and critical sorrow do leave behind be often far bad than the 
 sorrow itself have be . it be a change in the inner man , which strand 
 he , as Guy Darrell seem strand , upon the shoal of the Present ; 
 which the more he strive manfully to bear his burden warn he the more 
 from dwell on the past ; and the more impressively it enforce the 
 lesson of the vanity of human wish strike the more from his reckoning 
 illusive hope in the future . thus out of our threefold existence two 
 part be annihilate , - the what have be , the what shall be . we fold 
 our arm , stand upon the petty and steep cragstone , which alone loom 
 out of the Measureless Sea , and say to ourselves , look neither 
 backward nor beyond , " let we bear what be ; " and so for the moment the 
 eye can lighten and the lip can smile . 

 Lionel could no long glean from Mr. Fairthorn any stray hint upon 
 the family record . that gentleman have evidently be reprimand 
 for indiscretion , or warn against its repetition , and he become as 
 reserved and mum as if he have just emerge from the cave of Trophonius . 
 indeed he shun trust himself again alone to Lionel , and affect 
 a long arrear of correspondence on behalf of his employer , leave the lad 
 during the forenoon to solitary angling , or social intercourse with the 
 swan and the tame doe . but from some mystic concealment within door 
 would often float far into the open air the melody of that magic 
 flute ; and the boy would glide back , along the dark - red mournful wall 
 of the old house , or the futile pomp of pilastere arcade in the 
 uncompleted new one , to listen to the sound : listening , he , blissful 
 boy , forget the present ; he seize the unchallenged royalty of his 
 year . for he no rebel in the past conspire with poison to the 
 wine - cup , murder to the sleep . no desert in the future , arrest the 
 march of ambition , say , " here be sand for a pilgrim , not field for a 
 conqueror . " 




 CHAPTER X. 

  in which chapter the history quietly move on to the next . 

 thus nearly a week have go , and Lionel begin to feel perplex as to 
 the duration of his visit . should he be the first to suggest departure ? 
 Mr. Darrell rescue he from that embarrassment . on the seventh day , 
 Lionel meet his host in a lane near the house , return from his 
 habitual ride . the boy walk home by the side of the horseman , pat 
 the steed , admire its shape , and praise the beauty of another 
 saddle - horse , small and slighter , which he have see in the paddock 
 exercise by a groom . " do you ever ride that chestnut ? I think it even 
 handsomer than this . " 

 " half our preference be due to the vanity they flatter . few can ride 
 this horse ; any one , perhaps , that . " 

 " there speak the Dare - all ! " say Lionel , laugh . the host do not 
 look displeased . 

 " where no difficulty , there no pleasure , " say he in his curt laconic 
 diction . " I be in Spain two year ago . I have not an english horse 
 there , so I buy that andalusian jennet . what have serve he at need , 
 no preux chevalier would leave to the chance of ill - usage . so the 
 jennet come with I to England . you have not be much accustomed to 
 ride , I suppose ? " 

 " not much ; but my dear mother think I ought to learn . she pinch 
 for a whole year to have I teach at a riding - school during one school 
 vacation . " 

 " your mother 's relation be , I believe , well off . do they suffer she to 
 pinch ? " 

 " I do not know that she have relation live ; she never speak of they . " 

 " indeed ! " this be the first question on home matter that Darrell have 
 ever directly address to Lionel . he there drop the subject , and 
 say , after a short pause , " I be not aware that you be a horseman , or 
 I would have ask you to accompany I ; will you do so to - morrow , and 
 mount the jennet ? " 

 " oh , thank you ; I should like it so much . " 

 Darrell turn abruptly away from the bright , grateful eye . " I be only 
 sorry , " he add , look aside , " that our excursion can be but few . 
 on Friday next I shall submit to you a proposition ; if you accept it , we 
 shall part on Saturday , - like each other , I hope : speak for myself , 
 the experiment have not fail ; and on your ? " 

 " on mine ! - oh , Mr. Darrell , if I dare but tell you what recollection 
 of yourself the experiment will bequeath to I ! " 

 " do not tell I , if they imply a compliment , " answer Darrell , with 
 the low silvery laugh which so melodiously express indifference and 
 repel affection . he enter the stable - yard , dismount ; and on 
 return to Lionel , the sound of the flute steal forth , as if from the 
 eave of the gable roof . " could the pipe of Horace 's faunus be sweet 
 than that flute ? " say Darrell , 

  " ' utcunque dulci , Tyndare , fistula , 
  Valles , ' etc . 

 what a lovely ode that be ! what knowledge of town life ! what 
 susceptibility to the rural ! of all the Latins , Horace be the only one 
 with whom I could wish to have spend a week . but no ! I could not have 
 discuss the brief span of human life with lock steep in Malobathran 
 balm and wreathe with that silly myrtle . Horace and I would have 
 quarrel over the first heady bowl of Massie . we never can quarrel 
 now ! bless subject and poet - laureate of Queen Proserpine , and , I 
 dare swear , the most gentlemanlike poet she ever receive at court ; 
 henceforth his task be to uncoil the asp from the brow of Alecto , and 
 arrest the ambitious Orion from the chase after visionary lion . " 




 CHAPTER XI . 

  show that if a good face be a letter of recommendation , a good 
  heart be a letter of credit . 

 the next day they ride forth , host and guest , and that ride prove 
 an eventful crisis in the fortune of Lionel Haughton . Hitherto I have 
 elaborately dwelt on the fact that whatever the regard Darrell might 
 feel for he , it be a regard apart from that interest which accept a 
 responsibility and link to itself a fate . and even if , at moment , the 
 powerful and wealthy man have feel that interest , he have thrust it from 
 he . that he mean to be generous be indeed certain , and this he have 
 typically show in a very trite matter - of - fact way . the tailor , whose 
 visit have lead to such perturbation , have receive instruction beyond the 
 mere supply of the raiment for which he have be summon ; and a large 
 patent portmanteau , contain all that might constitute the liberal 
 outfit of a young man in the rank of gentleman , have arrive at Fawley , 
 and amazed and move Lionel , whom Darrell have by this time thoroughly 
 reconcile to the acceptance of benefit . the gift denote this : " in 
 recognize you as kinsman , I shall henceforth provide for you as 
 gentleman . " Darrell indeed meditate apply for an appointment in 
 one of the public office , the settlement of a liberal allowance , and 
 a parting shake of the hand , which should imply , " I have now behave as 
 become I : the rest belong to you . we may never meet again . there be 
 no reason why this good - by may not be forever . " 

 but in the course of that ride , Darrell 's intention change . wherefore ? 
 you will never guess ! nothing so remote as the distance between cause 
 and effect , and the cause for the effect here be - poor little Sophy . 

 the day be fresh , with a lovely breeze , as the two rider ride 
 briskly over the turf of rolling common , with the feathery bough of 
 neighbouring woodland toss joyously to and fro by the sportive summer 
 wind . the exhilarate exercise and air raise Lionel 's spirit , 
 and release his tongue from all trammel ; and when a boy be in high 
 spirit , ten to one but he grow a frank egotist , feel the teem 
 life of his individuality , and talk about himself . quite unconsciously , 
 Lionel rattle out gay anecdote of his school - day ; his quarrel with a 
 demoniacal usher ; how he run away ; what befell he ; how the doctor go 
 after , and bring he back ; how splendidly the doctor behave , - neither 
 flog nor expel he , but after patiently listen , while he 
 rebuke the pupil , dismiss the usher , to the joy of the whole academy ; 
 how he fight the head boy in the school for call the doctor a sneak ; 
 how , lick twice , he yet fight that head boy a third time , and lick 
 he ; how , when head boy himself , he have rouse the whole school into a 
 civil war , divide the boy into Cavaliers and roundhead ; how clay 
 be roll out into cannon - ball and pistol - shot , stick shape into 
 sword , the playground disturb to construct fortification ; how a 
 slovenly stout boy enact Cromwell ; how he himself be elevate into 
 Prince Rupert ; and how , reverse all history , and infamously degrade 
 Cromwell , Rupert would not consent to be beat ; and Cromwell at the 
 last , disable by an untoward blow across the knuckle , ignominiously 
 yield himself prisoner , be try by a court - martial , and sentence to 
 be shoot ! to all this rubbish do Darrell incline his patient ear , - not 
 encouraging , not interrupt , but sometimes stifle a sigh at the 
 sound of Lionel 's merry laugh , or the sight of his fair face , with 
 heighten glow on his cheek , and his long silky hair , worthy the name 
 of lovelock , blow by the wind from the open loyal feature , which 
 might well have grace the portrait of some youthful cavalier . on 
 bound the spanish jennet , on rattle the boy rider . he have leave school 
 now , in his headlong talk ; he be describe his first friendship with 
 Frank Vance , as a lodger at his mother 's ; how example fire he , and he 
 take to sketch - work and painting ; how kindly Vance give he lesson ; how 
 at one time he wish to be a painter ; how much the mere idea of such 
 a thing vex his mother , and how little she be move when he tell she 
 that Titian be of a very ancient family , and that Francis I. , archetype 
 of gentleman , visit Leonardo da Vinci 's sick - bed ; and that Henry VIII . 
 have say to a pert lord who have snub Holbein , " I can make a lord any 
 day , but I can not make a Holbein ! " how Mrs. Haughton still confound 
 all painter in the general image of the painter and the plumber who have 
 cheat she so shamefully in the renew window - sash and redecorate 
 wall , which Time and the four child of an irish family have make 
 necessary to the letting of the first floor . and these playful allusion 
 to the maternal idea be still not irreverent , but contrive so as 
 rather to prepossess Darrell in Mrs. Haughton 's favour by bring out 
 trait of a simple natural mother , too proud , perhaps , of her only son , 
 not care what she do , how she work , so that he might not lose caste 
 as a bear Haughton . Darrell understand , and nod his head approvingly . 
 " certainly , " he say , speak almost for the first time , " fame confer 
 a rank above that of gentleman and of king ; and as soon as she issue 
 her patent of nobility , it matter not a straw whether the recipient be 
 the son of a Bourbon or of a tallow - chandler . but if Fame withhold she 
 patent ; if a well - bear man paint aldermen , and be not famous   and I dare 
 say you would have be neither a titian nor a Holbein , - why , he might 
 as well be a painter and plumber , and have a well chance even of bread 
 and cheese by stand to his post as gentleman . Mrs. Haughton be 
 right , and I respect she . " 

 " quite right . if I live to the age of Methuselah , I could not paint a 
 head like Frank Vance . " 

 " and even he be not famous yet . never hear of he . " 

 " he will be famous : I be sure of it ; and if you live in London , you 
 would hear of he even now . oh , sir ! such a portrait as he paint 
 the other day ! but I must tell you all about it . " and therewith Lionel 
 plunge at once , media re , into the brief break epic of little Sophy , 
 and the eccentric infirm Belisarius for whose sake she first toil and 
 then beg ; with what artless eloquence he bring out the colour of 
 the whole story , - now its humour , now its pathos ; with what beautifying 
 sympathy he adorn the image of the little vagrant girl , with she 
 mien of gentlewoman and her simplicity of child ; the river excursion to 
 Hampton Court ; her still delight ; how annoyed he feel when Vance seem 
 ashamed of she before those fine people ; the orchard scene in which 
 he have read Darrell 's letter , that , for the time , drive she from the 
 foremost place in his thought ; the return home , the parting , her 
 wistful look back , the visit to the Cobbler 's next day ; even her 
 farewell gift , the nursery poem , with the line write on the fly - leaf , 
 he have they by heart ! Darrell , the grand advocate , feel he could not 
 have produce on a jury , with those element , the effect which that 
 boy - narrator produce on his granite self . 

 " and , oh , sir ! " cry Lionel , check his horse , and even arrest 
 Darrell 's with bold right hand - " oh , " say he , as he bring his moist 
 and plead eye in full battery upon the shaken fort to which he have 
 mine his way - " oh , sir ! you be so wise and rich and kind , do rescue 
 that poor child from the penury and hardship of such a life ! if you 
 could but have see and hear she ! she could never have be bear to it ! 
 you look away : I offend you ! I have no right to tax your benevolence 
 for other ; but , instead of shower favour upon I , so little would 
 suffice for she ! - if she be but above positive want , with that old man 
  she would not be happy without he , safe in such a cottage as you give 
 to your own peasant ! I be a man , or shall be one soon ; I can wrestle 
 with the world , and force my way somehow ; but that delicate child , a 
 village show , or a beggar on the high road ! - no mother , no brother , no 
 one but that break - down cripple , lean upon her arm as his crutch . I 
 can not bear to think of it . I be sure I shall meet she again somewhere ; 
 and when I do , may I not write to you , and will you not come to she 
 help ? do speak ; do say ' yes , ' Mr. Darrell . " 

 the rich man 's breast heave slightly ; he close his eye , but for a 
 moment . there be a short and sharp struggle with his well self , and 
 the well self conquer . 

 " let go my rein ; see , my horse put down his ear ; he may do you a 
 mischief . now canter on : you shall be satisfy . give I a moment to - to 
 unbutton my coat : it be too tight for I . " 




 CHAPTER XII . 

  Guy Darrell give way to an impulse , and quickly decide what he 
  will do with it . 

 " Lionel Haughton , " say Guy Darrell , regain his young cousin 's side , 
 and speak in a firm and measured voice , " I have to thank you for one 
 very happy minute ; the sight of a heart so fresh in the limpid purity of 
 goodness be a luxury you can not comprehend till you have come to my age ; 
 journey , like I , from Dan to Beersheba , and find all barren . heed 
 I : if you have be half - a - dozen year old , and this child for whom 
 you plead have be a fair young woman , perhaps just as innocent , just as 
 charming , - more in peril , - my benevolence would have lain as dormant

 true friend , I should have shrug my shoulder and say , ' beware ! ' have

 age , you , hearty , genial , and open - hearted boy , - you , catch but by the 
 chivalrous compassion for helpless female childhood , - oh , that you be 
 my son , - oh , that my dear father 's blood be in those knightly vein ! 
 I have a son once ! God take he ; " the strong man 's lip quiver : he 
 hurry on . " I feel there be manhood in you , when you write to fle my 
 churlish favour in my tooth ; when you would have leave my roof - tree in a

 of calculate submission , manhood , but only perhaps man 's pride as 
 man , - man 's heart not less cold than winter . to - day you have show 
 I something far well than pride ; that nature which constitute the 
 heroic temperament be complete by two attribute , - unflinche purpose , 
 disinterested humanity . I know not yet if you have the first ; you reveal 
 to I the second . yes ! I accept the duty you propose to I ; I will do

 direct my solicitor to take the right step to do so . I will see that

 blood ; other reason too , - let they pass . but in provide for your

 event , three - fourth of what we be in boyhood most of we owe to our

 certainly praise be rare upon Darrell 's lip ; but when he do praise , 
 he know how to do it ! and no man will ever command other who have not

 CHAPTER XIII . 

 he who see his heir in his own child , carry his eye over hope and 
 possession lie far beyond his gravestone , view his life , even 
 here , as a period but close with a comma . he who see his heir in 
 another man 's child , see the full stop at the end of the sentence . 

 Lionel 's departure be indefinitely postpone ; nothing more be say of 
 it . meanwhile Darrell 's manner towards he undergo a marked change . 
 the previous indifference the rich kinsman have hitherto show as to the 
 boy 's past life , and the peculiarity of his intellect and character , 
 wholly vanish . he seek now , on the contrary , to plumb thoroughly the 
 more hidden depth which lurk in the nature of every human being , and 
 which , in Lionel , be the more difficult to discern from the vivacity 
 and candour which cover with so smooth and charming a surface a pride 
 tremulously sensitive , and an ambition that startle himself in the 
 hour when solitude and revery reflect upon the vision of youth the 
 giant outline of its own hope . 

 Darrell be not dissatisfied with the result of his survey ; yet often , 
 when perhaps most pleased , a shade would pass over his countenance ; and 
 have a woman who love he be by to listen , she would have hear the

 of man 's friendship to recognize it as the sound of sorrow . 

 in Darrell himself , thus insensibly alter , Lionel daily discover 
 more to charm his interest and deepen his affection . in this man 's 
 nature there be , indeed , such wondrous under - current of sweetness , 
 so suddenly gush forth , so suddenly vanish again ! and exquisite in 
 he be the trait of that sympathetic tact which the world call

 tender , the more bewitching in Darrell from their contrast with a manner 
 usually cold , and a bearing so stamp with masculine , self - willed , 
 haughty power . thus - day go on as if Lionel have become a very child 
 of the house . but his sojourn be in truth draw near to a close not 
 less abrupt and unexpected than the turn in his host 's humour to which 
 he owe the delay of his departure . 

 one bright afternoon , as Darrell be stand at the window of his 
 private study , Fairthorn , who have creep in on some matter of business , 
 look at his countenance long and wistfully , and then , shamble up to 
 his side , put one hand on his shoulder with a light timid touch , and ,

 of the casement read the " Faerie Queene , " say , " why do you take he 
 to your heart if he do not comfort it ? " 

 Darrell wince and answer gently , " I do not know you be in the 
 room . Poor Fairthorn ; thank you ! " 

 " thank I ! - what for ? " 

 " for a kind thought . so , then , you like the boy ? " 

 " may not   I like he ? " ask Fairthorn , look rather frightened ; ' surely 
 you do ! " 

 " yes , I like he much ; I be try my good to love he . but , 
 but " - Darrell turn quickly , and the portrait of his father over 
 the mantelpiece come full upon his sight , - an impressive , a haunting 
 face , - sweet and gentle , yet with the high narrow brow and arch 
 nostril of pride , with restless melancholy eye , and an expression 
 that reveal the delicacy of intellect , but not its power . there be 
 something forlorn , but impose , in the whole effigy . as you continue 
 to look at the countenance , the mournful attraction grow upon you . truly 
 a touching and a most lovable aspect . Darrell 's eye moisten . 

 " yes , my father , it be so ! " he say softly . " all my sacrifice be in 
 vain . the race be not to be rebuild ! no grandchild of yours will succeed 
 I , - I , the last of the old line ! Fairthorn , how can I love that boy ? 
 he may be my heir , and in his vein not a drop of my father 's blood ! " 

 " but he have the blood of your father 's ancestor ; and why must you 
 think of he as your heir ? - you , who , if you would but go again into the 
 world , might yet find a fair wi - " 

 with such a stamp come Darrell 's foot upon the floor that the holy and 
 conjugal monosyllable dropping from Fairthorn 's lip be as much cut in 
 two as if a shark have snap it . unspeakably frighten , the poor man 
 sidle away , thrust himself behind a tall reading - desk , and , peer 
 aslant from that covert , whimper out , " do not , do not   now , do not   be so 
 awful ; I do not mean to offend , but I be always say something I do 
 not mean ; and really you look so young still "   coaxingly , " and , and - " 

 Darrell , the burst of rage over , have sink upon a chair , his face bow 
 over his hand , and his breast heaving as if with suppressed sob . 

 the musician forget his fear ; he spring forward , almost upsetting 
 the tall desk ; he fling himself on his knee at Darrell 's foot , and 
 exclaim in break word , " Master , master , forgive I ! Beast that I 
 be ! do look up - do smile or else beat I - kick I . " 

 Darrell 's right hand slide gently from his face , and fall into 
 Fairthorn 's clasp . 

 " hush , hush , " mutter the man of granite ; " one moment , and it will be 
 over . " 

 one moment ! that might be but a figure of speech ; yet before Lionel have 
 finish half the canto that be plunge he into fairyland , Darrell 
 be stand by he with his ordinary tranquil mien ; and Fairthorn 's

 and unicorn , and thing in fable . 

 on , on swell the mellow , mellow , witching music ; and now the worn 
 man with his secret sorrow , and the boy with his frank glad laugh , be

 sight , as if the dense green of the summer have close around they like 
 wave . but still the flute sound on , and still they hear it , soft and

 CHAPTER XIV . 

  there be certain event which to each man 's life be as comet to 
  the earth , seemingly strange and erratic portent ; distinct from the 
  ordinary light which guide our course and mark our season , yet 
  true to their own law , potent in their own influence . philosophy 
  speculate on their effect , and dispute upon their us ; man who 
  do not philosophize regard they as special messenger and bode of 
  evil . 

 they come out of the little park into a by - lane ; a vast tract of common 
 land , yellow with furze and undulate with swell and hollow , spread 
 in front ; to their right the dark beechwood , still beneath the weight 
 of the July noon . Lionel have be talk about the " Faerie Queene , " 
 knight - errantry , the sweet impossible dream - life that , safe from Time , 
 glide by bower and hall , through magic forest and by witch eave

 out then they come , this broad waste land before they ; and Lionel say 
 merrily , - 

 " but this be the very scene ! here the young knight , leave his father 's

 adventure . "

 build . vanity of human wish ! what but the several proportion of 
 their common folly distinguish the baffled squire from the arrest 
 conqueror ? Man 's characteristic cerebral organ must certainly be

 " be it his organ of acquisitiveness that move themistocle to

 " and yet , if I might , I should like to ask , what you now desire that you

 " i - nothing ; but I speak of the living ! I be dead . only , " add Darrell , 
 with his silvery laugh , " I say , as poor Chesterfield say before I , ' it

 Darrell 's manner repel the expression of sympathy or of interest ; and

 and thus silently they continue to wander on till the sound of the 
 flute have long be lose to their ear . be the musician play still ? 

 at length they come round to the other end of Fawley village , and 
 Darrell again become animate . 

 " perhaps , " say he , return to the subject of talk that have be 
 abruptly suspend , - " perhaps the love of power be at the origin of each 
 restless courtship of Fortune : yet , after all , who have power with less 
 alloy than the village thane ? with so little effort , so little thought , 
 the man in the manor - house can make man in the cottage happy here 
 below and more fit for a hereafter yonder . in leave the world I come 
 from contest and pilgrimage , like our sire the Crusaders , to reign at

 as he speak , he enter one of the cottage . an old paralytic man be 
 seat by the fire , hot though the July sun be out of door ; and his

 " sit down , Matthew , and let , I read you a chapter . " Darrell take the

 put it be the sad peculiarity of this remarkable man that all his mood

 turn then to the hostess , who be stand somewhat within the
