 CHAPTER XV . 

  every street have two side , the shady side and the sunny . when two 
  man shake hand and part , mark which of the two take the sunny 
  side : he will be the young man of the two . 

 the next morning , neither Darrell nor Fairthorn appear at breakfast ; 
 but as soon as Lionel have conclude that meal , Mr. Mills inform he , 
 with customary politeness , that Mr. Darrell wish to speak with he in 
 the study . study , across the threshold of which Lionel have never yet set 
 footstep ! he enter it now with a sentiment of mingled curiosity and 
 awe . nothing in it remarkable , save the portrait of the host 's father 
 over the mantelpiece . book strew table , chair , and floor in the 
 disorder love by habitual student . near the window be a glass bowl 
 contain gold - fish , and close by , in its cage , a singing - bird . Darrell 
 might exist without companionship in the human specie , but not without 
 something which he protect and cherish , - a bird , even a fish . 

 Darrell look really ill : his keen eye be almost dim , and the line 
 in his face seem deep . but he speak with his usual calm , passionless 
 melody of voice . 

 " yes , " he say , in answer to Lionel 's really anxious inquiry ; " I be ill . 
 idle person like I give way to illness . when I be a busy man , I never 
 do ; and then illness give way to I . my general plan be thus , if not 
 actually alter , at least hurry to their consummation soon than I 
 expect . before you come here , I tell you to come soon , or you might 
 not find I . I mean to go abroad this summer ; I shall now start at 
 once . I need the change of scene and air . you will return to London 
 to - day . " 

 " to - day ! you be not angry with I ? " 

 " angry ! boy and cousin - no ! " resume Darrell , in a tone of unusual 
 tenderness . " angry - fie ! but since the parting must be , ' t be well to 
 abridge the pain of long farewell . you must wish , too , to see your 
 mother , and thank she for rear you up so that you may step from 
 poverty into ease with a head erect . you will give to Mrs. Haughton this 
 letter : for yourself , your inclination seem to tend towards the army . 
 but before you decide on that career , I should like you to see something 
 more of the world . call to - morrow on Colonel Morley , in Curzon Street : 
 this be his address . he will receive by to - day 's post a note from I , 
 request he to advise you . follow his counsel in what belong to the 
 world . he be a man of the world , - a distant connection of mine , who 
 will be kind to you for my sake . be there more to say ? yes . it seem an 
 ungracious speech ; but I should speak it . consider yourself sure from 
 I of an independent income . never let idle sycophant lead you into 
 extravagance by tell you that you will have more . but indulge not the 
 expectation , however plausible , that you will be my heir . " 

 " Mr. Darrell - oh , sir - " 

 " Hush ! the expectation would be reasonable ; but I be a strange being . I 
 might marry again , - have heir of my own . eh , sir , - oh why not ? " Darrell 
 speak these last word almost fiercely , and fix his eye on Lionel 
 as he repeat , - " why not ? " but see that the boy 's face evince 
 no surprise , the expression of his own relaxed , and he continue 
 calmly , - " Enough ; what I have thus rudely say be kindly mean . it be 
 a treason to a young man to let he count on a fortune which at last 
 be leave away from he . now , Lionel , go ; enjoy your spring of life ! go , 
 hopeful and light - hearted . if sorrow reach you , battle with it ; if 
 error mislead you , come fearlessly to I for counsel . why , boy , what be 
 this ? - tear ? tut , tut . " 

 " it be your goodness , " falter Lionel . " I can not help it . and be there 
 nothing I can do for you in return ? " 

 " yes , much . keep your name free from stain , and your heart open to such 
 noble emotion as awaken tear like those . ah , by the by , I hear from 
 my lawyer to - day about your poor little protegee . not find yet , but he 
 seem sanguine of quick success . you shall know the moment I hear more . " 

 " you will write to I , then , sir , and I may write to you ? " 

 " as often as you please . always direct to I here . " 

 " shall you be long abroad ? " 

 Darrell 's brow meet . " I do not   know , " say he , curtly . " Adieu . " 

 he open the door as he speak . 

 Lionel look at he with wistful yearning , filial affection , through 
 his swimming eye . " God bless you , sir , " he murmur simply , and pass

 " that blessing should have come from I ! " say Darrell to himself , as he

 I once pour a blessing , where be they , - where ? and that man 's tale ,

 less guilty knave of the two , seek to palm on I year ago ! stop ; let 
 I weigh well what he say . if it be true ! oh , shame , shame ! "

 suppress feeling , and to exercise only judgment ; and his reasoning

 lie , - certainly a palpable and gross lie ; lie it must and shall be . 
 never will I accept it as truth . Father "   look full at the portrait

 BOOK III . 




 CHAPTER I. 

  Certes , the lizard be a shy and timorous creature . he run into 
  chink and cranny if you come too near to he , and shed his very 
  tail for fear , if you catch it by the tip . he have not his be in 
  good society : no one cage he , no one pet . he be an idle vagrant . 
  but when he steal through the green herbage , and bask unmoleste 
  in the sun , he crowd perhaps as much enjoyment into one summer hour 
  as a parrot , however pampered and erudite , spread over a whole 
  drawing - room life spend in say " how dye do " and " Pretty Poll . " 

 on that dull and sombre summer morning in which the grandfather and 
 grandchild depart from the friendly roof of Mr. Merle , very dull and 
 very sombre be the thought of little Sophy . she walk slowly behind 
 the gray cripple , who have need to lean so heavily on his staff , and her 
 eye have not even a smile for the golden buttercup that glitter on 
 dewy mead alongside the barren road . 

 thus have they proceed apart and silent till they have pass the second 
 milestone . there , Waife , rouse from his own revery , which be 
 perhaps yet more dreary than those of the dejected child , halt 
 abruptly , pass his hand once or twice rapidly over his forehead , and , 
 turn round to Sophy , look into her face with great kindness as she 
 come slowly to his side . 

 " you be sad , little one ? " say he . 

 " very sad , Grandy . " 

 " and displease with I ? yes , displease that I have take you suddenly 
 away from the pretty young gentleman , who be so kind to you , without 
 encourage the chance that you be to meet with he again . " 

 " it be not like you , Grandy , " answer Sophy ; and her under - lip 
 slightly pout , while the big tear swell to her eye . 

 " true , " say the vagabond ; " anything resemble common - sense be not like 
 I . but do not   you think that I do what I feel be good for you ? must I 
 not have some good cause for it , whenever I have the heart deliberately 
 to vex you ? " 

 Sophy take his hand and press it , but she could not trust herself to 
 speak , for she feel that at such effort she would have burst out into 
 hearty crying . then Waife proceed to utter many of those wise saying , 
 old as the hill , and as high above our sorrow as hill be from the 
 valley in which we walk . he say how foolish it be to unsettle the mind 
 by preposterous fancy and impossible hope . the pretty young gentleman 
 could never be anything to she , nor she to the pretty young gentleman . 
 it might be very well for the pretty young gentleman to promise to 
 correspond with she , but as soon as he return to his friend he would 
 have other thing to think of , and she would soon be forget ; while 
 she , on the contrary , would be think of he , and the Thames and the 
 butterfly , and find hard life still more irksome . of all this , and 
 much more , in the general way of consoler who set out on the principle 
 that grief be a matter of logic , do Gentleman Waife deliver himself 
 with a vigour of ratiocination which admit of no reply , and convey 
 not a particle of comfort . and feel this , that great actor - not that 
 he be act then - suddenly stop , clasp the child in his arm , and 
 murmur in broken accent , - " but if I see you thus cast down , I shall 
 have no strength leave to hobble on through the world ; and the soon I 
 lie down , and the dust be shovel over I , why , the well for you ; 
 for it seem that Heaven send you friend , and I tear you from they . " 

 and then Sophy fairly give way to her sob : she twin her little 
 arm round the old man 's neck convulsively , kiss his rough face with 
 implore pathetic fondness , and force out through her tear , " do not 
 talk so ! I have   be ungrateful and wicked . I do not   care for any one but 
 my own dear , dear Grandy . " 

 after this little scene , they both compose themselves , and feel much 
 light of heart . they pursue their journey , no long apart , but side 
 by side , and the old man lean , though very lightly , on the child 's 
 arm . but there be no immediate reaction from gloom to gayety . Waife 
 begin talk in soften undertone , and vaguely , of his own past 
 affliction ; and partial as be the reference , how vast do the old 
 man 's sorrow seem beside the child 's regret ; and yet he comment on 
 they as if rather in pity her state than grieve for his own . 

 " ah , at your age , my darling , I have not your trouble and hardship . 
 I have not to trudge these dusty road on foot with a break - down 
 good - for - nothing scatterle ; I trod rich carpet , and sleep under 
 silken curtain . I take the air in gay carriage , - I such a scapegrace ; 
 and you , little child , you so good ! all gone , all melt away from I , 
 and not able now to be sure that you will have a crust of bread this day 
 week . " 

 " oh , yes ! I shall have bread , and you too , Grandy , " cry Sophy , with 
 cheerful voice . " it be you who teach I to pray to God , and say that 
 in all your trouble God have be good to you : and he have be so good 
 to I since I pray to he ; for I have no dreadful Mrs. Crane to beat 
 I now , and say thing more hard to bear than beat ; and you have 
 take I to yourself . how I pray for that ! and I take care of you too , 
 Grandy , - do not   I ? I pray for that too ; and as to carriage , " add 
 Sophy , with superb air , " I do not   care if I be never in a carriage as 
 long as I live ; and you know I have be in a van , which be big than 
 a carriage , and I do not   like that at all . but how come people to behave 
 so ill to you , Grandy ? " 

 " I never say people behave ill to I , Sophy . " 

 " do not they take away the carpet and silk curtain , and all the fine 
 thing you have as a little boy ? " 

 " I do not   know , " reply Waife , with a puzzled look , " that people 
 actually take they away ; but they melt away . 

 " however , I have much still to be thankful for : I be so strong , and 
 have such high spirit , Sophy , and find people not behave ill to 
 I , - quite the contrary , so kind . I find no Crane   she monster   as you 
 do , my little angel . such prospect before I , if I have walk straight 
 towards they ! but I follow my own fancy , which lead I zigzag ; and now

 SOPHY . - " not without a livelihood ! - the what do you call

 WAIFE   admiringly . - " sensible child . that be true . yes , Heaven be very

 dear Lizzy , and yet no two person could live more from hand to mouth . " 

 SOPHY   rather jealously . - " tizzy ? " 

 WAIFE   with moistened eye , and look down . - " my wife . she be only 
 spar to I two year : such sunny year ! and how grateful I ought to be

 misery ! " a long pause .

 SOPHY . - " ah , how be that ? " 

 WAIFE . - " it be fair time in the village wherein I stop , and Rugge 's 
 principal actor be take off by delirium tremen , which be Latin for a

 " it be not the Spotted Boy , Grandy ? "

 " shut the door carefully , but see first that the woman be not listen .

 CHAPTER V. 

  Mop become a personage . - much thought be bestow on the verbal

  call himself king , Rome would have rise against he as a Tarquin ; 
  so he remain a simple equestrian , and modestly call himself 
  Imperator . - Mop choose his own title in a most mysterious manner , 
  and cease to be Mop . 

 " the first noticeable defect in your name of Mop , " say Gentleman Waife ,

 not impose , and in strike composition their meaning be elevate by

 Isaac look and grin affectionately ; and under that title learn a

 most abstruse could not accurately cast up a simple sum in addition . 
 nothing bring he to the end of his majestic tether like dot and carry 
 one . notable type of our human incompleteness , where man might deem our 
 study have make we most complete ! notable type , too , of that grandest 
 order of all human genius which seem to arrive at result by intuition , 
 which a child might pose by a row of figure on a slate , while it be 
 solve the law that link the star to infinity ! but revenon a nos 
 mouton , what be the astral attraction that incontestably bind the 
 reminiscence of Mop to the cognominal distinction of Sir Isaac ? I have 
 prepare a very erudite and subtle treatise upon this query , enliven 
 by quotation from the ancient mystic , - such as Iamblicus and

 modern Spiritualists , from Sir Kenelm Digby and Swedenborg , to Monsieur 
 Cahagnet and Judge Edwards . it be to be call Inquiry into the Law of 
 Affinities , by Philomopsos : when , unluckily for my treatise , I arrive 
 at the knowledge of a fact which , though it do not render the treatise 
 less curious , knock on the head the theory upon which it be base . 
 the baptismal name of the old soldier , Mop 's first proprietor and 
 early preceptor , be Isaac ; and his master be call in the homely 
 household by that christian name , the sound have enter into Mop 's 
 young and most endear association . his canine affection have do 
 much towards ripen his scholastic education . " where be Isaac ? " " call 
 Isaac ! " " Fetch Isaac his hat , " etc . still be that name when the 
 old soldier die ; but when hear again , Mop 's heart be move , and in 
 miss the old master , he feel more at home with the new . as for the 
 title , " Sir , " it be a mere expletive in his ear . such be the fact , 
 and such the deduction to be draw from it . not that it will satisfy 
 every one . I know that philosopher who deny all that they have not 
 witness , and refuse to witness what they resolve to deny , will reject 
 the story in toto ; and will prove , by reference to their own dog , 
 that a dog never recognize the name of his master , - never yet could be 
 teach arithmetic . I know also that there be mystic who will prefer 
 to believe that Mop be in direct spiritual communication with unseen 
 Isaacs , or in a state of clairvoyance , or under the influence of the 
 odic fluid . but do we ever yet find in human reason a question with 
 only one side to it ? be not truth a polygon ? have not sage arise in 
 our day to deny even the principle of gravity , for which we bad be 
 so long contentedly take the word of the great Sir Isaac ? it be that 
 blessed spirit of controversy which keep the world go ; and it be 
 that which , perhaps , explain why Mr. Waife , when his memory be fairly 
 put to it , could remember , out of the history of the myriad who have 
 occupy our planet from the date of Adam to that in which I now write , 
 so very few man whom the world will agree to call wise , and out of that 
 very few so scant a percentage with name sufficiently know to make 
 they more popularly significant of pre - eminent sagacity than if they have 
 be call - Mops . 




 CHAPTER VI . 

  the vagrant have get his dog , proceed to hunt fortune with it , 
  leave behind he a trap to catch rat . - what the trap do catch 
  be " just like his luck . " 

 Sir Isaac , to designate he by his new name , improve much upon 
 acquaintance . he be still in the ductile season of youth , and take to 
 learn as an amusement to himself . his last master , a stupid sot , 
 have not gain his affection ; and perhaps even the old soldier , though 
 gratefully remember and mourn , have not steal into his innermost 
 heart , as Waife and Sophy gently contrive to do . in short , in a very

 when Waife have ascertain the extent of his accomplishment , and add 
 somewhat to their range in matter which cost no great trouble , he 
 apply himself to the task of compose a little drama which might 
 bring they all into more interesting play , and in which though Sophy 
 and himself be performer the dog have the premier role . and as soon 
 as this be do , and the dog 's performance thus range into methodical 
 order and sequence , he resolve to set off to a considerable town at 
 some distance , and to which Mr. Rugge be no visitor . 

 his bill at the cottage make but slight inroad into his pecuniary 
 resource ; for in the interval of leisure from his instruction to Sir 
 Isaac , Waife have perform various little service to the lone widow 
 with whom they lodge , which Mrs. Saunders   such be her name   insist 
 upon regard as money 's worth . he have repair and regulate to a 
 minute an old clock which have take no note of time for the last three 
 year ; he have mend all the break crockery by some cement of his

 ingenuity be remarkable , for when there be only a fragment to be find 
 of a cup and a fragment or two of a saucer , he unite they both into 
 some pretty form , which , if not useful , at all event look well on a 
 shelf . he bind , in smart showy paper , sundry tattere old book which 
 have belong to his landlady 's defunct husband , a Scotch gardener , and 
 which she display on a side table , under the japan tea - tray . More than 
 all , he be of service to she in her vocation ; for Mrs. Saunders eke 
 out a small pension - which she derive from the affectionate providence 
 of her Scotch husband , in insure his life in her favour - by the 
 rearing and sale of poultry ; and Waife save she the expense of a 
 carpenter by the construction of a new coop , elevate above the reach of 
 the rat , who have hitherto make sad ravage amongst the chicken ; while 
 he confide to her certain secret in the improvement of breed and the 
 cheap process of fattening , which excite her gratitude no less than 
 her wonder . " the fact be , " say Gentleman Waife , " that my life have 
 know makeshift . once , in a foreign country , I keep poultry , upon the 
 principle that the poultry should keep I . " 

 strange it be to notice such versatility of invention , such readiness 
 of resource , such familiarity with diver nook and cranny in the 
 practical experience of life , in a man now so hard put to it for a 
 livelihood . there be person , however , who might have a good stock of 
 talent , if they do not turn it all into small change . and you , reader , 
 know as well as I do , that when a sovereign or a shilling be once 
 break into , the change scatter and dispend itself in a way quite 
 unaccountable . still copper be useful in household bill ; and when 
 Waife be really at a pinch , somehow or other , by hook or by crook , he 
 scrap together intellectual halfpence enough to pay his way . 

 Mrs. Saunders grow quite fond of her lodger . Waife she regard as a 
 prodigy of genius ; Sophy be the prettiest and good of child . Sir 
 Isaac , she take for grant , be worthy of his owner . but the Comedian 
 do not confide to she his dog 's learning , nor the use to which he 
 design to put it . and in still great precaution , when he take his 
 leave , he extract from Mrs. Saunders a solemn promise that she would 
 set no one on his track in case of impertinent inquiry . 

 " you see before you , " say he , " a man who have enemy , such as rat be 
 to your chicken : chicken despise rat when raise , as yours be now , 
 above the reach of claw and tooth . some day or other I may so raise a 
 coop for that little one : I be too old for coop . meanwhile , if a rat 
 come sneak here after we , send it off the wrong way , with a flea in 
 its ear . " 

 Mrs. Saunders promise , between tear and laughter ; bless Waife , 
 kissed Sophy , pat Sir Isaac , and stand long at her threshold watch 
 the three , as the early sun light their form recede in the narrow green 
 lane , - dewdrop sparkle on the hedgerow , and the skylark spring 
 upward from the young corn . 

 then she slowly turn indoor , and her home seem very solitary . we 
 can accustom ourselves to loneliness , but we should beware of infringe 
 the custom . once admit two or three face seat at your hearthside , 
 or gaze out from your window on the laugh sun , and when they be 
 go , they carry off the glow from your grate and the sunbeam from your 
 pane . poor Mrs. Saunders ! in vain she seek to rouse herself , to put 
 the room to right , to attend to the chicken to distract her thought . 
 the one - eyed cripple , the little girl , the shaggy - faced dog , still 
 haunt she ; and when at noon she din all alone off the remnant of 
 the last night 's social supper , the very click of the renovated clock 
 seem to say , " Gone , go ; " and mutter , " ah ! gone , " she recline 
 back on her chair , and indulge herself in a good womanlike cry . from 
 this luxury she be startle by a knock at the door . " could they have 
 come back ? " no ; the door open , and a genteel young man , in a black 
 coat and white neckcloth , step in . 

 " I beg your pardon , ma'am - your name 's Saunders - sell poultry ? " 

 " at your service , sir . spring chicken ? " poor people , whatever their 
 grief , must sell their chicken , if they have any to sell . 

 " thank you , ma'am ; not at this moment . the fact be , that I call to make 
 some inquiry have not you lodger here ? " 

 lodger ! at that word the expand soul of Mrs. Saunders reclose 
 hermetically ; the last warning of Waife revibrate in her ear this 
 white neckclothed gentleman , be he not a rat ? 

 " no , sir , I ha'n't   no lodger . " 

 " but you have have some lately , eh ? a crippled elderly man and a little 
 girl . " 

 " do not   know anything about they ; leastway , " say Mrs. Saunders , 
 suddenly remember that she be tell less to deny fact than to send 
 inquirer upon wrong direction , " leastway , at this blessed time . pray , 
 sir , what make you ask ? " 

 " why , I be instruct to come down to - - - , and find out where this 
 person , one William Waife , have go . arrive yesterday , ma'am . all I 
 could hear be , that a person answer to his description leave the place 
 several day ago , and have be see by a boy , who be tend sheep , to 
 come down the lane to your house , and you be suppose to have lodger 
  you take lodger sometimes , I think , ma'am , because you have be 
 buy some trifling article of food not in your usual way of custom . 
 circumstantial evidence , ma'am : you can have no motive to conceal the 
 truth . " 

 " I should think not indeed , sir , " retort Mrs. Saunders , whom the 
 ominous word " circumstantial evidence " set doubly on her guard . " I do 
 see a gentleman such as you mention , and a pretty young lady , about ten 
 day agone , or so , and they do lodge here a night or two , but they be 
 go to - " 

 " yes , ma'am , - go where ? " 

 " Lunnon . " 

 " really - very likely . by the train or on foot ? " 

 " on foot , I s'pose . " 

 " thank you , ma'am . if you should see they again , or hear where they be , 
 oblige I by convey this card to Mr. Waife . my employer , ma'am , Mr. 
 Gotobed , Craven Street , Strand , - eminent solicitor . he have something of 
 importance to communciate to Mr. Waife . " 

 " yes , sir , - a lawyer ; I understand . " and as of all ratlike animal in 
 the world Mrs. Saunders have the ignorance to deem a lawyer be the most 
 emphatically devouring , she congratulate herself with her whole heart

 the black - coat gentleman have thus obey his instruction and

 have leave at the turnstile . " back to the inn , " cry he , " quick : I must

 house ; and be altogether in the wrong , - in a manner highly creditable to

 " in London , of course : all people of that kind come back to London , "

 my distinguished client . Most satisfactory . that young man will push his 
 way , - businesslike and methodical . " 




 CHAPTER VII . 

  the cloud have its silver lining . 

 thus turn his back on the good fortune which he have so carefully 
 caution Mrs. Saunders against favour on his behalf , the vagrant be 
 now on his way to the ancient municipal town of Gatesboro ' , which , be 
 the near place of fitting opulence and population , Mr. Waife have 
 resolve to honour with the debut of Sir Isaac as soon as he have 
 appropriate to himself the service of that promising quadrupe . he 
 have consult a map of the county before quit Mr. Merle 's roof , 
 and ascertain that he could reach Gatesboro ' by a short cut for 
 foot - traveller along field and lane . he be always glad to avoid 
 the high road : doubtless for such avoidance he have good reason . 
 but prudential reason be in this instance support by vagrant 
 inclination . high road be for the prosperous . by - path and ill - luck 
 go together . but by - path have their charm , and ill - luck its pleasant 
 moment . 

 they pass then from the high road into a long succession of green 
 pasture , through which a straight public path conduct they into one 
 of those charming lane never see out of this bowery England , - a lane 
 deep sink amidst high bank with overhang oak , and quiver ash , 
 gnarl wych - elm , vivid holly and shaggy bramble , with wild convolvulus 
 and creep woodbine force sweet life through all . sometimes the 
 bank open abruptly , leave patch of green sward , and peep through 
 still sequester gate , or over moss - grow pale , into the park or 
 paddock of some rural thane . new villa or old manor - house on lawny 
 upland , knitting , as it be , together England 's feudal memory with 
 England 's freeborn hope , - the old land with its young people ; for 
 England be so old , and the English be so young ! and the gray cripple 
 and the bright - haired child often pause , and gaze upon the demesne 
 and home of owner whose lot be cast in such pleasant place . but 
 there be no grudging envy in their gaze ; perhaps because their life be 
 too remote from those grand belonging . and therefore they could enjoy 
 and possess every banquet of the eye . for at least the beauty of what we 
 see be ours for the moment , on the simple condition that we do not covet 
 the thing which give to our eye that beauty . as the measureless sky 
 and the unnumbered star be equally grant to king and to beggar ; and 
 in our wild ambition we do not sigh for a monopoly of the empyrean , 
 or the fee - simple of the planet : so the earth too , with all its fence 
 garden and embattle wall , all its landmark of stern property and 
 churlish ownership , be ours too by right of eye . our to gaze on the 
 fair possession with such delight as the gaze can give ; grudge to the 
 unseen owner his other , and , it may be , more troubled right , as little 
 as we grudge an astral proprietor his acre of light in Capricorn . 
 Benignant be the law that saith , " thou shalt not covet . " 

 when the sun be at the high our wayfarer find a shadowy nook for 
 their rest and repast . before they run a shallow limpid trout - stream ; 
 on the other side its margin , low grassy meadow , a farmhouse in the 
 distance , back by a still grove , from which rise a still church tower 
 and its still spire . behind they , a close - shaven slop lawn terminate 
 the hedgerow of the lane ; see clearly above it , with parterre of 
 flower on the sward , droop lilac and laburnum far back , and a 
 pervade fragrance from the brief - live and rich syringas . the cripple 
 have climb over a wooden rail that separate the lane from the rill , 
 and seat himself under the shade of a fantastic hollow thorn - tree . 
 Sophy , recline beside he , be gather some pale scentless violet 
 from a mound which the bramble have guard from the sun . the dog have 
 descend to the water to quench his thirst , but still stand knee - deep 
 in the shallow stream , and appear lose in philosophical contemplation 
 of a swarm of minnow , which his immersion have disturb , but which 
 now make itself again visible on the farth side of the glassy brook , 
 undulate round and round a tiny rocklet which interrupt the glide 
 of the wave , and cause they to break into a low melodious murmur . " for 
 these and all thy mercy , o Lord , make we thankful , " say the victim of 
 ill - luck , in the trite word of a pious custom . but never , perhaps , at 
 aldermanic feast be the grace more sincerely say . 

 and then he untie the bundle , which the dog , who have hitherto carry 
 it by the way , have now carefully deposit at his side . " as I live , " 
 ejaculate Waife , " Mrs. Saunders be a woman in ten thousand . see , Sophy , 
 not content with the bread and cheese to which I bid her stint her 
 beneficence , a whole chicken , - a little cake too for you , Sophy ; she have 
 not even forget the salt . Sophy , that woman deserve the handsome 
 token of our gratitude ; and we will present she with a silver teapot the 
 first moment we can afford it . " 

 his spirit exhilarate by the unexpected good cheer , the Comedian 
 give way to his naturally blithe humour ; and between every mouthful he 
 rattle or rather drolle on , now infant - like , now sage - like . he cast 
 out the ray of his liberal humour , careless where they fall , - on the 
 child , on the dog , on the fish that play beneath the wave , on the 
 cricket that chirp amidst the grass ; the woodpecker tap the tree , 
 and the cripple 's merry voice answer it in bird - like mimicry . to this 
 riot of genial babble there be a listener , of whom neither grandfather 
 nor grandchild be aware . conceal by thick brushwood a few pace 
 far on , a young angler , who might be five or six and twenty , have 
 seat himself , just before the arrival of our vagrant to those bank 
 and water , for the purpose of change an unsuccessful fly . at the 
 sound of voice , perhaps suspect an unlicensed rival , for that part 
 of the stream be preserve , - he have suspend his task , and noiselessly 
 put aside the cluster leaf to reconnoitre . the piety of Waife 's 
 simple grace seem to surprise he pleasingly , for a sweet approve 
 smile cross his lip . he continue to look and to listen . he forget 
 the fly , and a trout sail he by unheeded . but Sir Isaac , have 
 probably satisfy his speculative mind as to the natural attribute of 
 minnow , now slowly reascende the bank , and after a brief halt and a 
 sniff , walk majestically towards the hidden observer , look at he 
 with great solemnity , and utter an inquisitive bark , - a bark not 
 hostile , not menacing ; purely and dryly interrogative . thus detect , 
 the angler rise ; and Waife , whose attention be direct that way by the 
 bark , see he , call to Sir Isaac , and say politely , " there be no harm 
 in my dog , sir . " 

 the young man mutter some inaudible reply , and , lift up his rod as 
 in sign of his occupation or excuse for his vicinity , come out from 
 the intervene foliage , and step quietly to Waife 's side . Sir Isaac 
 follow he , sniff again , seem satisfied ; and seat himself on 
 his haunch , fix his attention upon the remain of the chicken which 
 lie defenceless on the grass . the new comer be evidently of the rank of 
 gentleman ; his figure be slim and graceful , his face pale , meditative , 
 refine . he would have impress you at once with the idea of what he 
 really be , - an Oxford scholar ; and you would perhaps have guess he 
 design for the ministry of the Church , if not actually in order . 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

  Mr. Waife excite the admiration , and benignly pity the infirmity , 
  of an Oxford scholar . 

 " you be str - str - stranger ? " say the Oxonian , after a violent exertion 
 to express himself , cause by an impediment in his speech . 

 WAIFE . - " yes , sir , traveller . I trust we be not trespass : this be 
 not private ground , I think ? " 

 OXONIAN . - " and if - f - f - f - it be , my f - f - father would not war - n - n you 
 off - ff - f. " 

 " be it your father 's ground , then ? Sir , I beg you a thousand pardon . " 

 the apology be make in the Comedian 's grand style : it impose 
 greatly on the young scholar . Waife might have be a duke in disguise ; 
 but I will do the angler the justice to say that such discovery of rank 
 would have impress he little more in the vagrant 's favour . it have 
 be that impromptu " grace " - that thanksgive which the scholar feel 
 be for something more than the carnal food - which have first command 
 his respect and waken his interest . then that innocent careless 
 talk - part utter to dog and child , part soliloquize , part throw out 
 to the ear of the lively teem nature - have touch a somewhat kindre 
 chord in the angler 's soul ; for he be somewhat of a poet and much of a 
 soliloquist , and could confer with Nature , nor feel that impediment 
 in speech which obstruct his intercourse with man . have thus far 
 indicate that oral defect in our new acquaintance , the reader will 
 cheerfully excuse I for not enforce it over much . let it be among 
 the thing subaudita , as the sense of it give to a gifted and aspiring 
 nature , thwart in the sublime career of Preacher , an exquisite 
 mournful pain . and I no more like to raise a laugh at his infirmity 
 behind his back , than I should before his pale , powerful , melancholy 
 face ; therefore I suppress the infirmity in give the reply . 

 OXONIAN . - " on the other side the lane , where the garden slop downward , 
 be my father 's house . this ground be his property certainly , but he put 
 it to its good use , in lend it to those who so piously acknowledge 
 that Father from whom all good come . your child , I presume , sir ? " 

 " my grandchild . " 

 " she seem delicate : I hope you have not far to go ? " 

 " not very far , thank you , sir . but my little girl look more delicate 
 than she be . you be not tired , darling ? " 

 " oh , not at all ! " there be no mistaking the look of real love 
 interchange between the old man and the child ; the scholar feel much 
 interested and somewhat puzzle . 

 " who and what could they be ? so unlike foot wayfarer ! " on the other 
 hand , too , Waife take a liking to the courteous young man , and conceive 
 a sincere pity for his physical affliction . but he do not for those 
 reason depart from the discreet caution he have prescribe to himself in 
 seek new fortune and shun old peril , so he turn the subject . 

 " you be an angler , sir ? I suppose the trout in the stream run small ? " 

 " not very : a little high up I have catch they at four pound weight . " 

 WAIFE . - " there go a fine fish yonder , - see ! balance himself between 
 those weed . " 

 OXONIAN . - " poor fellow , let he be safe to - day . after all , it be a cruel 
 sport , and I should break myself of it . but it be strange that whatever 
 our love for nature we always seek some excuse for trust ourselves 
 alone to she . a gun , a rod , a sketch - book , a geologist 's hammer , an 
 entomologist 's net , a something . " 

 WAIFE . - " be it not because all our idea would run wild if not 
 concentrate on a definite pursuit ? Fortune and Nature be earnest 
 female , though popular beauty ; and they do not look upon coquettish 
 trifler in the light of genuine wooer . " 

 the Oxonian , who , in vent his previous remark , have think it likely 
 he should be above his listener 's comprehension , look surprised . what 
 pursuit , too , have this one - eyed philosopher ? 

 " you have a definite pursuit , sir ? " 

 " I - alas ! when a man moralize , it be a sign that he have know error : 
 it be because I have be a trifl that I rail against trifler . and 
 talk of that , time fly , and we must be off and away . " 

 Sophy re - tie the bundle . Sir Isaac , on whom , meanwhile , she have 
 bestow the remain of the chicken , jump up and describe a circle . 

 " I wish you success in your pursuit , whatever it be , " stutter out the 
 angler . 

 " and I no less heartily , sir , wish you success in your . " 

 " mine ! success there be beyond my power . " 

 " how , sir ? do it rest so much with other ? " 

 " no , my failure be in myself . my career should be the Church , my pursuit 
 the cure of soul , and - and - this pitiful infirmity ! how can I speak the 
 Divine Word - I - I - a stutterer ! " 

 the young man do not pause for an answer , but plunge through the 
 brushwood that bespread the bank of the rill , and his hurried path 
 could be trace by the wave of the foliage through which he force his 
 way . 

 " we all have our burden , " say Gentleman Waife , as Sir Isaac take up 
 the bundle and stalk on , placid and refresh . 




 CHAPTER IX . 

  the nomad , enter into civilized life , adopt its art , shave his 
  poodle , and put on a black coat . - hint at the process by which a 
  Cast - off exalt himself into a take - in . 

 at twilight they stop at a quiet inn within eight mile of 
 Gatesboro ' . Sophy , much tired , be glad to creep to bed . Waife sit up 
 long after she ; and , in preparation for the eventful morrow , wash 
 and shave Sir Isaac . you would not have know the dog again ; he be 
 dazzle . not Ulysses , rejuvenate by Pallas Athene , could have be 
 more change for the well . his flank reveal a skin most daintily 
 mottle ; his tail become leonine , with an imperial tuft ; his mane fall 
 in long curl like the beard of a Ninevite king ; his boot be those 
 of a courtier in the reign of Charles II . ; his eye look forth in 
 dark splendour from lock white as the drive snow . this feat perform , 
 Waife sleep the sleep of the righteous , and Sir Isaac , stretch on the 
 floor beside the bed , lick his mottled flank and shiver : " il 
 faut souffrir pour etre beau . " much marvelling , Sophy the next morning 
 behold the dog ; but , before she be up , Waife have pay the bill and be 
 wait for she on the road , impatient to start . he do not heed she 
 exclamation , half compassionate , half admire ; he be absorb in 
 thought . thus they proceed slowly on till within two mile of the 
 town , and then Waife turn aside , enter a wood , and there , with the 
 aid of Sophy , put the dog upon a deliberate rehearsal of the anticipated 
 drama . the dog be not in good spirit , but he go through his part 
 with mechanical accuracy , though slight enthusiasm . 

 " he be to be rely upon , in spite of his french origin , " say Waife . 
 " all national prejudice fade before the sense of a common interest . 
 and we shall always find more genuine solidity of character in a french 
 poodle than in an english mastiff , whenever a poodle be of use to we 
 and the mastiff be not . but oh , waste of care ! oh , sacrifice of time 
 to empty name ! oh , emblem of fashionable education ! it never strike I 
 before , - do it not , child though thou art , strike thee now , - by the 
 necessity of our drama , this animal must be a french dog ? " 

 " well , Grandfather ? " 

 " and we have give he an english name ! precious result of our own 
 scholastic training , teach at preparatory academy precisely that 
 which avail we naught when we be to face the world ! what be to be 
 do ? unlearn he his own cognoman , - teach he another name , - too late , 
 too late . we can not afford the delay . " 

 " I do not   see why he should be call any name at all . he observe your 
 sign just as well without . " 

 " if I have but discover that at the beginning . pity ! such a fine name 
 too . Sir Isaac ! vanita vanitatum ! what desire chiefly kindle the 
 ambitious ? to create a name , perhaps bequeath a title , - exalt into Sir 
 Isaacs a progeny of slop ! and , after all , it be possible   let we hope 
 it in this instance   that a sensible young dog may learn his letter and 
 shoulder his musket just as well , though all the appellation by 
 which humanity know he be condense into a pitiful monosyllable . 
 nevertheless   as you will find when you be old , people be oblige 
 in practice to renounce for themselves the application of those rule 
 which they philosophically prescribe for other . thus , while I grant 
 that a change of name for that dog be a question belong to the policy 
 of Ifs and but , commonly call the policy of Expediency , about which 
 one may differ from other and one 's own self every quarter of an hour , 
 a change of name for I belong to the policy of Must and Shall ; namely 
 the policy of Necessity , against which let no dog bark , - though I have 
 know dog howl at it ! William Waife be no more : he be dead ; he be 
 bury ; and even Juliet Araminta be the baseless fabric of a vision . " 

 Sophy raise inquiringly her blue guileless eye . 

 " you see before you a man who have use up the name of Waife , and who on 
 enter the town of Gatesboro ' become a sober , staid , and respectable 
 personage , under the appellation of Chapman . you be Miss Chapman . Rugge 
 and his exhibition ' leave not a wrack behind . ' " 

 Sophy smile , and then sigh , - the smile for her grandfather 's gay 
 spirit ; wherefore the sigh ? be it that some instinct in that fresh , 
 loyal nature revolt from the thought of these alias , which , if 
 requisite for safety , be still akin to imposture ? if so , poor child , 
 she have much yet to set right with her conscience ! all I can say be , 
 that after she have smile she sigh . and more reasonably might a reader 
 ask his author to subject a zephyr to the microscope than a female 's 
 sigh to analysis . 

 " take the dog with you , my dear , back into the lane ; I will join you in 
 a few minute . you be neatly dress , and , if not , would look so . I , in 
 this old coat , have the air of a pedler , so I will change it , and enter 
 the town of Gatesboro ' in the character of - a man whom you will soon 
 see before you . leave those thing alone , de - Isaacized Sir Isaac ! follow 
 your mistress , - go ! " 

 Sophy leave the wood , and walk on slowly towards the town , with she 
 hand pensively rest on Sir Isaac 's head . in less than ten minute 
 she be join by Waife , attire in respectable black ; his hat and shoe 
 well brush ; a new green shade to his eye ; and with his fine air of 
  Pere noble . he be now in his favourite element . he be act : 
 call it not imposture . be Lord Chatham an impostor when he drape his 
 flannel into the fold of the toga , and arrange the curl of his wig 
 so as to add more sublime effect to the majesty of his brow and the 
 terror of its nod ? and certainly , consider that Waife , after all , 
 be but a professional vagabond , consider all the turn and shift to 
 which he have be put for bread and salt , the wonder be , not that he be 
 full of stage trick and small deception , but that he have contrive to 
 retain at heart so much childish simplicity . when a man for a series of 
 year have only have his wit to live by , I say not that he be necessarily 
 a rogue , - he may be a good fellow ; but you can scarcely expect his 
 code of honour to be precisely the same as Sir Philip Sidney 's . Homer 
 express through the lip of Achilles that sublime love of truth 
 which even in those remote time be the become characteristic of a 
 gentleman and a soldier . but then , Achilles be well off during his whole 
 life , which , though distinguish , be short . on the other hand Ulysses , 
 who be sorely put to it , keep out of his property in Ithaca , and , in 
 short , live on his wit , be not the less befriended by the immaculate 
 Pallas because his wisdom savour somewhat of stage trick and sharp

 commend to modern imitation . 

 just as our traveller near the town , the screech of a railway whistle

 the landlady be mollify in a moment . nor be it only the rivalry

 disdain of shilling . a vague remembrance of the well - know story how a

  show with what success Gentleman Waife assume the pleasing part

 serious turn of mind whose opinion that sentiment might shock . " 




 CHAPTER XI . 

  HISTORICAL problem : " be Gentleman Waife a swindler or a man of

 CHAPTER XIII . 

  Omne ignotum pro magnifico . - Rumour , know nothing of his 
  antecedent , exalt Gentleman Waife into Don Magnifico . 

 the Comedian and his two coadjutor be follow to the Saracen 's Head 
 inn by a large crowd , but at respectful distance . though I know few 
 thing less pleasing than to have be decoy and entrap into an 
 unexpected demand upon one 's purse , - when one only count , too , upon 
 an agreeable evening , - and hold , therefore , in just abhorrence the 
 circulate plate which sometimes follow a public oration , homily , or 
 other eloquent appeal to british liberality ; yet , I will venture to say , 
 there be not a creature whom the Comedian have surprise into impulsive 
 beneficence who regret his action , grudge its cost , or think he 
 have pay too dear for his entertainment . all have go through a series

 pleasure . but , strange to say , no one could satisfactorily explain to 
 himself these two question , - for what , and to whom have he give his 
 money ? it be not a general conjecture that the exhibitor want the 
 money for his own us . no ; despite the evidence in favour of that idea , 
 a person so respectable , so dignified , address they , too , with that 
 noble assurance to which a man who beg for himself be not morally 
 entitle , - a person thus characterize must be some high - hearted 
 philanthropist who condescend to display his power at an Institute 
 purely intellectual , perhaps on behalf of an eminent but decay author , 
 whose name , from the respect due to letter , be delicately conceal . 
 Mr. Williams , consider the hard head and most practical man in the 
 town , originate and maintain that hypothesis . probably the stranger 
 be an author himself , a great and affluent author . have not great and 
 affluent author - man who be the boast of our time and land - act , 
 yea , on a common stage , and act inimitably too , on behalf of some 
 lettered brother or literary object ? therefore in these guileless 
 mind , with all the pecuniary advantage of extreme penury and

 circumstance and high renown . but there be one universal wish

 and that wish be to renew the pleasure they have experience , even if 
 they pay the same price for it . could not the long - close theatre be 
 re - open , and the great man be induce by philanthropic motif , and 
 an assured sum raise by voluntary subscription , to gratify the whole 
 town , as he have gratify its select intellect ? Mr. Williams , in a 
 state of charitable thaw , now softest of the soft , like most hard man 
 when once soften , suggest this idea to the Mayor . the Mayor say 
 evasively that he would think of it , and that he intend to pay his 
 respect to Mr. Chapman before he return home , that very night : it be 
 proper . Mr. Williams and many other wish to accompany his worship . 
 but the kind magistrate suggest that Mr. Chapman would be greatly 
 fatigue : that the presence of many might seem more an intrusion than a 
 compliment ; that he , the Mayor , have well go alone , and at a somewhat 
 later hour , when Mr. Chapman , though not retire to bed , might have 
 have time for rest and refreshment . this delicate consideration have its 
 weight ; and the street be thin when the Mayor 's gig stop , on its 
 way villa - ward , at the saracen 's Head . 




 CHAPTER XIV . 

  it be the interval between our first repining and our final 
  resignation , in which , both with individual and community , be to 
  be find all that make a history worth tell . Ere yet we yearn 
  for what be out of our reach , we be still in the cradle . when 
  weary out with our yearning , desire again fall asleep ; we be on 
  the deathbed . 

 Sophy   lean on her grandfather 's arm as they ascend the stair of the 
 Saracen 's Head . - " but I be so tired , Grandy : I would rather go to bed at 
 once , please ! " 

 GENTLEMAN WAIFE . - " surely you could take something to eat

 " well , " say he , " you be a pretty child ! - a very pretty child , and you

 SOPHY   with a sigh . - " Thank Heaven ! that be over now ; and you will not be 
 in want of money for a long , long time ! dear Sir Isaac ! "

 pleasure . they be now in Sophy 's room ; and Waife , after again press

 blessing , and whistle " malbrook s'en va - t - en guerre " to Sir Isaac ,

 leave alone , the child breathe long and hard , press her hand to her 
 bosom , and sink wearily on the foot of the bed . there be no shutter 
 to the window , and the moonlight come in gently , steal across that 
 part of the wall and floor which the ray of the candle leave in shade . 
 the girl raise her eye slowly towards the window , - towards the glimpse 
 of the blue sky , and the slanting lustre of the moon . there be a certain 
 epoch in our childhood , when what be call the romance of sentiment 
 first make itself vaguely feel . and ever with the dawn of that 
 sentiment the moon and the star take a strange and haunt 
 fascination . few person in middle life - even though they be genuine 
 poet - feel the peculiar spell in the severe stillness and mournful 
 splendour of starry sky which impress most of we , even though no 
 poet at all , in that mystic age when Childhood nearly touch upon 
 Youth , and turn an unquiet heart to those marvellous riddle within we 
 and without , which we cease to conjecture when experience have teach we 
 that they have no solution upon this side the grave . lure by the light , 
 the child rise softly , approach the window , and , rest her upturned 
 face upon both hand , gaze long into the heaven , commune evidently 
 with herself , for her lip move and murmur indistinctly . slowly she 
 retire from the casement , and again seat herself at the foot of the 
 bed , disconsolate . and then her thought run somewhat thus , though she 
 might not have shape they exactly in the same word : " no , I can not 
 understand it . why be I content and happy before I know he ? why do 
 I see no harm , no shame in this way of life - not even on that stage with 
 those people - until he say , ' it be what he wish I have never stoop 
 to ' ? and Grandfather say our path be so different they can not cross 
 each other again . there be a path of life , then , which I can never 
 enter ; there be a path on which I must always , always walk , always , 
 always , always that path , - no escape ! never to come into that other one 
 where there be no disguise , no hiding , no false name , - never , never ! " 
 she start impatiently , and with a wild look , - " it be kill I ! " 

 then , terrify by her own impetuosity , she throw herself on the bed , 
 weep low . her heart have now go back to her grandfather ; it be 
 smite she for ingratitude to he . could there be shame or wrong in 
 what he ask , - what he do ? and be she to murmur if she aid he to 
 exist ? what be the opinion of a stranger boy compare to the approve 
 shelter love of her sole guardian and try foster friend ? and 
 could people choose their own calling and mode of life ? if one road 
 go this way , another that , and they on the one road be bear far 
 and far away from those on the other - as that idea come , consolation 
 stop , and in her noiseless weep there be a bitterness as of 
 despair . but the tear end by relieve the grief that cause they . 
 weary out of conjecture and complaint , her mind relapse into the 
 old native , childish submission . with a fervour in which there be 
 self - reproach she repeat her meek , nightly prayer , that God would 
 bless her dear grandfather , and suffer she to be his comfort and 
 support . then mechanically she undress , extinguish the candle , and 
 creep into bed . the moonlight become bold and bolder ; it advance tip 
 the floor , along the wall ; now it flood she very pillow , and seem to 
 her eye to take a holy love kindness , holy and more loving as the 
 lid droop beneath it . a vague remembrance of some tale of " guardian 
 spirit , " with which Waife have once charm her wonder , stir 
 through her lulling thought , link itself with the presence of that 
 encircle moonlight . there ! see the eyelid be closed , no tear upon 
 their fringe . see the dimple steal out as the sweet lip be part . 
 she sleep , she dream already ! where and what be the rude world of 
 wake now ? be there not guardian spirit ? deride the question if 
 thou wilt , stern man , the reasoning and self - reliant ; but thou , o fair 
 mother , who hast mark the strange happiness on the face of a child 
 that have weep itself to sleep , what sayest thou to the soft tradition , 
 which surely have its origin in the heart of the early mother ? 




 CHAPTER XV . 

  there be no man so friendless but what he can find a friend sincere

 ah , scamp , vagrant , be not heaven kind to thee at last ? a few more such

 CHAPTER XVI . 

  in every civilized society there be find a race of man who retain 
  the instinct of the aboriginal cannibal , and live upon their 
  fellow - man as a natural food . these interesting but formidable 
  bipeds , have catch their victim , invariably select one part of 
  his body on which to fasten their relentless grinder . the part 
  thus select be peculiarly susceptible , Providence have make it 
  alive to the least nibble ; it be situate just above the hip - joint , 
  it be protect by a tegument of exquisite fibre , vulgarly call 
  " the BREECHES pocket . " the thoroughbred Anthropophagite usually 
  begin with his own relation and friend ; and so long as he 
  confine his voracity to the domestic circle , the law interfere 
  little , if at all , with his venerable propensity . but when he have 
  exhaust all that allow itself to be edible in the bosom of 
  private life , the man - eater fall loose on society , and take to 
  prowling , - then " Sauve qui peut ! " the law rouse themselves , put on 
  their spectacle , call for their wig and gown , and the 
  Anthropophagite turn prowler be not always sure of his dinner . it 
  be when he have arrive at this stage of development that the man- 
  eater become of importance , enter into the domain of history , and 
  occupy the thought of Moralists . 

 on the same morning in which Waife thus go forth from the saracen 's 
 Head in quest of the doctor , but at a later hour , a man , who , to judge 
 by the elaborate smartness of his attire , and the jaunty assurance of 
 his saunter , must have wander from the gay purlieus of Regent Street , 
 thread his way along the silent and desolate thoroughfare that 
 intersect the remotest district of Bloomsbury . he stop at the turn 
 into a small street still more sequester than those which lead to it , 
 and look up to the angle on the wall whereon the name of the street 
 should have be inscribe . but the wall have be lately whitewash , 
 and the whitewash have obliterate the expect epigraph . the man 
 mutter an impatient execration ; and , turn round as if to seek a 
 passenger of whom to make inquiry , beheld on the opposite side of the 
 way another man apparently engage in the same research . involuntarily 
 each cross over the road towards the other . 

 " pray , sir , " quoth the second wayfarer in that desert , " can you tell I 
 if this be a street that be call a Place , - Podden Place , Upper ? " 

 " Sir , " return the sprucer wayfarer , " it be the question I would have 
 ask of you . " 

 " strange ! " 

 " very strange indeed that more than one person can , in this busy age , 
 employ himself in discover a Podden Place ! not a soul to inquire 
 of , - not a shop that I see , not an orange - stall ! " 

 " ha ! " cry the other , in a hoarse sepulchral voice , " ha ! there be 
 a pot - boy ! boy ! boy ! boy ! I say . hold , there ! hold ! be this Podden 
 Place , - Upper ? " 

 " yes , it be , " answer the pot - boy , with a sleepy air , catch in that 
 sleepy atmosphere ; and chime his pewter against an area rail with a 
 dull clang , he chant forth " pot oho ! " with a note as dirge - like as 
 that which in the City of the Plague chant " out with the dead ! " 

 meanwhile the two wayfarer exchange bow and part ; the sprucer 
 wayfarer whether from the indulgence of a reflective mood , or from an 
 habitual indifference to thing and person not concern he , cease 
 to notice his fellow - solitary , and rather busy himself in sundry 
 little coquetry appertain to his own person . he pass his hand 
 through his hair , re - arrange the cock of his hat , look complacently 
 at his boot , which still retain the gloss of the morning 's varnish , 
 draw down his wristband , and , in a word , give sign of a man who desire 
 to make an effect , and feel that he ought to do it . so occupy be he 
 in this self - commune that when he stop at length at one of the small 
 door in the small street and lift his hand to the knocker , he 
 start to see that Wayfarer the Second be by his side . the two man 
 now examine each other briefly but deliberately . Wayfarer the First be 
 still young , - certainly handsome , but with an indescribable look about 
 the eye and lip , from which the other recoil with an instinctive 
 awe , - a hard look , a cynical look , - a sidelong , quiet , defying , 
 remorseless look . his clothe be so new of gloss that they seem put 
 on for the first time , be shape to the prevail fashion , and of a 
 taste for colour less subdued than be usual with Englishmen , yet still 
 such as a person of good mien could wear without incur the charge 
 of vulgarity , though liable to that of self - conceit . if you doubt that 
 the man be a gentleman , you would have be puzzle to guess what else 
 he could be . be it not for the look we have mention , and which 
 be perhaps not habitual , his appearance might have be call 
 prepossessing . in his figure there be the grace , in his step the 
 elasticity which come from just proportion and muscular strength . 
 in his hand he carry a supple switch - stick , slight and innocuous 
 to appearance , but weight at the handle after the fashion of a 
 life - preserver . the tone of his voice be not displease to the ear , 
 though there might be something artificial in the swell of it , - the sort 
 of tone man assume when they desire to seem more frank and off - hand 
 than belong to their nature , - a sort of rollick tone which be to the 
 voice what swagger be to , the gait . still that look ! it produce on you 
 the effect which might be create by some strange animal , not without 
 beauty , but deadly to man . Wayfarer the Second be big and burly , 
 middle - aged , large - whisker , his complexion dirty . he wear a wig , - a 
 wig evident , unmistakable , - a wig curl and rusty , - over the wig a 
 dingy white hat . his black stock fit tight round his throat , and 
 across his breast he have throw the fold of a Scotch plaid . 

 wayfarer the FIRST . - " you call here , too , - on Mrs. Crane ? " 

 wayfarer the SECOND . - " Mrs. Crane ? you too ? strange ! " 

 wayfarer the FIRST   with constrain civility . - " Sir , I call on 
 business , - private business . " 

 wayfarer the second   with candid surliness . - " so do I. " 

 wayfarer the FIRST . - " oh ! " 

 wayfarer the SECOND . - " ha ! the lock unbar ! " 

 the door open , and an old meagre woman - servant present herself . 

 wayfarer the first   gliding before the big man with a serpent 's 
 undulate celerity of movement . - " Mrs. Crane live here ? " - " yes ! " 
 " she be at home I suppose ? " - " yes ! " - " take up my card ; say I come alone , 
 not with this gentleman . " 

 Wayfarer the Second seem to have be rather put out by the manner of 
 his rival . he recede a step . 

 " you know the lady of this mansion well , sir ? " " extremely well . " 

 " ha ! then I yield you the precedence ; I yield it , sir , but 
 conditionally . you will not be long ? "

 palm , look from the card to the visitor 's face , and then to the card

 " you , Mr. Losely ! you ! - Jasper Losely ! how you be change ! what ha ' ye 
 do to yourself ? where be your comeliness ? where be the look that stole 
 lady ' heart ? you , Jasper Losely ! you be his goblin ! " 

 " hold your peace , old hussey ! " say the visitor , evidently annoy at 
 remark so disparaging . " I be Jasper Losely , more bronzed of cheek , more

 might be in play , for the lip wear smile , or might be in earnest ,

 stride than her age seem to warrant , cry out , " mistress , mistress ! 
 here be Mr. Losely ! Jasper Losely himself ! " by the time the visitor have

 look up and start as he see it . a haggard face , - the face of one 
 over whose life there have pass a blight . when last see by he it have 
 possess beauty , though of a masculine rather than womanly character . 
 now of that beauty not a trace ! the cheek shrunk and hollow leave the

 its ebon hue , now grizzle , harsh , neglect , hang in torture , tangle

 as she thus stand , an acute student of Lavater might have recognize , in

 than on his smooth low forehead ; on her straight stern lip , less cause

 in the room , too , there be some evidence of a cultivate taste . on

 CHAPTER XVII . 

  in life it be difficult to say who do you the most mischief - enemy 
  with the bad intention , or friend with the good . 

 the conference between Mr. Rugge and Mr. Losely terminate in an 
 appointment to meet , the next day , at the village in which this story 
 open . meanwhile Mr. Rugge would return to his " orphan , " and arrange 
 performance in which for some day they might dispense with a father 's 
 part . losely , on his side , undertake to devote the intervene hour to 
 consultation with a solicitor to whom Mr. Rugge recommend he as 
 to the prompt obtaining of legal power to enforce the authority he 
 assert himself to possess . he would also persuade Mrs. Crane to 
 accompany he to the village and aid in the requisite investigation ; 
 entertain a tacit but instinctive belief in the superiority of her 
 acuteness . " set a female to catch a female , " quoth Mr. Rugge . 

 on the day and in the place thus fix the three hunter open their 
 chase . they throw off at the Cobbler 's stall . they soon catch the same 
 scent which have be follow by the lawyer 's clerk . they arrive at 
 Mrs. Saunders 's ; there the two man would have be at fault like their 
 predecessor . but the female be more astute . to drop the metaphor Mrs. 
 Saunders could not stand the sharp cross - examination of one of her own 
 sex . " that woman deceive we , " say Mrs. Crane on leave the house . " 
 they have not go to London . what could they do there ? any man with a 
 few stage juggle trick can get on in country village but would be 
 lose in city . perhaps , as it seem he have get a dog , - we have find 
 out that from Mrs. Saunders , - he will make use of it for an itinerant 
 puppet - show . " 

 " Punch ! " say Mr. Rugge ; " not a doubt of it . "

 we print handbill , offer a reward for their clew , and lure the old 
 man himself by an assurance that the inquiry be make in order that he 
 may learn of something to his advantage . " 

 in the course of the evening the handbill be print . the next day 
 they be post up on the wall , not only of that village , but on those 
 of the small town and hamlet for some mile round . the handbill run 
 invitingly thus , " if William Waife , who leave - on the 20th ult . , will 
 apply at the Red Lion Inn at - - - - , for X. X. , he will learn of 
 something greatly to his advantage . a reward of L5 will be give to any 
 one who will furnish information where the say William Waife and the 
 little girl who accompany he may be find . the say William Waife be 
 about sixty year of age , of middle stature , strongly build , have lose 
 one eye , and be lame of one leg . the little girl , call Sophy , be 
 twelve year old , but look young ; have blue eye and light brown hair . 
 they have with they a white french poodle dog . this bill be print by 
 the friend of the missing party . " the next day pass ; no information : 
 but on the day follow , a young gentleman of good mien , dress in 
 black , ride into the town , stop at the Red Lion Inn , and ask to 
 see X. X. the two man be out on their research ; Mrs. Crane stay at 
 home to answer inquiry . 

 the gentleman be request to dismount , and walk in . Mrs. Crane 
 receive he in the inn parlour , which swarm with fly . she stand in

 " I c - ca - call , " say the gentleman , stammer fearfully , in 
 con - consequence of a b - b - bill - i - ch - chance to see in my ri - ri - ri - ride 
 yesterday - on a wa - wa - wall . you - you , I - sup - sup - " 

 " be X. X. , " put in Mrs. Crane , grow impatient , " one of the friend of 
 Mr. Waife , by whom the handbill have be circulate ; it will indeed be 
 a great relief to we to know where they be , - the little girl more 
 especially . " 

 Mrs. Crane be respectably dress , - in silk iron - gray ; she have crisp 
 her flaky tress into stiff hard ringlet , that fall like long screw 
 from under a black velvet band . Mrs. Crane never wear a cap , nor could 
 you fancy she in a cap ; but the velvet band look as rigid as if 
 gum to a hoop of steel . her manner and tone of voice be those of 
 an educate person , not unused to some society above the vulgar ; and yet 
 the visitor , in whom the reader recognize the piscatorial Oxonian , 
 with whom Waife have interchange philosophy on the marge of the running 
 brooklet , draw back as she advance and speak ; and , bent on an errand of 
 kindness , he be seize with a vague misgiving . 

 MRS . CRANE   blandly . - " I fear they must be badly off . I hope they 
 be not want the necessary of life . but pray be seat , sir . " she 
 look at he again , and with more respect in her address than she

 herself by his side , " a clergyman of the Established Church , I presume ,

 OXONIAN   stammer , as on a former occasion , respectfully omit . - " with 
 this defect , ma'am ! - but to the point . some day ago I happen to fall 
 in with an elderly person , such as be describe , with a very pretty 
 female child and a french dog . the man - gentleman , perhaps I may call 
 he , judge from his conversation - interested I much ; so do the 
 little girl . and if I could be the mean of direct real friend 
 anxious to serve they - " 

 Mrs. CRANE . - " you would indeed be a benefactor . and where be they now ,

 MRS . CRANE . - " quite right , sir . his own friend would not interfere with 
 his roving way , his little whim on any account . poor man , why should 
 they ? he have no property for they to covet . but it be a long story . I 
 have the care of that dear little girl from her infancy , sweet child ! " 

 OXONIAN . - " so she seem . " 

 MRS . CRANE . - " and now she have a most comfortable home provide for she ; 
 and a young girl , with good friend , ought not to be tramp about the 
 country , whatever an old man may do . you must allow that , sir ? " 

 OXONIAN . - " well , - yes , I allow that ; it occur to I . but what be the 
 man ? - the gentleman ? " 

 MRS . CRANE . - " very ' eccentric , ' as you say , and inconsiderate , perhaps , 
 as to the little girl . we will not call it insane , sir . but - be you 
 marry ? " 

 oxonian   blushing . - " no , ma'am . " 

 MRS . CRANE . - " but you have a sister , perhaps ? " 

 OXONIAN . - " yes ; I have one sister . " 

 MRS . CRANE . - " would you like your sister to be run about the country 
 in that way , - carry off from her home , kindre , and friend ? " 

 OXONIAN . - " ah ! I understand . the poor little girl be fond of the old

 MRS . CRANE . - " an unsafe guide for a female child , delicately rear . 
 I rear she ; of good prospect , too . o sir , let we save the child ! 
 look - " she draw from a sidepocket in her stiff iron - gray apron a fold

 " I see , ma'am . I can not hesitate after this . it be a good many mile off

 MRS . CRANE . - " at Gatesboro ' ? be that far ? " 

 OXONIAN . - " some way ; but you can get a cross train from this village . I

 seem very fond of each other . " 

 MRS . CRANE . - " no doubt of it ; very fond : it would be cruel to separate

 OXONIAN . - " oh , ma'am , our poor want for nothing : my father be rich . but 
 if you would oblige I by a line after you have find these interesting 
 person ; I be go to a distant part of the country to - morrow , - to

 MRS . CRANE . - " to Lord Montfort , the head of the noble family of Vipont ? " 

 OXONIAN . - " yes ; do you know any of the family , ma'am ? if you could refer 
 I to one of they , I should feel more satisfied as to - " 

 MRS . CRANE   hastily . - " indeed , sir , every one must know that great 
 family by name and repute . I know no more . so you be go to Lord 
 Montfort 's ! the Marchioness , they say , be very beautiful . " 

 OXONIAN . - " and good as beautiful . I have the honour to be connect both

 Vipont . I should have tell you my name , - Morley ; George Vipont Morley . " 

 Mrs. Crane make a profound courtesy , and , with an unmistakable smile of

 noble family - to a Vipont - that the dear child will owe her restoration

 " I hope I have do right , " say George Vipont Morley , as he mount his

 right ; kiss I , Jane ! " 




 CHAPTER XVIII . 

  let a king and a beggar converse freely together , and it be the 
  beggar 's fault if he do not say something which make the king 
  lift his hat to he . 

 the scene shift back to Gatesboro ' , the forenoon of the day succeed

 MR . HARTOPP . - " what be the matter ? what have happen ? " 

 WAIFE . - " she be very ill , - in a bad way ; the doctor say so , - Dr. Gill . " 

 MR . HARTOPP   feelingly . - " your little girl in a bad way ! oh , no ; 
 doctor always exaggerate in order to get more credit for the cure . not 
 that I would disparage Dr. Gill , fellow - townsman , first - rate man . still 
 ' t be the way with doctor to talk cheerfully if one be in danger , and 
 to look solemn if there be nothing to fear . " 

 WAIFE . - " do you think so : you have child of your own , sir ? - of her

 in try for school prize . "

 thing now , cheek as red as an apple , and as firm as a cricket - ball . "

 Hartopp persist no more ; the excellent man be sincerely grieve at 
 his visitor 's obstinate avoidance of the true question at issue ; for the 
 Mayor could have find employment for a man of Waife 's evident education 
 and talent . but such employment would entail responsibility and trust . 
 how recommend to it a man of whose life and circumstance nothing could 
 be know , - a man without a character ? and Waife interest he deeply . 
 we have all feel that there be some person towards whom we be 
 attract by a peculiar sympathy not to be explain , - a something in 
 the manner , the cut of the face , the tone of the voice . if there be 
 fifty applicant for a benefit in our gift , one of the fifty win his 
 way to our preference at first sight , though with no well right to it 
 than his fellow . we can no more say why we like the man than we can say

 a charm . " there be , " say a Latin love - poet , " no why or wherefore 
 in like . " Hartopp , therefore , have take , from the first moment , to 
 Waife , - the staid , respectable , thriving man , all muffle up from head

 tricksome scatterling , who have not seemingly secure , through the course

 his hearthstone , beside his ledger - book , stand the Mayor , look with 
 a respectful admiration that puzzle himself upon the forlorn creature , 
 who could give no reason why he should not be rather in the Gatesboro ' 
 parish stock than in its chief magistrate 's easy - chair . yet , be 
 the Mayor 's sympathetic liking and respectful admiration wholly 
 unaccountable ? run there not between one warm human heart and another 
 the electric chain of a secret understanding ? in that maim outcast , so 
 stubbornly hard to himself , so tremulously sensitive for his sick child , 
 be there not the majesty to which they who have learn that Nature have 
 her noble , reverently bow the head ! a man true to man 's grave religion 
 can no more despise a life wreck in all else , while a hallowing 
 affection stand out sublime through the rent and chink of fortune , 
 than he can profane with rude mockery a temple in ruin , - if still leave 
 there the altar .

 CHAPTER XX . 

  Sophy hide heart and show temper . 

 the child be lie on a sofa draw near the window in her own room , and

 sophy   passionately . - " I think so ; I think he mean that . I try

 now , if I be anyway master of my own pen , and could write as I
