 CHAPTER XXI . 

 be an essay on temper in general , and a hazardous experiment on the 
 reader 's in particular . 

 there , the window be open ! how instinctively the eye rest upon the 
 green ! how the calm colour lur and soothe it ! but be there to the 
 green only a single hue ? see how infinite the variety of its tint ! what 
 sombre gravity in yon cedar , yon motionless pine - tree ! what lively but 
 unvarying laugh in yon glossy laurel ! do those tint charm we like the 
 play in the young leaf of the lilac , - light here , darker there , as 
 the breeze   and so slight the breeze !   stir they into checker , - into 
 ripple ? oh , sweet green , to the world what sweet temper be to man 's 
 life ! who would reduce into one dye all thy lovely variety ? who 
 exclude the dark steadfast verdure that live on through the winter day ; 
 or the mutinous caprice of the gentle , young tint that come fresh 
 through the tear of April , and will shadow with sportive tremor the 
 bloom of luxuriant June ? 

 happy the man on whose marriage - hearth temper smile kind from the eye 
 of woman ! " no deity present , " saith the heathen proverb , " where absent 
 Prudence ; " no joy long a guest where Peace be not a dweller , - peace , so 
 like Faith that they may be take for each other , and poet have clothe 
 they with the same veil . but in childhood , in early youth , expect not 
 the changeless green of the cedar . wouldst thou distinguish fine temper 
 from spiritless dulness , from cold simulation , - ask less what the temper 
 than what the disposition . 

 be the nature sweet and trustful ; be it free from the morbid self - love 
 which call itself ' sensitive feeling " and fret at imaginary offence ; 
 be the tendency to be grateful for kindness , yet take kindness meekly , 
 and accept as a benefit what the vain call a due ? from disposition thus 
 blessed , sweet temper will come forth to gladden thee , spontaneous and 
 free . quick with some , with some slow , word and look emerge out of the 
 heart . be thy first question , " be the heart itself generous and tender ? " 
 if it be so , self - control come with deepening affection . call not that 
 a good heart which , hasten to sting if a fibre be ruffle , cry , " I 
 be no hypocrite . " accept that excuse , and revenge become virtue . but 
 where the heart , if it give the offence , pine till it win back the 
 pardon ; if offend itself , bound forth to forgive , ever long to 
 soothe , ever grieve if it wind ; then be sure that its nobleness will 
 need but few trial of pain in each outbreak to refine and chastise its 
 expression . fear not then ; be but noble thyself , thou art safe ! 

 yet what in childhood be often call , rebukingly , " temper " be but the 
 cordial and puissant vitality which contain all the element that 
 make temper the sweetest at last . who amongst we , how wise soever , can 
 construe a child 's heart ? who conjecture all the spring that secretly 
 vibrate within , to a touch on the surface of feeling ? each child , but 
 especially the girl - child , would task the whole lore of a sage deep as 
 Shakspeare to distinguish those subtle emotion which we grow folk 
 have outlive . 

 " she have a strong temper , " say the Mayor , when Soppy snatch the doll 
 from his hand a second time , and pout at he , spoiled child , look 
 so divinely cross , so petulantly pretty ! and how on earth could the 
 Mayor know what association with that stupid doll make she think it 
 profane by the touch of a stranger ? be it to her eye as to his , - mere 
 waxwork and frippery ; or a symbol of holy remembrance , of gleam into 
 a fair world , of " devotion to something afar from the sphere of 
 her sorrow ? " be not the evidence of ' strong temper " the very sign of 
 affectionate depth of heart ? poor little Sophy ! hide it again , - safe out 
 of sight , close , inscrutable , unguessed , as childhood 's first treasure 
 of sentiment ever be ! 




 CHAPTER XXII . 

  the object of civilization be always to settle people one way or 
  the other , the Mayor of Gatesboro ' entertain a statesmanlike 
  ambition to settle Gentleman Waife ; no doubt a wise conception , and 
  in accordance with the genius of the Nation . every session of 
  Parliament England be employ in settle folk , whether at home or 
  at the Antipodes , who ignorantly object to be settle in her way ; in 
  short , " I will settle they , " have become a vulgar idiom , tantamount to 
  a threat of uttermost extermination or smash ; therefore the Mayor of 
  Gatesboro ' harbouring that benignant idea with reference to 
  " Gentleman Waife , " all kindly reader will exclaim , " Dii meliora ! 
  what will he do with it ? " 

 the doll once more safe behind the pillow , Sophy 's face gradually 
 soften ; she bend forward , touch the Mayor 's hand timidly , and look 
 at he with plead , penitent eye , still wet with tear , - eye that 
 say , though the lip be silent , " I will not hate you . I be ungrateful 
 and peevish ; may I beg pardon ? " 

 " I forgive you with all my heart , " cry the Mayor , interpret the 
 look aright . " and now try and compose yourself and sleep while I talk 
 with your grandpapa below . " 

 " I do not   see how it be possible that I can leave she , " say Waife , when 
 the two man have adjourn to the sitting - room . " I be sure , " quoth the 
 Mayor , seriously , " that it be the good thing for she : her pulse have much 
 nervous excitability ; she want a complete rest ; she ought not to move 
 about with you on any account . but come : though I must not know , it 
 seem , who and what you be , Mr. Chapman , I do not   think you will run off 
 with my cow ; and if you like to stay at the bailiff 's cottage for a week 
 or two with your grandchild , you shall be leave in peace , and ask no 
 question . I will own to you a weakness of mine : I value myself on be 
 seldom or never take in . I do not   think I could forgive the man who 
 do take I in . but take in I certainly shall be , if , despite all your 
 mystery , you be not as honest a fellow as ever stand upon shoe - leather ! 
 so come to the cottage . " 

 Waife be very much affect by this confide kindness ; but he shake 
 his head despondently , and that same abject , almost cringe humility 
 of mien and manner which have pain at time Lionel and Vance creep over 
 the whole man , so that he seem to cower and shrink as a Pariah before 
 a Brahmin . " no , sir ; thank you most humbly . no , sir ; that must not be . I 
 must work for my daily bread ; if what a poor vagabond like I may do can 
 be call work . I have make it a rule for year not to force myself to 
 the hearth and home of any kind man , who , not know my past , have 
 a right to suspect I . where I lodge , I pay as a lodger ; or whatever 
 favour show I spar my purse , I try to return in some useful humble 
 way . why , sir , how could I make free and easy with another man 's board 
 and roof - tree for day or week together , when I would not even come 
 to your hearthstone for a cup of tea ? " the Mayor remember , and be

 CHAPTER XXIV . 

  Laugh at foreboding of evil , but tremble after day - dream of

 the letter and the bag be take over at sunrise to Mr. Hartopp 's

 unutterable yearning of tenderness , gratitude , pity , veneration . but 
 when she have weep silently for some time , she kiss the letter with 
 devout passion , and turn to that Heaven to which the outcast have 
 teach she first to pray .

 never long quit the heart of childhood , brighten over her meditation ,

 twilight and under rain . 

 when she come downstairs , Mrs. Gooch be pleased and surprised to

 ing gate , footstep . she start , - she rise , - voice ; one strange to

 " yes , Mr. Mayor ; all right ! it be our little girl , - our dear Sophy . this 
 way , Mr. Losely . such a pleasant surprise for you , Sophy , my love ! " say

 BOOK IV . 




 CHAPTER I. 

  in the kindliest nature there be a certain sensitiveness , which , 
  when wound , occasion the same pain , and bequeath the same 
  resentment , as mortified vanity or gall self - love . 

 it be exactly that day week , towards the hour of five in the evening , 
 Mr. Hartopp , alone in the parlour behind his warehouse , be lock up 
 his book and ledger preparatory to the return to his villa . there be 
 a certain change in the expression of his countenance since we see 
 it last . if it be possible for Mr. Hartopp to look sullen , - sullen he 
 look ; if it be possible for the Mayor of Gatesboro ' to be crestfallen , 
 crestfallen he be . that smooth existence have surely receive some fatal 
 concussion , and have not yet recover the shock . but if you will glance 
 beyond the parlour at Mr. Williams give order in the warehouse , at 
 the warehouseman themselves , at the rough face in the tan - yard,-nay , at 
 Mike Callaghan , who have just bring a parcel from the railway , all of

 CHAPTER III . 

  Ecce iterum Crispinus !

 when Merle find it be in vain to press he to stay over the night ,

 Hartopp have be take in . at what Williams say , the Cobbler 's cheek

 strangely dissimilar in the abstract ! 




 CHAPTER IV . 

  " if , " say a great thinker   Degerando , " Du Perfectionment Moral , " 
  chapter ix . , " on the difficulty we encounter in Self - Study "   - " if 
  one concentrate reflection too much on one 's self , one end by no 
  long see anything , or see only what one wish . by the very 
  act , as it be , of capture one 's self , the personage we believe 
  we have seize escape , disappear . nor be it only the complexity 
  of our inner being which obstruct our examination , but its 
  exceed variability . the investigator 's regard should embrace all 
  the side of the subject , and perseveringly pursue all its phase . " 

 it be the race - week in Humberston , a county town far from Gatesboro ' , 
 and in the north of England . the race last three day : the first day 
 be over ; it have be a brilliant spectacle ; the course crowd with the 
 carriage of provincial magnate , with equestrian better of note from 
 the metropolis ; blackleg in great muster ; there have be gaming - booth 
 on the ground , and gypsy tell fortune ; much champagne imbibe by 
 the well - breed , much soda - water and brandy by the vulgar . thousand and 
 ten of thousand have be lose and win : some pauper have be for the 
 time enrich ; some rich man make poor for life . horse have win fame ; 
 some of their owner lose character . din and uproar , and coarse oath , 
 and rude passion , - all have have their hour . the amateur of the high 
 class have go back to dignified country - house , as courteous host 
 or favour guest . the professional speculator of a low grade have 
 pour back into the county town , and inn and tavern be crowd . 
 drink be hotly call for at reek bar ; waiter and chambermaid pass 
 to and fro , with dish and tankard and bottle in their hand . all be 
 noise and bustle , and eat and swilling , and disputation and slang , 
 wild glee , and wilder despair , amongst those who come back from the 
 race - course to the inn in the county town . at one of these tavern , 
 neither the good nor the bad , and in a small narrow slice of a room 
 that seem rob from the landing - place , sit Mrs. Crane , in her 
 iron - gray silk gown . she be seat close by the open window , as 
 carriage , chaise , fly , cart , van , and horseman succeed each 
 other thick and fast , watch the scene with a sour , scornful look . 
 for human joy , as for human grief , she have little sympathy . life have no 
 saturnalian holiday leave for she . some memory in her past have poison 
 the well - spring of her social being . hope and object she have 
 still , but out of the wreck of the natural and healthful existence of 
 womanhood , those object and hope stand forth exaggerated , intense , as 
 be the rule passion in monomania . a bad woman be popularly say 
 to be bad than a wicked man . if so , partly because woman , be more 
 solitary , brood more unceasingly over cherish idea , whether good or 
 evil ; partly also , for the same reason that make a wicked gentleman , 
 who have lose caste and character , more irreclaimable than a wicked 
 clown , low - bear and lowbre , namely , that in proportion to the loss of 
 shame be the gain in recklessness : but principally , perhaps , because in 
 extreme wickedness there be necessarily a distortion of the reasoning 
 faculty ; and man , accustom from the cradle rather to reason than to 
 feel , have that faculty more firm against abrupt twist and lesion than 
 it be in woman ; where virtue may have leave he , logic may still linger ; 
 and he may decline to push evil to a point at which it be clear to his 
 understanding that profit vanishe and punishment rest ; while woman , 
 once abandon to ill , find sufficient charm in its mere excitement , 
 and , regardless of consequence , where the man ask , " can I ? " rave 
 out , " I will ! " thus man may be criminal through cupidity , vanity , love , 
 jealousy , fear , ambition ; rarely in civilized , that is , reason life , 
 through hate and revenge ; for hate be a profitless investment , and 
 revenge a ruinous speculation . but when woman be thoroughly deprave 
 and harden , nine time out of ten it be hatred or revenge that make 
 they so . Arabella Crane have not , however , attain to that last state 
 of wickedness , which , consistent in evil , be callous to remorse ; she 
 be not yet unsexed . in her nature be still that essence , " vary and 
 mutable , " which distinguish woman while womanhood be leave to she . and 
 now , as she sit gaze on the throng below , her haggard mind recoil 
 perhaps from the conscious shadow of the Evil Principle which , invoke 
 as an ally , remain as a destroyer . her dark front relaxed ; she move in 
 her seat uneasily . " must it be always thus ? " she mutter , - " always this 
 hell here ! even now , if in one large pardon I could include the undoer , 
 the earth , myself , and again be human , - human , even as those slight 
 trifler or coarse brawler that pass yonder ! oh , for something in 
 common with common life ! " 

 her lip close , and her eye again fall upon the crowded street . at 
 that moment three or four heavy van or wagon fill with operative or 
 labourer and their wife , come back from the race - course , obstruct 
 the way ; two outrider in satin jacket be expostulate , crack 
 their whip , and seek to clear space for an open carriage with four 
 thoroughbred impatient horse . towards that carriage every gazer 
 from the window be direct eager eye ; each foot - passenger on the 
 pavement lift his hat : evidently in that carriage some great person ! 
 like all who be at war with the world as it be , Arabella Crane abhor 
 the great , and despise the small for worship the great . but still 
 her own fierce dark eye mechanically follow those of the vulgar . the 
 carriage bear a marquess 's coronet on its panel , and be fill with 
 lady ; two other carriage bear a similar coronet , and evidently 
 belong to the same party , be in the rear . Mrs. Crane start . in 
 that first carriage , as it now slowly move under her very window , and 
 pause a minute or more till the obstruct vehicle in front be 
 marshal into order , there flash upon her eye a face radiant with 
 female beauty in its most glorious prime . amongst the crowd at that 
 moment be a blind man , add to the various discord of the street by 
 a miserable hurdy - gurdy . in the movement of the throng to get near 
 to a sight of the lady in the carriage , this poor creature be throw 
 forward ; the dog that lead he , an ugly brute , on his own account or his 
 master 's take fright , break from the string , and run under the horse ' 
 hoof , snarling . the horse become restive ; the blind man make a plunge 
 after his dog , and be all but run over . the lady in the first carriage , 
 alarm for his safety , rise up from her seat , and make her outrider 
 dismount , lead away the poor blind man , and restore to he his dog . thus 
 engage , her face shine full upon Arabella Crane ; and with that face 
 rush a tide of early memory . long , very long , since she have see

 CHAPTER VI . 

 Threadbare be the simile which compare the world to a stage . Schiller , 
 less complimentary than Shakspeare , lower the illustration from a stage 
 to a puppet - show . but ever between reality and show there be a secret 
 communication , an undetected interchange , - sometimes a stern reality in 
 the heart of the ostensible actor , a fantastic stage - play in the brain 
 of the unnoticed spectator . the bandit 's child on the proscenium be 
 still poor little Sophy , in spite of garland and rouge . but that honest 
 rough - look fellow to whom , in respect for service to sovereign and 
 country , the apprentice yield way , may he not be - the crafty Comedian ? 

 TARAN - TARANTARA ! rub - a - dub - dub ! play up horn ! roll drum ! a quarter to

 but do you mean to say , Mr. Author , that that british tar   gallant , 
 no doubt , but hideous   be Gentleman Waife , or that stygian animal the

 upon my word , when I look at they myself , I , the Historian , be puzzle .

  a sham carry off a reality .

 CHAPTER VIII . 

  corollary from the problem suggest in chapter VI . and VII . 

 broad daylight , nearly nine o'clock indeed , and Jasper Losely be walk 
 back to his inn from the place at which he have din the evening before . 
 he have spend the night drinking , gambling , and though he look heated , 
 there be no sign of fatigue . nature , in waste on this man many of 
 her most glorious element of happiness , have not forget an herculean 
 constitution , - always restless and never tired , always drink and 
 never drunk . certainly it be some consolation to delicate invalid 
 that it seldom happen that the sickly be very wicked . criminal be 
 generally athletic ; constitution and conscience equally tough ; large 
 back to their head ; strong suspensorial muscle ; digestion that save 
 they from the over - fine nerve of the virtuous . the native animal must 
 be vigorous in the human being , when the moral safeguard be daringly 
 overleapt . Jasper be not alone , but with an acquaintance he have make 
 at the dinner , and whom he invite to his inn to breakfast ; they be 
 walk familiarly arm - in - arm . very unlike the brilliant losely , - a 
 young man under thirty , who seem to have wash out all the colour of 
 youth in dirty water . his eye dull , their white yellow ; his complexion 
 sodden . his form be thickset and heavy ; his feature pug , with a cross 
 of the bull - dog . in dress , a speciman of the flash style of sporting 
 man , as exhibit on the Turf , or more often perhaps in the Ring ; 
 Belcher neckcloth , with an immense pin represent a jockey at full 
 gallop ; cut - away coat , corduroy breech , and boot with top of a 
 chalky white . yet , withal , not the air and walk of a genuine bear and 
 breed sporting man , even of the vulgar order . something about he 
 which reveal the pretender . a would - be hawk with a pigeon 's liver , - a 
 would - be sportsman with a Cockney 's nurture . 

 Samuel Adolphus Poole be an orphan of respectable connection . his 
 future expectation chiefly rest on an uncle from whom , as godfather , he 
 take the loathed name of Samuel . he prefer to sign himself Adolphus ; 
 he be popularly style dolly . for his present existence he rely 
 ostensibly on his salary as an assistant in the house of a London 
 tradesman in a fashionable way of business . Mr. Latham , his employer , 
 have make a considerable fortune , less by his shop than by discount 
 the bill of his customer , or of other borrower whom the loan draw 
 into the net of the custom . Mr. Latham connive at the sporting taste 
 of Dolly Poole . dolly have often thus be enable to pick up useful 
 piece of information as to the name and repute of such denizen of the 
 sporting world as might apply to Mr. Latham for temporary accommodation . 
 dolly Poole have many sporting friend ; he have also many debt . he have

 nor have you or I , my venerate reader , any right whatsoever to doubt

 often do it happen to we , venerate reader , not to recognize a man

 man of genius may be for ten year our next - door neighbour ; he may dine 
 in company with we twice a week ; his face may be as familiar to our 
 eye as our armchair ; his voice to our ear as the click of our 
 parlour - clock : yet we be never more astonished than when all of a 
 sudden , some bright day , it be discover that our next - door neighbour 
 be - a man of genius . do you ever hear tell of the life of a man of

 good creature , - probably a poor creature , - but a MAN of geniu ! they

 candid reader , be there not some of you who refuse to the last to

 his passport to immortality have be duly examine by the customhouse 
 officer of Styx ! when one half the world drag forth that same next - door 
 neighbour , place he on a pedestal , and have he cry , " Oyez ! Oyez ! 
 find a man of genius ! public property ! open to inspection ! " do not

 cry , " that a man of genius , indeed ! pelt he ! - pelt he ! " then of course 
 there be a clatter , what the vulgar call " a shindy , " round the pedestal . 
 squeeze by his believer , shy at by his scoffer , the poor man get

 then they shovel he over , clap a great stone on his relic , wipe their 
 forehead , shake hand , compromise the dispute , the one half the world

 other half allow that though he be an ordinary man he be still a 
 genius . and so on to the next pedestal with its " hic stet , " and the next 
 great stone with its " Hic jacet . "

 CHAPTER IX . 

  the aboriginal man - eater , or pocket - cannibal , be susceptible of the

 hand elegantly white , nor unadorned with ring , - or lift the weight

 CHAPTER X. 

  " be there a heart that never love , 
  nor feel soft woman 's sigh ? " 

  if there be such a heart , it be not in the breast of a pocket- 
  cannibal . your true man - eater be usually of an amorous temperament : 
  he can be indeed sufficiently fond of a lady to eat she up . Mr. 
  losely make the acquaintance of a widow . for further particular

 the dignified serenity of Gloucester Place , Portman Square , be agitate 
 by the intrusion of a new inhabitant . a house in that favour locality , 
 which have for several month maintain " the solemn stillness and the 
 dread repose " which appertain to dwelling that be to be let upon

 no reader will be at a loss to discover in the new tenant of 
 no . - Gloucester Place the widow mother of Lionel Haughton . the letter

 she be seat by the window in her front drawing - room , survey with

 a very win countenance : lively eye , that in themselves may be 
 over - quick and petulant ; but their expression be chasten by a gentle 
 kindly mouth . and over the whole face , the attitude , the air , even

 tone and manner which stamp the high - breed gentlewoman of convention ; she 
 might evince the deficiency of an imperfect third - rate education : but 
 she be save from vulgarity by a certain undefinable grace or person 
 and music of voice , - even when she say or do thing that well - breed 
 people do not say or do ; and there be an engaging intelligence in those 
 quick hazel eye that make you sure that she be sensible , even when she 
 utter what be silly . 

 Mrs. Haughton turn from the interior of the room to the open window .

 not at home , - very awkward ! I wish Lionel be here ! what do he mean , 
 neglect his own mother , and leave she a prey to tempter ? " 

 while the footman be respond to the smart knock of the visitor ,

 our gentleman , whose estimate of his attraction be not humble ,

 the lady bow in surprise . 

 " ah , madam , your lamented husband be one of my most particular 
 friend . " 

 " you do not   say so ! " cry Mrs. Haughton . and look more attentively at 
 the stranger , there be in his dress and appearance something that she

 CHAPTER XI . 

  a man of the world , have accept a troublesome charge , consider 
  " what he will do with it ; " and , have promptly decide , be sure ,

 reserve to a later occasion anymore detailed description of Colonel 
 Morley , it suffice for the present to say that he be a man of a very 
 fine understanding as apply to the special world in which he live . 
 though no one have a more numerous circle of friend , and though with

 Darrell 's recommendation would have secure to any young protege Colonel

 he see that Lionel 's heart be set upon the military profession , and

 as a university education , nor bad than a university without its 
 education . well throw a youth at once into the wide sphere of a 
 capital - provide you there secure to his social life the ordinary 
 check of good company , the restraint impose by the presence of 
 decorous woman , and man of grave year and dignified repute - than 
 confine he to the exclusive society of youth of his own age , the 
 age of wild spirit and unreflecte imitation , unless he cle to the 
 safeguard which be find in hard reading , less by the book - knowledge it 
 bestow than by the serious and preoccupied mind which it abstract from 
 the coarser temptation . " 

 but Lionel , young in character than in year , be too boyish as yet to 
 be safely consign to those trial of tact and temper which await the 
 neophyte who enter on life through the door of a mess - room . his pride 
 be too morbid , too much on the alert for offence ; his frankness too 
 crude , his spirit too untamed by the insensible discipline of social 
 commerce . 

 Quoth the observant man of the world : " place his honour in his own 
 keeping , and he will carry it about with he on full cock , to blow off 
 a friend 's head or his own before the end of the first month . Huffy ! 
 decidedly huffy ! and of all cause that disturb regiment , and induce 
 court - martial , the commonest cause be a huffy lad ! pity ! for that 
 youngster have in he the right metal , - spirit and talent that should

 cut he off from his mother 's apron - string , and the particular friend 
 of his poor father who prowl unannounced into the widow 's drawing - room . 
 he shall go to Paris ; no well place to learn military theory , and 
 be civilized out of huffy disposition . no doubt my old friend , the 
 chevalier , who have the art strategic at his fingerend , might be induce

 way . I can secure to he the entree into the circle of the rigid old 
 Faubourg St. Germain , where manner be well breed , and household tie 
 most respected . besides , as I be so often at Paris myself , I shall have

 the plan thus promptly form Alban Morley briefly explain to Lionel

 arm with these quotation , many a sentence from the " Polite

 but as he approach the close of epistle the first , it occur to

 the villain could not have find a moment in Mrs. Haughton 's widow

 and when that note , so neatly fold , so elegantly seal , lie in her

 five letter in five day do Jasper write . in the course of those

 CHAPTER XIII . 

  Unhappy be the man who put his trust in a woman .

  no author ever draw a character consistent to human nature , but what

  those poor pocket - cannibal , how society do persecute they ! even

 CHAPTER XVII . 

  " Dices laborante in uno

 CHAPTER XVIII . 

  Hope , tell a flattering tale to Mr. Rugge . he be undeceive by a

 " I beg your pardon , sir , " say Mr. Gotobed , with formal politeness ,

 on the play - bill   here be one   ' Juliet Araminta , ' and whom , as I be

 fiendish child , - a three year ' engagement , - and I have be rob .

 at hear so bad a character lavish upon object recommend to his 
 client 's disinterested charity , the wary solicitor draw in his pecuniary

 to inform I where she be . have you a lawful claim on she ? "

 hand . perhaps she be - in Heaven ! " 

 " confound she , sir ! no - in America ! or on the sea to it . " 

 " be you sure ? " 

 " I have just come from the steam - packet office , and see the name in 
 their book . William and Sophy Waife sail from Liverpool last Thursday 
 week . " 

 " and they form an engagement with you , receive your money ; break the 
 one , abscond with the other . bad character indeed ! " 

 " Bad ! you may well say that , - a set of swindle scoundrel , the whole 
 kit and kin . and the ingratitude ! " continue Rugge ; " I be more than 
 a father to that child "   he begin to whimper ; " I have a babe of my own

 Mr. Gotobed have now , however , learn all that he care to learn , and 
 not be a soft - hearted man   first - rate solicitor rarely be , here 
 pull out his watch , and say , 

 " Sir , you have be very ill - treat , I perceive . I must wish you 
 good - day ; I have an engagement in the City . I can not help you back to 
 your l100 , but accept this trifle   a l5 note   for your loss of time in 
 call "   ring the bell violently . " door , - show out this gentleman . " 

 that evening Mr. Gotobed write at length to Guy Darrell , inform he 
 that , after great pain and prolonged research , he have be so fortunate

 Mr. Darrell have so benevolently request he to look up be very bad 
 character , and have leave the country for the United States , as happily 
 for England bad character be will not to do . 

 that letter reach Guy Darrell when he be far away , amidst the forlorn 
 pomp of some old italian city , and Lionel 's tale of the little girl not 
 very fresh in his gloomy thought . naturally , he suppose that the boy 
 have be dupe by a pretty face and his own inexperienced kindly heart . 
 and so , and so , - why , so end all the effort of man who entrust to 
 other the troublesome execution of humane intention ! the scale of 
 earthly justice be poise in their quivering equilibrium , not by huge 
 hundred - weight , but by infinitesimal grain , need the most wary 
 caution , the most considerate patience , the most delicate touch , to 
 arrange or readjust . few of our error , national or individual , come 
 from the design to be unjust ; most of they from sloth , or incapacity to 
 grapple with the difficulty of be just . sin of commission may not , 
 perhaps , shock the retrospect of conscience . large and obtrusive to 
 view we have confess , mourn , repent , possibly atone they . sin of 
 omission so veil amidst our hourly emotion , blent , confused , unseen , 
 in the conventional routine of existence , - alas ! could these suddenly 
 emerge from their shadow , group together in serrie mass and accuse 
 order , - alas , alas ! would not the good of we then start in dismay , and 
 would not the proud humble himself at the Throne of Mercy ? 




 CHAPTER XIX .

 three day afterwards , in a quiet street in a quiet town of 
 Belgium , - wherein a sharper , strive to live by his profession , would

 innocuous , and profoundly miserable . in another house , the window 
 of which - face those of Jasper 's sitting - room , from an upper 
 story - command so good a view therein that it place he under 
 a surveillance akin to that design by Mr. Bentham 's reformatory 
 Panopticon , sit Arabella Crane . whatever her real feeling towards 
 Jasper Losely   and what those feeling be no virile pen can presume 
 authoritatively to define ; for live there ever a man who thoroughly 
 understand a woman ? , or whatever in early life might have be their 
 reciprocated vow of eternal love , - not only from the day that Jasper , 
 on his return to his native shore , present himself in Podden Place , 
 have their intimacy be restrict to the austere bond of friendship , 
 but after Jasper have so rudely decline the hand which now feed he , 
 Arabella Crane have probably perceive that her sole chance of retain 
 intellectual power over his lawless being necessitate the utter 
 relinquishment of every hope or project that could expose she again to 
 his contempt . suit appearance to reality , the decorum of a separate 
 house be essential to the maintenance of that authority with which 
 the rigid nature of their intercourse invest she . the additional cost 
 strain her pecuniary resource , but she save in her own accommodation 
 in order to leave Jasper no cause to complain of any stinting in his . 
 there , then , she sit by her window , herself unseen , eye he in his 
 opposite solitude , accept for her own life a barren sacrifice , but 
 a jealous sentinel on his . meditate as she sit and as she eye 
 he , - meditate what employment she could invent , with the bribe of 
 emolument to be pay furtively by she , for those strong hand that 
 could have fell an ox , but be nerveless in turn an honest penny , 
 and for that restless mind hunger for occupation , and with the 
 digestion of an ostrich for dice and debauch , riot and fraud , but queasy 
 as an exhausted dyspeptic at the reception of one innocent amusement , 
 one honourable toil . but while that woman still scheme how to rescue 
 from hulk or halter that execrable man , who shall say that he be 
 without a chance ? a chance he have : what will he do with it ?

 CHAPTER I. 

  Envy will be a science when it learn the use of the microscope . 

 when leave fall and flower fade , great people be find in their 
 country - seat . look ! - that be Montfort Court , - a place of regal

 satisfy the pride of ownership , or inspire the visitor with the respect 
 due to wealth and power . an artist could have make nothing of it . the 
 Sumptuous everywhere ; the Picturesque nowhere . the house be build 
 in the reign of George I. , when first commence that horror of the 
 beautiful , as something in bad taste , which , agreeably to our natural 
 love of progress , progressively advanced through the reign of 
 succeed george . an enormous fafade , in dull brown brick ; two wing 
 and a centre , with double flight of step to the hall - door from the 
 carriage - sweep . no tree allow to grow too near the house ; in front , a

 a cornfield in sight , not a roof - tree , not a spire , only those lata 
 silentia , - still width of turf , and , somewhat thinly scattered and 
 afar , those grove of giant tree . the whole prospect so vast and

 close - neighbour poetic thicket into which to plunge , uncertain

 fat and heavy , seem bored by pasture it would take they a week to 
 traverse . People of moderate wish and modest fortune never envy

 it . but never do they say - 

  " oh , that for I some home like this would smile ! " 

 not so , very , very great people ! - they rather coveted than admire . 
 those oak tree so large , yet so undecayed ; that park , eighteen mile at 
 least in circumference ; that solid palace which , without inconvenience ,

 George Morley be the young son of a country gentleman , with a good

 CHAPTER II . 

  truly saith the proverb , " much corn lie under the straw that be not 
  see . " 

 meanwhile George Morley follow the long shady walk , - very handsome 
 walk , full of prize rose and rare exotic , artificially wind 
 too , - walk so well keep that it take thirty - four man to keep it , - noble 
 walk , tiresome walk , till it bring he to the great piece of water , 
 which , perhaps , four time in the year be visit by the great folk 
 in the Great House . and be thus out of the immediate patronage of

 refresh : you begin to breathe ; to unbutton your waistcoat , loosen

 shilling a piece , moss - grow , hollow - trunke , some pollard , - tree 
 invaluable ! ha , the hare ! how she scud ! see , the deer marching down to 
 the water side . what grove of bulrush ! island of water - lily ! and to 
 throw a Gothic bridge there , bring a great gravel road over the bridge ! 
 oh , shame , shame ! 

 so would have say the scholar , for he have a true sentiment for Nature , 
 if the bridge have not clean go out of his head . wander alone , he 
 come at last to the most umbrageous and sequester bank of the wide 
 water , close round on every side by brushwood , or still , patriarchal 
 tree . suddenly he arrest his step ; an idea strike he , - one of 
 those old , whimsical , grotesque idea which often when we be alone come 
 across we , even in our quiet or most anxious mood . be his infirmity

 do he no good . yet have not the great orator the world ever know a 
 defect in utterance ? he , too , demosthene , have , no doubt , pay fee to 
 elocution master , the good in Athens , where elocution master must have 
 study their art ad unguem , and the defect have baffle they . but do 
 demosthene despair ? no , he resolve to cure himself , - how ? be it 
 not one of his method to fill his mouth with pebble , and practise , 
 manfully to the roar sea ? George Morley have never try the effect of 
 pebble . be there any virtue in they ? why not try ? no sea there , it be 
 true ; but a sea be only useful as represent the noise of a stormy 
 democratic audience . to represent a peaceful congregation that still 
 sheet of water would do as well . pebble there be in plenty just by 
 that gravelly cove , near which a young pike lie sun his green 
 back . half in jest , half in earnest , the scholar pick up a handful of 
 pebble , wipe they from sand and mould , insert they between his tooth 
 cautiously , and , look round to assure himself that none be by , 
 begin an extempore discourse . so interested do he become in that 
 classical experiment , that he might have torture the air and astonish 
 the magpie   three of whom from a neighbouring thicket listen 
 perfectly spell - bind   for more than half an hour , when seize with 
 shame at the ludicrous impotence of his exertion , with despair that so 
 wretche a barrier should stand between his mind and its expression , he 
 fling away the pebble , and sink on the ground , he fairly weep , weep 
 like a baffled child . 

 the fact be , that Morley have really the temperament of an orator ; he 
 have the orator 's gift in warmth of passion , rush of thought , logical 
 arrangement ; there be in he the genius of a great preacher . he feel 
 it , - he know it ; and in that despair which only genius know when some 
 pitiful cause obstruct its energy and strike down its power , make 
 a confidant of Solitude he weep loud and freely . 

 " do not despond , sir , I undertake to cure you , " say a voice behind . 

 George start up in confusion ; a man , elderly , but fresh and vigorous , 
 stand beside he , in a light fustian jacket , a blue apron , and with 
 rush in his hand , which he continue to plait together nimbly and 
 deftly as he bow to the startled scholar . 

 " I be in the shade of the thicket yonder , sir ; pardon I , I could not 
 help hear you . " 

 the Oxonian rub his eye , and star at the man with a vague 
 impression that he have see he before ; - when ? where ? 

 " you can cure I , " he stutter out ; " what of ? - the folly of try to 
 speak in public ? thank you , I be cure . " 

 " Nay , sir , you see before you a man who can make you a very good 
 speaker . your voice be naturally fine . I repeat , I can cure a defect 
 which be not in the organ , but in the management ! " 

 " you can ! you - who and what be you ? " 

 " a basketmaker , sir ; I hope for your custom . " " surely this be not the 
 first time I have see you ? " 

 " true , you once kindly suffer I to borrow a resting - place on your 
 father 's land . one good turn deserve another . " 

 at that moment Sir Isaac peer through the bramble , and restore 
 to his original whiteness , and relieve from his false , horn ear , 
 march gravely towards the water , sniff at the scholar , slightly 
 wag his tail , and bury himself amongst the reed in search of a 
 water - rat he have therein disturb a week before , and always expect to 
 find again . 

 the sight of the dog immediately clear up the cloud in the scholar 's 
 memory ; but with recognition come back a keen curiosity and a sharp pang 
 of remorse . 

 " and your little girl ? " he ask , look down abash . 

 " well than she be when we last meet . providence be so kind to we . " 

 Poor Waife ! he never guess that to the person he thus reveal himself 
 he owe the grief for Sophy 's abduction . he divine no reason for the 
 scholar 's flush cheek and embarrassed manner . 

 " yes , sir , we have just settle in this neighbourhood . I have a pretty 
 cottage yonder at the outskirt of the village , and near the park pale . 
 I recognize you at once ; and as I hear you just now , I call to mind 
 that when we meet before , you say your calling should be the Church , 
 be it not for your difficulty in utterance ; and I say to myself , ' no 
 bad thing those pebble , if his utterance be thick , which be it not ; ' 
 and I have not a doubt , sir , that the true fault of demosthene , whom I 
 presume you be imitate , be that he speak through his nose . " 

 " eh ! " say the scholar , " through his nose ? I never know that ? - and I - " 

 " and you be try to speak without lung ; that be without air in they . 
 you do not   smoke , I presume ? " 

 " no ; certainly not . " 

 " you must learn ; speak between each slow puff of your pipe . all you want 
 be time , - time to quiet the nerve , time to think , time to breathe . the 
 moment you begin to stammer , stop , fill the lung thus , then try again ! 
 it be only a clever man who can learn to write , - that be , to compose ; 
 but any fool can be teach to speak . courage ! " 

 " if you really can teach I , " cry the learn man , forget all 
 self - reproach for his betrayal of Waife to Mrs. Crane in the absorbing 
 interest of the hope that spring up within he , " if you can teach I ; if 
 I can but con - con - con - conq - " 

 " slowly , slowly , breath and time ; take a whiff from my pipe ; that be 
 right . yes , you can conquer the impediment . " 

 " then I will be the good friend to you that man ever have . there be my 
 hand on it . " 

 " I take it , but I ask leave to change the party in the contract . 
 I do not   want a friend : I do not   deserve one . you will be a friend to my 
 little girl instead ; and if ever I ask you to help I in aught for her 
 welfare and happiness - " 

 " I will help , heart and soul ! slight indeed any service to she or to 
 you compare with such service to I . free this wretched tongue from its 
 stammer , and thought and zeal will not stammer whenever you say , ' keep 
 your promise . ' I be so glad your little girl be still with you . " 

 Waife look surprised , " be still with I ! - why not ? " the scholar bit 
 his tongue . that be not the moment to confess ; it might destroy all 
 Waife 's confidence in he . he would do so later . " when shall I begin my 
 lesson ? " 

 " now , if you like . but have you a book in your pocket ? " 

 " I always have . " 

 " not greek , I hope , sir ? " 

 " no , a volume of Barrow 's sermon . Lord Chatham recommend those 
 sermon to his great son as a study for eloquence . " 

 " good ! will you lend I the volume , sir ? and now for it . listen to I ; 
 one sentence at a time ; draw your breath when I do . " 

 the three magpie prick up their ear again , and , as they listen , 
 marvel much . 




 CHAPTER III . 

  could we know by what strange circumstance a man 's genius become 
  prepared for practical success , we should discover that the most 
  serviceable item in his education be never enter in the bill 
  which his father pay for it . 

 at the end of the very first lesson George Morley see that all the 
 elocution master to whose skill he have be consign be blunderer 
 in comparison with the basketmaker . 

 Waife do not puzzle he with scientific theory . all that the great 
 comedian require of he be to observe and to imitate . observation , 
 imitation , lo ! the groundwork of all art ! the primal element of all 
 genius ! not there , indeed to halt , but there ever to commence . what 
 remain to carry on the intellect to mastery ? two step , - to reflect , 
 to reproduce . observation , imitation , reflection , reproduction . in these 
 stand a mind complete and consummate , fit to cope with all labour , 
 achieve all success . 

 at the end of the first lesson George Morley feel that his cure be 
 possible . make an appointment for the next day at the same place , he 
 come thither stealthily and so on day by day . at the end of a week he 
 feel that the cure be nearly certain ; at the end of a month the cure 
 be self - evident . he should live to preach the Word . true , that he 
 practise incessantly in private . not a moment in his wake hour that 
 the one thought , one object , be absent from his mind ! true , that with 
 all his patience , all his toil , the obstacle be yet serious , might 
 never be entirely overcome . nervous hurry , rapidity of action , vehemence 
 of feeling , bring back , might at unguarded moment always bring back , 
 the gasp breath , the empty lung , the struggle utterance . but the 
 relapse , rarer and rarer now with each trial , would be at last scarce 
 a drawback . " Nay , " quoth Waife , " instead of a drawback , become but an 
 orator , and you will convert a defect into a beauty . " 

 thus justly sanguine of the accomplishment of his life 's choose object , 
 the scholar 's gratitude to Waife be unspeakable . and see the man 
 daily at last in his own cottage , - Sophy 's health restore to her 
 cheek , smile to her lip , and cheer at her light fancy - work beside 
 her grandsire 's elbow - chair , with fairy legend instill perhaps 
 golden truth , - see Waife thus , the scholar mingle with gratitude a 
 strange tenderness of respect . he know nought of the vagrant 's past , his 
 reason might admit that in a position of life so at variance with 
 the gift natural and acquire of the singular basketmaker , there be 
 something mysterious and suspicious . but he blush to think that he have 
 ever ascribe to a flawed or wander intellect the eccentricity of 
 glorious Humour , - abet an attempt to separate an old age so innocent 
 and genial from a childhood so foster and so foster . and sure I be 
 that if the whole world have rise up to point the finger of scorn at the 
 one - eyed cripple , George Morley - the well - bear gentleman , the refine 
 scholar , the spotless churchman - would have give he his arm to lean 
 upon , and walk by his side unashamed . 




 CHAPTER IV . 

  to judge human character rightly , a man may sometimes have very 
  small experience , provide he have a very large heart . 

 Numa Pimpilius do not more conceal from notice the lesson he receive 
 from Egeria than do George Morley those which he receive from the 
 basketmaker . natural , indeed , must be his wish for secrecy ; pretty story 
 it would be for Humberston , its future rector learn how to preach a 
 sermon from an old basketmaker ! but he have a nobler and more imperious 
 motive for discretion : his honour be engage to it . Waife exact a 
 promise that he would regard the intercourse between they as strictly 
 private and confidential . 

 " it be for my sake I ask this , " say Waife , frankly , " though I might say 
 it be for your ; " the Oxonian promise , and be bind . Fortunately Lady 
 Montfort quit the great house the very day after George have first 
 encounter the basketmaker , and write word that she should not return 
 to it for some week , George be at liberty to avail himself of her 
 lord 's general invitation to make use of Montfort Court as his lodging 
 when in the neighbourhood ; which the propriety of the world would not 
 have allow he to do while Lady Montfort be there without either host

 the vast palace , and be easily enable , when he please , to traverse 
 unobserve the solitude of the park , gain the waterside , or stroll 
 thence through the thick copse lead to Waife 's cottage , which 
 border the park pale , solitary , sequester , beyond sight of the 
 neighbouring village . the great house all to himself , George be bring 
 in contact with no one to whom , in unguarded moment , he could even 
 have let out a hint of his new acquaintance , except the clergyman of 
 the parish , a worthy man , who live in strict retirement upon a scanty 
 stipend . for the Marquess be the lay impropriator ; the living be 
 therefore but a very poor vicarage , below the acceptance of a Vipont or 
 a Vipont 's tutor , sure to go to a worthy man force to live in strict 
 retirement . George see too little of this clergyman , either to let 
 out secret or pick up information . from he , however , George do 
 incidentally learn that Waife have some month previously visit the 
 village , and propose to the bailiff to take the cottage and osi land , 
 which he now rent ; that he represent himself as have know an old 
 basketmaker who have dwell there many year ago , and as have learn 
 the basket craft of that long deceased operative . as he offer a high 
 rent than the bailiff could elsewhere obtain , and as the bailiff be 
 desirous to get credit with Mr. Carr Vipont for improve the property , 
 by revive thereon an art which have fall into desuetude , the bargain 
 be strike , provide the candidate , be a stranger to the place , could 
 furnish the bailiff with any satisfactory reference . Waife have go 
 away , say he should shortly return with the requisite testimonial . in 
 fact , poor man , as we know , he be then count on a good word from 
 Mr. Hartopp . he have not , however , return for some month . the 
 cottage , have be meanwhile want for the temporary occupation of an

 the bailiff to a respectable house - agent and collector of street rent 
 in Bloomsbury , who write word that a lady , then abroad , have authorize 
 he , as the agent employ in the management of a house property from 
 which much of her income be derive , not only to state that Waife be a 
 very intelligent man , likely to do well whatever he undertake , but also 
 to guarantee , if require , the punctual payment of the rent for any 
 holding of which he become the occupier . on this the agreement be 
 conclude , the basketmaker instal . in the immediate neighbourhood 
 there be no custom for basket - work , but Waife 's performance be so 
 neat , and some so elegant and fanciful , that he have no difficulty 
 in contracting with a large tradesman   not at Humberston , but a more 
 distant and yet more thriving town about twenty mile off   for as much 
 of such work as he could supply . each week the carrier take his good 
 and bring back the payment ; the profit amply suffice for Waife 's 
 and Sophy 's daily bread , with even more than the surplus set aside for 
 the rent . for the rest , the basketmaker 's cottage be at the farth 
 outskirt of the straggle village inhabit by a labour peasantry , 
 his way of life be not much know nor much inquire into . he seem a 
 harmless , hard - work man ; never see at the beer - house ; always see 
 with his neatly - dress little grandchild in his quiet corner at church 
 on Sundays ; a civil , well - behave man too ; who touch his hat to the 
 bailiff and take it off to the vicar . 

 an idea prevail that the basketmaker have spend much of his life in 
 foreign country , favour partly by a sobriety of habit which be 
 not altogether national , partly by something in his appearance , which , 
 without be above his lowly calling , do not seem quite in keeping 
 with it , - outlandish in short , - but principally by the fact that he have 
 receive since his arrival two letter with a foreign postmark . the idea 
 befriend the old man , - allow it to be infer that he have probably 
 outlive the friend he have formerly leave behind he in England , and , 
 on his return , be sufficiently fatigue with his ramble to drop 
 content in any corner of his native soil wherein he could find a quiet 
 home , and earn by light toil a decent livelihood . 

 George , though naturally curious to know what have be the result of his 
 communication to Mrs. Crane , - whether it have lead to Waife 's discovery or 
 cause he annoyance , - have hitherto , however , shrink from touch upon 
 a topic which subject himself to an awkward confession of officious 
 intermeddling , and to which any indirect allusion might appear an 
 indelicate attempt to pry into painful family affair . but one day 
 he receive a letter from his father which disturb he greatly , and

 this letter , the eld Mr. Morley mention incidentally , amongst other 
 scrap of local news , that he have see Mr. Hartopp , who be rather out 
 of sort , his good heart not have recover the shock of have be 
 abominably " take in " by an impostor for whom he have conceive a great 
 fancy , and to whose discovery George himself have providentially lead   the 
 father refer here to what George have tell he of his first meeting 
 with Waife , and his visit to Mrs. Crane ; the impostor , it seem , from 
 what Mr. Hartopp let fall , not be a little queer in the head , as

 add the eld Morley , " a character so bad that Mr. Hartopp be too 
 glad to give up to her lawful protector the child , whom the man appear 
 to have abduct ; and I suspect , from what Hartopp say , though he do 
 not like to own that he be take in to so gross a degree , that he 
 have be actually introduce to his fellow - townsfolk and confer 
 familiarly with a regular jail - bird , - perhaps a burglar . how lucky for 
 that poor , soft - headed , excellent Jos Hartopp , whom it be positively as 
 inhuman to take in as it would be to defraud a bear natural , that 
 the lady you see arrive in time to expose the snare lay for his 
 benevolent credulity . but for that , Jos might have take the fellow into 
 his own house   just like he ! , and be rob by this time , perhaps 
 murder , - Heaven know ! " 

 incredulous and indignant , and long to be empower to vindicate his 
 friend 's fair name , George seize his hat , and stride quick along the 
 path towards the basketmaker 's cottage . as he gain the water - side , 
 he perceive Waife himself , seat on a mossy bank , under a gnarl 
 fantastic thorntree , watch a deer as it come to drink , and whistle 
 a soft mellow tune , - the tune of an old english border - song . the deer 
 lift his antler from the water , and turn his large bright eye 
 towards the opposite bank , whence the note come , listening and wistful . 
 as George 's step crush the wild thyme , which the thorn - tree shadow , 
 " hush ! " say Waife , " and mark how the rude musical sound can affect 
 the brute creation . " he resume the whistle , - a clear , louder , 
 wilder tune , - that of a lively hunting - song . the deer turn quickly 
 round , - uneasy , restless , toss its antler , and bound through the 
 fern . Waife again change the key of his primitive music , - a melancholy 
 belliny note , like the belling itself of a melancholy hart , but more 
 modulate into sweetness . the deer arrest its flight , and , lure by 
 the mimic sound , return towards the water - side , slowly and statelily . 

 " I do not   think the story of Orpheus charming the brute be a fable ; do 
 you , sir ? " say Waife . " the rabbit about here know I already ; and , if 
 I have but a fiddle , I would undertake to make friend with that reserve 
 and unsocial water - rat , on whom Sir Isaac in vain endeavour at 
 present to force his acquaintance . Man commit a great mistake in not 
 cultivate more intimate and amicable relation with the other branch 
 of earth 's great family . few of they not more amusing than we be ; 
 naturally , for they have not our care . and such variety of character 
 too , where you would least expect it ! " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " very true . cowper notice marked difference of 
 character in his favourite hare . " 

 WAIFE . - " Hares ! I be sure that there be not two house - fly on a 
 window - pane , two minnow in that water , that would not present to 
 we interesting point of contrast as to temper and disposition . 
 if house - fly and minnow could but coin money , or set up a 
 manufacture , - contrive something , in short , to buy or sell attractive to 
 anglo - Saxon enterprise and intelligence , - of course we should soon have 
 diplomatic relation with they ; and our despatch and newspaper would 
 instruct we to a t in the character and propensity of their lead 
 personage . but , where man have no pecuniary nor ambitious interest 
 at stake in his commerce with any class of his fellow - creature , his 
 information about they be extremely confused and superficial . the good 
 naturalist be mere generalizer , and think they have do a vast deal 
 when they classify a specie . what should we know about mankind if 
 we have only a naturalist 's definition of man ? we only know mankind by 
 knock classification on the head , and study each man as a class in 
 himself . Compare Buffon and Shakspeare ! alas , sir ! can we never have a 
 Shakspeare for house - fly and minnow ? " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " with all respect for minnow and house - fly , if 
 we find another Shakspeare , he might be well employ , like his 
 predecessor , in select individuality from the classification of 
 man . " 

 WAIFE . - " be yourself a man , you think so : a housefly might be of a 
 different opinion . but permit I , at least , to doubt whether such an 
 investigator would be well employ in reference to his own happiness , 
 though I grant that he would be so in reference to your intellectual 
 amusement and social interest . poor Shakspeare ! how much he must have 
 suffer ! " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " you mean that he must have be rack by the passion 
 he describe , - bruise by collision with the heart he dissect . that be 
 not necessary to genius . the judge on his bench , sum up evidence and 
 charge the jury , have no need to have share the temptation or be 
 privy to the act of the prisoner at the bar . yet how consummate may be 
 his analysis ! " 

 " no , " cry Waife , roughly . " no ! your illustration destroy your 
 argument . the judge know nothing of the prisoner . there be the 
 circumstance ; there be the law . by these he generalize , by these 
 he judge , - right or wrong . but of the individual at the bar , of the 
 world - the tremendous world - within that individual heart , I repeat , he 
 know nothing . do he know , law and circumstance might vanish , human 
 justice would be paralyze . Ho , there ! place that swart - visage , 
 ill - look foreigner in the dock , and let counsel open the case ; 
 hear the witness depose ! oh , horrible wretch ! a murderer ! unmanly 
 murderer ! - a defenceless woman smother by caitiff hand ! hang he up ! 
 hang he up ! ' softly , ' whisper the poet , and lift the veil from the 
 assassin 's heart . ' lo ! it be Othello the Moor ! ' what jury now dare find 
 that criminal guilty ? what judge now put on the black cap ? who now say , 
 ' hang he up ! hang he up ! " 

 with such lifelike force do the comedian vent this passionate outburst 
 that he thrill his listener with an awe akin to that which the 
 convict Moor gather round himself at the close of the sublime drama . 
 even Sir Isaac be startle ; and leave his hopeless pursuit of the 
 water - rat , utter a low bark , come to his master , and look into his 
 face with solemn curiosity . 

 WAIFE   relapse into colloquial accent . - " why do we sympathize with 
 those above we more than with those below ? why with the sorrow of a 
 king rather than those of a beggar ? why do Sir Isaac sympathize with 
 I more than   let that water - rat vex he ever so much   I can possibly 
 sympathize with he ? whatever be the cause , see at least , Mr. Morley , 
 one reason why a poor creature like myself find it well employment 
 to cultivate the intimacy of brute than to prosecute the study of man . 
 among man , all be too high to sympathize with I ; but I have know 
 two friend who never injure nor betray . Sir Isaac be one ; Wamba be 
 another . Wamba , sir , the native of a remote district of the globe   two 
 friend civilized Europe be not large enough to afford any one man , 
 Wamba , sir , be less gifted by nature , less refine by education , than 
 Sir Isaac ; but he be a safe and trustworthy companion : Wamba , sir , 
 be - an opossum . " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " alas , my dear Mr. Waife , I fear that man must have 
 behave very ill to you . " 

 WAIFE . - " I have no right to complain . I have behave very ill to myself . 
 when a man be his own enemy , he be very unreasonable if he expect other 
 man to be his benefactor . " 

 GEORGE MORLEY   with emotion . - " listen , I have a confession to make to 
 you . I fear I have do you an injury , where , officiously , I mean to 
 do a kindness . " the scholar hurry on to narrate the particular of his 
 visit to Mrs. Crane . on conclude the recital , he add , " when again 
 I meet you here , and learn that your Sophy be with you , I feel 
 inexpressibly relieve . it be clear then , I think , that your 
 grandchild have be leave to your care unmoleste , either that you have 
 prove not to be the person of whom the party be in search , or 
 family affair have be so explain and reconcile that my interference 
 have occasion you no harm . but to - day I have a letter from my father 
 which disquiet I much . it seem that the person in question do visit 
 Gatesboro ' , and have malign you to Mr. Hartopp . understand I , I ask 
 for no confidence which you may be unwilling to give ; but if you will 
 arm I with the power to vindicate your character from aspersion which 
 I need not your assurance to hold unjust and false , I will not rest till 
 that task be triumphantly accomplish . " 

 WAIFE   in a tone calm but dejected . - " I thank you with all my heart . 
 but there be nothing to be do . I be glad that the subject do not 
 start up between we until such little service as I could render you , Mr. 
 Morley , be pretty well over . it would have be a pity if you have be 
 compel to drop all communication with a man of attainted character , 
 before you have learn how to manage the power that will enable you 
 hereafter to exhort sinner bad than I have be . hush , sir ! you feel 
 that , at least now , I be an inoffensive old man , labour for a humble 
 livelihood . you will not repeat here what you may have hear , or yet 
 hear , to the discredit of my former life . you will not send I and my 
 grandchild forth from our obscure refuge to confront a world with which 
 we have no strength to cope . and , believe this , it only remain for I 
 to say , Fare - you - well , sir . " 

 " I should deserve to lose spe - spe - speech altogether , " cry the Oxonian , 
 gasp and stammer fearfully as he catch Waife firmly by the arm , 
 " if I suffer - suff - suff - suff - " 

 " one , two ! take time , sir ! " say the Comedian , softly . and with a sweet 
 patience he reseat himself on the bank . the Oxonian throw himself at 
 length by the outcast 's side ; and , with the noble tenderness of a nature 
 as chivalrously Christian as Heaven ever give to priest , he rest his 
 fold hand upon Waife 's shoulder , and look he full and close in 
 the face , say thus , slowly , deliberately , not a stammer , " you do not 
 guess what you have do for I ; you have secure to I a home and a 
 career ; the wife of whom I must otherwise have despair ; the Divine 
 Vocation on which all my earthly hope be set , and which I be on 
 the eve of renouncing : do not think these be obligation which can 
 be lightly shake off . if there be circumstance which forbid I to 
 disabuse other of impression which wrong you , imagine not that their 
 false notion will affect my own gratitude , - my own respect for you ! " 

 " Nay , sir ! they ought ; they must . perhaps not your exaggerated gratitude 
 for a service which you should not , however , measure by its effect 
 on yourself , but by the slightness of the trouble it give to I ; not 
 perhaps your gratitude , but your respect , yes . " 

 " I tell you no ! do you fancy that I can not judge of a man 's nature 
 without call on he to trust I with all the secret - all the error , 
 if you will - of his past life ? will not the calling to which I may now 
 hold myself destine give I power and commandment to absolve all those 
 who truly repent and unfeignedly believe ? oh , Mr. Waife ! if in early 
 day you have sin , do you not repent ? and how often , in many a lovely 
 gentle sentence drop unawares from your lip , have I have cause 
 to know that you unfeignedly believe ! be I now clothe with sacred 
 authority , could I not absolve you as a priest ? think you that , in the 
 meanwhile , I dare judge you as a man ? I , - Life 's new recruit , guard 
 hitherto from temptation by careful parent and favour fortune , - I 
 presume to judge , and judge harshly , the gray - haired veteran , weary by 
 the march , wound in the battle ! " 

 " you be a noble - hearted human being , " say Waife , greatly affect . 
 " and , mark my word , a mantle of charity so large you will live to 
 wear as a robe of honour . but hear I , sir ! Mr. Hartopp also be a 
 man infinitely charitable , benevolent , kindly , and , through all his 
 simplicity , acutely shrewd ; Mr. Hartopp , on hear what be say 
 against I , deem I unfit to retain my grandchild , resign the trust 
 I have confide to he , and would have give I alm , no doubt , have I 
 ask they , but not his hand . take your hand , sir , from my shoulder , 
 lest the touch sully you . " 

 George do take his hand from the vagrant 's shoulder , but it be 
 to grasp the hand that waive they off and struggle to escape the 
 pressure . " you be innocent ! you be innocent ! forgive I that I 
 speak to you of repentance as if you have be guilty . I feel you be 
 innocent , - feel it by my own heart . you turn away . I defy you to say 
 that you be guilty of what have be lay to your charge , of what have 
 darken your good name , of what Mr. Hartopp believe to your prejudice . 
 look I in the face and say , ' I be not innocent ; I have not be 
 belie . " ' 

 Waife remain voiceless , motionless . 

 the young man , in whose nature lie yet unprove all those grand 
 quality of heart , without which never be there a grand orator , a 
 grand preacher , - quality which grasp the result of argument , and 
 arrive at the end of elaborate reasoning by sudden impulse , - here 
 release Waife 's hand , rise to his foot , and , face Waife , as the old 
 man sit with face avert , eye downcast , breast heaving , say loftily , 

 " forget that I may soon be the christian minister whose duty bow 
 his ear to the lip of Shame and Guilt ; whose hand , when it point 
 to Heaven , no mortal touch can sully ; whose sublime post be by the 
 sinner 's side . look on I but as man and gentleman . see , I now extend 
 this hand to you . if , as man and gentleman , you have do that which , 
 could all heart be read , all secret know , human judgment reverse by 
 divine omniscience , forbid you to take this hand , - then reject it , go 
 hence : we part ! but if no such act be on your conscience , however 
 you submit to its imputation , - then , in the name of Truth , as man and 
 gentleman to man and gentleman , I command you to take this right hand , 
 and , in the name of that Honour which bear no paltering , I forbid you 
 to disobey . " 

 the vagabond rise , like the Dead at the spell of a Magician , - take , as 
 if irresistibly , the hand hold out to he . and the scholar , overjoyed , 
 fall on his breast , embrace he as a son . 

 " you know , " say George , in tremble accent , " that the hand you have 
 take will never betray , never desert ; but be it - be it really powerless 
 to raise and to restore you to your place ? " 

 " Powerless amongst your kind for that indeed , " answer Waife , in 
 accent still more tremulous . " all the king of the earth be not strong 
 enough to raise a name that have once be trample into the mire . learn 
 that it be not only impossible for I to clear myself , but that it be 
 equally impossible for I to confide to mortal be a single plea in 
 defence if I be innocent , in extenuation if I be guilty . and say 
 this , and entreat you to hold it more merciful to condemn than to 
 question I , - for question be torture , - I can not reject your pity ; but 
 it would be mockery to offer I respect ! " 

 " what ! not respect the fortitude which calumny can not crush ? would that 
 fortitude be possible if you be not calm in the knowledge that 
 no false witness can mislead the Eternal Judge ? respect you ! 
 yes , - because I have see you happy in despite of man , and therefore I 
 know that the cloud around you be not the frown of Heaven . " 

 " oh , " cry Waife , the tear roll down his cheek , " and not an hour 
 ago I be jest at human friendship , vent graceless spleen on my 
 fellow - man ! and now - now - ah , sir ! providence be so kind to I ! and , " 
 say he , brush away his tear , as the old arch smile begin to play 
 round the corner of his mouth , " and kind to I in the very quarter in 
 which unkindness have so sorely smite I . true , you direct towards I 
 the woman who take from I my grandchild , who destroy I in the esteem 
 of good Mr. Hartopp . well , you see , I have my sweet Sophy back again ; we 
 be in the home of all other I most long for ; and that woman , yes , I 
 can , at least , thus far , confide to you my secret , so that you may not 
 blame yourself for send she to Gatesboro ' , - that very woman know of 
 my shelter ; furnish I with the very reference necessary to obtain it ; 
 have free my grandchild from a loathsome bondage , which I could not have 
 legally resist ; and should new persecution chase we will watch and 
 warn and help we . and if you ask I how this change in she be effect ; 
 how , when we have abandon all hope of green field , and deem that 
 only in the crowd of a city we could escape those who pursue we when 
 discover there , though I fancy myself an adept in disguise , and the 
 child and the dog be never see out of the four garret wall in which 
 I hide they , - if you ask I , I say , to explain how that very woman be 
 suddenly convert from a remorseless foe into a save guardian , I can 
 only answer ' by no wit , no device , no persuasive art of mine . providence 
 soften her heart , and make it kind , just at a moment when no other 
 agency on earth could have rescue we from - from - " 

 " say no more : I guess ! the paper this woman show I be a legal form 
 authorize your poor little Sophy to be give up to the care of a 
 father . I guess ! of that father you would not speak ill to I ; yet from 
 that father you would save your grandchild . say no more . and yon quiet 
 home , your humble employment , really content you ? " 

 " oh , if such a life can but last ! Sophy be so well , so cheerful , so 
 happy . do not you bear she sing the other day ? she never use 
 to sing ! but we have not be here a week when song break out from 
 she , - untaught , as from a bird . but if any ill report of I travel 
 hither from Gatesboro ' or elsewhere , we should be send away , and the 
 bird would be mute in my thorn - tree : Sophy would sing no more . " 

 " do not fear that slander shall drive you hence . Lady Montfort , you 
 know , be my cousin , but you know not - few do - how thoroughly generous 
 and gentle - hearted she be . I will speak of you to she , - oh ! do not look 
 alarmed . she will take my word when I tell she , ' that be a good man ; ' 
 and if she ask more , it will be enough to say , ' those who have know 
 well day be loth to speak to stranger of the past . ' " 

 " I thank you earnestly , sincerely , " say Waife , brighten up . " one 
 favour more : if you see in the formal document show to you , or retain 
 on your memory , the name of - of the person authorize to claim Sophy as 
 his child , you will not mention it to Lady Montfort . I be hot sure 
 if ever she hear that name , but she may have do so , and - and - " he 
 pause a moment , and seem to muse ; then go on , not conclude his 
 sentence . " you be so good to I , Mr. Morley , that I wish to confide in 
 you as far as I can . now , you see , I be already an old man , and my chief 
 object be to raise up a friend for Sophy when I be gone , - a friend in 
 her own sex , sir . oh , you can not guess how I long , how I yearn , to view 
 that child under the holy foster eye of a woman . perhaps if Lady 
 Montfort see my pretty Sophy she might take a fancy to she . oh , if 
 she do ! if she do ! and Sophy , " add Waife , proudly , " have a right to 
 respect . she be not like I , - any hovel be good enough for I ; but for 
 she ! do you know that I conceive that hope , that the hope help to 
 lead I back here when , month ago , I be at Humberston , intent upon 
 rescue Sophy ; and see - though , " observe Waife , with a sly twitch of 
 the muscle round his mouth , " I have no right at that precise moment
