 impostor , who be try to save his dog - a black dog , sir , who have dye 
 his hair - from her carriage wheel . and the hope become strong still , 
 when , the first Sunday I attend yon village church , I again see that 
 fair - wondrously fair - face at the far end , - fair as moonlight and as 
 melancholy . strange it be , sir , that I - naturally a boisterous , mirthful 
 man , and now a shy , skulk fugitive - feel more attract , more allure 
 towards a countenance , in proportion as I read there the trace of 
 sadness . I feel less abased by my own nothingness , more emboldened to 
 approach and say , ' not so far apart from I : thou too hast suffer . ' 
 why be this ? " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " ' the fool hath say in his heart that there be no God ; ' 
 but the fool hath not say in his heart that there be no sorrow , - pithy 
 and most profound sentence ; intimate the irrefragable claim that bind 
 man to the Father . and when the chain tighten , the child be close 
 draw together . but to your wish : I will remember it . and when my cousin 
 return , she shall see your Sophy . " 




 CHAPTER V. 

  Mr. Waife , be by nature unlucky , consider that , in proportion as 
  fortune bring he good luck , nature convert it into bad . he 
  suffer Mr. George Morley to go away in his debt , and Sophy fear 
  that he will be dull in consequence . 

 George Morley , a few week after the conversation last record , take 
 his departure from Montfort Court , prepare , without a scruple , to 
 present himself for ordination to the friendly bishop . from Waife he 
 derive more than the cure of a disable infirmity ; he receive those 
 hint which , to a man who have the natural temperament of an orator , so 
 rarely unite with that of the scholar , expedite the mastery of the art 
 that make the fleeting human voice an abide , imperishable power . the 
 grateful teacher exhaust all his lore upon the pupil whose genius he 
 have free , whose heart have subdue himself . before leave , George be 
 much perplex how to offer to Waife any other remuneration than that 
 which , in Waife 's estimate , have already overpay all the benefit he have 
 receive ; namely , unquestione friendship and pledge protection . 
 it need scarcely be say that George think the man to whom he owe 
 fortune and happiness be entitle to something beyond that moral 
 recompense . but he find , at the first delicate hint , that Waife would 
 not hear of money , though the ex - Comedian do not affect any very 
 quixotic notion on that practical subject . " to tell you the truth , sir , 
 I have rather a superstition against have more money in my hand than 
 I know what to do with . it have always bring I bad luck . and what 
 be very hard , - the bad luck stay , but the money go . there be that 
 splendid sum I make at Gatesboro ' . you should have see I count it 
 over . I could not have have a prouder or more swell heart if I have 
 be , that great man Mr. Elwes the miser . and what bad luck it bring 
 I , and how it all fritter itself away ! nothing to show for it but a 
 silk ladder and an old hurdy - gurdy , and I sell they at half price . then 
 when I have the accident , which cost I this eye , the railway people 
 behave so generously , give I l120 , - think of that ! and before three 
 day the money be all go ! " 

 " how be that ? " say George , half - amuse , half - pain , - ' steal 
 perhaps ? " 

 " not so , " answer Waife , somewhat gloomily , " but restore . a poor dear 
 old man , who think very ill of I , and I do not   wonder at it , - be 
 reduce from great wealth to great poverty . while I be lay up , my 
 landlady read a newspaper to I , and in that newspaper be an account 
 of his reverse and destitution . but I be accountable to he for the 
 balance of an old debt , and that , with the doctor 's bill , quite cover 
 my l120 . I hope he do not think quite so ill of I now . but the money 
 bring good luck to he , rather than to I . well , sir , if you be 
 now to give I money , I should be on the look - out for some mournful 
 calamity . gold be not natural to I . some day , however , by and by , when 
 you be induct into your living , and have become a renowned preacher , 
 and have plenty to spare , with an idea that you will feel more 
 comfortable in your mind if you have do something royal for the 
 basketmaker , I will ask you to help I to make up a sum , which I be 
 try by degree to save , - an enormous sum , almost as much as I pay 
 away from my railway compensation : I owe it to the lady who lend it to 
 release Sophy from an engagement which I - certainly without any remorse 
 of conscience - make the child break . " 

 " oh , yes ! what be the amount ? let I at least repay that debt . " 

 " not yet . the lady can wait ; and she would be pleased to wait , because 
 she deserve to wait : it would be unkind to she to pay it off at 
 once . but in the meanwhile if you could send I a few good book for 
 Sophy , - instructive , yet not very , very dry,-and a french dictionary , 
 I can teach she French when the winter day close in . you see I be not 
 above be pay , sir . but , Mr. Morley , there be a great favour you can 
 do I . " 

 " what be it ? speak . " 

 " cautiously refrain from do I a great disservice ! you be go back 
 to your friend and relation . never speak of I to they . never describe 
 I and my odd way . name not the lady , nor - nor - nor - the man who 
 claim Sophy . 

 " your friend might not hurt I ; other might . talk travel . the hare be 
 not long in its form when it have a friend in a hound that give tongue . 
 promise what I ask . promise it as ' man and gentleman . ' " 

 " certainly . yet I have one relation to whom I should like , with your 
 permission , to speak of you , with whom I could wish you acquaint . he 
 be so thorough a man of the world , that he might suggest some method 
 to clear your good name , which you yourself would approve . my uncle , 
 Colonel Morley - " 

 " on no account ! " cry Waife , almost fiercely , and he evince so much 
 anger and uneasiness that it be long before George could pacify he by 
 the most earnest assurance that his secret should be inviolably keep , 
 and his injunction faithfully obey . no man of the world consult how 
 to force he back to the world of man that he flee from ! no colonel to 
 scan he with martinet eye , and hint how to pipeclay a tarnish ! Waife 's 
 apprehension gradually allay and his confidence restore , one fine 
 morning George take leave of his eccentric benefactor . 

 Waife and Sophy stand gaze after he from their garden - gate , the 
 cripple lean lightly on the child 's arm . she look with anxious 
 fondness into the old man 's thoughtful face , and clung to he more 
 closely as she look . 

 " will you not be dull , poor Grandy ? will you not miss he ? " 

 " a little at first , " say Waife , rouse himself . " education be a great 
 thing . an educated mind , provide that it do we no mischief , - which 
 be not always the case , - can not be withdraw from our existence without 
 leave a blank behind . Sophy , we must seriously set to work and educate 
 ourselves ! " 

 " we will , Grandy dear , " say Sophy , with decision ; and a few minute 
 afterwards , " if I can become very , very clever , you will not pine so 
 much after that gentleman , - will you , Grandy ? " 




 CHAPTER VI . 

  be a chapter that come to an untimely end . 

 Winter be far advanced when Montfort Court be again brighten by the 
 presence of its lady . a polite letter from Mr. Carr Vipont have reach 
 she before leave Windsor , suggest how much it would be for the 
 advantage of the Vipont interest if she would consent to visit for a 
 month or two the seat in Ireland , which have be too long neglect , 
 and at which my lord would join she on his departure from his Highland 
 moor . so to Ireland go Lady Montfort . my lord do not join she there ; 
 but Mr. Carr Vipont deem it desirable for the Vipont interest that 
 the wed pair should reunite at Montfort Court , where all the Vipont 
 family be invite to witness their felicity or mitigate their ennui . 

 but before proceed another stage in this history , it become a just 
 tribute of respect to the great House of Vipont to pause and place 
 its past record and present grandeur in full display before the 
 reverential reader . the House of Vipont ! - what be I about ? the House of 
 Vipont require a chapter to itself . 




 CHAPTER VII . 

  the House of Vipont , - " Majora canamus . " 

 the House of Vipont ! look back through age , it seem as if the 
 House of Vipont be one continuous living idiosyncrasy , have in its 
 progressive development a connected unity of thought and action , so that 
 through all the change of its outward form it have be move and guide 
 by the same single spirit , - " Le roi est mort ; vive le roi ! " - a Vipont 
 dy ; live the Vipont ! despite its high - sound Norman name , the House 
 of Vipont be no House at all for some generation after the Conquest . 
 the first Vipont who emerge from the obscurity of time be a rude 
 soldier of Gascon origin , in the reign of Henry II . , - one of the 
 thousand fighting - man who sail from Milford Haven with the stout Earl 
 of Pembroke , on that strange expedition which end in the conquest of 
 Ireland . this gallant man obtain large grant of land in that fertile 
 island ; some Mac or some O ' - - - vanished , and the House of Vipont rise . 

 during the reign of Richard I. , the House of Vipont , though recall to 
 England   leave its irish acquisition in charge of a fierce cadet , who 
 serve as middleman , excuse itself from the Crusade , and , by marriage 
 with a rich goldsmith 's daughter , be enable to lend money to those 
 who indulge in that exciting but costly pilgrimage . in the reign 
 of John , the House of Vipont foreclose its mortgage on land thus 
 pledge , and become possessed of a very fair property in England , as 
 well as its fief in the sister isle . 

 the House of Vipont take no part in the troublesome politic of that 
 day . discreetly obscure , it attend to its own fortune , and feel small 
 interest in Magna Charta . during the reign of the Plantagenet Edwards , 
 who be great encourager of mercantile adventure , the House of Vipont , 
 shun Crecy , Bannockburn , and such profitless brawl , intermarry 
 with London trader , and get many a good thing out of the Genoese . in 
 the reign of Henry IV . the House of Vipont reap the benefit of its 
 past forbearance and modesty . now , for the first time , the vipont 
 appear as belt knight ; they have armorial bearing ; they be 
 lancasterian to the backbone ; they be exceedingly indignant against 
 heretic ; they burn the Lollards ; they have place in the household of 
 Queen Joan , who be call a witch , - but a witch be a very good friend 
 when she wield a sceptre instead of a broomstick . and in proof of its 
 grow importance , the House of Vipont marry a daughter of the then 
 mighty House of Darrell . in the reign of Henry V. , during the invasion 
 of France , the House of Vipont - be afraid of the dysentery which 
 carry off more brave fellow than the field of Agincourt - contrive to 
 be a minor . the Wars of the Roses puzzle the House of Vipont sadly . but 
 it go through that perilous ordeal with singular tact and success . 
 the manner in which it change side , each change safe , and most change 
 lucrative , be beyond all praise . 

 on the whole , it prefer the Yorkists ; it be impossible to be 
 actively lancasterian with Henry VI . of Lancaster always in prison . and 
 thus , at the death of Edward IV . , the House of Vipont be Baron Vipont 
 of Vipont , with twenty manor . Richard III . count on the House of 
 Vipont , when he leave London to meet Richmond at Bosworth : he 
 count without his host . the House of Vipont become again intensely 
 Lancasterian , and be amongst the first to crowd round the litter in 
 which Henry VII . enter the metropolis . in that reign it marry a 
 relation of Empson 's , do the great House of Vipont ! and as noble of 
 eld date have become scarce and poor , Henry VII . be pleased to make 
 the House of Vipont an Earl , - the Earl of Montfort . in the reign of 
 Henry VIII . , instead of burn Lollards , the House of Vipont be all 
 for the Reformation : it obtain the land of two priory and one 
 abbey . gorge with that spoil , the House of Vipont , like an anaconda in 
 the process of digestion , sleep long . but no , it sleep not . though it 
 keep itself still as a mouse during the reign of Bloody Queen Mary   only 
 let it be know at Court that the House of Vipont have strong papal 
 leaning ; though during the reign of Elizabeth and James it make 
 no noise , the House of Vipont be silently inflate its lung and 
 improve its constitution . Slept , indeed ! it be wide awake . then it 
 be that it begin systematically its grand policy of alliance ; then be 
 it sedulously graft its olive branch on the stem of those fruitful 
 New Houses that have spring up with the Tudors ; then , alive to the spirit 
 of the day , provident of the want of the morrow , over the length 
 and breadth of the land it wove the interlace network of useful 
 cousinhood ! then , too , it begin to build palace , to enclose park ; it 
 travel , too , a little , do the House of Vipont ! it visit Italy ; it 
 conceive a taste : a very elegant House become the House of Vipont ! 
 and in James 's reign , for the first time , the House of Vipont get the 
 Garter . the Civil Wars break out : England be rent asunder . peer and 
 knight take part with one side or the other . the House of Vipont be 
 again perplexed . certainly at the commencement it , be all for King 
 Charles . but when King Charles take to fighting , the House of Vipont 
 shake its sagacious head , and go about , like Lord Falkland , sighing , 
 " peace , peace ! " finally , it remember its neglect estate in Ireland : 
 its duty call it thither . to Ireland it go , discreetly sad , and , 
 marry a kinswoman of Lord Fauconberg , - the connection least expose 
 to Fortune 's caprice of all the alliance form by the Lord Protector 's 
 family , - it be safe when Cromwell visit Ireland ; and no less safe 
 when Charles II . be restore to England . during the reign of the merry 
 monarch the House of Vipont be a courtier , marry a beauty , get the 
 Garter again , and , for the first time , become the fashion . fashion 
 begin to be a power . in the reign of James II . the House of Vipont again 
 contrive to be a minor , who come of age just in time to take the oath 
 of fealty to William and Mary . in case of accident , the House of Vipont 
 keep on friendly term with the exile stuart , but it write no letter , 
 and get into no scrape . it be not , however , till the Government , under 
 Sir Robert Walpole , establish the constitutional and parliamentary 
 system which characterize modern freedom , that the puissance 
 accumulate through successive century by the House of Vipont become 
 pre - eminently visible . by that time its land be vast ; its wealth 
 enormous ; its parliamentary influence , as " a Great House , " be a part of 
 the British Constitution . at this period , the House of Vipont find it 
 convenient to rend itself into two grand division , - the peer 's branch 
 and the commoner 's . the House of Commons have become so important that it 
 be necessary for the House of Vipont to be represent there by a great 
 commoner . thus arise the family of Carr Vipont . that division , owe 
 to a marriage settlement favour a young son by the heiress of the 
 Carrs , carry off a good slice from the estate of the earldom : uno 
 averso , non deficit alter ; the earldom mourn , but replace the loss 
 by two wealthy wedlock of its own ; and have long since see cause to 
 rejoice that its power in the Upper Chamber be strengthen by such aid 
 in the low . for , thank to its parliamentary influence , and the aid 
 of the great commoner , in the reign of George III . the House of Vipont 
 become a Marquess . from that time to the present day , the House of 
 Vipont have go on prospering and progressive . it be to the aristocracy 
 what the " Times " newspaper be to the press . the same quick sympathy 
 with public feeling , the same unity of tone and purpose , the same 
 adaptability , and something of the same lofty tone of superiority to the 
 petty interest of party . it may be concede that the House of Vipont 
 be less brilliant than the " Times " newspaper , but eloquence and wit , 
 necessary to the duration of a newspaper , be not necessary to that of 
 the House of Vipont . have they be so , it would have have they . 

 the head of the House of Vipont rarely condescend to take office . with 
 a rent - roll loosely estimate at about l170,000 a year , it be beneath 
 a man to take from the public a paltry five or six thousand a year , and 
 undergo all the undignified abuse of popular assembly , and " a ribald 
 press . " but it be a matter of course that the House of Vipont should 
 be represent in any Cabinet that a constitutional monarch could be 
 advise to form . since the time of Walpole , a Vipont be always in the 
 service of his country , except in those rare instance when the country 
 be infamously misgovern . the cadet of the House , or the senior 
 member of the great commoner 's branch of it , sacrifice their ease to 
 fulfil that duty . the Montfort marquess in general be content 
 with situation of honour in the household , as of Lord Steward , Lord 
 Chamberlain , or Master of the Horse , etc . , - not onerous dignity ; and 
 even these they only deign to accept on those special occasion when 
 danger threaten the star of Brunswick , and the sense of its exalt 
 station forbid the House of Vipont to leave its country in the dark . 

 great Houses like that of Vipont assist the work of civilization by the 
 law of their existence . they be sure to have a spirited and wealthy 
 tenantry , to whom , if but for the sake of that popular character which 
 double political influence , they be liberal and kindly landlord . 
 under their sway fen and sand become fertile ; agricultural experiment 
 be test on a large scale ; cattle and sheep improve in breed ; national 
 capital augment , and , spring beneath the ploughshare , circulate 
 indirectly to speed the ship and animate the loom . have there be no 
 Woburn , no Holkham , no Montfort Court , England would be the poor by 
 many a million . our great Houses tend also to the refinement of national 
 taste ; they have their show place , their picture gallery , their 
 beautiful ground . the humblest drawing - room owe an elegance or 
 comfort , the small garden a flower or esculent , to the importation 
 which luxury borrow from abroad , or the invention it stimulate at 
 home , for the original benefit of great house . have a fair share of 
 such merit , in common with other great house , the House of Vipont be 
 not without good quality peculiar to itself . precisely because it 
 be the most egotistical of house , fill with the sense of its own 
 identity , and guide by the instinct of its own conservation , it be 
 a very civil , good - natured House , - courteous , generous , hospitable ; a 
 House   I mean the head of it , not of course all its subordinate member , 
 include even the august Lady Selina   that could bow graciously and 
 shake hand with you . even if you have no vote yourself , you might have 
 a cousin who have a vote . and once admit into the family , the House 
 adopt you ; you have only to marry one of its remotest relation and the 
 House send you a wedding present ; and at every general election , invite 
 you to rally round your connection , - the Marquess . therefore , next 
 only to the Established Church , the House of Vipont be that british 
 institution the root of which be the most widely spread . 

 now the Viponts have for long generation be an energetic race . 
 whatever their defect , they have exhibit shrewdness and vigour . the 
 late Marquess   grandfather to the present   have be perhaps the able 
  that be , do most for the House of Vipont   of they all . of a grandiose 
 and superb mode of living ; of a majestic deportment ; of princely 
 manner ; of a remarkable talent for the management of all business , 
 whether private or public ; a perfect enthusiast for the House of Vipont , 
 and aid by a marchioness in all respect worthy of he , - he might be 
 say to be the culminate flower of the venerable stem . but the 
 present lord , succeed to the title as a mere child , be a melancholy 
 contrast , not only to his grandsire , but to the general character of his 
 progenitor . before his time , every Head of the House have do something 
 for it ; even the most frivolous have contribute one have collect the 
 picture , another the statue , a third the medal , a fourth have amass 
 the famous Vipont library ; while other have at least married heiress , 
 or augment , through ducal line , the splendour of the interminable 
 cousinhood . the present Marquess be literally nil . the pith of the 
 Viponts be not in he . he look well ; he dress well : if life be 
 only the dumb show of a tableau , he would have be a paragon of a 
 Marquess . but he be like the watch we give to little child , with 
 a pretty gilt dial - plate , and no work in they . he be thoroughly inert ; 
 there be no wind he up : he could not manage his property ; he could 
 not answer his letter , - very few of they could he even read through . 
 politic do not interest he , nor literature , nor field - sport . he 
 shoot , it be true , but mechanically ; wonder , perhaps , why he do 
 shoot . he attend race , because the House of Vipont keep a racing 
 stud . he bet on his own horse , but if they lose show no vexation . 
 admirer   no Marquess of Montfort could be wholly without they 
 say , " what fine temper ! what good breeding ! " it be nothing but 
 constitutional apathy . no one could call he a bad man : he be not a 
 profligate , an oppressor , a miser , a spendthrift ; he would not have 
 take the trouble to be a bad man on any account . those who behold his 
 character at a distance would have call he an exemplary man . the 
 more conspicuous duty of his station - subscription , charity , the 
 maintenance of grand establishment , the encouragement of the fine 
 art - be virtue admirably perform for he by other . but the phlegm 
 or nullity of his being be not , after all , so complete as I have make 
 it , perhaps , appear . he have one susceptibility which be more common with 
 woman than with man , - the susceptibility to pique . his amour propre 
 be unforgive : pique that , and he could do a rash thing , a foolish 
 thing , a spiteful thing ; pique that , and , prodigious ! the watch go ! he 
 have a root pique against his marchioness . apparently he have conceive 
 this pique from the very first . he show it passively by supreme 
 neglect ; he show it actively by remove she from all the sphere 
 of power which naturally fall to the wife when the husband shun the 
 detail of business . evidently he have a dread lest any one should say , 
 " Lady Montfort influence my lord . " accordingly , not only the management 
 of his estate fall to Carr Vipont , but even of his garden , his 
 household , his domestic arrangement . it be Carr Vipont or Lady Selina 
 who say to Lady Montfort , " give a ball ; " " you should ask so and so to 
 dinner ; " " montfort be much hurt to see the old lawn at the Twickenham 
 villa break up by those new bosquet . true , it be settle on you as a 
 jointure - house , but for that very reason montfort be sensitive , " etc . 
 in fact , they be virtually as separate , my lord and my lady , as if 
 legally disunite , and as if Carr Vipont and Lady Selina be trustee 
 or intermediary in any polite approach to each other . but , on the 
 other hand , it be fair to say that where Lady Montfort 's sphere of 
 action do not interfere with her husband 's plan , habit , liking , 
 disliking , jealous apprehension that she should be suppose to 
 have any ascendency over what exclusively belong to himself as Roi 
 faineant of the Viponts , she be leave free as air . no attempt at 
 masculine control or conjugal advice . at her disposal be wealth without 
 stint , every luxury the soft could desire , every gewgaw the vain could 
 covet . have her pin - money , which in itself be the revenue of an ordinary 
 peeress , fail to satisfy she want ; have she grow tired of wear the 
 family diamond , and covet new gem from Golconda , - a single word to 
 Carr Vipont or Lady Selina would have be answer by a carte blanche 
 on the Bank of England . but Lady Montfort have the misfortune not to be 
 extravagant in her taste . strange to say , in the world Lord Montfort 's 
 marriage be call a love - match ; he have marry a portionless girl , 
 daughter to one of his poor and obscurest cousin , against the 
 uniform policy of the House of Vipont , which do all it could for poor 
 cousin except marry they to its chief . but Lady Montfort 's conduct 
 in these try circumstance be admirable and rare . few affront can 
 humiliate we unless we resent they - and in vain . Lady Montfort have 
 that exquisite dignity which give to submission the grace of cheerful 
 acquiescence . that in the gay world flatterer should gather round a 
 young wife so eminently beautiful , and so wholly leave by her husband 
 to her own guidance , be inevitable . but at the very first insinuate 
 compliment or pathetic condolence , Lady Montfort , so meek in her 
 household , be haughty enough to have daunt Lovelace . she be thus 
 very early feel to be beyond temptation , and the bold pass on , nor 
 presume to tempt . she be unpopular ; call " proud and freezing ; " she 
 do not extend the influence of " the House ; " she do not confirm its 
 fashion , - fashion which necessitate social ease , and which no rank , no 
 wealth , no virtue , can of themselves suffice to give . and this failure 
 on her part be a great offence in the eye of the House of Vipont . " she 
 do absolutely nothing for we , " say Lady Selina ; but Lady Selina in 
 her heart be well pleased that to she in reality thus fall , almost 
 without a rival , the female representation , in the great world , of the 
 Vipont honour . Lady Selina be fashion itself . 

 Lady Montfort 's social peculiarity be in the eagerness with which she 
 seek the society of person who enjoy a reputation for superior 
 intellect , whether statesman , lawyer , author , philosopher , artist . 
 intellectual intercourse seem as if it be her native atmosphere , 
 from which she be habitually banish , to which she return with an 
 instinctive yearning and a new zest of life ; yet be she call , even 
 here , nor seemingly without justice , capricious and unsteady in her 
 liking . these clever personage , after a little while , all seem to 
 disappoint her expectation of they ; she seek the acquaintance of 
 each with cordial earnestness ; slide from the acquaintance with weary 
 languor , - never , after all , less alone than when alone . 

 and so wondrous lovely ! nothing so rare as beauty of the high type : 
 genius and beauty , indeed , be both rare ; genius , which be the beauty 
 of the mind,-beauty , which be the gen ius of the body . but , of the two , 
 beauty be the rarer . all of we can count on our finger some forty 
 or fifty person of undoubted and illustrious genius , include those 
 famous in action , letter , art . but can any of we remember to have see 
 more than four or five specimen of first - rate ideal beauty ? Whosoever 
 have see Lady Montfort would have rank she amongst such four or five 
 in his recollection . there be in her face that lustrous dazzle to which 
 the latin poet , perhaps , refer when he speak of the - 

  " nitor 
  Splendentis Pario marmore purius ... 
  Et voltus , niminm lubricus adspici , " 

 and which an english poet , with the less sensuous but more spiritual 
 imagination of northern genius , have describe in line that an English 
 reader may be pleased to see rescue from oblivion , - 

  " her face be like the milky way I ' the sky , 
  a meeting of gentle light without a name . " 
   suckle 

 the eye so purely bright , the exquisite harmony of colour between 
 the dark   not too dark   hair and the ivory of the skin ; such sweet 
 radiance in the lip when it break into a smile . and it be say that in 
 her maiden day , before Caroline Lyndsay become Marchioness of Montfort , 
 that smile be the most joyous thing imaginable . Absurd now ; you would 
 not think it , but that stately lady have be a wild , fanciful girl , with 
 the merry laugh and the quick tear , fill the air round she 
 with April sunshine . certainly , no being ever yet live the life 
 nature intend they to live , nor have fair play for heart and mind , who 
 contrive , by hook or by crook , to marry the wrong person ! 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

  the interior of the great house . - the British Constitution at home 
  in a family party . 

 Great be the family gathering that Christmas - tide at Montfort Court . 
 Thither flock the cousin of the House in all degree and of various 
 rank . from duke , who have nothing leave to wish for that king and 
 cousinhood can give , to briefless barrister and aspire cornet , of 
 equally good blood with the duke , - the superb family unite its motley 
 scion . such reunion be frequent : they belong to the hereditary 
 policy of the House of Vipont . on this occasion the muster of the 
 clan be more significant than usual ; there be a " crisis " in the 
 constitutional history of the british empire . a new Government have be 
 suddenly form within the last six week , which certainly portend 
 some direful blow on our ancient institution ; for the House of Vipont 
 have not be consult in its arrangement , and be wholly unrepresented 
 in the Ministry , even by a lordship of the Treasury . Carr Vipont have 
 therefore summon the patriotic and resentful kindre . 

 it be an hour or so after the conclusion of dinner . the gentleman have 
 join the lady in the state suite , a suite which the last Marquess 
 have rearrange and redecorate in his old age , during the long illness 
 that finally conduct he to his ancestor . during his early year 
 that princely Marquess have desert Montfort Court for a seat nearer 
 to London , and therefore much more easily fill with that brilliant 
 society of which he have be long the ornament and centre , - railway not 
 then exist for the annihilation of time and space , and a journey to a 
 northern county four day with posthorse make the invitation even 
 of a Marquess of Montfort unallure to languid beauty and gouty 
 minister . but near the end of his worldly career , this long 
 neglect of the dwelling identify with his hereditary title smote the 
 conscience of the illustrious sinner . and other occupation begin 
 to pall , his lordship , accompany and cheer by a chaplain , who have 
 a fine taste in the decorative art , come resolutely to Montfort Court ; 
 and there , surround with architect and gilder and upholsterer , 
 redeem his error ; and , soothe by the reflection of the palace 
 provide for his successor , add to his vault - a coffin . 

 the suite expand before the eye . you be in the grand drawing - room , 
 copy from that of Versailles . that be the picture , full length , of 
 the late Marquess in his robe ; its pendant be the late Marchioness , 
 his wife . that table of malachite be a present from the Russian Emperor 
 Alexander ; that vase of Sevres which rest on it be make for Marie 
 Antoinette , - see her portrait enamel in its centre . through the open 
 door at the far end your eye lose itself in a vista of other pompous 
 chamber , - the music - room , the statue hall , the orangery ; other room 
 there be appertain to the suite , a ballroom fit for Babylon , a 
 library that might have adorn Alexandria , - but they be not light , 
 nor require , on this occasion ; it be strictly a family party , sixty 
 guest and no more . 

 in the drawing - room three whist - table carry off the more elderly and 
 grave . the piano , in the music - room , attract a young group . Lady 
 Selina Vipont 's eld daughter , Honoria , a young lady not yet bring 
 out , but about to be bring out the next season , be thread a 
 wonderfully intricate german piece , 

  " link'd sweetness , long draw out , " 

 with variation . her science be consummate . no pain have be spar 
 on her education ; elaborately accomplish , she be form to be the 
 sympathizing spouse of a wealthy statesman . Lady Montfort be seat by 
 an elderly duchess , who be good - natured and a great talker ; near she be 
 seat two middle - aged gentleman , who have be converse with she till 
 the duchess , have cut in , turn dialogue into monologue . 

 the elder of these two gentleman be Mr. Carr Vipont , bald , with clip 
 parliamentary whisker ; value himself on a likeness to canning , but 
 with a portlier presence ; look a large - acred man . Carr Vipont have about 
 L40,000 a year ; have often refuse office for himself , while take care 
 that other Viponts should have it ; be a great authority in committee 
 business and the rule of the House of Commons ; speak very seldom , and 
 at no great length , never argue , merely state his opinion , carry 
 great weight with he , and as he vote vote fifteen other member of 
 the House of Vipont , besides admire satellite . he can therefore 
 turn division , and have decide the fate of cabinet . a pleasant man , 
 a little consequential , but the reverse of haughty , - unctuously 
 overbearing . the other gentleman , to whom he be listen , be our old 
 acquaintance Colonel Alban Vipont Morley , Darrell 's friend , George 's 
 uncle , - a man of importance , not inferior , indeed , to that of his 
 kinsman Carr ; an authority in clubroom , an oracle in drawing - room , a 
 first - rate man of the beau monde . Alban Morley , a young brother , have 
 enter the Guards young ; retire young also from the Guards with the 
 rank of Colonel , and on receipt of a legacy from an old aunt , which , 
 with the interest derive from the sum at which he sell his commission , 
 allow he a clear income of L1,000 a year . this modest income suffice 
 for all his want , fine gentleman though he be . he have refuse to go 
 into Parliament , - refuse a high place in a public department . single 
 himself , he show his respect for wedlock by the interest he take in 
 the marriage of other people ; just as Earl Warwick , too wise to set 
 up for a king , gratify his passion for royalty by become the 
 king - maker . the Colonel be exceedingly accomplish , a very fair 
 scholar , know most modern language . in paint an amateur , in music a 
 connoisseur ; witty at time , and with wit of a high quality , but thrifty 
 in the expenditure of it ; too wise to be know as a wit . manly too , a 
 daring rider , who have win many a fox 's brush ; a famous deer - stalker , 
 and one of the few english gentleman who still keep up the noble art of 
 fencing , - twice a week to be see , foil in hand , against all comer in 
 Angelo 's room . thin , well - shape , - not handsome , my dear young lady , 
 far from it , but with an air so thoroughbred that , have you see he in 
 the day when the opera - house have a crushroom and a fop ' alley , - see 
 he in either of those resort , surround by elaborate dandy and 
 showy beauty - man , dandy and beauty - man would have seem to 
 you secondrate and vulgar ; and the eye , fascinate by that quiet 
 form , - plain in manner , plain in dress , plain in feature , - you would 
 have say , " how very distinguished it be to be so plain ! " know the 
 great world from the core to the cuticle , and on that knowledge basing 
 authority and position , Colonel Morley be not calculate , not cunning , 
 not suspicious , - his sagacity the more quick because its movement be 
 straightforward ; intimate with the great , but seek , not seek ; 
 not a flatterer nor a parasite , but when his advice be ask   even if 
 advice necessitate reproof   give it with military candour : in fine , 
 a man of such social reputation as render he an ornament and prop 
 to the House of Vipont ; and with unsuspected depth of intelligence and 
 feeling , which lie in the low strata of his knowledge of this world 
 to witness of some other one , and justify Darrell in commend a 
 boy like Lionel Haughton to the Colonel 's friendly care and admonitory 
 counsel . the Colonel , like other man , have his weakness , if weakness it 
 can be call : he believe that the House of Vipont be not merely 
 the corinthian capital , but the embattle keep - not merely the dulce 
 decus , but the praesidium columenque rerum - of the british monarchy . 
 he do not boast of his connection with the House ; he do not provoke 
 your spleen by enlarge on its manifold virtue ; he would often have 
 his harmless jest against its member , or even against its pretension : 
 but such seeming evidence of forbearance or candour be cunning 
 device to mitigate envy . his devotion to the House be not obtrusive : 
 it be profound . he love the House of Vipont for the sake of England : 
 he love England for the sake of the House of Vipont . have it be 
 possible , by some tremendous reversal of the ordinary law of nature , to 
 dissociate the cause of England from the cause of the House of Vipont , 
 the Colonel would have say , " save at least the Ark of the Constitution ! 
 and rally round the old House ! " 

 the Colonel have none of Guy Darrell 's infirmity of family pride ; he 
 care not a rush for mere pedigree , - much too liberal and enlighten 
 for such obsolete prejudice . no ! he know the world too well not to be 
 quite aware that old family and long pedigree be of no use to a man if 
 he have not some money or some merit . but it be of use to a man to be 
 a cousin of the House of Vipont , though without any money , without 
 any merit at all . it be of use to be part and parcel of a british 
 institution ; it be of use to have a legitimate indefeasible right to 
 share in the administration and patronage of an empire , on which   to use 
 a novel illustration   " the sun never set . " you might want nothing 
 for yourself ; the Colonel and the Marquess equally want nothing for 
 themselves but man be not to be a selfish egotist ! Man have cousin : his 
 cousin may want something . demosthene denounce , in word that inflame 
 every manly breast , the ancient Greek who do not love his POLIS 
 or State , even though he take nothing from it but barren honour , and 
 contribute towards it - a great many disagreeable taxis . as the POLIS to 
 the Greek , be the House of Vipont to Alban Vipont Morley . it be the 
 most beautiful , touch affection imaginable ! whenever the House be in 
 difficulty , whenever it be threaten by a CRISIS , the Colonel be by 
 its side , spar no pain , neglect no mean , to get the Ark of the 
 Constitution back into smooth water . that duty do , he retire again 
 into private life , and scorn all other reward than the still whisper 
 of applaud conscience .

 Waife be seat on the stump of a tree , material for his handicraft

 here slowly , lingeringly , let fall the curtain . in the frank license 
 of narrative , year will have roll away ere the curtain rise again . 
 event that may influence a life often date from moment the most 
 serene , from thing that appear as trivial and unnoticeable as the great 
 lady 's visit to the basketmaker 's cottage . which of those life 
 will that visit influence hereafter , - the woman 's , the child 's , the 
 vagrant 's ? whose ? probably little that pass now would aid conjecture , 
 or be a visible link in the chain of destiny . a few desultory question ; 
 a few guard answer ; a look or so , a musical syllable or two , 
 exchange between the lady and the child ; a basket buy , or a promise 
 to call again . nothing worth the telling . be it then untold . view only 
 the scene itself as the curtain drop reluctantly . the rustic cottage , 
 its garden - door open , and open its old - fashioned lattice casement . you 
 can see how neat and cleanly , how eloquent of healthful poverty , how 
 remote from squalid penury , the whitewashed wall , the homely furniture 
 within . creeper lately train around the doorway ; Christmas holly , 
 with berry red against the window - pane ; the bee - hive yonder ; a 
 starling , too , outside the threshold , in its wicker cage ; in the 
 background   all the rest of the neighbouring hamlet out of sight , the 
 church spire taper away into the clear blue wintry sky . all have an 
 air of repose , of safety . close beside you be the Presence of HOME ; that 
 ineffable , shelter , love Presence , which amidst solitude murmur 
 " not solitary , " - a Presence unvouchsafe to the great lady in the palace 
 she have leave . and the lady herself ? she be rest on the rude gnarl 
 root - stump from which the vagrant have rise ; she have draw Sophy towards 
 she ; she have take the child 's hand ; she be speak now , now listen ; 
 and on her face kindness look like happiness . perhaps she be happy 
 that moment . and Waife ? he be turn aside his weatherbeaten mobile 
 countenance with his hand anxiously tremble upon the young scholar 's 
 arm . the scholar whisper , " be you satisfied with I ? " and Waife 
 answer in a voice as low but more break , " God reward you ! oh , joy ! if 
 my pretty one have find at last a woman friend ! " poor vagabond , he have 
 now a calm asylum , a fixed humble livelihood ; more than that , he have 
 just achieve an object fondly cherish . his past life , - alas ! what have 
 he do with it ? his actual life , break fragment though it be , be 
 at rest now . but still the everlasting question , - mock terrible 
 question , with its phrasing of farce and its enigma of tragical 
 sense , - " what will he do with it ? " do with what ? the all that remain to 
 he , the all he hold ! the all which man himself , betwixt Free - will and 
 Pre - decree , be permit to do . ask not the vagrant alone : ask each of 
 the four there assemble on that fly bridge call the Moment . time 
 before thee , - what wilt thou do with it ? ask thyself ! ask the wise ! 
 out of effort to answer that question , what dream - school have rise , 
 never wholly to perish , - the science of seer on the Chaldee 's 
 Pur - Tor , or in the rock - cave of Delphi , gasp after and grasp at by 
 horn - hand mechanic to - day in their lane and alley . to the heart of 
 the populace sink down the blurred relic of what once be the law of 
 the secret sage , hieroglyphical tatter which the credulous vulgar 
 attempt to interpret . " what will he do with it ? " ask Merle and his 
 Crystal ! but the curtain descend ! yet a moment , there they be , - age 
 and childhood , - poverty , wealth , station , vagabondage ; the preacher 's 
 sacred learning and august ambition ; fancy of dawn reason ; hope 
 of intellect mature ; memory of existence wreck ; household sorrow ; 
 untold regret ; elegy and epic in low , close , human sigh , to which 
 Poetry never yet give voice : all for the moment personify there 
 before you , - a glimpse for the guess , no more . lower and low fall the 
 curtain ! all be blank ! 




 BOOK VI . 




 CHAPTER I. 

  Etchings of Hyde Park in the month of June , which , if this history 
  escape those villain the trunk - maker , may be of inestimable value 
  to unborn antiquarian . - character , long absent , reappear and give 
  some account of themselves . 

 five year have pass away since this history open . it be the month 
 of June once more , - June , which clothe our London in all its glory , 
 fill its languid ballroom with living flower , and its stony causeway 
 with human butterfly . it be about the hour of six P.M. the lounge in 
 Hyde Park be crowd ; along the road that skirt the serpentine crawl 
 the carriage one after the other ; congregate by the rail the lazy 
 looker - on , - lazy in attitude , but with active eye , and tongue 
 sharpen on the whetstone of scandal , - the scaliger of club window 
 air their vocabulary in the Park . slowly saunter on foot idler 
 of all degree in the hierarchy of London idlesse : dandy of 
 establish - fame ; youthful tiro in their first season . yonder in the 
 Ride , form less inanimate seem condemn to active exercise ; young 
 lady do penance in a canter ; old beaux at hard labour in a 
 trot . sometimes , by a more thoughtful brow , a still brisker pace , you 
 recognize a busy member of the Imperial Parliament , who , advise by 
 physician to be as much on horseback as possible , snatch an hour or 
 so in the interval between the close of his Committee and the interest 
 of the Debate , and shirk the opening speech of a well - know bore . among 
 such truant lawgiver   grief it be to say it   may be see that once 
 model member , Sir Gregory Stollhead . grim dyspepsia seize on he at 
 last , " relaxation from his duty " become the adequate punishment for 
 all his sin . Solitary he rid , and commune with himself , yawn at 
 every second . upon chair beneficently locate under the tree towards 
 the north side of the walk be intersperse small knot and coterie 
 in repose . there you might see the Ladies Prymme , still the lady 
 Prymme , - Janet and Wilhelmina ; Janet have grow fat , Wilhelmina thin . but 
 thin or fat , they be no less prymme . they do not lack male attendant ; 
 they be girl of high fashion , with whom young man think it a 
 distinction to be see talk ; of high principle , too , and high 
 pretension   unhappily for themselves , they be co - heiress , by whom 
 young man under the rank of earl need not fear to be artfully entrap 
 into " honourable intention . " they coquet majestically , but they 
 never flirt ; they exact devotion , but they do not ask in each victim a 
 sacrifice on the horn of the altar ; they will never give their hand 
 where they do not give their heart ; and be ever afraid that they 
 be court for their money , they will never give their heart save to 
 wooer who have much more money than themselves . many young man stop to 
 do pass homage to the Ladies Prymme : some linger to converse ; safe 
 young man , - they be all young son . far on , Lady Frost and Mr. 
 Crampe , the wit , sit amicably side by side , peck at each other with 
 sarcastic beak ; occasionally desist , in order to fasten nip and 
 claw upon that common enemy , the pass friend ! the Slowes , a numerous 
 family , but taciturn , sit by themselves ; bow to much , accost rarely . 

 note that man of good presence , somewhere about thirty , or a year or two 
 more , who , recognize by most of the lounger , seem not at home in the 
 lounge . he have pass by the various coterie just describe , make his 
 obeisance to the Ladies Prymme , receive an icy epigram from Lady Frost , 
 and a laconic sneer from Mr. Crampe , and exchange silent bow with 
 seven silent Slowes . he have wander on , look high in the air , but 
 still look for some one not in the air , and evidently disappoint in 
 his search , come to a full stop at length , take off his hat , wipe 
 his brow , utter a petulant " Prr - r - pshaw ! " and see , a little in 
 the background , the chairless shade of a thin , emaciated , dusty tree , 
 thither he retire , and seat himself with as little care whether there 
 to seat himself be the right thing in the right place , as if in the 
 honeysuckle arbour of a village inn . " it serve I right , " say he to 
 himself : " a precocious villain burst in upon I , break my day , make 
 an appointment to meet I here , in these very walk , ten minute before 
 six ; decoy I with the promise of a dinner at Putney , - room look on 
 the river and fried flounder . I have the credulity to yield : I derange 
 my habit ; I leave my cool studio ; I put off my easy blouse ; I imprison 
 my freeborn throat in a cravat invent by the Thugs ; the dog - day 
 be at hand , and I walk rashly over scorch pavement in a black 
 frock - coat and a brimless hat ; I annihilate 3s . 6d . in a pair of kid 
 glove ; I arrive at this haunt of spleen ; I run the gauntlet of frost , 
 Slowes , and prymme : and my traitor fail I ! half - past six , - not a sign 
 of he ! and the dinner at Putney , - fry flounder ? dream ! patience , 
 five minute more ; if then he come not , breach for life between he and 
 I ! ah , voila ! there he come , the laggard ! but how those fine folk be 
 catch at he ! have he ask they also to dinner at Putney , and do they 
 care for fried flounder ? " 

 the soliloquist 's eye be on a young man , much young than himself , who 
 be thread the motley crowd with a light quick step , but be compel 
 to stop at each moment to interchange a word of welcome , a shake of the 
 hand . evidently he have already a large acquaintance ; evidently he be 
 popular , on good term with the world and himself . what free grace 
 in his bearing ! what gay good - humour in his smile ! power above ! Lady 
 Wilhelmina surely blush as she return his bow . he have pass Lady 
 Frost unblighte ; the Slowes evince emotion , at least the female Slowes , 
 as he shoot by they with that slide bow . he look from side to side , 
 with the rapid glance of an eye in which light seem all dance and 
 sparkle : he see the soliloquist under the meagre tree ; the pace 
 quicken , the lip part half laugh . 

 " do not   scold , Vance . I be late , I know ; but I do not make allowance for 
 interception . "

 I not give to have and to hold it for an hour ? " " hold an eel , less

 companion 's heighten colour - " but , " he add seriously , and with an

 of arm ! never heed what be say on the subject by a querulous painter ! 
 the desire of fame may be folly in civilian : in soldier it be wisdom . 
 Twin - bear with the martial sense of honour , it cheer the march ; it 
 warm the bivouac ; it give music to the whir of the bullet , the roar

 bond of brother ; comfort the survivor when the brother fall ; take 
 from war its grim aspect of carnage ; and from homicide itself extract 
 lesson that strengthen the safeguard to humanity , and perpetuate life 
 to nation . right : pant for fame ; you be a soldier ! "

 five year of that critical age , from seventeen to twenty - two , spend in

 divine credulity in its own unexhausted resource ; but borrow from

 " so ! " say Vance , after a prolonged pause , " I do not   know whether I have

 disciple in the sacred walk of the Queen 's Bench .
