 CHAPTER VI . 

  fresh TOUCHES to the three vignette for the BOOK of BEAUTY . 

 week pass - the London season be begin - Darrell have decide 
 nothing - the prestige of his position be undiminishe , - in politic , 
 perhaps high . he have succeed in reconcile some great man ; he have 
 strengthen - it might be save - a jarring cabinet . in all this he have 
 show admirable knowledge of mankind , and prove that time and disuse 
 have not lessen his power of perception . in his matrimonial design , 
 Darrell seem more bent than ever upon the hazard - irresolute as 
 ever on the choice of a partner . still the choice appear to be 
 circumscribe to the fair three who have be subject to Colonel 
 Morley 's speculative criticism - Lady Adela , Miss Vipont , Flora Vyvyan . 
 much pro and con might be say in respect to each . Lady Adela be so 
 handsome that it be a pleasure to look at she ; and that be much when 
 one see the handsome face every day , - provide the pleasure do 
 not wear off . she have the reputation of a very good temper ; and the 
 expression of her countenance confirm it . there , panegyric stop ; 
 but detraction do not commence . what remain be inoffensive 
 commonplace . she have no salient attribute , and no rule passion . 
 certainly she would never have waste a thought on Mr. Darrell , nor have 
 discover a single merit in he , if he have not be quote as a very 
 rich man of high character in search of a wife , and if her father have 
 not say to she : " Adela , Mr. Darrell have be greatly strike with your 
 appearance - he tell I so . he be not young , but he be still a very fine 
 look man , and you be twenty - seven . ' tis a great distinction to be 
 notice by a person of his year and position , than by a pack of silly 
 young fellow , who think more of their own pretty face than they would 
 ever do of your . " 

 " if you do not mind a little disparity of year , he would make you a 
 happy wife ; and , in the course of nature , a widow , not too old to enjoy 
 liberty , and with a jointure that might entitle you to a still well 
 match . " 

 Darrell thus put into Lady Adela 's head , he remain there , and become 
 an idee fixe . view in the light of a probable husband , he be 
 elevate into an " interesting man . " she would have receive his 
 address with gentle complacency ; and , be more the creature of habit 
 than impulse , would no doubt , in the intimacy of connubial life , have 
 bl he , or any other admire husband , with a reasonable modicum of 
 languid affection . nevertheless , Lady Adela be an unconscious impostor ; 
 for , owe to a mild softness of eye and a susceptibility to blush , 
 a victim ensnare by her beauty would be apt to give her credit for a 
 nature far more accessible to the romance of the tender passion than , 
 happily perhaps for her own peace of mind , she possess ; and might 
 flatter himself that he have produce a sensation which give that 
 softness to the eye and that damask to the blush . 

 Honoria Vipont would have be a choice far more creditable to the good 
 sense of so mature a wooer . few well specimen of a young lady bring 
 up to become an accomplished woman of the world . she have sufficient 
 instruction to be the companion of an ambitious man - solid judgment to 
 fit she for his occasional adviser . she could preside with dignity over 
 a stately household - receive with grace distinguish guest . fit 
 to administer an ample fortune , ample fortune be necessary to the 
 development of her excellent quality . if a man of Darrell 's age be 
 bold enough to marry a young wife , a safe wife amongst the young 
 lady of London he could scarcely find ; for though Honoria be only 
 three - and - twenty , she be as staid , as sensible , and as remote from all 
 girlish frivolity , as if she have be eight - and - thirty . certainly have 
 Guy Darrell be of her own year , his fortune unmade , his fame to 
 win , a lawyer reside at the back of Holborn , or a pretty squire in 
 the petty demesne of Fawley , he would have have no charm in the eye of 
 Honoria Vipont . disparity of year be in this case no drawback but his 
 advantage , since to that disparity Darrell owe the establish name and 
 the eminent station which make Honoria think she elevate her own self 
 in prefer he . it be but justice to she to distinguish here between 
 a woman 's veneration for the attribute of respect which a man gather 
 round he , and the more vulgar sentiment which sink the man altogether , 
 except as the necessary fixture to be take in with general valuation . 
 it be not fair to ask if a girl who entertain a preference for one of 
 our toil , stirring , ambitious sex , who may be double her age or 
 have a snub nose , but who look dignified and impose on a pedestal 
 of state , whether she would like he as much if strip of all his 
 accessory , and leave unredeemed to his baptismal register or unbecoming 
 nose . just as well ask a girl in love with a young Lothario if she would 
 like he as much if he have be ugly and crooked . the high name of the 
 one man be as much a part of he as good look be to the other . thus , 
 though it be say of Madame de la Valliere that she love Louis XIV : 
 for himself and not for his regal grandeur , be there a woman in the 
 world , however disintereste , who believe that Madame de la Valliere 
 would have like Louis XIV . as much if Louis XIV . have be Mr. John 
 Jones ; Honoria would not have bestow her hand on a brainless , 
 worthless nobleman , whatever his rank or wealth . she be above that sort 
 of ambition ; but neither would she have marry the well - look and 
 worthy John Jones who ever bear that british appellation , if he have 
 not occupy the social position which bring the merit of a Jones 
 within range of the eyeglass of a Vipont . 

 many girl in the nursery say to their juvenile confidant , " I will 
 marry the man I love . " Honoria have ever say , " I will only marry the man 
 I respect . " thus it be her respect for Guy Darrell that make her honour 
 he by her preference . she appreciate his intellect - she fall in love 
 with the reputation which the intellect have acquire . and Darrell might 
 certainly choose bad . his cool reason incline he much to Honoria . 
 when Alban Morley argue in her favour , he have no escape from 
 acquiescence , except in the turn and double of his ironical humour . 
 but his heart be a rebel to his reason ; and , between you and I , 
 Honoria be exactly one of those young woman by whom a man of grave 
 year ought to be attract , and by whom , somehow or other , he never be ; 
 I suspect , because the old we grow the more we love youthfulness of 
 character . when Alcides , have go through all the fatigue of life , 
 take a bride in Olympus , he ought to have select Minerva , but he choose 
 Hebe . 

 will Darrell find his Hebe in Flora Vyvyan ? Alban Morley become more and 
 more alarmed by the apprehension . he be shrewd enough to recognise in 
 she the girl of all other form to glad the eye and plague the heart 
 of a grave and reverend seigneur . and it might well not only flatter the 
 vanity , but beguile the judgment , of a man who fear his hand would be 
 accept only for the sake of his money , that Flora just at this moment 
 refuse the great match in the kingdom , young Lord Vipont , son of 
 the new Earl of Montfort , a young man of good sense , high character , 
 well - look as man go - heir to estate almost royal ; a young man whom 
 no girl on earth be justify in refuse . but would the whimsical 
 creature accept Darrell ? be she not merely make sport of he , and if , 
 catch by her art , he , sage and eld , solemnly offer homage and 
 hand to that belle dedaigneuse who have just doom to despair a comely 
 young magnet with five time his fortune , would she not hasten to make 
 hirer the ridicule of London . 

 Darrell have perhaps his secret reason for think otherwise , but 
 he do not confide they even to Alban Morley . this much only will the 
 narrator , more candid , say to the reader : if out of the three whom his 
 thought flutter round , Guy Darrell wish to select the one who would 
 love he well - love he with the whole fresh unreasone heart of a girl 
 whose childish forwardness spring from childlike innocence , let he dare 
 the hazard of refusal and of ridicule ; let he say to Flora Vyvyan , in 
 the pathos of his sweet deep voice : " come and be the spoiled darling of 
 my gladdened age ; let my life , ere it sink into night , be rejoice by 
 the bloom and fresh breeze of the morning . " 

 but to say it he must wish it ; he himself must love - love with all 
 the lavish indulgence , all the knightly tenderness , all the grateful 
 sympathise joy in the youth of the beloved , when youth for the lover 
 be no more , which alone can realise what we sometimes see , though loth 
 to own it - congenial union with unequal year . if Darrell feel not that 
 love , woe to he , woe and thrice shame if he allure to his hearth one 
 who might indeed be a Hebe to the spouse who give up to she his whole 
 heart in return for her ; but to the spouse who have no heart to give , 
 or give but the chip of it , the Hebe indignant would be bad than 
 Erinnys ! 

 all thing consider , then , they who wish well to Guy Darrell 
 must range with Alban Morley in favour of Miss Honoria Vipont . she , 
 proffer affectionate respect - darrell respond by rational esteem . 
 so , perhaps , Darrell himself think , for whenever Miss Vipont be 
 name he become more taciturn , more absorb in reflection , and sigh 
 heavily , like a man who slowly make up his mind to a decision , wise , 
 but not tempting . 




 CHAPTER VII . 

  contain MUCH of that information which the WISEST MEN in the 
  world could not give , but which the AUTHOR CAN . 

 " Darrell , " say Colonel Morley , " you remember my nephew George as a 
 boy ? he be now the rector of Humberston ; married - a very nice sort of 
 woman - suit he Humberston be a fine living ; but his talent be waste 
 there . he preach for the first time in London last year , and make a 
 considerable sensation . this year he have be much out of town . he have 
 no church here as yet . 

 " I hope to get he one . Carr be determined that he shall be a Bishop . 
 meanwhile he preach at - Chapel tomorrow ; come and hear he with I , 
 and then tell I frankly - be he eloquent or not ? " 

 Darrell have a prejudice against fashionable preacher ; but to please 
 Colonel Morley he go to hear George . he be agreeably surprise by 
 the pulpit oratory of the young divine . it have that rare combination of 
 impassione earnestness with subdue tone , and decorous gesture , which 
 suit the ideal of ecclesiastical eloquence conceive by an educate 
 English Churchman 

  " Strong without rage , without o'erflowe full . " 

 occasionally the old defect in utterance be discernible ; there be 
 a gasp as for breath , or a prolong dwelling upon certain syllable , 
 which , occur in the most animate passage , and apparently evince 
 the preacher 's struggle with emotion , rather serve to heighten the 
 sympathy of the audience . but , for the most part , the original stammer 
 be replace by a felicitous pause , the pause as of a thoughtful 
 reasoner or a solemn monitor knitting idea , that come too quick , into 
 method , or chasten impulse into discipline zeal . the mind of the 
 preacher , thus not only free from trammel , but armed for victory , come 
 forth with that power which be peculiar to an original intellect - the 
 power which suggest more than it demonstrate . he do not so much 
 preach to his audience as wind himself through unexpected way into the 
 heart of the audience ; and they who hear suddenly find their heart 
 preach to themselves . he take for his text : " cast down , but not 
 destroy ; " and out of this text he frame a discourse full of true 
 Gospel tenderness , which seem to raise up comfort as the saving , 
 against despair as the evil , principle of mortal life . the congregation 
 be what be call " brilliant " - statesman , and peer , and great author , 
 and fine lady - people whom the inconsiderate believe to stand little 
 in need of comfort , and never to be subject to despair . in many an 
 intent or droop farce in that brilliant congregation might be read 
 a very different tale . but of all present there be no one whom the 
 discourse so move as a woman who , chance to pass that way , have 
 follow the throng into the Chapel , and with difficulty obtain a seat 
 at the far end ; a woman who have not be within the wall of a chapel 
 or church for long year - a grim woman , in iron grey . there she sate 
 unnoticed , in her remote corner ; and before the preacher have do , she 
 face be hide behind her clasp hand , and she be weep such tear 
 as she have not weep since childhood . 

 on leave church , Darrell say little more to the Colonel than this : 
 " your nephew take I by surprise . the Church want such man . he will 
 have a grand career , if life be spar to he . " then he sink into a 
 reverie , from which he break abruptly : " your nephew be , at school with 
 my boy . have my son live , what have be his career ? " 

 the Colonel , never encourage painful subject , make no rejoinder . 

 " bring George to see I to - morrow . I shrink from ask it before : I 
 think the sight of he would too much revive old sorrow ; but I feel I 
 should accustom myself to face every memory . bring he . " 

 the next day the Colonel take George to Darrell 's ; but George have be 
 pre - engaged till late at noon , and Darrell be just leave home , and at 
 his street door , when the uncle and nephew come . they respect his time 
 too much to accept his offer to come in , but walk beside he for a few 
 minute , as he bestow upon George those compliment which be sweet to 
 the ear of rise man from the lip of those who have rise . 

 " I remember you , George , as a boy , " say Darrell , " and thank you then 
 for good advice to a schoolfellow , who be lose to your counsel now . " he 
 falter an instant , but go on firmly : " you have then a slight defect 
 in utterance , which , I understand from your uncle , increase as you 
 grow old ; so that I never anticipate for you the fame that you be 
 achieve . orator fit - you must have be admirably teach . in the 
 management of your voice , in the excellence of your delivery , I see 
 that you be one of the few who deem that the Divine Word should not 
 be unworthily utter . the debater on beer bill may be excuse from 
 study the orator 's effect ; but all that enforce , dignify , adorn , 
 make the become study of he who strive by eloquence to people 
 heaven ; whose task it be to adjure the thoughtless , animate the languid , 
 soften the callous , humble the proud , alarm the guilty , comfort the 
 sorrowful , call back to the fold the lost . be the culture to be slovenly 
 where the glebe be so fertile ? the only field leave in modern time for 
 the ancient orator 's sublime conception , but laborious training , be the 
 Preacher 's . and I own , George , that I envy the master who skille to 
 the Preacher 's art an intellect like your . " 

 " master , " say the Colonel . " I think all those elocution master 
 fail with you , George . you cure and teach yourself . do not you ? no ! 
 why , then , who be your teacher ? " 

 George look very much embarrassed , and , attempt to answer , begin 
 horribly to stutter . 

 Darrell , conceive that a preacher whose fame be not yet confirm 
 might reasonably dislike to confess those obligation to elaborate 
 study , which , if know , might detract from his effect or expose he to 
 ridicule , hasten to change the subject . " you have be to the country , 
 I hear , George ; at your living , I suppose ? " 

 " no . I have not be there very lately ; travel about . " 

 " have you see Lady Montfort since your return ? " ask the Colonel . 

 " I only return on Saturday night . I go to Lady Montfort 's at 
 Twickenham , this evening . " 

 " she have a delightful retreat , " say the Colonel . " but if she wish 
 to avoid admiration , she should not make the bank of the river her 
 favourite haunt . I know some romantic admirer , who , when she re - appear 
 in the world , may be rival aspirant , and who have much take to row 
 since Lady Montfort have retire to Twickenham . they catch a glimpse of 
 she , and return to boast of it . but they report that there be a young 
 lady see walk with she an extremely pretty one - who be she ? People 
 ask I - as if I know everything . "

 CHAPTER VIII . 

  be but one of the considerate pause in a long journey , 
  CHARITABLY AFFORDED to the READER . 

 Colonel Morley find Mr. Poole at home , just return from his office ; 
 he stay with that gentleman nearly an hour , and then go straight 
 to Darrell . as the time appoint to meet the french acquaintance , who 
 depend on his hospitality for a dinner , be now nearly arrive , 
 Alban 's conference with his english friend be necessarily brief 
 and hurry , though long enough to confirm one fact in Mr. Poole 's 
 statement , which have be unknown to the Colonel before that day , and 
 the admission of which inflict on Guy Darrell a pang as sharp as ever 
 wrench confession from the lip of a prisoner in the cell of the 
 Inquisition . on return from Greenwich , and deposit his Frenchman 
 in some melancholy theatre , time enough for that resentful foreigner 
 to witness theft and murder commit upon an injure countryman 's 
 vaudeville , Alban hasten again to Carlton Gardens . he find Darrell 
 alone , pace his floor to and fro , in the habit he have acquire in 
 early life , perhaps when meditate some complicated law case , or 
 wrestle with himself against some secret sorrow . there be man of 
 quick nerve who require a certain action of the body for the well 
 composure of the mind ; Darrell be one of they . 

 during these restless movement , alternate by abrupt pause , equally 
 inharmonious to the supreme quiet which characterise his listener 's 
 taste and habit , the haughty gentleman disburden himself of at least 
 one of the secret which he have hitherto guard from his early friend . 
 but as that secret connect itself with the history of a Person about 
 whom it be well that the reader should now learn more than be know 
 to Darrell himself , we will assume our privilege to be ourselves the 
 narrator , and at the cost of such dramatic vivacity as may belong to 
 dialogue , but with the gain to the reader of clear insight into those 
 portion of the past which the occasion permit we to reveal - we will 
 weave into something like method the more imperfect and desultory 
 communication by which Guy Darrell add to Alban Morley 's distasteful 
 catalogue of painful subject . the reader will allow , perhaps , that we 
 thus evince a desire to gratify his curiosity , when we state that of 
 Arabella Crane Darrell speak but in one brief and angry sentence , and 
 that not by the name in which the reader as yet alone know she ; and 
 it be with the antecedent of Arabella Crane that our explanation will 
 tranquilly commence . 




 CHAPTER IX . 

  GRIM ARABELLA CRANE . 

 once on a time there live a merchant name Fossett , a widower with 
 three child , of whom a daughter , Arabella , be by some year the 
 eld . he be much respect , deem a warm man , and a safe - attend 
 diligently to his business - suffer no partner , no foreman , to dictate 
 or intermeddle - like his comfort , but make no pretence to fashion . his 
 villa be at Clapham , not a showy but a solid edifice , with lodge , lawn , 
 and garden chiefly notable for what be technically call glass - viz . 
 a range of glass - house on the most improved principle , the heavy 
 pine , the early strawberry . " I be no judge of flower , " quoth Mr. 
 Fossett , meekly . " give I a plain lawn , provide it be close - shaven . but 
 I say to my gardener : ' force be my hobby - a cucumber with my fish all 
 the year round ! ' " yet do not suppose Mr Fossett ostentatious - quite the 
 reverse . he would no more ruin himself for the sake of dazzle other , 
 than he would for the sake of serve they . he like a warm house , 
 spacious room , good living , old wine , for their inherent merit : he 
 care not to parade they to public envy . when he din alone , or with 
 a single favour guess , the good Lafitte , the old sherry ! - when 
 extend the rite of miscellaneous hospitality to neighbour , 
 relation , or other slight acquaintance - for Lafitte , Julien ; and 
 for sherry , Cape ! - thus not provoke vanity , nor court notice , Mr. 
 Fossett be without an enemy , and seem without a care . Formal be his 
 manner , formal his household , formal even the stout cob that bear he 
 from Cheapside to Clapham , from Claphain to Cheapside . that cob could 
 not even prick up its ear if it wish to shy - its ear be crop , 
 so be its mane and its tail . 

 Arabella early give promise of beauty , and more than ordinary power of 
 intellect and character . her father be stow on her every advantage 
 of education . she be send to a select boarding - school of the high 
 reputation ; the strict discipline , the good master , the long 
 bill . at the age of seventeen she have become the show pupil of the 
 seminary . friend wonder somewhat why the prim merchant take such 
 pain to lavish on his daughter the worldly accomplishment which seem 
 to give he no pleasure , and of which he never speak with pride . but 
 certainly , if she be so clever - first - rate musician , exquisite artist , 
 accomplished linguist , " it be very nice in old Fossett to bear it 
 so meekly , never cry she up , nor show she off to less fortunate 
 parent - very nice in he - good sense - greatness of mind . " 

 " Arabella , " say the worthy man one day , a little time after his eld 
 daughter have leave school for good ; " Arabella , " say he , " Mrs. - - - - , " 
 name the head teacher in that famous school , " pay you a very high 
 compliment in a letter I receive from she this morning . she say it be 
 a pity you be not a poor man 's daughter - that you be so steady and so 
 clever that you could make a fortune for yourself as a teacher . " 

 Arabella at that age could smile gaily , and gaily she smile at the 
 notion convey in the compliment . 

 " no one can guess , " resume the father , twirl his thumb and speak 
 rather through his nose ; " the up and down in this mortal sphere of 
 trial , ' specially in the mercantile community . if ever , when I be dead 
 and go , adversity should come upon you , you will gratefully remember 
 that I have give you the good of education , and take care of your 
 little brother and sister , who be both - stupid ! " 

 these doleful word do not make much impression on Arabella , utter as 
 they be in a handsome drawing - room , open on the neat - shaven lawn 
 it take three gardener to shave , with a glitter side - view of those 
 gallery of glass in which strawberry be ripe at Christmas , 
 and cucumber never fail to fish . time - go on . Arabella be now 
 twenty - three - a very fine girl , with a decide manner - much occupy by 
 her music , her drawing , her book , and her fancy . fancy - for , like 
 most girl with very active head and idle heart , she have a vague 
 yearning for some excitement beyond the monotonous routine of a young 
 lady 's life ; and the latent force of her nature incline she to admire 
 whatever be out of the beat track - whatever be wild and daring . 
 she have receive two or three offer from young gentleman in the same 
 mercantile community as that which surround her father in this sphere 
 of trial . but they do not please she ; and she believe her father when 
 he say that they only court she under the idea that he would come 
 down with something handsome ; " whereas , " say the merchant , " I hope you 
 will marry an honest man , who will like you for yourself ; and wait for 
 your fortune till my will be read . as King William say to his son , in 
 the history of England , ' I do not   mean to strip till I go to bed . ' " 

 one night , at a ball in Clapham , Arabella see the man who be destine 
 to exercise so baleful an influence over her existence . Jasper Losely 
 have be bring to this ball by a young fellow - clerk in the same 
 commercial house as himself ; and then in all the bloom of that 
 conspicuous beauty , to which the miniature Arabella have place before 
 his eye so many year afterwards do but feeble justice , it may well 
 be conceive that he concentre on himself the admire gaze of the 
 assembly . Jasper be young than Arabella ; but , what with the height of 
 his stature and the self - confidence of his air , he look four or five 
 and twenty . certainly , in so far as the distance from childhood may be 
 estimate by the loss of innocence , Jasper might have be any age ! he 
 be tell that old Fossett 's daughter would have a very fine fortune ; 
 that she be a strong - minded young lady , who govern her father , and 
 would choose for herself ; and accordingly he devote himself to Arabella 
 the whole of the evening . the effect produce on the mind of this 
 ill - fat woman by her dazzle admirer be as sudden as it prove to 
 be last . there be a strange charm in the very contrast between his 
 rattle audacity and the bashful formality of the swain who have 
 hitherto woo she as if she frighten they . even his good look 
 fascinate she less than that vital energy and power about the lawless 
 brute , which to she seem the element of heroic character , though 
 but the attribute of riotous spirit , magnificent formation , flattered 
 vanity , and imperious egotism . she be a bird gaze spell - bind on 
 a gay young boa - constrictor , dart from bough to bough , sun its 
 brilliant hue , and show off all its beauty , just before it take the 
 bird for its breakfast . 

 when they part that night , their intimacy have so far advanced 
 that arrangement have be make for its continuance . Arabella have 
 an instinctive foreboding that her father would be less charmed than 
 herself with Jasper Losely ; that , if Jasper be present to he , 
 he would possibly forbid she farth acquaintance with a young clerk , 
 however superb his outward appearance . she take the first false step . 
 she have a maiden aunt by the mother 's side , who live in Bloomsbury , 
 give and go to small party , to which Jasper could easily get 
 introduce . she arrange to pay a visit for some week to this aunt , who 
 be then very civil to she , accept with mark kindness seasonable 
 present of strawberry , pine , spring chicken , and so forth , and 
 offering in turn , whenever it be convenient , a spare room , and whatever 
 amusement a round of small party , and the innocent flirtation 
 incidental thereto , could bestow . Arabella say nothing to her father 
 about Jasper Losely , and to her aunt 's she go . Arabella see Jasper 
 very often ; they become engaged to each other , exchange vow and 
 love - token , lock of hair ,   c. Jasper , already much trouble by dun , 
 become naturally ardent to insure his felicity and Arabella 's suppose 
 fortune . arabella at last summon courage , and speak to her father . to 
 her delighted surprise , Mr. Fossett , after some moralising , more on 
 the uncertainty of life in general than her clandestine proceeding 
 in particular , agree to see Mr. Jasper Losely , and ask he down to 
 dinner . after dinner , over ' a bottle of Lafitte , in an exceedingly plain 
 but exceedingly weighty silver jug , which make Jasper 's mouth water   I 
 mean the jug , Mr. Fossett , commence with that somewhat coarse though 
 royal saying of William the Conqueror , with which he have before edify 
 his daughter , assure Jasper that he give his full consent to the young 
 gentleman 's nuptial with Arabella , provide Jasper or his relation

 day , " - the trinket wear at the first ball , the ring that be give

 soon the sum thus miserably raise be as miserably dole away . with a 
 sullen apathy the woman contemplate famine . she would make no effort to 
 live - appeal to no relation , no friend . it be a kind of vengeance she

 she could no long pay rent for the humblest room ; now , she be tell 
 to go forth - whither ? she know not - care not - take her way towards 
 the River , as by that instinct which , when the mind be disease , tend 
 towards self - destruction , scarce less involuntarily than it turn , 
 in health , towards self - preservation . just as she pass under the 
 lamp - light at the foot of Westminster Bridge , a man look at she , and 
 seize her arm . she raise her head with a chilly , melancholy scorn , as 
 if she have receive an insult - as if she fear that the man know the 
 stain upon her name , and dream , in his folly , that the dread of death 
 might cause she to sin again . 

 " do you not know I ? " say the man ; " more strange that I should 
 recognise you ! dear , dear , and what a dress ! - how you be alter ! poor 
 thing ! " 

 at the word " poor thing " Arabella burst into tear ; and in those tear 
 the heavy cloud on her brain seem to melt away . 

 " I have be inquire , seek for you everywhere , Miss , " resume 
 the man . " surely , you know I now ! your poor aunt 's lawyer ! she be no 
 more - die last week . she have leave you all she have in the world ; and a 
 very pretty income it be , too , for a single lady . " 

 thus it be that we find Arabella instal in the dreary comfort

 memory , her own name for that of Crane , which her aunt have bear - her 
 own mother 's maiden name . " she assume , though still so young , that 
 title of " Mrs. " which spinster , grow venerable , moodily adopt when 
 they desire all mankind to know that henceforth they relinquish the 
 vanity of tender miss - that , become mistress of themselves , they 
 defy and spit upon our worthless sex , which , whatever its repentance , 
 be warn that it repent in vain . Most of her aunt 's property be in 
 house , in various district of Bloombury . Arabella move from one to

 of all . to make it dull yet , by contrast with the past , the Golgotha 
 for once give up its bury treasure - break lute , birdless cage ! 

 somewhere about two year after Matilda 's death , Arabella happen to 
 be in the office of the agent who collect her house - rent , when a 
 well - dress man enter , and , lean over the counter , say : " there 
 be an advertisement in to - day 's Times about a lady who offer a home , 
 education , and so forth , to any little motherless girl ; term moderate , 
 as say lady love child for their own sake . advertiser refer to 
 your office for particular - give they ! " 

 the agent turn to his book ; and Arabella turn towards the inquirer . 
 " for whose child do you want a home , Jasper Losely ? " 

 Jasper start . " Arabella ! good of creature ! and can you deign to speak 
 to such a vil - - " 

 " Hush - let we walk . never mind the advertisement of a stranger . I may 
 find a home for a motherless child - a home that will cost you nothing . " 

 she draw he into the street . " but can this be the child of - of - Matilda 
 Darrell ? " - 

 " Bella ! " reply , in coax accent , that most execrable of 
 lady - killers , " can I trust you ? - can you be my friend in spite of my

 " yes ; I read of Mrs. Hammond 's death in an old newspaper . " 

 " and you do not read of her baby 's death , too - some week afterwards ? " ' 

 " no ; it be seldom that I see a newspaper . be the infant dead ? " 

 " Hum - you shall hear . " and Jasper enter into a recital , to which

 and now the reader know more than Alban Morley , or Guy Darrell , perhaps

 CHAPTER X. 

  " Sweet be the us of Adversity , 
  which , like the toad , ugly and venomous , 
  bear yet a precious jewel in its head . " 

  MOST person will agree that the TOAD be UGLY and VENOMOUS , but few 
  indeed be the person who can BOAST of have ACTUALLY discover 
  that " precious jewel in its head , " which the poet assures US be 
  place there . but CALAMITY may be classed in two great division - 
  1st , the affliction , which no prudence can avert ; 2nd , the 
  MISFORTUNES , which MEN take all possible pains to bring upon 
  themselves . affliction of the first class MAY but CALL forth OUR 
  VIRTUES , and result in our ultimate good . SUCH be the ADVERSITY 
  which may give US the JEWEL . but to get at the jewel we must kill 
  the TOAD . MISFORTUNES of the second class but TOO often increase 
  the errors or the vices by which they be create . SUCH be the 
  ADVERSITY which be all TOAD and no JEWEL . if you choose to BREED 
  and FATTEN your OWN TOADS , the increase of the venom absorbs every 
  BIT of the JEWEL . 

 never do I know a man who be an habitual gambler , otherwise than 
 notably inaccurate in his calculation of probability in the ordinary 
 affair of life . be it that such a man have become so chronic a drunkard 
 of hope , that he see double every chance in his favour ? 

 Jasper Losely have count upon two thing as matter of course . 

 1st . Darrell 's speedy reconciliation with his only child . 2nd . that 
 Darrell 's only child must of necessity be Darrell 's heiress . 

 in both these expectation the gambler be deceive . Darrell do not 
 even answer the letter that Matilda address to he from France , to 
 the shore of which Jasper have bear she , and where he have hasten 
 to make she his wife under the assumed name of Hammond , but his true 
 christian name of Jasper . 

 in the disreputable marriage Matilda have make , all the bad part of 
 her character seem suddenly reveal to her father 's eye , and he see 
 what he have hitherto seek not to see , the true child of a worthless 
 mother . a mere mesalliance , if palliate by long or familiar 
 acquaintance with the object , however it might have gall he , his 
 heart might have pardon ; but here , without even a struggle of duty , 
 without the ordinary coyness of maiden pride , to be win with so scanty 
 a wooing , by a man who she know be betroth to another - the 
 dissimulation , the perfidy , the combined effrontery and meanness of the 
 whole transaction , leave no force in Darrell 's eye to the common place 
 excuse of experience and youth . Darrell would not have be Darrell if 
 he could have take back to his home or his heart a daughter so old in 
 deceit , so experienced in thought that dishonour . 

 Darrell 's silence , however , little sadden the heartless bride , and 
 little dismay the sanguine bridegroom . both think that pardon and 
 plenty be but the affair of time a little more or little less . but 
 their fund rapidly diminish ; it become necessary to recruit they . one 
 can not   live in hotel entirely upon hope . leave his bride for a while 
 in a pleasant provincial town , not many hour distant from Paris , Jasper 
 return to London , intent upon see Darrell himself ; and , should the 
 father - in - law still defer article of peace , Jasper believe that he 
 could have no trouble in raise a present supply upon such an El Dorado 
 of future expectation . darrell at once consent to see Jasper , not at 
 his own house , but at his solicitor 's . smother all oppose disgust , 
 the proud gentleman deem this condescension essential to the clear and 
 definite understanding of those resolve upon which depend the worldly 
 station and prospect of the wed pair . 

 when Jasper be show into Mr. Gotobed 's office , Darrell be alone , 
 stand near the hearth , and by a single quiet gesture repel that 
 tender rush towards his breast which Jasper have elaborately prepare ;

 perhaps yet more resentfully mortified while recognise those personal 
 advantage in the showy profligate which have render a daughter of his 
 house so facile a conquest : jasper   who have choose to believe that a 
 father - in - law so eminent must necessarily be old and broken   shock 
 into the most disagreeable surprise by the sight of a man still young , 
 under forty , with a countenance , a port , a presence , that in any 
 assemblage would have attract the general gaze from his own brilliant 
 self , and look altogether as unfavourable an object , whether for 
 pathos or for post - obit , as unlikely to breathe out a blessing or to 
 give up the ghost , as the bad brute of a father - in - law could possibly 
 be . nor be Darrell 's word more comforting than his aspect . 

 " Sir , I have consent to see you , partly that you may learn from my own 
 lip once for all that I admit no man 's right to enter my family without 
 my consent , and that consent you will never receive ; and partly that , 
 thus know each other by sight , each may know the man it become 
 he most to avoid . the lady who be now your wife be entitle by my 
 marriage - settlement to the reversion of a small fortune at my death ; 
 nothing more from I be she likely to inherit . as I have no desire that 
 she to whom I once give the name of daughter should be dependent wholly 
 on yourself for bread , my solicitor will inform you on what condition 
 I be willing , during my life , to pay the interest of the sum which will 
 pass to your wife at my death . Sir , I return to your hand the letter 
 that lady have address to I , and which , it be easy to perceive , be 
 write at your dictation . no letter from she will I answer . across my 
 threshold her foot will never pass . thus , sir , conclude all possible 
 intercourse between you and myself ; what rest be between you and that 
 gentleman . " 

 Darrell have open a side - door in speak the last word - point 
 towards the respectable form of Mr. Gotobed stand tall beside his 
 tall desk - and , before Jasper could put in a word , the father - in - law be 
 go . 

 with become brevity , Mr. Gotobed make Jasper fully aware that not only 
 all , Mr. Darrell 's funded or personal property be entirely at his own 
 disposal - that not only the large landed estate he have purchase   and 
 which Jasper have vaguely deem inherit and in strict entail   be in

 the bridegroom of the proprietor 's sole daughter ; but that even the

 in the absence of marriage - settlement between Jasper and Matilda , that

 his wife , so as to leave no certainty that provision would remain for

 all . 

 " Sir , " say the lawyer , " I will be quite frank with you . it be my wish ,

 by a private verbal arrangement , not even commit to writing , to

 scandalise to the last nerve of his respectable system by the

 Darrell , on return from one of the occasional ramble on the 
 Continent , " remote , unfriended , melancholy , " by which he break the

 one day , somewhat more than five year after Matilda 's death , Darrell ,

 this stranger be William Losely , return from penal exile ; and while

 in proportion to the delicacy , the earnestness , the depth of a man 's 
 nature , will there be a something in his character which no male friend

 CHAPTER XI . 

  our old FRIEND the pocket - cannibal evince unexpected patriotism and

 Colonel Morley have a second interview with Mr. Poole . it need not 
 Alban 's knowledge of the world to discover that Poole be no partial

 remote from Great Britain in which a spirit hitherto so restless might 
 consent to settle . and although Mr. Poole have evidently take offence

 Poole wait Jasper 's next visit with impatience and sanguine delight .

 Sophy 's parentage , and that there be nothing in England so peculiarly

 expatriate himself merely for the sake of living . 

 " I be not so young as I be , " say the bravo ; " I do not   speak of year , 
 but feel . I have not the same energy ; once I have high spirit - they 
 be break ; once I have hope - I have none : I be not up to exertion ; I 
 have get into lazy habit . to go into new scene , form new plan , live

 mind to it ; and to be blow off to the antipode as if I be the down 
 of a pestilent thistle , I be not in the mind for that , dolly Poole ! " 

 " Hum ! " say Poole , try to smile . " this be funny talk . you always 
 be a funny fellow . but I be quite sure , from Colonel Morley 's decide 
 manner , that you can get nothing from Darrell if you choose to remain in

 " well , when I have nothing else leave , I may go to Darrell myself , and

 carnivorous animal , yawn - yawn much as a bored tiger do in the

 Gardens .

 " but I should have think no subject could bore you less than the 
 consideration of how you be to live ? " 

 " why , dolly , I have learn to be easily content , and you see at 
 present I live upon you . " 

 " yes , " groan Poole , " but that can not   go on for ever ; and , besides , you 
 promise that you would leave I in peace as soon as I have get Darrell 
 to provide for you . " 

 " so I will . Zounds , sir , do you doubt my word ? so I will . but I do not 
 call exile ' a provision ' - Basta ! I understand from you that Colonel 
 Morley offer to restore the niggardly l200 a year Darrell formerly 
 allow to I , to be pay monthly or weekly , through some agent in Van 
 Diemen 's land , or some such uncomfortable half - way house to Eternity , 
 that be not even in the Atlas when I study geography at school . but 
 l200 a year be exactly my income in England , pay weekly too , by your 
 agreeable self , with whom it be a pleasure to talk over old time . 
 therefore that proposal be out of the question . tell Colonel Morley , 
 with my compliment , that if he will double the sum , and leave I to 
 spend it where I please , I scorn haggle , and say ' do . ' and as to the 
 girl , since I can not find she   which , on penalty of be thresh to 
 a mummy , you will take care not to let out , I would agree to leave Mr. 
 Darrell free to disown she . but be you such a dolt as not to see that I 
 put the ace of trump on my adversary 's pitiful deuce , if I depose 
 that my own child be not my own child , when all I get for it be what 
 I equally get out of you , with my ace of trump still in my hand ? 
 Basta ! - I say again Basta ! it be evidently an object to Darrell to get

 Poole have the chagrin to report to the Colonel , Jasper 's refusal of the

 Jasper , after a moment 's moody deliberation , decline to meet Colonel

 father - in - law , and in person . " 

 " yes , hang it ! " cry Poole , exasperate ; " go and see Darrell yourself . 
 he be easily find . " 

 " ay , " answer Jasper , with the hard look of his downcast sidelong 
 eye - " Ay ; some day or other it may come to that . I would rather not , if 
 possible . I might not keep my temper . it be not merely a matter of money

 darrell fifty year ago ; and what if my father be bear out of wedlock ,

 you see I five year ago ; but as I leapt into youth ! be I bear to cast 
 sum and nib pen as a City clerk ? aha , my poor father , you be wrong 
 there ! blood will out ! mad devil , indeed , be a racer in a citizen 's gig ! 
 Spavined , and wind - gall , and foundered - let the brute go at last to 
 the knocker ; but by his eye , and his pluck , and his bone , the brute 
 show the stock that he come from ! " 

 dolly open his eye and - blink . never in his gaudy day have Jasper 
 half so openly reveal what , perhaps , have be always a sore in his 
 pride ; and his outburst now may possibly aid the reader to a subtler 
 comprehension of the arrogance , and levity , and egotism , which 
 accompany his insensibility to honour , and have convert his very 
 claim to the blood of a gentleman into an excuse for a cynic 's disdain

 " Humph ! " say Poole , after a pause . " if Darrell be as uncivil to you as 
 he be to I , I do not   wonder that you owe he a grudge . but even if you 
 do lose temper in see he , it might rather do good than not . you can 
 make yourself cursedly unpleasant if you choose it ; and perhaps you will 
 have a well chance of get your own term if they see you can bite

 " dolly , do not   bluster ! if I could stand at his door , or stop he in the

 son - in - law . but an interesting young lady , who call he grandfather ,

 " but I can not   find the girl , and you know it . and I tell you what , Mr. 
 Losely , Colonel Morley , who be a very shrewd man , do not believe in

 " do not he ! I begin to doubt it myself . but , at all event , you can not 
 doubt of mine , and I be grateful for your ; and since you have give I

 CHAPTER XII . 

  another HALT - change of horse - and a turn on the road . 

 Colonel Morley , on learn that Jasper decline a personal conference 
 with himself , and that the proposal of an interview with Jasper 's

 sharp be thus unprovide with a means of annoyance , which , skilfully

 peremptorily and irrevocably withdraw . I myself , " add the Colonel ,

 secure he from want , I refer he to Mr. Darrell 's solicitor . to that 
 proposal , accord to your account of his destitution , he must come

 miserable Poole . 

 in the brief word , Alban inform Darrell of his persuasion that 
 Jasper be not only without evidence to support a daughter 's claim , 
 but that the daughter herself be still in that part of Virgil 's Hades 
 appropriate to soul that have not yet appear upon the upper earth ; 
 and that Jasper himself , although hold back , as might be naturally 
 expect , in the hope of condition more to his taste , have only to be 
 leave quietly to his own meditation in order to recognise the advantage 
 of emigration . another l100 a - year or so , it be true , he might bargain 
 for , and such a demand might be worth concede . but , on the whole , 
 Alban congratulate Darrell upon the probability of hear very little 
 more of the son - in - law , and no more at all of the son - in - law 's daughter . 

 Darrell make no comment nor reply . a grateful look , a warm pressure of 
 the hand , and , when the subject be change , a clear brow and livelier 
 smile , thank the English Alban well than all word . 




 CHAPTER XIII . 

  COLONEL MORLEY show that it be not without reason that he enjoy 
  his reputation of know something about EVERYBODY . 

 " well meet , " say Darrell , the day after Alban have convey to he the

 " do you speak of Frank Vance ? " 

 " the same . " 

 " you could not visit a worthy man , nor compliment a more promising

 " you do not   say so . the Branthwaites be of good old family ; what be Mr. 
 Vance 's ? " 

 " respectable enough . Vance 's father be one of those clever man who have

 apiece for they . " 

 " that be the true soul in which genius lodge , and out of which fire

 " no ; while Vance be still a boy . Young Arthur Branthwaite be an

 see Mary Vance a little before her father die ; fall in love with she ;

 " what ! - below contempt ? " 

 " on the contrary , above comprehension ! all poetry praise by critic

 CHAPTER XIV . 

  ROMANTIC LOVE PATHOLOGICALLY REGARDED BY FRANK VANCE and ALBAN 
  MORLEY . 

 Vance be before his easel , Lionel look over his shoulder . never be 
 darrell more genial than he be that day to Frank Vance . the two man 
 take to each other at once , and talk as familiarly as if the retire

 ideal have be suggest - the portrait of Sophy as Titania .

 Bottom .

 Sophy we have fail to find , sir - be it not a lovely face ? "

 DARRELL   somewhat gravely . - " a hazardous remedy - it might kill , if it 
 do not cure . " 

 COLONEL MORLEY . - " I suspect , from Vance 's manner , that he have test its 
 efficacy on his own person . " 

 LIONEL . - " no , mon Colonel - I will answer for Vance . he in love ! never . " 

 vance colour - give a touch to the nose of a roman senator in the 
 famous classical picture which he be then paint for a merchant at 
 Manchester - and make no reply . Darrell look at the artist with a sharp 
 and search glance . 

 COLONEL MORLEY . - " then all the more credit to Vance for his intuitive 
 perception of philosophical truth . suppose , my dear Lionel , that we 
 light , one idle day , on a beautiful novel , a glow romance - suppose 
 that , by chance , we be tear from the book in the middle of the 
 interest - we remain under the spell of the illusion - we recall the 
 scene - we try to guess what should have be the sequel - we think that 
 no romance ever be so captivating , simply because we be not allow 
 to conclude it . well , if , some year afterwards , the romance fall again 
 in our way , and we open at the page where we leave off , we cry , in the 
 maturity of our sober judgment , ' mawkish stuff ! - be this the same thing 
 that I once think so beautiful ? - how one 's taste do alter ! ' " 

 DARRELL . - " do it not depend on the age in which one begin the 
 romance ? " 

 lionel . - " rather , let I think , sir , upon the real depth of the 
 interest - the true beauty of the - " 

 VANCE   interrupting . - " Heroine ? - not at all , Lionel . I once fall in 
 love - incredible as it may seem to you - nine year ago last January . I 
 be too poor then to aspire to any young lady 's hand - therefore I do 
 not tell my love , but ' let concealment , ' et cetera , et cetera . she 
 go away with her mamma to complete her education on the continent . I 
 remain ' patience on a monument . ' she be always before my eye - the 
 slender , shyest creature just eighteen . I never have an idea that 
 she could grow any old , less slender , or less shy . well , four year 
 afterwards   just before we make our excursion into Surrey , Lionel , she 
 return to England , still unmarried . I go to a party at which I know 
 she be to be - see she , and be cure . " 

 " bad case of small - pox , or what ? " ask the Colonel , smile . 

 vance - " Nay ; everybody say she be extremely improved - that be the 
 mischief - she have improve herself out of my fancy . I have be faithful 
 as wax to one settle impression , and when I see a fine , full - form , 
 young Frenchified lady , quite at her ease , arm with eyeglass and 
 bouquet and bustle , away go my dream of the slim blushing maiden . 
 the Colonel be quite right , Lionel ; the romance once suspend , ' tis 
 a haunt remembrance till throw again in our way , but complete 
 disillusion if we try to renew it ; though I swear that in my case the 
 interest be deep , and the heroine improve in her beauty . so with you 
 and that dear little creature . see she again , and you will tease , I no 
 more to give you that portrait of Titania at watch over Bottom 's soft 
 slumber . all a Midsummer Night 's Dream , Lionel . Titania fade back into 
 the arm of Oberon , and would not be Titania if you could make her - Mrs. 
 Bottom . " 




 CHAPTER XV . 

  even COLONEL MORLEY ,   know EVERYBODY and everything , be puzzled 
  when it come to the plain question - " what will he do with it ? " 

 " I be delighted with Vance , " say Darrell , when he and the Colonel be 
 again walk arm - in - arm . " his be not one of those meagre intellect 
 which have nothing to spare out of the professional line . he have humour . 
 humour - strength 's rich superfluity . " 

 " I like your definition , " say the Colonel . " and humour in Vance , though 
 fantastic , be not without subtlety . there be much real kindness in his 
 obvious design to quiz Lionel out of that silly enthusiasm for - "

 enthusiasm - it would be degradation . but do you believe , then , that 
 Vance 's confession of first love , and its cure , be but a whimsical 
 invention ? " 

 COLONEL MORLEY . - " not so . many a grave truth be speak jestingly . I 
 have no doubt that , allow for the pardonable exaggeration of a 
  raconteur , Vance be narrate an episode in his own life . " 

 DARRELL . - " do you think that a grown man , who have ever really feel love , 
 can make a jest of it , and to mere acquaintance ? " 

 COLONEL MORLEY . - " yes ; if he be so thoroughly cure , that he have make a 
 jest of it to himself . and the more lightly he speak of it , perhaps

 passion that fool he . " 

 DARRELL . - " you be evidently an experienced philosopher in the lore of 
 such folly . ' consulta insapientis sapientiae . ' yet I can scarcely 
 believe that you have ever be in love . " 

 " yes , I have , " say the Colonel bluntly , " and very often ! everybody 
 at my age have - except yourself . so like a man 's observation , that , " 
 continue the Colonel with much tartness . " no man ever think another 
 man capable of a profound and romantic sentiment ! " 

 DARRELL . - " true ; I own my shallow fault , and beg you ten thousand 
 pardon . so then you really believe , from your own experience , that 
 there be much in Vance 's theory and your own very happy illustration ? 
 could we , after many year , turn back to the romance at the page at 
 which we leave off , we should - " 

 COLONEL MORLEY . - " not care a straw to read on ! certainly , half the 
 peculiar charm of a person beloved must be ascribe to locality and 
 circumstance . " 

 DARRELL . - " I do not   quite understand you . " 

 COLONEL MORLEY . - " then , as you like my former illustration , I will 
 explain myself by another one more homely . in a room to which you be 
 accustomed there be a piece of furniture , or an ornament , which so 
 exactly suit the place that you say : ' the prettiest thing I ever see ! ' 
 you go away - you return - the piece of furniture or the ornament have be 
 move into another room . you see it there , and you say : ' bless I , 
 be that the thing I so much admire ! ' the strange room do not suit 
 it - lose its old association and accessory , it have lose its charm . 
 so it be with human being - see in one place , the place would be 
 nothing without they ; see in another , the place without they would be 
 all the well ! " 

 DARRELL   musingly   - " there be some puzzle in life which resemble the 
 riddle a child ask you to solve . your imagination can not descend low 
 enough for the right guess . yet , when you be tell , you be oblige to 
 say , ' how clever ! ' man live to learn . " 

 " since you have arrive at that conviction , " reply Colonel Morley , 
 amuse by his friend 's gravity , " I hope that you will rest satisfied 
 with the experience of Vance and myself ; and that if you have a mind 
 to propose to one of the young lady whose merit we have already 
 discuss , you will not deem it necessary to try what effect a prolonged 
 absence might produce on your good resolution . " 

 " no ! " say Darrell , with sudden animation . " before three day be over 
 my mind shall be make up . " " Bravo ! - as to whom of the three you would 
 ask in marriage ? " 

 " or as to the idea of ever marry again . Adieu , I be go to knock at 
 that door . " 

 " Mr. Vyvyan 's ! ah , be it so , indeed ? verily , you be a true Dare - all . "

 and I dine with the Carr Viponts . my choice be not yet make , and my hand 
 still free . " 

 " his hand still free ! " mutter the Colonel , pursue his walk alone . 
 " yes - but three day hence - o - what will he do with it ? " 




 CHAPTER XVI . 

  GUY DARRELL 's decision . 

 Guy Darrell return home from Carr Vipont 's dinner at a late hour . 
 on his table be a note from Lady Adela 's father , cordially invite 
 Darrell to pass the next week at his country - house ; London be now 
 empty fast . on the table too be a parcel , contain a book which 
 Darrell have lend to Miss Vyvyan some week ago , and a note from herself . 
 in call at her father 's house that morning , he have learn that Mr. 
 Vyvyan have suddenly resolve to take she into Switzerland , with the view 
 of pass the next winter in Italy . the room be fill with lounger 
 of both sex . Darrell have stay but a short time . the leave - taking have 
 be somewhat formal - Flora unusually silent . he open her note , and 
 read the first line listlessly ; those that follow , with a change 
 cheek and an earnest eye . he lay down the note very gently , again take 
 it up and reperuse . then he hold it to the candle , and it drop 
 from his hand in tinder . " the innocent child , " murmur he , with a soft 
 paternal tenderness ; ' she know not what she write . " he begin to pace 
 the room with his habitual restlessness when in solitary thought - often 
 stop - often sigh heavily . at length his face clear - his lip 
 become firmly set . he summon his favourite servant . " mill , " say he , 
 " I shall leave town on horseback as soon as the sun rise . put what I 
 may require for a day or two into the saddle - bag . possibly , however I 
 may be back by dinner - time . call I at five o'clock , and then go round 
 to the stable . I shall require no groom to attend I . " 

 the next morning , while the street be desert , no house as yet 
 astir , but the sun bright , the air fresh , Guy Darrell ride from his 
 door . he do not return the same day , nor the next , nor at all . but , 
 late in the evening of the second day , his horse , reek hot and 
 evidently hard - ridden , stop at the porch of Fawley Manor - House ; and 
 Darrell fling himself from the saddle , and into Fairthorn 's arm . " back 
 again - back again - and to leave no more ! " say he , look round ; " spe 
 et Fortuna valete ! " 




 CHAPTER XVII . 

  a MAN 'S LETTER - UNSATISFACTORY and PROVOKING as a MAN 's letter 
  always ARE . 

 guy darrell to COLONEL MORLEY . 

 Fawley Manor - House , August 11 , 18 - . I have decide , my dear Alban . I 
 do not take three day to do so , though the third day may be just over 
 ere you learn my decision . I shall never marry again : I abandon that 
 last dream of decline year . my object in return to the London 
 world be to try whether I could not find , amongst the fairest and 
 most attractive woman that the world produce - at least to an English 
 eye - some one who could inspire I with that singleness of affection 
 which could alone justify the hope that I might win in return a wife 's 
 esteem and a contented home . that object be now finally relinquish , 
 and with it all idea of resume the life of city . I might have 
 re - enter a political career , have I first secure to myself a mind 
 sufficiently serene and healthful for duty that need the concentration 
 of thought and desire . such a state of mind I can not secure . I have 
 strive for it ; I be baffle . it be say that politic be a jealous 
 mistress - that they require the whole man . the saying be not invariably 
 true in the application it commonly receive - that is , a politician 
 may have some other employment of intellect , which rather enlarge his 
 power than distract their political us . successful politician have 
 unite with great parliamentary toil and triumph legal occupation or 
 learn study . but politic do require that the heart should be free , 
 and at peace from all more absorbing private anxiety - from the gnawing 
 of a memory or a care , which dull ambition and paralyse energy . in 
 this sense politic do require the whole man . if I return to politic 
 now , I should fail to they , and they to I . I feel that the brief 
 interval between I and the grave have need of repose : I find that repose 
 here . I have therefore give the necessary order to dismiss the pompous 
 retinue which I leave behind I , and instruct my agent to sell my 
 London house for whatever it may fetch . I be unwilling to sell it 
 before - unwilling to abandon the hope , however faint , that I might 
 yet regain strength for action . but the very struggle to obtain such 
 strength leave I exhausted more . 

 you may believe that it be not without a pang , less of pride than of 
 remorse , that I resign unfulfilled the object towards which all my 
 early life be so resolutely shape . the house I promise my father 
 to re - found dy to dust in my grave . to my father 's blood no heir to my 
 wealth can trace . yet it be a consolation to think that Lionel Haughton 
 be one on whom my father would have smile approvingly . at my death , 
 therefore , at least the old name will not die ; Lionel Haughton will take 
 and be worthy to bear it . strange weakness of mine , you will say ; but I 
 can not endure the thought that the old name should be quite blot out 
 of the land . I trust that Lionel may early form a suitable and happy 
 marriage . sure that he will not choose ignobly , I impose no fetter on 
 his choice . 

 one word only on that hateful subject , confide so tardily to your 
 friendship , leave so thankfully to your discretion . now that I have once 
 more bury myself in Fawley , it be very unlikely that the man it pain 
 I to name will seek I here . if he do , he can not molest I as if 
 I be in the London world . continue , then , I pray you , to leave he 
 alone . and , in adopt your own shrewd belief , that after all there be 
 no such child as he pretend to claim , my mind become tranquillise on 
 all that part of my private grief . 

 Farewell , old school - friend ! here , so far as I can foretell - here , where 
 my life begin , it return , when Heaven please , to close . here I could 
 not ask you to visit I : what be rest to I would be loss of time to 
 you . but in my late and vain attempt to re - enter that existence in which 
 you have calmly and wisely gather round yourself , " all that should 
 accompany old age - honour , love , obedience , troop of friend " - nothing 
 so repay the effort - nothing now so pleasantly remain to 
 recollection - as the brief renewal of that easy commune which man like 
 I never know , save with those whose laughter bring back to they a gale 
 from the old playground . " Vive , vale ; " I will not add , " Sis memor 
 mei . " so many my obligation to your kindness , that you will be force 
 to remember I whenever you recall the not " painful subject " of early 
 friendship and lasting gratitude . recall only those when remind of GUY 
 DARRELL . 




 CHAPTER XVIII . 

  no COINAGE in circulation SO fluctuate in value as the worth of a 
  MARRIAGEABLE MAN . 

 Colonel Morley be not surprised   that , we know , he could not be , by any 
 fresh experience of human waywardness and caprice , but much disturb 
 and much vex by the unexpected nature of Darrell 's communication . 
 scheme for Darrell 's future lead become plan of his own . talk with his 
 old school - fellow have , within the last three month , enter into the 
 pleasure of his age . Darrell 's abrupt and final renunciation of this

 remembrance - some rankle grief - distinct from those of which Alban be 
 cognisant , from those in which he have be consult , be imply , but 
 by vague and general hint . but what be the remembrance or the grief , 
 Alban Morley , who know everything , be quite persuade that Darrell 
 would never suffer he to know . could it be in any way connect with 
 those three young lady to whom Darrell 's attention have be so

 advanced in year ever like to own even to his near friend - hazard 
 a proposal , and meet with a rebuff ? if so , Alban conjecture the 
 female culprit by whom the sentiment have be inspire , and the rebuff 
 administer . " that mischievous kitten , Flora Vyvyan , " growl the

  patte de velour ! " rouse by this suspicion , he sally forth to call 
 on the Vyvyans . Mr. Vyvyan , a widower , one of those quiet gentleman - like 
 man who sit much in the drawing - room and like receive morning 
 visitor , be at home to he . " so Darrell have leave town for the season , " 
 say the Colonel , push straight to the point . 

 " yes , " say Mr. Vyvyan . " I have a note from he this morning to say he 
 have renounce all hope of - " 

 " what ? " cry the Colonel . 

 " join we in Switzerland . I be so sorry . Flora still more sorry . she 
 be accustomed to have her own way , and she have set her heart on hear 
 Darrell read ' manfred ' in sight of the Jungfrau ! " 

 " um ! " say the Colonel . " what might be sport to she might be death to 
 he . a man at his age be not too old to fall in love with a young lady 
 of her . but he be too old not to be extremely ridiculous to such a 
 young lady if he do . " 

 " Colonel Morley - Fie ! " cry an angry voice behind he . Flora have 
 enter the room unobserve . her face be much flushed , and her eyelid 
 look as if tear have lately swell beneath they , and be swell 
 still . 

 " what have I say to merit your rebuke ? " ask the Colonel composedly . 

 " say ! couple the thought of ridicule with the name of Mr. Darrell ! " 

 " take care , Morley , " say Mr. Vyvyan , laugh . " Flora be positively 
 superstitious in her respect for Guy Darrell ; and you can not offend she 
 more than by imply that he be mortal . Nay , child , it be very natural . 
 quite apart from his fame , there be something in that man 's familiar 
 talk , or rather , perhaps , in the very sound of his voice , which make 
 most other society seem flat and insipid . 

 " I feel it myself . and when Flora 's young admirer flutter and babble 
 round she - just after Darrell have quit his chair beside she - they 
 seem very poor company . I be sure , Flora , " continue Vyvyan kindly , 
 " that the mere acquaintance of such a man have do you much good ; and 
 I be now in great hope that , whenever you marry , it will be a man of 
 sense . " 

 " um ! " again say the Colonel , eye Flora aslant , but with much 
 attention . " how I wish , for my friend 's sake , that he be of an age 
 which inspire Miss Vyvyan with less - veneration . " 

 Flora turn she back on the Colonel , look out of the window , and her 
 small foot beat the ground with nervous irritation . 

 " it be give out that Darrell intend to marry again , " say Mr. 
 Vyvyan . " a man of that sort require a very superior highly - educate 
 woman ; and if Miss Carr Vipont have be a little more of his age she 
 would have just suit he . but I be patriot enough to hope that he will 
 remain single , and have no wife but his country , like Mr. Pitt . " the 
 Colonel have now satisfy his curiosity , and assure himself that 
 Darrell be , there at least , no reject suitor , rise and approach 
 Flora to make peace and to take leave . as he hold out his hand , he be 
 strike with the change in a countenance usually so gay in its aspect - it 
 speak of more than dejection , it betray distress ; when she take his 
 hand , she retain it , and look into his eye wistfully ; evidently 
 there be something on her mind which she wish to express and do not 
 know how . at length she say in a whisper : " you be Mr. Darrell 's most 
 intimate friend ; I have hear he say so ; shall you see he soon ? " 

 " I fear not ; but why ? " 

 " why ? you , his friend ; do you not perceive that he be not happy ? I , a 
 mere stranger , see it at the first . you should cheer and comfort he ; 
 you have that right - it be a noble privilege . " 

 " my dear young lady , " say the Colonel , touch , " you have a well 
 heart than I think for . it be true Darrell be not a happy man ; but can 
 you give I any message that might cheer he more than an old bachelor 's 
 commonplace exhortation to take heart , forget the rain of yesterday , 
 and hope for some gleam of sun on the morrow ? " 

 " no , " say Flora , sadly , " it would be a presumption indeed in I , to 
 affect the consoler 's part ; but " -   her lip quiver   - " but if I may 
 judge by his letter , I may never see he again . " 

 " his letter ! he have write to you , then , as well as to your father ? " 

 " yes , " say Flora , confused and colouring , " a few line in answer to 
 a silly note of mine ; yes , tell he that I shall never forget his kind 
 counsel , his delicate , indulgent construction of - of - in short , tell 
 he my father be right , and that I shall be well and wiser all my life 
 for the few short week in which I have know Guy Darrell . " 

 " what secret be you two whisper there ? " ask Mr. Vyvyan from his 
 easy - chair . 

 " ask she ten year hence , " say the Colonel , as he retreat to the 
 door . " the fair leaf in the flower be the last that the bud will 
 disclose . " 

 from Mr. Vyvyan the Colonel go to Lord - - - 's . his lordship have also

 CHAPTER XIX . 

  MAN be not permit , with ULTIMATE IMPUNITY , to EXASPERATE the 
  envy and insult the misery of those around HIM , BY a SYSTEMATIC

 the Colonel , before return home , drop into the Clubs , and

 " there be a class of poetry which displease middle age by the very

 CHAPTER XX . 

  each generation have its own CRITICAL canon in POETRY as well as in 
  POLITICAL CREEDS , FINANCIAL SYSTEMS , or WHATEVER other changeable 
  matter of TASTE be call " settle question " and " fix opinion . " 

 George , muse much over all that his uncle have say respect Darrell ,

 seem its stern infliction . that poor boy must have change his

 LIONEL . - " you amaze I . Mr. Darrell speak of he as a boy of great

 bright - eye , bold - tongue boy , with just enough awe of his father to

 GEORGE . - " Headstrong arrogance - relentless cruelty . he have a pride which 
 would have sham his father out of pride , have Guy Darrell detect its 
 nature - purse pride ! I remember his father say to I with a half - laugh : 
 ' my boy must not be gall and mortify as I be every hour at 
 school - clothe patch and pocket empty . ' and so , out of mistaken 
 kindness , Mr. Darrell run into the opposite extreme , and the son be 
 proud , not of his father 's fame , but of his father 's money , and withal 
 not generous , nor exactly extravagant , but use money as power - power

 drive this child into the river out of his depth that cramp seize 
 himself , and he be drown . yes , when I think what that boy would 
 have be as a man , succeed to Darrell 's wealth - and have darrell

 career - to the rank and title he would probably have acquire and 
 bequeath - again , I say , in man 's affliction be often Heaven 's mercy . " 

 Lionel listen aghast . George continue : " would that I could speak as 
 plainly to Mr. Darrell himself ! for we find constantly in the world that 
 there be no error that mislead we like the error that be half a truth 
 wrench from the other half ; and nowhere be such an error so common as 
 when man apply it to the judgment of some event in his own life , and 
 separate calamity from consolation . " 

 LIONEL . - " true ; but who could have the heart to tell a mourning father 
 that his dead son be worthless ? " 

 GEORGE . - " alas ! my young friend , the preacher must sometimes harden his 
 own heart if he would strike home to another 's soul . but I be not sure 
 that Mr. Darrell would need so cruel a kindness . I believe that his 
 clear intellect must have divine some portion of his son 's nature

 Vance . " 

 " Frank Vance ! true , he have a brother - in - law a poet . I admire Frank so 
 much ; and , though he profess to sneer at poetry , he be so associate 
 in my mind with poetical image that I be prepossess beforehand in

 " tell I then , " say George , point out a passage in the volume , " what 
 you think of these line . my good uncle would call they gibberish . I be
