 could - what say you ? " 

 Lionel glance . " Exquisite indeed ! - nothing can be clear - they express 
 exactly a sentiment in myself that I could never explain . " 

 " just so , " say George , laugh . " Youth have a sentiment that it can not 
 explain , and the sentiment be express in a form of poetry that middle 
 age can not construe . it be true that poetry of the grand order interest 
 equally all age ; but the world ever throw out a poetry not of the 
 grandest ; not mean to be durable - not mean to be universal , but 
 follow the shift and change of human sentiment , and just like those 
 pretty sundial form by flower , which bloom to tell the hour , open 
 their bud to tell it , and , tell it , fade themselves from time . " 

 not listen to the critic , Lionel continue to read the poem , 
 exclaim , " how exquisite ! - how true ! " 




 CHAPTER XXI . 

  in LIFE , as in ART , the BEAUTIFUL move in CURVES . 

 they have din . - George Morley take the oar , and the boat cut 
 through the dance of wave flush by the golden sunset . beautiful 
 river ! which might furnish the english tale - teller with legend wild 
 as those cull on shore lick by Hydaspes , and sweet as those which 
 Cephisus ever blend with the song of nightingale and the breath of 
 violet ! but what true english poet ever name thee , o Father Thames , 
 without a melodious tribute ? and what child ever while away summer 
 noon along thy grassy bank , nor hallow thy remembrance among the 
 fairy day of life ? 

 Silently Lionel bent over the side of the gliding boat ; his mind carry 
 back to the same soft stream five year ago . how vast a space in his 
 short existence those five year seem to fill ! and how distant from 
 the young man , rich in the attribute of wealth , arm with each 
 weapon of distinction , seem the hour when the boy have groan aloud , 
 " ' Fortune be so far , Fame so impossible ! ' " far and far yet than 
 his present worldly station from his past seem the image that have 
 first call forth in his breast the dreamy sentiment , which the 
 sternest of we in after life never , utterly forget . passion rage and 
 vanish , and when all their storm be go , yea , it may be , at the verge 
 of the very grave , we look back and see like a star the female face , 
 even though it be a child 's , that first set we vaguely wonder at the 
 charm in a human presence , at the void in a smile withdraw ! how many of 
 we could recall a Beatrice through the gap of ruin hope , see , as by 
 the Florentine , on the earth a guileless infant , in the heaven a spirit 
 glorify ! yes - Laura be an affectation - Beatrice a reality ! 

 George 's voice break somewhat distastefully on Lionel 's reverie . " we 
 near our destination , and you have not ask I even the name of the 
 lady to whom you be to render homage . it be Lady Montfort , widow to the 
 last Marquess . you have no doubt hear Mr. Darrell speak of she ? " 

 " never Mr. Darrell - Colonel Morley often . and in the world I have hear 
 she cite as perhaps the handsome , and certainly the haughty , woman 
 in England . " 

 " never hear Mr. Darrell mention she ! that be strange indeed , " say 
 George Morley , catch at Lionel 's first word , and unnotice his 
 after comment . " she be much in his house as a child , share in his 
 daughter 's education . " 

 " perhaps for that very reason he shun her name . never but once do 
 I hear he allude to his daughter ; nor can I wonder at that , if it be 
 true , as I have be tell by people who seem to know very little of the 
 particular , that , while yet scarcely out of the nursery , she flee from 
 his house with some low adventurer - a Mr. Hammond - die abroad the first 
 year of that unhappy marriage . " 

 " yes , that be the correct outline of the story ; and , as you guess , it 
 explain why Mr. Darrell avoid mention of one , whom he associate 
 with his daughter 's name ; though , if you desire a theme dear to Lady 
 Montfort , you can select none that more interest her grateful heart 
 than praise of the man who save her mother from penury , and secure to 
 herself the accomplishment and instruction which have be her chief 
 solace . " 

 " chief solace ! be she not happy with Lord Montfort ? what sort of man 
 be he ? " 

 " I owe to Lord Montfort the living I hold , and I can remember the good 
 quality alone of a benefactor . if Lady Montfort be not happy with 
 he , it be just to both to say that she never complain . but there be 
 much in Lady Montfort 's character which the Marquess apparently fail 
 to appreciate ; at all event , they have little in common , and what be 
 call Lady Montfort 's haughtiness be perhaps but the dignity with 
 which a woman of grand nature check the pity that would debase she - the 
 admiration that would sully - guard her own beauty , and protect she 
 husband 's name . here we be . will you stay for a few minute in the 
 boat , while I go to prepare Lady Montfort for your visit ? " 

 George leapt ashore , and Lionel remain under the covert of mighty 
 willow that dip their leaf into the wave . look through the 
 green interstice of the foliage , he see at the far end of the lawn , 
 on a curve bank by which the glitter tide shoot oblique , a simple 
 arbour - an arbour like that from which he have look upon summer star 
 five year ago - not so densely cover with the honeysuckle ; still the 
 honeysuckle , recently train there , be fast creep up the side ; 
 and through the trelli of the woodwork and the leaf of the flower 
 shrub , he just catch a glimpse of some form within - the white robe of 
 a female form in a slow gentle movement - tend perhaps the flower that 
 wreathe the arbour . now it be still , now it stir again ; now it be 
 suddenly lose to view . have the inmate leave the arbour ? be the inmate 
 Lady Montfort ? George Morley 's step have not pass in that direction . 




 CHAPTER XXII . 

  a QUIET SCENE - an unquiet heart . 

 meanwhile , not far from the willow - bank which shelter Lionel , but 
 far enough to be out of her sight and beyond her hearing , George Morley 
 find Lady Montfort seat alone . it be a spot on which Milton might 
 have place the lady in " Comus " - a circle of the smooth sward , ring 
 everywhere   except at one opening which leave the glassy river in 
 full view   with thick bosk of dark evergreen and shrub of lively 
 verdure ; oak and chest nut backing and overhang all . flower , too , 
 raise on rustic tier and stage ; a tiny fountain , shoot up from a 
 basin star with the water - lily ; a rustic table , on which lie hook 
 and the implement of woman 's graceful work ; so that the place have the 
 home - look of a chamber , and speak that intense love of the out - door 
 life which abound in our old poet from Chaucer down to the day when 
 minstrel , polish into wit , take to Will 's Coffee - house , and the lark 
 come no more to bid bard 

  " good morrow 
  from his watch - tower in the sky . " 

 but long since , thank Heaven we have again get back the english poetry 
 which chime to the babble of the water , and the riot of the bird ; and 
 just as that poetry be the fresh which the out - door life have the most 
 nourish , so I believe that there be no surer sign of the rich vitality 
 which find its racy joy in source the most innocent , than the 
 childlike taste for that same out - door life . whether you take from 
 fortune the palace or the cottage , add to your chamber a hall in the 
 court of Nature . let the earth but give you room to stand on ; well , 
 look up - be it nothing to have for your roof - tree - Heaven ? 

 Caroline Montfort   be her title drop   be change since we last 
 see she . the beauty be not less in degree , but it have gain in one 
 attribute , lose in another ; it command less , it touch more . still in 
 deep mourning , the sombre dress throw a pale shade over the cheek . the 
 eye , more sink beneath the brow , appear large , soft . there be 
 that expression of fatigue which either accompany impaired health or 
 succeed to mental struggle and disquietude . but the coldness or pride 
 of mien which be peculiar to Caroline as a wife be go - as if in 
 widowhood it be no long need . a something like humility prevail 
 over the look and the bearing which have be so tranquilly majestic . 
 as at the approach of her cousin she start from her seat , there be 
 a nervous tremor in her eagerness ; a rush of colour to the cheek ; an 
 anxious quivering of the lip ; a flutter in the tone of the sweet low 
 voice : " well , George . " 

 " Mr. Darrell be not in London ; he go to Fawley three day ago ; at 
 least he be there now . I have this from my uncle , to whom he write ; and 
 whom his departure have vex and sadden . " 

 " three day ago ! it must have be he , then ! I be not deceive , " 
 murmur Caroline , and her eye wander mound . 

 " there be no truth in the report you hear that he be to marry Honoria 
 Vipont . my uncle think he will never marry again , and imply that he 
 have resume his solitary life at Fawley with a resolve to quit it no 
 more . " 

 Lady Montfort listen silently , bend her face over the fountain , 
 and drop amidst its playful spray the leaf of a rose which she have 
 abstractedly pluck as George be speak . 

 " I have , therefore , fulfil your commission so far , " renew George 
 Morley . " I have ascertain that Mr. Darrell be alive , and doubtless 
 well ; so that it could not have be his ghost that startle you amidst 
 yonder thicket . but I have do more : I have forestall the wish you 
 express to become acquainted with young Haughton ; and your object in 
 postpone the accomplishment of that wish while Mr. Darrell himself be 
 in town have cease with Mr. Darrell 's departure , I have venture to 
 bring the young man with I . he be in the boat yonder . will you receive 
 he ? or - but , my dear cousin , be you not too unwell today ? what be the 
 matter ? oh , I can easily make an excuse for you to Haughton . I will run 
 and do so . " 

 " no , George , no . I be as well as usual . I will see Mr. Haughton . all 
 that you have hear of he , and have tell I , interest I so much in 
 his favour ; and besides - " she do not finish the sentence ; but lead away 
 by some other thought , ask , " have you no news of our miss friend ? " 

 " none as yet ; but in a few day I shall renew my search . now , then , I 
 will go for Haughton . " 

 " do so ; and George , when you have present he to I , will you kindly 
 join that dear anxious child yonder ! 

 " she be in the new arbour , or near it - her favourite spot . you must 
 sustain her spirit , and give her hope . you can not guess how eagerly 
 she look forward to your visit , and how gratefully she rely on your 
 exertion . " 

 George shake his head half despondingly , and say briefly , " my 
 exertion have establish no claim to her gratitude as yet , " go 
 quickly back for Lionel . 




 CHAPTER XXIII . 

  something on an OLD subject , which have never be say before 

 although Lionel be prepared to see a very handsome woman in Lady 
 Montfort , the beauty of her countenance take he by surprise . no 
 preparation by the eulogy of description can lessen the effect that 
 the first sight of a beautiful object produce upon a mind to which 
 refinement of idea give an accurate and quick comprehension of beauty . 
 be it a work of art , a scene in nature , or , rare of all , a human face 
 divine , a beauty never before beheld strike we with hidden pleasure , 
 like a burst of light . and it be a pleasure that elevate ; the 
 imagination feel itself rich by a new idea of excellence ; for not 
 only be real beauty wholly original , have no prototype , but its 
 immediate influence be spiritual . it may seem strange - I appeal to every 
 observant artist if the assertion be not true - but the first sight of 
 the most perfect order of female beauty , rather than court , rebuke 
 and strike back , every gross instinct that would alloy admiration . 
 there must be some meanness and blemish in the beauty which the 
 sensualist no soon behold than he covet . in the high incarnation 
 of the abstract idea which run through all our notion of moral good 
 and celestial purity - even if the moment the eye see the heart love 
 the image - the love have in it something of the reverence which it be 
 say the charm of Virtue would produce could her form be make visible ; 
 nor could mere human love obtrude itself till the sweet awe of the 
 first effect have be familiarise away . and I apprehend that it be this 
 exalt or etherealise attribute of beauty to which all poet , all 
 writer who would poetise the reality of life , have unconsciously 
 render homage , in the rank to which they elevate what , strip of 
 such attribute , would be but a gaudy idol of paint clay . if , from the 
 lofty epic to the trite novel , a heroine be often little more than 
 a name to which we be call upon to bow , as to a symbol represent 
 beauty , and if we ourselves   be we ever so indifferent in our common 
 life to fair face   feel that , in art at least , imagination need an 
 image of the Beautiful - if , in a word , both poet and reader here would 
 not be leave excuseless , it be because in our inmost heart there be a 
 sentiment which link the ideal of beauty with the Supersensual . wouldst 
 thou , for instance , form some vague conception of the shape wear by a 
 pure soul release ? wouldst thou give to it the likeness of an ugly 
 hag ? or wouldst thou not ransack all thy remembrance and conception of 
 form most beauteous to clothe the holy image ? do so : now bring it thus 
 rob with the rich grace before thy mind 's eye . well , seest thou 
 now the excuse for poet in the rank they give to BEAUTY ? seest thou now 
 how high from the realm of the sens soar the mysterious Archetype ? 
 without the idea of beauty , couldst thou conceive a form in which to 
 clothe a soul that have enter heaven ? 




 CHAPTER XXIV . 

  AGREEABLE surprise be the perquisite of youth . 

 if the beauty of Lady Montfort 's countenance take Lionel by surprise , 
 still more might he wonder at the win kindness of her address - a 
 kindness of look , manner , voice , which seem to welcome he not as

 sentence , in give they a subject of common interest , introduce into

 a turn in the walk bring Lionel and Lady Montfort before the window

 commodious , with no pretence to architectural beauty - dark - red brick , a

 " our work - room , " say Lady Montfort , with a warm cheerful smile , and yet

 yes , that harp be her . be he still fond of music - I mean Mr. Darrell ? "

 lip could not presume to ask . " your adopt child - what be she ? - who ? "

 will go for she . " 

 she leave he , descend the stair into the garden , join George Morley

 the arm of the latter within her own , lead she back into the room , while

 BOOK VIII . 




 CHAPTER I. 

  " a little fire burn up a great deal of CORN . " - OLD PROVERB . 

 Guy Darrell resume the thread of solitary life at Fawley with a calm 
 which be deep in its gloom than it have be before . the experiment of 
 return to the social world have fail . the resolution which have induce 
 the experiment be finally renounce . five year near to death , 
 and the last hope that have flit across the narrow passage to the 
 grave , fall like a faithless torch from his own hand , and trodden out 
 by his own foot . 

 it be peculiarly in the nature of Darrell to connect his object with 
 posterity - to regard eminence in the Present but as a beacon - height from 
 which to pass on to the Future the name he have take from the Past . all 
 his early ambition , sacrifice pleasure to toil , have place its goal 
 at a distance , remote from the huzzas of bystander ; and Ambition halt 
 now , baffled and despairing . Childless , his line would perish with 
 himself - himself , who have so vaunted its restoration in the land ! his 
 genius be childless also - it would leave behind it no offspring of the 
 brain . by toil he have amass ample wealth ; by talent he have achieve a 
 splendid reputation . but the reputation be as perishable as the wealth . 
 let a half - century pass over his tomb , and nothing would be leave to 
 speak of the successful lawyer the applaud orator , save traditional 
 anecdote , a laudatory notice in contemporaneous memoir - perhaps , at 
 most , quotation of eloquent sentence lavish on forget case 
 and obsolete debate - shred and fragment of a great intellect , which 
 another half - century would sink without a bubble into the depth of 
 Time . he have enact no law - he have administer no state - he have 
 compose no book . like the figure on a clock , which adorn the case 
 and have no connection with the movement , he , so prominent an ornament 
 to time , have no part in its work . remove , the eye would miss he for 
 a while ; but a nation 's literature or history be the same , whether 
 with he or without . some with a tithe of his ability have the luck to 
 fasten their name to thing that endure ; they have be responsible for 
 measure they do not not invent , and which , for good or evil , influence 
 long generation . they have write volume out of which a couplet of 
 verse , a period in prose , may cling to the rock of age , as a shell 
 that survive a deluge . but the orator , whose effect be immediate - who 
 enthrall his audience in proportion as he nick the hour - who , be he 
 speak like Burke what , apart from the subject - matter , closet student 
 would praise , must , like Burke , thin his audience , and exchange present 
 oratorical success for ultimate intellectual renown - a man , in short , 
 whose oratory be emphatically that of the debater be , like an actor , 
 reward with a loud applause and a complete oblivion . Waife on the 
 village stage might win applause no less loud , follow by oblivion not 
 more complete . 

 Darrell be not blind to the brevity of his fame . in his previous 
 seclusion he have be resign to that conviction - now it sadden he . 
 then , unconfesse by himself , the idea that he might yet reappear in 
 active life , and do something which the world would not willingly let 
 die , have soften the face of that tranquil Nature from which he must 
 soon now pass out of reach and sight . on the tree of Time he be a leaf 
 already sear upon the bough - not an inscription grave into the rind . 

 ever slow to yield to weak regret - ever seek to combat his own 
 enemy within - Darrell say to himself one night , while Fairthorn 's 
 flute be breathe an air of romance through the melancholy wall : " be 
 it too late yet to employ this still busy brain upon work that will 
 live when I be dust , and make posterity supply the heir that fail to my 
 house ? " 

 he shut himself up with immortal author - he meditate on the choice of 
 a theme ; his knowledge be wide , his taste refine ; - word ! - he could 
 not want word ! why should he not write ? alas ; why indeed ? - he who have 
 never be a writer in his youth , can no more be a writer in his age 
 than he can be a painter - a musician . what ! not write a book ! oh , 
 yes - as he may paint a picture or set a song . but a writer , in the 
 emphatic sense of the word - a writer as Darrell be an orator - oh , 
 no ! and , least of all , will he be a writer if he have be an orator 
 by impulse and habit - an orator too happily gifted to require , and 
 too laboriously occupy to resort to , the tedious aid of written 
 preparation - an orator as modern life form orator - not , of course , an 
 orator like those of the classic world , who elaborate sentence 
 before delivery , and who , after delivery , polish each extemporaneous 
 interlude into rhetorical exactitude and musical perfection . and how 
 narrow the range of composition to a man burden already by a grave 
 reputation ! he can not have the self - abandonment - he can not venture the 
 headlong charge - with which youth fling the rein to genius , and dash 
 into the rank of Fame . few and austere his theme - fastidious and 
 hesitate his taste . restrict be the movement of he who walk 
 for the first time into the Forum of Letters with the purple hem on his 
 senatorial toga . Guy Darrell , at his age , enter among author as 
 a novice ! - he , the great lawyer , to whom attorney would have send no 
 brief have he be suspect of coquet with a muse , - he , the great 
 orator who have electrify audience in proportion to the sudden effect 
 which distinguish oral inspiration from write eloquence - he achieve 
 now , in an art which his whole life have neglect , any success 
 commensurate to his contemporaneous repute ; - how unlikely ! but a success 
 which should outlive that repute , win the " everlasting inheritance " 
 which could alone have nerve he to adequate effort - how impossible ! 
 he could not himself comprehend why , never at a loss for language 
 felicitously opposite or richly ornate when it have but to flow from his 
 thought to his tongue , nor want ease , even eloquence , in epistolary 
 correspondence confidentially familiar - he should find word fail idea , 
 and idea fail word , the moment his pen become a wand that conjure up 
 the Ghost of the dread Public ! the more copious his thought , the more 
 embarrass their selection ; the more exquisite his perception of 
 excellence in other , the more timidly frigid his effort at faultless 
 style . it would be the same with the most skilful author , if the Ghost 
 of the Public have not long since cease to haunt he . while he write , 
 the true author 's solitude be absolute or people at his will . but 
 take an audience from an orator , what be he ? he command the living 
 public - the Ghost of the Public awe himself . 

 " surely once , " sigh Darrell , as he give his blurred page to the 
 flame - ' surely once I have some pittance of the author 's talent , and 
 have spend it upon lawsuit ! " 

 the author 's talent , no doubt , Guy Darrell once have - the author 's 
 temperament never . what be the author 's temperament ? too long a task to 
 define . but without it a man may write a clever book , a useful book , a 
 book that may live a year , ten year , fifty year . he will not stand 
 out to distant age a representative of the age that rather live in 
 he than he in it . the author 's temperament be that which make he an 
 integral , earnest , original unity , distinct from all before and all 
 that may succeed he . and as a Father of the Church have say that 
 the consciousness of individual being be the sign of immortality , 
 not grant to the inferior creature - so it be in this individual 
 temperament one and indivisible , and in the intense conviction of it , 
 more than in all the work it may throw off , that the author become 
 immortal . Nay , his work may perish like those of Orpheus or Pythagoras ; 
 but he himself , in his name , in the footprint of his being , remain , 
 like Orpheus or Pythagoras , undestroyed , indestructible . 

 resign literature , the Solitary return to Science . there he be

 CHAPTER II . 

  the learned COMPUTE that seven hundred and seven MILLIONS of 
  million of vibration have penetrate the eye before the eye can

 while Guy Darrell thus pass his hour within the unfinished fragment

 your eye from I , or I shall think that there be tear in they ! "

 tear , and you would hide they from I . ungrateful ! "

 should I not be so if I be gay and happy ? " 

 and in self - reproach for not be sufficiently unhappy while that young

 CHAPTER III . 

  comprise many NEEDFUL explanation illustrative of wise SAWS ; as

 the reader have be already make aware how , by an impulse of womanhood

 to associate her fate henceforth with the life of his lawless son ; and ,

 openly express . Arabella have write to Waife from the continent , for

 chance of Losely 's ultimate reformation . then the interval of silence 
 become gradually more prolonged , and the letter more brief . but still , 
 whether from the wish not unnecessarily to pain the old man , or , as 
 would be more natural to her character , which , even in its good aspect ,

 meanwhile Sophy have be present to Caroline Montfort . as Waife have so

 not of a curious turn of mind ; profoundly indifferent even to the gossip 
 of drawing - room , she have no rankling desire to know the secret 
 of village hearthstone . little acquainted even with the great 
 world - scarcely at all with any world below that in which she have she

 contrast between Waife 's calling and his conversation rouse in her no

 touch upon the order of gentleman , but of no practical or professional 
 culture - with whimsical taste - with rove eccentric habit - have , in

 relation in a high sphere , whom the confession would shame ; he might

 Montfort . naturally ; for if , in her essentially woman - like character ,

  " under the glassy , cool , translucent wave , "

 noble - hearted woman ! and nobler for her ignorance of the practical

 poor old man ! his heart be break ; but his soul be so brightly

 CHAPTER IV . 

  a woman TOO often reason from her heart - hence two - thirds of her 
  mistakes and her trouble . a man of geniu , TOO , often reason from 
  his HEART - WENCE , also , two - third of his trouble and mistakes . 
  WHEREFORE , between woman and genius there be a SYMPATHETIC AFFINITY ; 
  each have some INTUITIVE comprehension of the secrets of the OTHER , 
  and the MORE FEMININE the woman , the MORE EXQUISITE the geniu , the 
  MORE SUBTLE the intelligence between the TWO . but note well that 
  this TACIT understand become OBSCURED , if HUMAN love PASS across 
  its relation . shakespeare interpret aright the MOST INTRICATE 
  RIDDLES in WOMAN . a woman be the first to interpret aright the ART 
  that be latent in SHAKESPEARE . but do ANNE HATHAWAY and 
  SHAKESPEARE understand each other ? 

 unobserve by the two young people , Lady Montfort sate watch they as 
 they move along the river bank . she be seat where Lionel have first 
 see she - in the kind of grassy chamber that have be win from the 
 foliage and the sward , close round with interlaced autumnal branch , 
 save where it open towards the water . if ever woman 's brain can 
 conceive and plot a scheme thoroughly pure from one ungentle , selfish 
 thread in its web , in such a scheme have Caroline Montfort bring 
 together those two fair young nature . and yet they be not uppermost 
 in her thought as she now gaze on they ; nor be it wholly for they 
 that her eye be fill with tear at once sweet , yet profoundly 
 mournful - holy , and yet intensely human . 

 woman love to think themselves uncomprehende - nor often without reason 
 in that foible ; for man , howsoever sagacious , rarely do entirely 
 comprehend woman , howsoever simple . and in this her sex have the 
 advantage over ours . our heart be bare to their eye , even though they 
 can never know what have be our life . but we may see every action 
 of their life , guard and circumscribe in conventional form , while 
 their heart will have many mystery to which we can never have the 
 key . but , in more than the ordinary sense of the word , Caroline Montfort 
 never have be a woman uncomprehende . nor even in her own sex do she 
 possess one confidante . only the outward leaf of that beautiful flower 
 open to the sunlight . the leaf round the core be gather fold 
 upon fold closely as when life itself be in the bud . 

 as all the year of her wed existence her heart have be deny the 
 natural household vent , so by some strange and unaccountable chance she 
 intellect also seem restrained and pen from its proper freedom and 
 play . during those barren year , she have read - she have ponder - she have 
 enjoy a commune with those whose mind instruct other , and still 
 her own intelligence , which in early youth have be characterise by 
 singular vivacity and brightness , and which Time have enrich with every 
 womanly accomplishment , seem chilled and objectless . it be not enough 
 that a mind should be cultured - it should have movement as well as 
 culture . Caroline Montfort 's lie quiescent like a beautiful form 
 spellbound to repose , but not to sleep . look on she once , as he stand 
 amongst a crowd whom her beauty dazzle , a poet say abruptly : " be my 
 guess not a sacrilege to one so spotless and so haughty , I should say 
 that I have hit on the solution of an enigma that long perplex I ; 
 and in the core of that queen of the lily , could we strip the leaf 
 fold round it , we should find remorse . " 

 Lady Montfort start ; the shadow of another form than her own fall 
 upon the sward . George Morley stand behind she , his finger on his lip . 
 " hush , " he say in a whisper , ' see , Sophy be look for I up the river . 
 I know she would be - I steal this way on purpose - for I would speak to 
 you before I face her question . "

 " Nay , my dear cousin , there be less cause for alarm than for anxious 
 deliberation , and that upon more matter than those which directly

 the police in aid of our search . I be too sensible of the pain and

 anxiety might produce on Sophy 's health , make I resolve to waive former 
 scruple . since I last see you I have apply to one of the high 
 police - officer accustom to confidential investigation of a similar

 friend of his , who have be formerly a distinguished agent in the 
 detective police , have be engage for month in track a person whom 
 he conjecture to be the same as the one whom I have commission he 
 to discover , and with somewhat less caution and delicacy than I have 
 enjoin . the fugitive 's real name have be give to this ex - agent - the 
 cause for search , that he have abduct and be conceal his 
 granddaughter from her father . it be easy for I to perceive why this 
 novel search have hitherto fail , no suspicion be entertain that 
 Waife have separate himself from Sophy , and the inquiry be therefore 
 rather direct towards the grandchild than the grandfather . but that 
 inquiry have altogether cease of late , and for this terrible reason - a 
 different section of the police have fix its eye upon the father on 
 whose behalf the search have be institute . this Jasper Losely   ah ! 
 our poor friend might well shudder to think Sophy should fall into 
 his hand !   haunt the resort of the most lawless and formidable 
 desperado of London . he appear to be a kind of authority amongst 
 they ; but there be no evidence that as yet he have commit himself to 
 any participation in their habitual course . he live profusely , for a

 and courage which be describe as extraordinary , but with out any 
 visible mean . it seem that the ex - agent , who have be thus previously 
 employ in Jasper Losely 's name , have be engage , not by Jasper 
 himself , but by a person in very respectable circumstance , whose name 
 I have ascertain to be Poole . and the ex - agent deem it right to 
 acquaint this Mr. Poole with Jasper 's evil character and ambiguous mode 
 of life , and to intimate to his employer that it might not be prudent to 
 hold any connection with such a man , and still less proper to assist

 agitated , and express himself so incoherently as to his relation with 
 Jasper , that the ex - agent conceive suspicion against Poole himself ,

 service , through whom they reach the very man whom I myself be

 Poole , decline all further interference , have since then , through a 
 correspondent in a country town , whom he have employ at the first , 
 obtain a clue to my dear old friend 's wandering , more recent , and 
 I think more hopeful , than any I have yet discover . you will remember 
 that when question Sophy as to any friend in her former life to whom 
 it be probable Waife might have address himself , she could think of

 once lodge , and of whom he have often speak to she with much gratitude

 also that I could not find this Merle ; he have leave the village , near 
 this very place , in which he have spend the great part of his life - his

 more and more absorbed . he have fall into poverty , his effect have be 
 sell off ; he have go away no one know whither . well , the ex - agent , who 
 have also be direct to this Merle by his employer , have , through his

 he pass under the name of the Wise Man , and where he be in perpetual 
 danger of be send to the house of correction as an impostor , deal 
 in astrology , crystal - seeing , and such silly or nefarious practice . 
 very odd , indeed , and very melancholy , too , " quoth the scholar , lift 
 up his hand and eye , " that a man so gifted as our poor friend should

 " Sophy have talk to I much about that cobbler , " say Lady Montfort , 
 with her sweet half - smile . " it be under his roof that she first see

 GEORGE . - " possibly . but to go on with my story : a few week ago , an

 Grant that her father do not discover she , but that he be lead by

 " to expedite your own project - to make at once the resolute attempt to

 " but you forget that our friend tell I that he have appeal to - to 
 Mr. Darrell on his return to England : that Mr. Darrell have peremptorily

 " true ; and yet you hop reasonably enough to succeed where he , poor 
 outcast , have fail . " 

 " yes , yes ; I do hope that Sophy - her manner form , her education 
 complete - all her natural exquisite grace so cultured and refine , as

 " my dear Lady Montfort , my noble cousin , with repute as spotless as the 
 ermine of your robe - who but you ? " 

 " who but I ? any one . Mr. Darrell would not even read through a letter 
 address to he by I . " 

 George star with astonishment . Caroline 's face be downcast - her 
 attitude that of profound humiliate dejection . 

 " incredible ! " say he at length . " I have always suspect , and so indeed

 " oh yes , yes - old day go for ever ! " 

 " do I not remember how you promise that , before I go back to school , 
 I should hear Darrell read aloud - how you bring the volume of Milton

 and do you not remember another time , how - " 

 LADY montfort   interrupt with nervous impatience . - " ay , ay - I need

 affect his own fortune , but Lionel Haughton ? - hold ! - look where yonder

 " certainly , " say George , " if he form new tie , Sophy would be less

 GEORGE   much affected . - " how , in proportion as we know you , the beauty

 losely in former day , and speak of he to Lionel with great affection .

 CHAPTER V. 

  LIONEL haughton have lose his heart , it be no long a question of 
  what he will do with it . but what will be do with it be a very 
  GRAVE QUESTION indeed . 

 Lionel forestall Lady Montfort in the delicate and embarrassing 
 subject which her cousin have urge she to open . for while George , 
 lead away Sophy , inform she of his journey to Norwich , and his 
 interview with Merle , Lionel draw . Lady Montfort into the house , and 
 with much agitation , and in abrupt hurried accent , implore she to 
 withdraw the promise which forbid he to inform his benefactor how and 
 where his time have be spend of late . he burst forth with a declaration 
 of that love with which Sophy have inspire he , and which Lady Montfort 
 could not be but prepare to hear . " nothing , " say he , " but a respect 
 for her more than filial anxiety at this moment could have keep my 
 heart thus long silent . but that heart be so deeply pledge - so utterly 
 her - that it have grow an ingratitude , a disrespect - to my generous 
 kinsman , to conceal from he any long the feeling which must colour 
 my whole future existence . nor can I say to she , ' can you return my 
 affection ? - will you listen to my vow ? - will you accept they at the 
 altar ? ' - until I have win , as I be sure to win , the approve consent of 
 my more than father . " 

 " you feel sure to win that consent , in spite of the stain on her 
 grandfather 's name ? " 

 " when Darrell learn that , but for my poor father 's fault , that name 
 might be spotless now ! - yes ! I be not Mr. Darrell 's son - the transmitter 
 of his line . I believe yet that he will form new tie . by my mother 's 
 side I have no ancestor to boast of ; and you have own to I that 
 Sophy 's mother be of gentle birth . Alban Morley tell I , when I last 
 see he , that Darrell wish I to marry , and leave I free to choose 
 my bride . yes ; I have no doubt of Mr. Darrell 's consent . my dear mother 
 will welcome to her heart the prize so coveted by mine ; and Charles 
 Haughton 's son will have a place at his hearth for the old age of 
 William Losely . withdraw your interdict at once , dearest Lady Montfort , 
 and confide to I all that you have hitherto leave unexplained , but have 
 promise to reveal when the time come . the time have come . " 

 " it have come , " say Lady Montfort , solemnly ; " and Heaven grant that it 
 may bear the blessed result which be in my thought when I take Sophy 
 as my own adopt daughter , and hail in yourself the reconciler of 
 conflict circumstance . not under this roof should you woo William 
 Losely 's grandchild . doubly be you bind to ask Guy Darrell 's consent 
 and blessing . at his hearth woo your Sophy - at his hand ask a bride in 
 his daughter 's child . " 

 and to her wonder listener , Caroline Montford tell her ground for 
 the belief that connect the last of the Darrells with the convict 's 
 grandchild . 




 CHAPTER VI . 

  CREDULOUS crystal - seer , young lover , and GRAVE wise MEN - ALL in 
  the same category . 

 George Morley set out the next day for Norwich , in which antique city , 
 ever since the ' dane people it , some wizard or witch , star - reader , or 
 crystal - seer ' have enjoy a mysterious renown , perpetuate thus through 
 all change in our land 's social progress the long line of Vala and 
 Saga , who come with the Raven and Valkyr from scandinavian pine shore . 
 Merle 's reserve vanish on the perusal of Sophy 's letter to he . he 
 inform George that Waife declare he have plenty of money , and have even 
 force a loan upon Merle ; but that he like an active , wander life ; 
 it keep he from think , and that a pedlar 's pack would give he a 
 license for vagrancy , and a budget to defray its expense ; that Merle 
 have be consult by he in the choice of light popular ware , and as 
 to the route he might find the most free from compete rival . Merle 
 willingly agree to accompany George in quest of the wanderer , whom , 
 by the help of his crystal , he seem calmly sure he could track and 
 discover . accordingly , they both set out in the somewhat devious and 
 desultory road which Merle , who have some old acquaintance amongst the 
 ancient profession of hawker , have advise Waife to take . but Merle , 
 unhappily confide more in his crystal than Waife 's steady adherence 
 to the chart prescribe , lead the Oxford scholar the life of a 
 will - of - the - wisp ; zigzag , and shoot to and fro , here and there , till , 
 just when George have lose all patience , Merle chance to see , not in 
 the crystal , a pelerine on the neck of a farmer 's daughter , which he 
 be morally certain he have himself select for Waife 's pannier . and 
 the girl state in reply to his inquiry that her father have buy that 
 pelerine as a present for she , not many day before , of a pedlar in a 
 neighbouring town , to the market of which the farmer resort weekly , 
 Merle cast an horary scheme , and find the Third House   of short 
 journey   in favourable aspect to the Seventh House   contain the 
 object desire , and in conjunction with the Eleventh House   friend , 
 he gravely inform the scholar that their toil be at an end , and 
 that the Hour and the Man be at hand . not over - sanguine , George 
 consign himself and the seer to an early train , and reach the 
 famous town of Oazelford , whither , when the chronological order of our 
 narrative   which we have so far somewhat forestalled   will permit , we 
 shall conduct the inquisitive reader . 

 Meanwhile Lionel , subscribe without a murmur to Lady Montfort 's 
 injunction to see Sophy no more till Darrell have be confer with 
 and his consent won , return to his lodging in London , sanguine of 
 success , and flush with joy . his intention be to set out at once to 
 Fawley ; but on reach town he find there a few line from Dairell 
 himself , in reply to a long and affectionate letter which Lionel 
 have write a few day before ask permission to visit the old 
 manor - house ; for amidst all his absorb love for Sophy , the image of 
 his lonely benefactor in that gloomy hermitage often rise before he . 
 in these line , Darrell , not unkindly , but very peremptorily , decline 
 Lionel 's overture . 

 " in truth , my dear young kinsman , " write the recluse - " in truth I 
 be , with slowness , and with frequent relapse , labour through 
 convalescence from a moral fever . my nerve be yet unstrung . I be as 
 one to whom be prescribe the most complete repose ; - the visit , even 
 of friend the dearest , forbid as a perilous excitement . the sight of 
 you - of any one from the great world - but especially of one whose 
 rich vitality of youth and hope affront and mock my own fatigued 
 exhaustion , would but irritate , unsettle , torture I . when I be quite 
 well I will ask you to come . I shall enjoy your visit . till then , on no 
 account , and on no pretext , let my morbid ear catch the sound of your 
 footfall on my quiet floor . write to I often , but tell I nothing 
 of the news and gossip of the world . tell I only of yourself , your 
 study , your thought , your sentiment , your wish . nor forget my 
 injunction . Marry young , marry for love ; let no ambition of power , no 
 greed of gold , ever mislead you into give to your life a companion 
 who be not the half of your soul . choose with the heart of a man ; I know 
 that you will choose with the self - esteem of a gentleman ; and be assure 
 beforehand of the sympathy and sanction of your ' churlish but LOVING 
 KINSMAN . ' " 

 after this letter , Lionel feel that , at all event , he could not at once 
 proceed to the old manor - house in defiance of its owner 's prohibition . 
 he write briefly , entreat Darrell to forgive he if he persist 
 in the prayer to be receive at Fawley , state that his desire for a 
 personal interview be now suddenly become special and urgent ; that it 
 not only concern himself , but affect his benefactor . by return 
 of post Darrell reply with curt frigidity , repeat , with even 
 sternness , his refusal to receive Lionel , but profess himself ready 
 to attend to all that his kinsman might address to he by letter . " if it 
 be as you state , " write Darrell , with his habitual irony , " a matter 
 that relate to myself , I claim , as a lawyer for my own affair - the 
 precaution I once enjoin to my client - a write brief should always 
 precede a personal consultation . " 

 in fact , the proud man suspect that Lionel have be directly or 
 indirectly address on behalf of Jasper Losely ; and certainly that 
 be the last subject on which he would have grant an interview to

 one of those man whose presence inspire a certain awe - one of those

 letter than in person . Lionel 's pen move rapidly - his whole heart and

 efface for her sake , as for mine , her grandsire 's shame , my father 's

 in her pure vein - I know , alas ! then that I should have no right to 
 aspire to such nuptial . who would even think of her descent from a 
 William Losely ? who would not be too proud to remember only her descent 
 from you ? all spot would vanish in the splendour of your renown ; the 
 high in the land would court her alliance . and I be but the pensioner

 sheet upon sheet the young eloquence flow on - seeking , with an art of

 Darrell 's character . 

 he have not to wait long for a reply . at the first glance of the address

 but in the letter which lie in Lionel 's hand the writer be scarcely

 " unhappy boy ! " begin the ominous epistle , " be it through you that the

 Lionel be stun . not for several hour could he recover

 but send this letter to Lady Montfort ? a letter so wholly at variance

 CHAPTER IX . 

  the wreck and the life - BOAT in a FOG . 

 the next day , a little after noon , Jasper Losely , come back from 
 Alhambra Villa - furious , desperate , know not where to turn for bread , 
 or on whom to pour his rage - beheld suddenly , in a quiet , half - build 
 street , which lead from the suburb to the New Road , Arabella Crane 
 stand right in his path . she have emerge from one of the many 
 straight intersect road which characterise that crude nebula of 
 a future city ; and the woman and the man meet thus face to face ; not

 it be a serene noonday in that melancholy interlude of the season when

 " I ask nothing - not even pardon , " say the miserable woman . " I might say

 the flute have cease its music ; the air have grow cold and pierce ; the

 CHAPTER II . 

 RETROSPECT . 

  there be a PLACE AT which three ROADS MEET , sacred to that 
  MYSTERIOUS GODDESS call DIANA ON EARTH , LUNA , or the MOON , in 
  HEAVEN , or HECATE in the INFERNAL region . at this PLACE PAUSE THE 
  virgin permit to take their choice of the three roads . few give 
  their preference to that which be VOWED to the GODDESS in her name 
  of diana : that ROAD , COLD and BARREN , be CLOTHED by no ROSES and 
  MYRTLES . rose and myrtles veil the entrance to both the other , 
  and in both the other HYMEN have MUCH the same GAY - look temples . 
  but which of those two LEADS to the celestial LUNA , or which of they 
  conduct to the INFERNAL HECATE , not one NYMPH in FIFTY DIVINES . if 
  THY HEART should misgive THEE , O NYMPH ! - IF , THOUGH CLOUD VEIL the 
  PATH to the MOON , and sunshine GILD that to PALE HECATETHINE 
  instinct recoils from the SUNSHINE , while THOU DAREST not adventure 
  the CLOUD - THOU HAST still a choice LEFT - THOU HAST still the SAFE 
  road of diana . HECATE , o NYMPH , be the goddess of ghost . if THOU 
  takest her PATH , look not back , for the ghost be behind THEE ... 

 when we slowly recover from the tumult and passion of some violent 
 distress , a peculiar stillness fall upon the mind , and the atmosphere 
 around it become in that stillness appallingly clear . we know not , 
 while wrestle with our woe , the extent of its ravage . as a land the 
 day after a flood , as a field the day after a battle , be the sight 
 of our own sorrow , when we no long have to steer its raging , but to 
 endure the destruction it have make . distinct before Caroline Montfort 's 
 vision stretch the waste of her misery - the Past , the Present , the 
 Future , all seem to blend in one single Desolation . a strange thing it 
 be how all time will converge itself , as it be , into the burning - glass 
 of a moment ! there run a popular superstition that it be thus , in the 
 instant of death ; that our whole existence crowd itself on the glazing 
 eye - a panorama of all we have do on earth just as the soul restore 
 to the earth its garment . certe , there be hour in our being , long 
 before the last and dread one , when this phenomenon come to warn 
 we that , if memory be always active , time would be never go . 
 Rose before this woman - who , whatever the justice of Darrell 's bitter 
 reproach , have a nature lovely enough to justify his anguish at her 
 loss - the image of herself at that turning point of life , when the 
 morning mist be dim on our way , yet when a path choose be a fate 
 decide . yes ; she have excuse , not urge to the judge who sentence , nor 
 estimate to their full extent by the stern equity with which , amidst 
 suffering and wrath , he have desire to weigh her cause . 

 Caroline 's mother , Mrs. Lindsay , be one of those parent who acquire 
 an extraordinary influence over their child by the union of caress 
 manner with obstinate resolve . she never lose control of her temper 
 nor hold on her object . a slight , delicate , languid creature too , who 
 would be sure to go into a consumption if unkindly cross . with much 
 strong common sense , much knowledge of human nature , egotistical , 
 worldly , scheming , heartless , but withal so pleasing , so gentle , so 
 bewitchingly despotic , that it be like live with an electrobiologist , 
 who unnerve you by a look to knock you down with a feather . in only one 
 great purpose of her life have Mrs. Lyndsay fail . when Darrell , rich 
 by the reward of his profession and the bequest of his namesake , have 
 enter Parliament , and rise into that repute which confer solid and 
 brilliant station , Mrs. Lyndsay conceive the idea of appropriating to 
 herself his honour and his wealth by a second hyman . have so long 
 be domesticate in his house during the life of Mrs. Darrell , an 
 intimacy as of near relation have be establish between they . her 
 soft manner attach to she his child ; and after Mrs. Darrell 's 
 death render it necessary that she should find a home of her own , she

 more frequently before Darrell 's eye , and consult by he yet more 
 frequently , than when actually a resident in his house . to her Darrell 
 confide the proposal which have be make to he by the old Marchioness 
 of Montfort , for an alliance between her young grandson and his sole 
 survive child . wealthy as be the House of Vipont , it be amongst 
 its traditional maxim that wealth waste if not perpetually recruit . 
 every third generation , at farth , it be the duty of that house to 
 marry an heiress . Darrell 's daughter , just seventeen , not yet bring 
 out , would be an heiress , if he please to make she so , second to 
 none whom the research of the Marchioness have detect within the 
 drawing - room and nursery of the three kingdom . the proposal of the 
 venerable peeress be at first very naturally gratify to Darrell . it 
 be an euthanasia for the old knightly race to die into a House that 
 be an institution in the empire , and revive phoenix - like in a line of 
 peer , who might perpetuate the name of the heiress whose quartering 
 they would annex to their own , and sign themselves " Darrell Montfort . " 
 say Darrell inly , " on the whole , such a marriage would have please 
 my poor father . " it do not please Mrs. Lyndsay . the bulk of Darrell 's 
 fortune thus settle away , he himself would be a very different match 
 for Mrs. Lyndsay ; nor be it to her convenience that Matilda should be 
 thus hastily dispose of , and the strong link of connection between 
 Fulham and Carlton Gardens sever . Mrs. Lyndsay have one golden rule , 
 which I respectfully point out to lady who covet popularity and power : 
 she never speak ill of any one whom she wish to injure . she do not , 
 therefore , speak ill of the Marquess to Darrell , but she so praise he 
 that her praise alarm . she ought to know the young peer well ; she be 
 a good deal with the Marchioness , who like her pretty manner . till 
 then , Darrell have only notice this green Head of the Viponts as a 
 neat - look head , too modest to open its lip . but he now examine the 
 Head with anxious deliberation , and find it of the poor possible 
 kind of wood , with a heart to match , Guy Darrell have the audacity to 
 reject , though with great courtesy , the idea of graft the last plant 
 of his line on a stem so pithless . though , like man who be at once very 
 affectionate and very busy , he see few fault in his child , or indeed

 the Marquess , who obey his grandmother from habit , and who have

 and what be Caroline 's real sentiment for Guy Darrell ? she understand

 a project so sly ; he must know you too well , pretty innocent . " no match 
 for Gabrielle Desmarets , Matilda fling from the house , leave Jasper 
 whistle an air from Figaro ; return alone to the french town from

 every epithet that just indignation could suggest to a feeble mind . the

 but it be in vain to ask from any man that which be not in he ! Lord

 that be perhaps the long speech Lord Montfort ever address to his

 another 's wife . in that high and narrow sphere of the great London

 but to Caroline Montfort , and to she alone , the secret of a career

 at last she become free once more ; and then she have dare thoroughly to

 CHAPTER III . 

  whatever the number of a man 's friend , there will be times in his 
  life when he have one TOO FEW ; but if he have only one ENEMY , he be 
  lucky indeed if he have not one TOO many . 

 a cold night ; sharp frost ; winter set in . the shutter be closed , the 
 curtain draw , the fire burn clear , and the light be softly shade 
 in Alban Morley 's drawing - room . 

 the old bachelor be at home again . he have return that day ; send

 down - eyed and listless , he sink into that stupor of despondency which so 
 drearily simulate the calm of resignation . 

 " I have but one wish now , " say he , " and that be to change at once into 
 some regiment on active service . I do not talk of court danger 
 and seek death . that would be either a senseless commonplace , or a 
 threat , as it be , to Heaven ! but I need some vehemence of action - some 
 positive and irresistible call upon honour or duty that may force I 
 to contend against this strange heaviness that settle down on my whole 
 life . therefore , I entreat you so to arrange for I , and break it to Mr. 
 Darrell in such term as may not needlessly pain he by the obtrusion

 nothing could move he from resolve in which he have entrench , as in a 
 citadel , his pride or his creed of honour , I be sure that he would take

 another 's . " 

 " you do he justice there , " cry Alban ; " you be a noble fellow to 
 understand he so well ! Sir , you have in you the stuff that make 
 english gentleman such generous soldier . " 

 " action , action , action , " exclaim Lionel . " Strife , strife ! no other 
 chance of cure . rest be so crush , solitude so dismal . " 

 lo ! how contrast the effect of a similar cause of grief at different 
 stage of life ! chase the first day - dream of our youth , and we cry , 
 " action - Strife ! " in that cry , unconsciously to ourselves , HOPE speak 
 and proffer world of emotion not yet exhaust . disperse the last 
 golden illusion in which the image of happiness cheat our experienced 
 manhood , and HOPE be silent ; she have no more world to offer - unless , 
 indeed , she drop her earthly attribute , change she less solemn name , 
 and float far out of sight as " faith ! " 

 Alban make no immediate reply to Lionel ; but , seat himself more 
 comfortably in his chair - plant his foot still more at ease upon his 
 fender - the kindly Man of the World silently revolve all the possible 
 mean by which Darrell might yet be soften and Lionel render happy . 
 his reflection dismay he . " be there ever such untoward luck , " he 
 say at last , and peevishly , " that out of the whole world you should

  prejudice if you please   must be mail in adamant ! convince , and 
 apparently with every reason , that she be not his daughter 's child , but ,

 kinsman 's bride ? how can we expect it ? " 

 " but , " say Lionel , " if , on farth investigation , she prove to be his 
 daughter 's child - the sole survive representative of his line and 
 name ? " 

 " his name ! no ! of the name of Losely - the name of that turbulent 
 sharper , who may yet die on the gibbet - of that poor , dear , lovable

 " alas ! she see but Sophy 's exquisite , simple virtue , and inborn grace ;

 " her own ! " 

 " yes ; I see it all now . " 

 " see what ? you puzzle I . " 

 " I tell you that Darrell , in his letter to I , write with great 
 bitterness of Lady Montfort . " 

 " very natural that he should . who would not resent such interference ? " 

 " listen . I tell you that , at his own command , I send to she that letter ;

 " why ? " 

 " because he who be in love have a wondrous intuition into all the 
 mystery of love in other ; and when I read Darrell 's letter I feel

 vehemently reproach . " 

 " ha ! " say the Man of the World , intimate with Guy Darrell from his 
 school - day - " ha ! be it possible ! and they say that I know everything ! 
 you be sanguine , - I understand . yes , if your belief be true - if

 your hand , my dear boy ; I see hope for you at last . for if the sole

 unconquered memory of a woman like Lady Montfort   where , indeed , she 
 equal in beauty , in disposition so akin to his own ideal of womanly 
 excellence ?   - and if she too have some correspondent sentiment for he , 
 why then , indeed , you might lose all chance of be Darrell 's sole 
 heir ; your Sophy might forfeit the hateful claim to be the sole scion 
 on his ancient tree ; but it be precisely by those loss that Lionel 
 Haughton might gain the bride he covet ; and if this girl prove to be 
 what these Loselys affirm , that very marriage , which be now so repugnant

 you would receive a fortune sufficiently ample to restore your ancestral 
 station ; your career will add honour to fortune . yes , yes ; that be the 
 sole way out of all these difficulty . Darrell must marry again ; Lady 
 Montfort must be his wife . Lionel shall be free to choose she whom Lady 
 Montfort approve - be friend - no matter what her birth ; and I - I - Alban 
 Morley - shall have an arm - chair by two smile hearth . " 

 at this moment there be hear a violent ring at the bell , a loud knock 
 at the street door ; and presently , follow close on the servant , and 
 push he aside as he ask what name to announce , a woman , severely 
 dress in irongrey , with a strongly - mark and haggard countenance ,

 " Guy Darrell ! " exclaim Lionel , overhear she , despite the undertone 
 of her voice . 

 " who be you ? " she say , turn fiercely ; " be you one of his family ? " 

 " his kinsman - almost his adopt son - Mr. Lionel Haughton , " say the

 little cause I have to care for he or his . look again ; I be that

 BOOK X. 




 CHAPTER I. 

 BRUTE - FORCE . 

 we leave Jasper Losely rest for the night at the small town near 
 Fawley . the next morning he walk on to the old Manor - house . it be the 
 same morning in which Lady Montfort have hold her painful interview with 
 Darrell ; and just when losely near the gate that lead into the small 
 park , he see she re - enter the hire vehicle in wait for she . as the 
 carriage rapidly drive past the miscreant , Lady Montfort look forth 
 from the window to snatch a last look at the scene still so clear to 
 she , through eye blind by despair tear . Jasper thus catch sight 
 of her countenance , and recognise she , though she do not even notice 
 he . surprised at the sight , he halt by the paling . what could have 
 bring Lady Montfort there ? could the intimacy his fraud have break off 
 so many year ago be renew ? if so , why the extreme sadness on the face 
 of which he have catch but a hurried , rapid glance ? be that as it might , 
 it be no long of the interest to he it have once be ; and after 
 ponder on the circumstance a minute or two , he advance to the gate . 
 but while his hand be on the latch , he again pause ; how should he 
 obtain admission to Darrell ? - how announce himself ? if in his own name , 
 would not exclusion be certain ? - if as a stranger on business , would 
 Darrell be sure to receive he ? as he be thus cogitate , his ear , 
 which , with all his other organ of sense , be constitutionally fine as 
 a savage 's , catch sound of a faint rustle among the bough of a thick 
 copse which cover a part of the little park , terminate at its pale . 
 the rustle come near and nearer ; the branch be rudely displace ; 
 and in a few moment more Guy Darrell himself come out from the copse , 
 close by the gate , and open it quickly , stand face to face with his 
 abhorrent son - in - law . Jasper be startle , but the opportunity be 
 not to be lose . " Mr. Darrell , " he say , " I come here again to see you ; 
 vouchsafe I , this time , a calm hearing . " so change be losely , so 
 absorb in his own emotion Darrell , that the word do not at once 
 waken up remembrance . " another time , " say Darrell , hastily move on 
 into the road ; " I be not at leisure now . " " pardon I , now , " say losely , 
 unconsciously bring himself back to the tone and bearing of his 
 early and more civilised year . " you do not remember I , sir ; no 
 wonder . but my name be Jasper Losely . " 

 Darrell halt ; then , as if spellbound , look fixedly at the 
 broad - shoulder burly frame before he , case in its coarse pea - jacket , 
 and in that rude form , and that defeature , bloated face , detect , 
 though with strong effort , the wreck of the masculine beauty which have 
 ensnare his deceitful daughter . Jasper could not have select a more 
 unpropitious moment for his cause . Darrell be still too much under the 
 influence of recent excitement and immense sorrow for that supremacy of 
 prudence over passion which could alone have make he a willing listener 
 to overture from Jasper Losely . and about the man whose connection 
 with himself be a thought of such bitter shame , there be now so 
 unmistakably the air of settled degradation , that all Darrell 's 
 instinct of gentleman be revolt - just at the very time , too , when 
 his pride have be most chafe and assail by the obtrusion of all that 
 render most gall to he the very name of Jasper Losely . what ! 
 be it that man 's asserted child whom Lionel Haughton desire as 
 a wife ? - be the alliance with that man to be thus renew and 
 strengthen ? - that man have another claim to he and his in right of 
 parentage to the bride of his near kinsman ? what ! be it that man 's 
 child whom he be ask to recognise as of his own flesh and blood ? - the 
 last representative of his line ? that man ! - that ! a flash shot from 
 his bright eye , deepen its grey into dark ; and , turn on his heel , 
 Darrell say , through his compress lip - 

 " you have hear , sir , I believe , through Colonel Morley , that only on 
 condition of your permanent settlement in one of our distant colony , 
 or America if you prefer it , would I consent to assist you . I be of the 
 same mind still . I can not parley with you myself . Colonel Morley be 
 abroad , I believe . I refer you to my solicitor ; you have see he year 
 ago ; you know his address . no more , sir . " 

 " this will not do , Mr. Darrell , " say losely , doggedly ; and , plant 
 himself right before Darrell 's way , " I have come here on purpose to have 
 all difference out with you , face to face - and I will - " 

 " you will ! " say Darrell , pale with haughty anger , and with the impulse 
 of his passion , his hand clench . in the bravery of his nature , and 
 the warmth of a temper constitutionally quick , he think nothing of the 
 strength and bulk of the insolent obtruder - nothing of the peril of odd 
 so unequal in a personal encounter . but the dignity which pervade all 
 his habit , and often supply to he the place of discretion , come , 
 happily for himself , to his aid now . he strike a man whom he so 
 despise ! - he raise that man to his own level by the honour of a blow 
 from his hand ! impossible ! " you will ! " he say . " well , be it so . be you 
 come again to tell I that a child of my daughter live , and that you 
 win my daughter 's fortune by a deliberate lie ? " 

 " I be not come to speak of that girl , but of myself . I say that I have 
 a claim on you , Mr. Darrell ; I say that turn and twist the truth as you 
 will , you be still my father - in - law , and that it be intolerable that I 
 should be want bread , or drive into actual robbery , while my wife 's 
 father be a man of countless wealth , and have no heir except - but I 
 will not now urge that child 's cause ; I be content to abandon it if so 
 obnoxious to you . do you wish I to cut a throat , and to be hang , 
 and all the world to hear the last die speech and confession of Guy 
 Darrell 's son - in - law ? answer I , sir ? " 

 " I answer you briefly and plainly . it be simply because I would not have 
 that last disgrace on Guy Darrell 's name that I offer you a subsistence 
 in land where you will be less expose to those temptation which 
 induce you to invest the sum that , by your own tale , have be obtain 
 from I on false pretence , in the sink of a Paris gambling house . a 
 subsistence that , if it do not pamp vice , at least place you beyond 
 the necessity of crime , be at your option . choose it or reject it as you 
 will . " 

 " look you , Mr. Darrell , " say Jasper , whose temper be fast give way 
 beneath the cold and gall scorn with which he be thus cast aside , 
 " I be in a state so desperate , that , rather than starve , I may take what 
 you so contemptuously fle to - your daughter 's husband ; but - " 

 " Knave ! " cry Darrell , interrupt he , " do you again and again urge 
 it as a claim upon I , that you decoy from her home , under a false 
 name , my only child ; that she die in a foreign land - break - hearted , if 
 I have rightly hear be that a claim upon your dupe victim 's father ? " 

 " it seem so , since your pride be compel to own that the world would 
 deem it one , if the jail chaplain take down the last word of your 
 son - in - law ! but , basta , basta ! hear I out , and spare hard name ; for 
 the blood be mount into my brain , and I may become dangerous . have any 
 other man eye , and scoff , and rail at I as you have do , he 
 would be lie dead and dumb as this stone at my foot ; but you - be my 
 father - in - law ! now , I care not to bargain with you what be the precise 
 amount of my stipend if I obey your wish , and settle miserably in one 
 of those raw , comfortless corner into which they who burthen this Old 
 World be thrust out of sight . I would rather live my time out in this 
 country - live it out in peace and for half what you may agree to give in 
 transport I . if you be to do anything for I , you have well do 
 it so as to make I content on easy term to your own pocket , rather 
 than to leave I dissatisfied , and willing to annoy you , which I could 
 do somehow or other , even on the far side of the Herring Pond . I might 
 keep to the letter of a bargain , live in Melbourne or Sydney , and take 
 your money , and yet molest and trouble you by deputy . that girl , for 
 instance - your grandchild ; well , well , disown she if you please ; but 
 if I find out where she be , which I own I have not do yet , I might 
 contrive to render she the plague of your life , even though I be in 
 Australia . "

 Mr. Cutts hold a very peculiar position in that section of the great 
 world to which he belong . he possess the advantage of an education
