 secure an unwitnessed interview - should he , instead , alarm the 
 household , it would be a proof of the integrity of his intention that 
 he have no weapon save those which nature bestow on the wild man as the 
 mighty of her wild beast . at night he mount his horse , but go 
 out of his way , keep the high - road for an hour or two , in order to 
 allow ample time for the farmer to have quit the rent - feast , and the 
 old Manor - house to be hush in sleep . at last , when he judge the coast 
 clear and the hour ripe , he wind back into the lane towards Fawley ; 
 and when the spire of its hamlet - church come in sight through the 
 frosty starlit air , he dismount - lead the horse into one of the thick 
 beech - wood that make the prevail characteristic of the wild country 
 round that sequester dwelling - place - fasten the animal to a tree , 
 and stalk towards the park - pale on foot . lightly , as a wolf enter 
 a sheepfold , he swing himself over the moss - grow fence ; he gain the 
 buttress of the great raw pile ; high and clear above , from Darrell 's 
 chamber , stream the light ; all the rest of the old house be close 
 and dark , bury no doubt in slumber . 

 he be now in the hollow of the skeleton pile ; he mount the ladder ; 
 the lock of the door before he yield to his rude implement but artful 
 hand . he be in the long gallery ; the moonlight come broad and clear 
 through the large casement . what wealth of art be on the wall ! but how 
 profitless to the robber 's greed . there , through the very hall which 
 the master have build in the day of his ambition , say to himself , 
 " these be for far posterity , " the step of violence , it may be of 
 Murder , take its stealthy way to the room of the childless man ! through 
 the uncompleted pile , towards the uncompleted life , stride the terrible 
 step . 

 the last door yield noiselessly . the small wooden corridor , narrow 
 as the drawbridge which in ancient fortress be swing between the 
 commandant 's room in the topmost story and some oppose wall , be before 
 he . and Darrell 's own door be half open ; light on the table - log 
 burn bright on the hearth . Cautiously losely look through the 
 aperture . Darrell be not there ; the place be solitary ; but the 
 opposite door be open also . losely 's fine ear catch the sound of a 
 slight movement of a footstep in the room just below , to which that 
 opposite door admit . in an instant the robber glide within the 
 chamber - closed and lock the door by which he have enter , retain 
 the key about his person . the next stride bring he to the hearth . 
 beside it hang the bell - rope common in old - fashioned house . losely 
 look round ; on the table , by the writing implement , lie a pen - knife . 
 in another moment the rope be cut , high out of Darrell 's reach , and 
 fling aside . the hearth , be adapt but for logwood fire , furnish 
 not those implement in which , at a moment of need , the owner may find 
 an available weapon - only a slight pair of brass wood - pincer , and a 
 shovel equally frail . such as they be , however , Jasper quietly remove 
 and hide they behind a heavy old bureau . step be now hear mount 
 the stair that lead into the chamber ; losely shrink back into the recess 
 beside the mantelpiece . Darrell enter , with a book in his hand , for 
 which he have indeed quit his chamber - a volume contain the last 
 Act of Parliament relate to Public Trusts , which have be send to he 
 by his solicitor ; for he be create a deed of trust , to insure to 
 the nation the Darrell antiquity , in the name of his father , the 
 antiquarian . 

 Darrell advance to the writing - table , which stand in the centre of the 
 room ; lay down the book , and sigh - the short , quick , impatient sigh 
 which have become one of his peculiar habit . the robber steal from the 
 recess , and , glide round to the door by which Darrell have enter , 
 while the back of the master be still towards he , set fast the lock , 
 and appropriate the key as he have do at the door which have admit 
 himself . though the noise in that operation be but slight , it rouse 
 darrell from his abstract thought . he turn quickly , and at the same 
 moment losely advance towards he . 

 at once Darrell comprehend his danger . his rapid glance take in 
 all the precaution by which the intruder proclaim his lawless 
 purpose - the closed door , the bell rope cut off . there , between those 
 four secret wall , must pass the interview between himself and the 
 desperado . he be unarmed , but he be not daunt . it be but man to 
 man . losely have for he his vast physical strength , his penury , despair , 
 and vindictive purpose . Darrell have in his favour the intellect which 
 give presence of mind ; the energy of nerve , which be no more to be see 
 in the sinew and bone than the fluid which fell can be see in the jar 
 and the wire ; and that superb kind of pride , which , if terror be feel , 
 make its action impossible , because a disgrace , and bravery a matter of 
 course , simply because it be honour . 

 as the bravo approach , by a calm and slight movement Darrell draw to 
 the other side of the table , place that obstacle between himself 
 and losely , and , extend his arm , say : " hold , sir ; I forbid you 
 to advance another step . you be here , no matter how , to re - urge your 
 claim on I . be seat ; I will listen to you . " 

 Darrell 's composure take losely so by surprise that mechanically he 
 obey the command thus tranquilly lay upon he , and sink into a 
 chair - face Darrell with a sinister under - look from his sullen brow . 
 " ah ! " he say , " you will listen to I now ; but my term have rise . " 

 Darrell , who have also seat himself , make no answer ; but his face be 
 resolute and his eye watchful . the ruffian resume , in a gruffer tone : 
 " my term have rise , Mr. Darrell . " 

 " have they , sir ? and why ? " 

 " why ! because no one can come to your aid here ; because here you can not 
 escape ; because here you be in my power ! " 

 " rather , sir , I listen to you because here you be under my roof - tree ; 
 and it be you who be in my power ! " 

 " your ! look round ; the door be lock on you . perhaps you think 
 your shout , your cry might bring aid to you . attempt it - raise your 
 voice - and I strangle you with these hand . " 

 " if I do not raise my voice , it be , first , because I should be ashamed 
 of myself if I require aid against one man ; and , secondly , because I 
 would not expose to my dependent a would - be assassin in he whom my 
 lose child call husband . Hush , sir , hush , or your own voice will alarm 
 those who sleep below . and now , what be it you ask ? be plain , sir , and 
 be brief . " 

 " well , if you like to take matter coolly , I have no objection . these 
 be my term . you have receive large sum this day ; those sum be in 
 your house , probably in that bureau ; and your life be at my will . " 

 " you ask the monie pay for rent to - day . true , they be in the house ; 
 but they be not in my apartment . they be receive by another ; they 
 be keep by another . in vain , through the winding and passage of this 
 old house , would you seek to find the room in which he store they . in 
 do so you will pass by the door of a servant who sleep so lightly 
 that the chance be that he will hear you ; he be arm with a 
 blunderbuss , and with pistol . you say to I , ' your money or your life . ' 
 I say to you , in reply , ' neither : attempt to seize the money , and your 
 own life be lose . " 

 " Miser ! I do not   believe that sum so large be not in your own keeping . 
 and even if they be not , you shall show I where they be ; you shall 
 lead I through those winding and passage of which you so tenderly 
 warn I , my hand on your throat . and if servant wake , or danger 
 threaten I , it be you who shall save I , or die ! ha ! you do not fear 
 I - eh , Mr. Darrell ! " and losely rise . 

 " I do not fear you , " reply Darrell , still seat . " I can not conceive 
 that you be here with no other design than a profitless murder . you be 
 here , you say , to make term ; it will be time enough to see whose life 
 be endanger when all your proposition have be state . as yet 
 you have only suggest a robbery , to which you ask I to assist you . 
 impossible ! Grant even that you be able to murder I , you would be 
 just as far off from your booty . and yet you say your term have rise ! 
 to I they seem fall to nothing ! have you anything else to say ? " 

 the calmness of Darrell , so supremely display in this irony , begin to 
 tell upon the ruffian - the magnetism of the great man 's eye and voice , 
 and steadfast courage , gradually gain power over the wild , inferior 
 animal . try to recover his constitutional audacity , Jasper say , with 
 a tone of the old rollick voice : " well , Mr. Darrell , it be all one to 
 I how I wring from you , in your own house , what you refuse I when I 
 be a suppliant on the road . fair mean be pleasanter than foul . I be a 
 gentleman - the grandson of Sir Julian Losely , of Losely Hall ; I be your 
 son - in - law ; and I be starve . this must not be ; write I a cheque . " 

 Darrell dip his pen in the ink , and draw the paper towards he . 

 " Oho ! you do not   fear I , eh ? this be not do from fear , mind - all out 
 of pure love and compassion , my kind father - in - law . you will write I a 
 cheque for five thousand pound - come , I be moderate - your life be worth 
 a precious deal more than that . hand I the cheque - I will trust to your 
 honour to give I no trouble in cash it , and bid you good - night - my 
 father - in - law . " 

 as losely cease with a mocking laugh , Darrell spring up quickly , throw 
 open the small casement which be within his reach , and fling from it 
 the paper on which he have be write , and which he wrapt round the 
 heavy armorial seal that lie on the table . 

 losely bound towards he . " what mean that ? what have you do ? " 

 " save your life and mine , Jasper Losely , " say Darrell , solemnly , and 
 catch the arm that be raise against he . " we be now upon equal 
 term . " 

 " I understand , " growl the tiger , as the slaver gather to his 
 lip - " you think by that paper to summon some one to your aid . " 

 " not so - that paper be useless while I live . look forth - the moonlight 
 be on the roof below - can you see where that paper have fall ? on the 
 ledge of a parapet that your foot could not reach . it face the window 
 of a room in which one of my household sleep ; it will meet his eye in 
 the morning when the shutter be unbarred ; and on that paper be 
 writ these word : ' if I be this night murder , the murderer be Jasper 
 Losely , ' and the paper be sign by my name . back , sir - would you doom 
 yourself to the gibbet ? " 

 Darrell release the dread arm he have arrest , and losely star at 
 he , amazed , bewildered . 

 Darrell resume : " and now I tell you plainly that I can accede to 
 no term put to I thus . I can sign my hand to no order that you may 
 dictate , because that would be to sign myself a coward - and my name be 
 Darrell ! " 

 " down on your knee , proud man - sign you shall , and on your knee ! I 
 care not now for gold - I care not now a rush for my life . I come here to 
 humble the man who from first to last have so scornfully humble I . - and 
 I will , I will ! on your knee - on your knee ! " 

 the robber fling himself forward ; but Darrell , whose eye have never 
 quit the foe , be prepare for and elude the rush . losely , miss 
 his object , lose his balance , strike against the edge of the table which 
 partially interpose between himself and his prey , and be only save 
 from fall by the close neighbourhood of the wall , on which he come 
 with a shock that for the moment well - nigh stun he . meanwhile 
 Darrell have gain the hearth , and snatch from it a large log 
 half - burning . Jasper , recover himself , dash the long matted hair 
 from his eye , and , see undismaye the formidable weapon with which 
 he be menace , cower for a second and deadly spring . 

 " stay , stay , stay , parricide and madman ! " cry Darrell , his eye 
 flash bright than the brand . " it be not my life I plead for - it be 
 your . remember , if I fall by your hand , no hope and no refuge be leave 
 to you ! in the name of my dead child , and under the eye of avenge 
 Heaven , I strike down the fury that blind you , and I scare back your 
 soul from the abyss ! " 

 so ineffably grand be the man 's look and gesture - so full of sonorous 
 terror the swell of his matchless all - conquer voice , that losely , in 
 his midmost rage , stand awed and spellbound . his breast heave , his eye 
 fall , his frame collapse , even his very tongue seem to cleave to the 
 parch roof of his mouth . whether the effect so suddenly produce might 
 have continue , or whether the startled miscreant might not have 
 lash himself into renew wrath and inexpiable crime , pass out of 
 conjecture . at that instant simultaneously be hear hurried footstep 
 in the corridor without , violent blow on the door , and voice 
 exclaim , " open , open ! - Darrell , Darrell ! " - while the bell at the 
 portal of the old house ring fast and shrill . 

 " Ho - be it so ? " growl losely , recover himself at those unwelcome 
 sound . " but do not think that I will be catch thus , like a rat in a 
 trap . no - I will - " 

 " Hist ! " interrupt Darrell , drop the brand , and advance quickly 
 on the ruffian - " Hist ! - let no one know that my daughter 's husband come 
 here with a felon 's purpose . sit down - down I say ; it be for my house 's 
 honour that you should be safe . " and suddenly place both hand on 
 Losely 's broad shoulder , he force he into a seat . during these few 
 hurry word , the stroke at the door and the shout without have be 
 continue , and the door shake on its yielding hinge . 

 " the key - the key ! " whisper Darrell . 

 but the bravo be stupefy by the suddenness with which his rage 
 have be cow , his design baffle , his position change from the 
 man dictate law and threaten life , to the man protect by his 
 intend victim . and he be so slow in even comprehend the meaning of 
 Darrell 's order , that Darrell have scarcely snatch the key less from 
 his hand than from the pouch to which he at last mechanically point , 
 when the door be burst open , and Lionel Haughton , Alban Morley , and 
 the Colonel 's servant be in the room . not one of they , at the first 
 glance , perceive the inmate of the chamber , who be at the right of 
 their entrance , by the angle of the wall and in shadow . but out come 
 Darrell 's calm voice : 

 " Alban ! Lionel ! - welcome always ; but what bring you hither at such an 
 hour , with such clamour ? armed too ! " 

 the three man stand petrify . there sate , peaceably enough , a large 
 dark form , its hand on its knee , its head bend down , so that the 
 feature be not , distinguishable ; and over the chair in which this 
 bend figure be thus confusedly gather up leant Guy Darrell , with 
 quiet ease - no trace of fear nor of past danger in his face , which , 
 though very pale , be serene , with a slight smile on the firm lip . 

 " well , " mutter Alban Morley , slowly lower his pistol - " well , I be 
 surprised ! - yes , for the first time in twenty year , I and surprised ! " 

 " surprised perhaps to find I at this hour still up , and with a person 
 upon business - the door lock . however , mutual explanation later . of 
 course you stay here to night . my business with this - this visitor , be 
 now over . Lionel , open that door - here be the key . - Sir " -   he touch 
 losely by the shoulder , and whisper in his ear , " rise and speak 
 not , "   -   aloud   " Sir , I need not detain you long . allow I to show you 
 the way out of this ramble old house . " 

 Jasper rise like one half asleep , and , still bend his form and hiding 
 his face , follow Darrell down the private stair , through the study , 
 the library , into the hall , the Colonel 's servant light the way ; and 
 Lionel and Morley , still too amazed for word , bring up the rear . the 
 servant draw the heavy bolt from the front door ; and now the household 
 have catch alarm . mill first appear with the blunderbuss , then the 
 footman , then Fairthorn . 

 " stand back , there ! " cry Darrell , and he open the door himself to 
 losely . " Sir , " say he then , as they stand in the moonlight , " mark that 
 I tell you truly - you be in my power ; and if the event of this night 
 can lead you to acknowledge a watchful providence , and recall with a 
 shudder the crime from which you have be save , why , then , I too , out 
 of gratitude to Heaven , may think of mean by which to free other from 
 the peril of your despair . " 

 losely make no answer , but slunk off with a fast , furtive stride , 
 hasten out of the moonlit sward into the gloom of the leafless tree . 




 CHAPTER II . 

  if the LION ever wear the FOX 's HIDE , still he wear it as the LION . 

 when Darrell be alone with Lionel and Alban Morley , the calm with which 
 he have before startle they vanish . he pour out his thank with deep 
 emotion . " forgive I ; not in the presence of a servant could I say , ' you 
 have save I from an unnatural strife , and my daughter 's husband from 
 a murderer 's end . ' but by what wondrous mercy do you learn my danger ? 
 be you send to my aid ? " 

 Alban briefly explain . " you may judge , " he say in conclusion , " how 
 great be our anxiety , when , follow the instruction of our guide , 
 while our driver ring his alarum at the front portal , we make our 
 entrance into yon rib of stone , find the door already open , and 
 fear we might be too late . but , meanwhile , the poor woman wait 
 without in the carriage that bring we from the station . I must go and 
 relieve her mind . " 

 " and bring she hither , " cry Darrell , " to receive my gratitude . stay , 
 Alban ; while you leave I with she , you will speak aside to Mills ; tell 
 he that you hear there be an attempt to be make on the house , and 
 come to frustrate it , but that your fear be exaggerated ; the man be 
 more a half - insane mendicant than a robber . be sure , at least , that his 
 identity with losely be not surmise , and bid Mills treat the affair 
 lightly . public man be expose , you know , to assault from crackbraine 
 enthusiast ; or stay - I once be a lawyer , and "   continue Darrell , 
 whose irony have become so integral an attribute of his mind as to be 
 proof against all trial   " there be man so out of their wit as to fancy 
 a lawyer have ruin they ! Lionel , tell poor Dick Fairthorn to come to 
 I . " when the musician enter , Darrell whisper to he : " go back to 
 your room - open your casement - step out on to the parapet - you will see 
 something white ; it be a scrap of paper wrap round my old armorial 
 seal . bring it to I just as it be , Dick . that poor young Lionel , we 
 must keep he here a day or two ; mind , no prickle for he , Dick . " 




 CHAPTER III . 

  ARABELLA CRANE versus GUY DARRELL ; or , WOMAN versus LAWYER . in the 
  COURTS , LAWYER would WIN ; but in a private PARLOUR , foot to foot , 
  and TONGUE TO TONGUE , LAWYER have not a CHANCE . 

 Arabella Crane enter the room : Darrell hesitate - the remembrance 
 attach to she be so painful and repugnant . but do he not now owe to 
 she perhaps his very life ? he pass his hand rapidly over his brow , 
 as if to sweep away all early recollection , and , advance quickly , 
 extend that hand to she . the stern woman shake her head , and reject 
 the proffered greeting . 

 " you owe I no thank , " she say , in her harsh , ungracious accent ; " I 
 seek to save not you , but he . " 

 " how ! " say Darrell , startle ; " you feel no resentment against the man 
 who injure and betray you ? " 

 " what my feeling may be towards he be not for you to conjecture ; man 
 could not conjecture they ; I be woman . what they once be I might 
 blush for ; what they be now , I could own without shame . but you , Mr. 
 Darrell , - you , in the hour of my uttermost anguish , when all my future 
 be lay desolate , and the world lie crush at my foot - you - man , 
 chivalrous man ! - you have for I no human compassion - you thrust I in 
 scorn from your door - you see in my woe nothing but my error - you send 
 I forth , strip of reputation , brand by your contempt , to famine or 
 to suicide . and you wonder that I feel less resentment against he who 
 wrong I than against you , who , know I wrong , only disdain 
 my grief . the answer be plain - the scorn of the man she only reverence 
 leaf to a woman no memory to mitigate its bitterness and gall . the 
 wrong inflict by the man she love may leave , what they have leave to 
 I , an undying sense of a past existence - radiant , joyous , hopeful ; of 
 a time when the earth seem cover with blossom , just ready to burst 
 into bloom ; when the sky through their haze take the rose - hue as the 
 sun seem about to rise . the memory that I once be happy , at least 
 then , I owe to he who injure and betray I . to you , when happiness 
 be lose to I forever , what do I owe ? tell I . " 

 strike by her word , more by her impressive manner , though not 
 recognise the plea by which the defendant thus raise herself into the 
 accuser , Darrell answer gently " Pardon I ; this be no moment to revive 
 recollection of anger on my part ; but reflect , I entreat you , and you 
 will feel that I be not too harsh . in the same position any other man 
 would not have be less severe . " 

 " any other man ! " she exclaim ; " ay , possibly ! but would the scorn of 
 any other man so have crush self - esteem ? the injury of the wicked 
 do not sour we against the good ; but the scoff of the good leave we 
 malignant against virtue itself . any other man ! tut ! Genius be bind to 
 be indulgent . it should know human error so well - have , with its large 
 luminous force , such error itself when it deign to be human , that , 
 where other may scorn , genius should only pity . " she pause a moment , 
 and then slowly resume . " and pity be my due . have you , or have any one 
 lofty as yourself in reputed honour , but say to I , ' Thou hast sin , 
 thou must suffer ; but sin itself need compassion , and compassion 
 forbid thee to despair , ' why , then , I might have be gentle to the 
 thing of earth , and less steel against the influence of Heaven than 
 I have be . that be all no matter now . Mr. Darrell , I would not part 
 from you with angry and bitter sentiment . Colonel Morley tell I that 
 you have not only let the man , whom we need not name , go free , but that 
 you have guard the secret of his design . for this I thank you . I 
 thank you , because what be leave of that blasted and deform existence I 
 have take into mine . and I would save that man from his own device 
 as I would save my soul from its own temptation . be you large - hearted 
 enough to comprehend I ? look in my face - you have see his ; all earthly 
 love be erase and blot out of both . " 

 Guy Darrell bow his head in respect that partook of awe . 

 " you , too , " say the grim woman , after a pause , and approach he 
 nearer - " you , too , have love , I be tell , and you , too , be forsake . " 

 he recoil and - shudder . 

 " what be leave to your heart of its ancient folly ? I should like to know ! 
 I be curious to learn if there be a man who can feel as woman ! have you 
 only resentment ? have you only disdain ? have you only vengeance ? have 
 you pity ? or have you the jealous absorbing desire , survive the 
 affection from which it sprang , that still the life wrench from you 
 shall owe , despite itself , a melancholy allegiance to your own ? " 

 Darrell impatiently wave his hand to forbid further question ; and it 
 need all his sense of the service this woman have just render he to 
 repress his haughty displeasure at so close an approach to his torturing 
 secret . 

 Arabella 's dark bright eye rest on his knitted brow , for a moment , 
 wistfully , musingly . then she say : " I see ! man 's inflexible pride - no 
 pardon there ! but own , at least , that you have suffer . " 

 " suffer ! " groan Darrell involuntarily , and press his hand to his 
 heart . 

 " you have ! - and you own it ! fellow - sufferer , I have no more anger 
 against you . neither should pity , but let each respect the other . a few 
 word more , - this child ! " 

 " ay - ay - this child ! you will be truthful . you will not seek to deceive 
 I - you know that she - she - claim by that assassin , rear by his 
 convict father - she be no daughter of my line ! " 

 " what ! would it then be no joy to know that your line do not close with 
 yourself - that your child might - " 

 " Cease , madam , cease - it matter not to a man nor to a race when it 
 perish , so that it perish at last with honour . who would have either 
 himself or his lineage live on into a day when the escutcheon be blot 
 and the name disgrace ? no ; if that be Matilda 's child , tell I , and I 
 will bear , as man may do , the last calamity which the will of Heaven may 
 inflict . if , as I have all reason to think , the tale be an imposture , 
 speak and give I the sole comfort to which I would cling amidst the 
 ruin of all other hope . " 

 " verily , " say Arabella , with a kind of musing wonder in the tone of she 
 soften voice ; " verily , have a man 's heart the same throb and fibre as a 
 woman 's ? have I a child like that blue - eyed wanderer with the frail form 
 need protection , and the brave spirit that ennoble softness , what 
 would be my pride ! my bliss ! talk of shame - disgrace ! fie - fie - the more 
 the evil of other darken one so innocent , the more cause to love and 
 shelter she . but - I - be childless ! shall I tell you that the offence 
 which lie heavy on my conscience have be my cruelty to that girl ? 
 she be give an infant to my care . I see in she the daughter of that 
 false , false , mean , deceive friend , who have take my confidence , and 
 buy , with her suppose heritage , the man swear by all oath to I . I 
 see in she , too , your descendant , your rightful heiress . I rejoice in 
 a revenge on your daughter and yourself . think not I would have foist 
 she on your notice ! no . I would have keep she without culture , without 
 consciousness of a high lot ; and when I give she up to her grandsire , 
 the convict , it be a triumph to think that Matilda 's child would be 
 an outcast . terrible thought ! but I be mad then . but that poor convict 
 whom you , in your worldly arrogance , so loftily despise - he take to his 
 breast what be fling away as a worthless weed . and if the flower keep 
 the promise of the bud , never flower so fair bloomed from your vaunted 
 stem ! and yet you would bless I if I say , ' pass on , childless man ; she 
 be nothing to you ! ' " 

 " Madam , let we not argue . as you yourself justly imply , man 's heart 
 and woman 's must each know throb that never be , and never should be , 
 familiar to the other . I repeat my question , and again I implore your 
 answer . " 

 " I can not answer for certain ; and I be fearful of answer at all , lest 
 on a point so important I should mislead you . Matilda 's child ? Jasper 
 affirm it to I . his father believe he - I believe he . I never have 
 the shadow of a doubt till - " 

 " till what ? for Heaven 's sake speak . " 

 " till about five year ago , or somewhat more , I see a letter from 
 Gabrielle Desmarets , and - " 

 " ah ! which make you suspect , as I do , that the child be Gabrielle 
 Desmaret 's daughter . " 

 Arabella rear her crest as a serpent before it strike . " Gabrielle 's 
 daughter ! you think so . her child that I shelter ! her child for whom 
 I have just plead to you ! her ! " she suddenly become silent . evidently 
 that idea have never before strike she ; evidently it now shock she ; 
 evidently something be pass through her mind which do not allow 
 that idea to be dismiss . as Darrell be about to address she , she 
 exclaim abruptly : " no ! say no more now . you may hear from I again 
 should I learn what may decide at least this doubt one way or the other . 
 Farewell , sir . " 

 " not yet . permit I to remind you that you have save the life of a man 
 whose wealth be immense . " 

 " Mr. Darrell , my wealth in relation to my want be perhaps immense as 
 your , for I do not spend what I possess . " 

 " but this unhappy outlaw , whom you would save from himself , can 
 henceforth be to you but a burthen and a charge . after what have pass 
 to - night , I do tremble to think that penury may whisper other house to 
 rob , other life to menace . let I , then , place at your disposal , to be 
 employ in such mode as you deem the good , a sum that may suffice to 
 secure an object which we have in common . " 

 " no , Mr. Darrell , " say Arabella , fiercely ; " whatever he be , never with 
 my consent shall Jasper Losely be beholden to you for alm . if money can 
 save he from shame and a dreadful death , that money shall be mine . I 
 have say it . and , hark you , Mr. Darrell , what be repentance without 
 atonement ? I say not that I repent ; but I do know that I seek to atone . " 

 the iron - grey robe flutter an instant , and then vanish from the 
 room . 

 when Alban Morley return to the library , he see Darrell at the far 
 corner of the room , on his knee . well might Guy Darrell thank Heaven 
 for the mercy vouchsafe to he that night . life preserve ? be that 
 all ? might life yet be better and gladden ? be there aught in the 
 grim woman 's word that might bequeath thought which reflection would 
 ripen into influence over action ? - aught that might suggest the case 
 in which , not ignobly , Pity might subjugate Scorn ? in the royal abode of 
 that Soul , do Pride only fortify Honour ? - be it but the mild king , not 
 the imperial despot ? would it blind , as its rival , the Reason ? would it 
 chain , as a rebel , the heart ? would it man the dominion , that might 
 be serene , by the treasure it waste - by the war it provoke ? 
 self - knowledge ! self - knowledge ! from Heaven , indeed , descend the 
 precept , " know thyself . " that truth be tell to we by the old heathen 
 oracle . but what old heathen oracle have tell we how to know ? 




 CHAPTER IV . 

  the MAN - EATER humiliate . he encounter an old acquaintance in a 
  TRAVELLER , who , like SHAKESPEARE 's JAQUES , be " a MELANCHOLY fellow " ; 
  who also , LIKE JAQUES , HATH " great reason to be bad " ; and WHO , still 
  like JAQUES , be " full of MATTER . " 

 Jasper Losely ride slowly on through the clear frosty night ; not back to 
 the country town which he have leave on his hateful errand , nor into the 
 broad road to London . with a strange desire to avoid the haunt of man , 
 he select - at each choice of way in the many path branch right and 
 leave , between waste and woodland - the lane that seem the narrow 
 and the dim . it be not remorse that gnaw he , neither be it mere 
 mercenary disappointment , nor even the pang of baffle vengeance - it 
 be the profound humiliation of diseased self - love - the conviction that , 
 with all his brute power , he have be powerless in the very time and 
 scene in which he have picture to himself so complete a triumph . even 
 the quiet with which he have escape be a mortifying recollection . 
 capture itself would have be preferable , if capture have be precede 
 by brawl and strife - the exhibition of his hardihood and prowess . 
 gloomily bend over his horse 's neck , he curse himself as fool and 
 coward . what would he have have ! - a new crime on his soul ? perhaps he 
 would have answer , " anything rather than this humiliating failure . " he 
 do not rack his brain with conjecture if Cutts have betray he , 
 or by what other mode assistance have be send in such time of need to 
 Darrell . nor do he feel that hunger for vengeance , whether on Darrell 
 or on his accomplice   should that accomplice have play the traitor , 
 which might have be expect from his characteristic ferocity . on 
 the contrary , the thought of violence and its excitement have in it 
 a sickness as of shame . darrell at that hour might have ride by he 
 scathless . Cutts might have jeer and say , " I blab your secret , and 
 send the aid that foil it " ; and losely would have continue to hang 
 his head , nor lift the herculean hand that lie nerveless on the 
 horse 's mane . be it not commonly so in all reaction from excitement in 
 which self - love have be keenly gall ? do not vanity enter into the 
 lust of crime as into the desire of fame ? 

 at sunrise losely find himself on the high - road into which a labyrinth 
 of lane have lead he , and opposite to a milestone , by which he learn 
 that he have be long turn his back on the metropolis , and that he 
 be about ten mile distant from the provincial city of Ouzelford . by 
 this time his horse be knock up , and his own chronic pain begin to 
 make themselves acutely feel ; so that , when , a little far on , he 
 come to a wayside inn , he be glad to halt ; and after a strong drain , 
 which have the effect of an opiate , he betake himself to bed , and sleep 
 till the noon be far advanced . 

 when losely come down - stair , the common room of the inn be occupy by 
 a meeting of the trustee of the highroad ; and , on demand breakfast , 
 he be show into a small sand parlour adjoining the kitchen . two 
 other occupant - a man and a woman - be there already , seat at a 
 table by the fireside , over a pint of half - and - half . losely , warm 
 himself at the hearth , scarcely notice these humble reveller by a 
 glance . and they , after a displeased stare at the stalwart frame which 
 obscure the cheering glow they have hitherto monopolise , resume a 
 muttered conversation ; of which , as well as of the vile modicum that 
 refresh their lip , the man take the lion 's share . shabbily forlorn 
 be that man 's habiliment - turn and re - turn , patch , darn , 
 weather - stain , grease - stain - but still retain that kind of 
 mouldy , grandiose , bastard gentility , which imply that the wearer have 
 know well day ; and , in the downward progress of fortune when they 
 once fall , may probably know still bad . 

 the woman be some year old than her companion , and still more 
 forlornly shabby . her garment seem literally compose of particle of 
 dust glue together , while her face might have insure her condemnation 
 as a witch before any honest jury in the reign of King James the First . 
 his breakfast , and the brandy - bottle that flank the loaf , be now 
 place before losely ; and , as distastefully he force himself to eat , 
 his eye once more glanced towards , and this time rest on , the shabby 
 man , in the sort of interest with which one knave out of elbow 
 regard another . as Jasper thus look , gradually there steal on he 
 a reminiscence of those coarse large feature - that rusty disreputable 
 wig . the recognition , however , be not mutual ; and presently , after a 
 whisper interchange between the man and the woman , the latter rise , 
 and approach losely , drop a curtsey , and say , in a weird , under 
 voice : " Stranger ! luck 's in store for you . tell your fortune ! " as she 
 speak , from some dust - hole in her garment she produce a pack of card , 
 on whose half - obliterate face seem incrust the dirt of age . 
 thrust these antiquity under Jasper 's nose , she add , " wish and 
 cut . " 

 " Yshaw , " say Jasper , who , though sufficiently superstitious in some 
 matter and in regard to some person , be not so completely under the 
 influence of that imaginative infirmity as to take the creature before 
 he for a sibyl . " get away ; you turn my stomach . your card smell ; so do 
 you ! " 

 " forgive she , worthy sir , " say the man , lean forward . " the hag 
 may be unsavoury , but she be wise . the Three Sisters who accost the 
 Scottish Thane , sir   Macbeth - you have see it on the stage ?   be not 
 savoury . withered , and wild in their attire , sir , but they know a thing 
 or two ! she see luck in your face . cross her hand and give it vent ! " 

 " Fiddledee , " say the irreverent losely . " take she off , or I shall scald 
 she , " and he seize the kettle . 

 the hag retreat grumble ; and losely , soon despatch his meal , 
 place his foot ' on the hob , and begin to meditate what course to adopt 
 for a temporary subsistence . he have break into the last pound leave 
 of the money which he have extract from Mrs. Crane 's purse some day 
 before . he recoil with terror from the thought of return to town 
 and place himself at her mercy . yet what option have he ? while thus 
 musing , he turn impatiently round , and see that the shabby man and the 
 dusty hag be engage in an amicable game of ecarte , with those very 
 card which have so offend his olfactory organ . at that sight the 
 old instinct of the gambler struggle back ; and , raise himself up , he 
 look over the card of the player . the miserable wretch be , of 
 course , play for nothing ; and losely see at a glance that the man 
 be , nevertheless , try to cheat the woman ! positively he take that 
 man into more respect ; and that man , notice the interest with which 
 losely survey the game , look up , and say : 

 " while the time , sir ? what say you ? a game or two ? I can stake my 
 pistoles - that be , sir , so far as a fourpenny bit go . if ignorant of 
 this french game , sir , cribbage or all four ? " 

 " no , " say losely , mournfully ; " there be nothing to be get out of you ; 
 otherwise " - he stop and sigh . " but I have see you under other 
 circumstance . what have become of your Theatrical exhibition ? gamble it 
 away ? yet , from what I see of your play , I think you ought not to have 
 lose , Mr. Rugge . " 

 the ex - manager start . 

 " what ! you know I before the storm ? - before the lightning strike I , 
 as I may say , sir - and fall into difficulty , I became - a wreck ? you 
 know I ? - not of the Company ? - a spectator ? " 

 " as you say - a spectator . you have once in your employ an actor - clever 
 old fellow . Waife , I think , he be call . "

 resettle his foot on the hob , and snatch at any diversion from his

 " Sir , when a gentleman , who be a gentleman , ask as a favour a speciman

 " warm it , Mr. Rugge . help yourself to the brandy - and the lady too . " 

 " Sir , you be a gentleman ; sir , your health . hag , drink well day 
 to we both . that woman , sir , be a hag , but she be an honour to her 
 sex - faithful ! " 

 " it be astonish how faithful lady be when not what be call 
 beautiful . I speak from painful experience , " say losely , grow 
 debonnair as the liquor relax his gloom , and regain that levity of 
 tongue which sometimes stray into wit , and which - spring originally 
 from animal spirit and redundant health - still come to he mechanically 
 whenever rouse by companionship from alternate interval of lethargy 
 and pain . " but , now , Mr. Rugge , I be all ear ; perhaps you will be kind 
 enough to be all tale . " 

 with tragic aspect , unrelaxe by that jeu de mots , and still wholly 
 unrecognise in the massive form and discolour swollen countenance

 " yes ! you do live , " say Jasper , much interested - for how to live at all

 losely , who , in his small way , have all the liberality of a Catiline ,

 in a few minute more , losely be again on horseback ; and as he ride

 opposite direction - shamble on , footsore and limping , along the wide , 
 waste , wintry thoroughfare - vanish from the eye , as their fate 
 henceforth from this story . there they go by the white hard milestone ;

 leafless - cumber the wayside , till the time come to cast it off to

 yon stagnant pool , with the great dung - heap by its side . there the road 
 turn aslant ; the dung - heap hide they . go ! and not a speck on the 
 Immemorial , Universal Thoroughfare . 




 CHAPTER V. 

  no wind so cutting as that which set in the quarter from which the 
  SUN rise . 

 the town to which I lend the disguising name of Ouzelford , which , in 
 year bygone , be represent by Guy Darrell , and which , in year to 
 come , may preserve in its municipal hall his effigy in canvas or 
 stone , be one of the handsome in England . as you approach its suburb 
 from the London Road , it rise clear and wide upon your eye , crown 
 the elevated table - land upon which it be build ; - a noble range of 
 prospect on either side , rich with hedgerow not yet sacrifice to the 
 stern demand of modern agriculture - venerable woodland , and the green 
 pasture round many a rural thane 's frank , hospitable hall ; - no one 
 Great House banish from league of landscape the abode of knight and 
 squire , nor menacing , with " the legitimate influence of property , " the 
 vote of rebellious burgher . everywhere , like finger - post to heaven , 
 you may perceive the church - tower of rural hamlet embosome in 
 pleasant valley , or climb up gentle slope . at the horizon , the blue 
 fantastic outline of girdle hill mingle with the cloud . a famous 
 old cathedral , neighbour by the romantic ivy - grow wall of a ruin 
 castle , soar up from the centre of the town , and dominate the whole 
 survey - calm , as with conscious power . near the town , the villa of 
 merchant and trader , release perhaps from business , skirt the road , 
 with trim garden and shaven lawn . now the small river , or rather 
 rivulet , of Ouzel , from which the town take its name , steal out from 
 deep bank cover with brushwood or aged tree , and widen into brief 
 importance , glide under the arch of an ancient bridge ; run on , clear 
 and shallow , to refresh low fertile dairy - meadow , dot with kine ; and 
 finally quit the view , as brake and copse close round its narrowing , 
 winding way ; and that which , under the city bridge , be an impose 
 noiseless stream , become , amidst rustic solitude , an insignificant 
 babbling brook . 

 from one of the large villa in these charming suburb come forth 
 a gentleman , middle - aged , and of a very mild and prepossess 
 countenance . a young lady without a bonnet , but a kerchief throw over 
 her sleek dark hair , accompany he to the garden - gate , twin both 
 hand affectionately round his arm , and entreat he not to stand in 
 thorough draught and catch cold , nor to step into puddle and wet his 
 foot , and to be sure to be back before dark , as there be such shocking 
 account in the newspaper of person rob and garotte even in the 
 most populous highway ; and , above all , not to listen to the beggar 
 in the street , and allow himself to be take in ; and before finally 
 release he at the gate , she button his greatcoat up to his chin , 
 thrust two pellet of cotton into his ear , and give he a parting kiss . 
 then she watch he tenderly for a minute or so as he stride on with 
 the step of a man who need not all those foster admonition and 
 coddling care . 

 as soon as he be out of sight of the lady and the window of the villa , 
 the gentleman cautiously unbutton his greatcoat , and remove the 
 cotton from his ear . " she take much after her mother , do Anna 
 Maria , " mutter the gentleman ; " and I be very glad she be so well 
 married . " 

 he have not advance many pace when , from a branchroad to the right that 
 lead to the railway station , another gentleman , much young , and whose 
 dress unequivocally bespoke he a minister of our Church , come suddenly 
 upon he . each with surprise recognise the other . 

 " what ! - Mr. George Morley ! " 

 " Mr. Hartopp ! - how be you , my dear sir ? - what bring you so far from 
 home ? " 

 " I be on a visit to my daughter , Anna Maria . she have not be long 
 married - to young Jessop . Old Jessop be one of the principal merchant 
 at Ouzelford - very respectable worthy family . the young couple be 
 happily settle in a remarkably snug villa - that be it with the portico , 
 not a hundred yard behind we , to the right . very handsome town , 
 Ouzelford ; you be bind to it , of course ? - we can walk together . I be 
 go to look at the paper in the City Rooms - very fine room they 
 be . but you be straight from London , perhaps , and have see the day 's 
 journal ? any report of the meeting in aid of the Ragged Schools ? " 

 " not that I know of . I have not come from London this morning , nor see 
 the paper . " 

 " oh ! - there be a strange - look fellow follow we ; but perhaps he be 
 your servant ? " 

 " not so , but my travel companion - indeed my guide . in fact , I come 
 to Ouzelford in the faint hope of discover there a poor old friend of 
 mine , of whom I have long be in search . " 

 " perhaps the Jessops can help you ; they know everybody at Ouzelford . but 
 now I meet you thus by surprise , Mr. George , I should very much like 
 to ask your advice on a matter which have be much on my mind the last 
 twenty - four hour , and which concern a person I contrive to discover 
 at Ouzelford , though I certainly be not in search of he - a person 
 about whom you and I have a conversation a few year ago , when you be 
 stay with your worthy father . " 

 " Eh ? " say George , quickly ; " whom do you speak of ? " " that singular 
 vagabond who take I in , you remember - call himself Chapman - real 
 name William Losely , a return convict . you would have it that he be 
 innocent , though the man himself have plead guilty on his trial . " 

 " his whole character belie his lip then . oh , Mr. Hartopp , that man 
 commit the crime impute to he ! - a plan , deliberate robbery - an 
 ungrateful , infamous breach of trust ! that man - that ! he who reject the 
 money he do not earn , even when press on he by anxious imploring 
 friend - he who have now go voluntarily forth , aged and lonely , to 
 wring his bread from the humblest call rather than incur the risk 
 of injure the child with whose existence he have charge himself ! - the 
 dark midnight thief ! believe he not , though his voice may say it . to 
 screen , perhaps , some other man , he be tell you a noble lie . but what 
 of he ? have you really see he , and at Ouzelford ? " 

 " yes . " 

 " when ? " 

 " yesterday . I be in the City Reading - Room , look out of the window . I 
 see a great white dog in the street below ; I know the dog at once , sir , 
 though he be disguise by restoration to his natural coat , and his hair 
 be as long as a peruvian lama 's . " Tis Sir Isaac , ' say I to myself ; and 
 behind Sir Isaac I see Chapman , so to call he , carry a basket with 
 pedlar 's ware , and , to my surprise , Old Jessop , who be a formal man , 
 with a great deal of reserve and dignity , pompous indeed   but do not   let 
 that go far , talk to Chapman quite affably , and actually buy 
 something out of the basket . presently Chapman go away , and be soon 
 lose to sight . Jessop come into the Reading - Room . ' I see you , ' say I , 
 ' talk to an old fellow with a french dog . ' ' such a good old fellow , ' 
 say Jessop ; ' have a way about he that get into your very heart while 
 he be talk . I should like to make you acquainted with he . ' ' thank 
 you for nothing , ' say I ; ' I should be - take in . ' ' never fear , ' say 
 Jessop , ' he would not take in a fly - the simple creature . ' I own I 
 chuckle at that , Mr. George . ' and do he live here , ' say I , ' or be he 
 merely a wandering pedlar ? ' then Jessop tell I that he have see he for 
 the first time two or three week ago , and accost he rudely , look 
 on he as a mere tramp ; but Chapman answer so well , and show so many 
 pretty thing in his basket , that Jessop soon find himself buy a 
 pair of habit - cuff for Anna Maria , and in the course of talk it come 
 out , I suppose by a sign , that Chapman be a Freemason , and Jessop be an 
 enthusiast in that sort of nonsense , master of a lodge or something , 
 and that be a new attraction . in short , Jessop take a great fancy to 
 he - patronise he , promise he protection , and actually recommend 
 he to a lodging in the cottage of all old widow who live in the 
 outskirt of the town , and have once be a nurse in the Jessop family . 
 and what do you think Jessop have just buy of this simple creature ' ! 
 a pair of worst mitten as a present for I , and what be more , I have 
 get they on this moment - look ! neat , I think , and monstrous warm . now , 
 I have hitherto keep my own counsel . I have not say to Jessop , 
 ' beware - that be the man who take I in . ' but this concealment be a 
 little on my conscience . on the one hand , it seem very cruel , even if 
 the man do once commit a crime , in spite of your charitable conviction 
 to the contrary , that I should be blab out his disgrace , and 
 destroy perhaps his livelihood . on the other hand , if he should still 
 be really a rogue , a robber , perhaps dangerous , ought I - ought I - in 
 short - you be a clergyman and a fine scholar , sir - what ought I to do ? " 

 " my dear Mr. Hartopp , do not vex yourself with this very honourable 
 dilemma of conscience . let I only find my poor old friend , my

 " yes - Halse ; and I know the town well enough to conduct you , if not to

 losely , and be prejudice against he by the misdeed of the young , to

 very delicate . "

 " what be it you want ? even if Sophy be in your power , Darrell would

 " believe - yes , for that belief be everything in the world , to I ! ah ,

 " for I ! - no , no ; for honour - for honour - for honour ! I think you

 here the old man , hitherto seat , rose - slowly , with feebleness and

 once more , and this time thoroughly , Jasper 's hideous levity and coarse

 CHAPTER VI . 

  GENTLEMAN WAIFE do not forget an OLD FRIEND . the OLD FRIEND 
  RECONCILES ASTROLOGY to PRUDENCE , and be under the INFLUENCE of 
  benefice . MR . HARTOPP HAT in HAND to GENTLEMAN WAIFE . 

 Waife fall on the floor of his threshold , exclaim , sobbing , moaning , 
 as voice itself gradually die away . the dog , who have be shut out from 
 the house , and remain , ear erect , head drooping , close at the door , 
 rush in as Jasper burst forth . the two listener at the open casement 
 now steal round ; there be the dog , its paw on the old man 's shoulder , 
 try to attract his notice , and whine low . 

 tenderly - reverentially , they lift the poor martyr - evermore clear in 
 their eye from stain , from question ; the dishonour brand transmute 
 into the hallow cross ! and when the old man at length recover 
 consciousness , his head be pillow on the breast of the spotless , 
 noble Preacher ; and the decorous English Trader , with instinctive 
 deference for repute and respect for law , be kneel by his side , 
 clasp his hand ; and as Wife glance down , confusedly wonder , 
 Hartopp exclaim , half sob : " forgive I ; you say I should repent , 
 if I know all ! I do repent ! I do ! forgive I - I shall never forgive 
 myself . " 

 " have I be dream ? what be all this ? you here , too , Mr. George ! 
 but - but there be another . go ! ah - go - go ! lose , lose ! ha ! do 
 you overhear we ? " 

 " we overheard you - at that window ! see , spite of yourself , Heaven 
 let your innocence be know , and , in that innocence , your sublime 
 self - sacrifice . " 

 " Hush ! you will never betray I , either of you - never . a father turn 
 against his son ! - horrible ! "

 CHAPTER VII . 

  JASPER LOSELY in his element . o young READER , WHOMSOEVER THOU ART , 
  ON WHOM NATURE have bestow her MAGNIFICENT GIFT of PHYSICAL power 
  with the JOYS IT command , with the daring that spring from it - ON 
  close this chapter , PAUSE A MOMENT , and think " what WILT thou DO 
  with it ? " shall it be BRUTE - LIKE or GOD - LIKE ? with what advantage 
  for life - its delights or its PERILS - toils borne with EASE , and 
  glory CHEAP - BOUGHT - DOST thou start AT life 's onset ? give THY 
  sinews a mind that CONCEIVES the HEROIC , and what NOBLE thing thou 
  mayst do , but value THY sinews for rude STRENGTH alone , and that 
  strength may be turn to THY SHAME and THY TORTURE . the wealth of 
  THY life will but tempt to its WASTE . ABUSE , AT FIRST FELT not , 
  will poison the us of SENSE . WILD BULLS GORE and TRAMPLE their 
  foe . THOU HAST SOUL ! WILT THOU TRAMPLE and GORE IT ? 

 Jasper Losely , on quit his father , spend his last coin in payment 
 for his horse 's food , and in fiery drink for himself . in haste he 
 mount - in haste he spur on to London ; not even penny for the 
 toll - bar . where he find the gate open , he dash through they 
 headlong ; where closed , as the night advance , he force his horse 
 across the field over hedge and ditch - more than once the animal 
 fall with he - more than once throw from the saddle ; for , while a 
 most daring , he be not a very practised rider ; but it be not easy to 
 break bone so strong , and though bruised and dizzy , he continue his 
 fierce way . at morning his horse be thoroughly exhausted , and at the 
 first village he reach after sunrise he leave the poor beast at an inn , 
 and succeed in borrowing of the landlord l1 on the pawn of the horse 
 thus leave as hostage . resolve to husband this sum , he perform the 
 rest of his journey on foot . he reach London at night , and go 
 straight to Cutts ' lodging . Cutts be , however , in the club - room of 
 those dark associate against whom losely have be warn . oblivious 
 of his solemn promise to Arabella , Jasper startle the reveller as he 
 stalk into the room , and towards the chair of honour at the far end of 
 it , on which he have be accustom to lord it over the fell group he 
 have treat out of Poole 's purse . one of the big and most redoubte 
 of the Black Family be now in that seat of dignity , and refuse 
 surlily to yield it at Jasper 's rude summon , be seize by the scruff 
 of the neck , and literally hurl on the table in front , come down 
 with clatter and clash amongst mug and glass . Jasper seat himself 
 coolly , while the hubbub begin to swell - and roar for drink . an old 
 man , who serve as drawer to these cavalier , go out to obey the 
 order ; and when he be go , those near the door swing across it a heavy 
 bar . wrath against the domineering intruder be gather , and wait 
 but the moment to explode . Jasper , turn round his bloodshot eye ; see 
 Cutts within a few chair of he , seek to shrink out of sight . 

 " Cutts , come hither , " cry he , imperiously . Cutts do not stir . 

 " throw I that cur this way - you , who sit next he . " 

 " do not , do not ; his mad fit be on he ; he will murder I - murder I , who 
 have help and save you all so often . stand by I . " 

 " we will , " say both his neighbour , the one groping for his case - knife , 
 the other for his revolver . 

 " do you fear I should lop your ear , dog , " cry Jasper , for shrink 
 from my side with your tail between your leg ! Pooh ! I scorn to waste 
 force on a thing so small . after all , I be glad you leave I ; I do not 
 want you . you will find your horse at an inn in the village of - - - . 
 I will pay for its hire whenever we meet again . meanwhile , find another 
 master - I discharge you . Mille tonnerre ! why do that weasel - face 
 snail not bring I the brandy ! by your leave , " - and he appropriate to 
 himself the brimming glass of his next neighbour . thus refresh , he 
 glance round through the reek of tobacco smoke ; see the man he have 
 dislodge , and who , rather amazed than stunned by his fall , have keep 
 silence on rise , and be now ominously interchange muttered word 
 with two of his comrade , who be also on their leg . Jasper turn 
 from he contemptuously ; - with increase contempt in his hard fierce 
 sneer , note the lower frown on either side the Pandemonium ; and it 
 be only with an angry flash from his eye that he mark , on close 
 his survey , the bar drop across the door , and two form , knife in 
 hand , station at the threshold . 

 " aha ! my jolly companion , " say he then , " you do right to bar the 
 door . prudent family can not   settle their quarrel too snugly amongst 
 themselves . I be come here on purpose to give you all a proper scolding , 
 and if some of you do not   hang your head for shame before I have do , 
 you will die more game than I think for , whenever you come to the last 
 drop . " 

 he rise as he thus speak , fold his sinewy arm across his wide chest . 
 Most of the man have rise too - some , however , remain seat ; there 
 might be eighteen or twenty in all . every eye be fix on he , and many 
 a hand be on a deadly weapon . 

 " Scum of the earth ! " burst forth Jasper , with voice like a roll of 
 thunder , " I stoop to come amongst you - I share amongst you my money . 
 be any one of you too poor to pay up his club fee - to buy a draught 
 of Forgetfulness - I say , ' brother , take ! ' do brawl break out in your 
 jollity - be knife draw - a throat in danger - this right band strike 
 down the uproar , crush back the coward murder . if I do not join in 
 your roguery , it be because they be sneak and pitiful . I come 
 as your Patron , not as your Pal ; I do not meddle with your secret - do 
 not touch your plunder . I owe you nothing . Offal that you be ! to I 
 you owe drink , and meat , and good fellowship . I give you mirth , and 
 I give you Law ; and in return ye lay a plot amongst you to get rid 
 of I ; - how , ye white - livere scoundrel ? Oho ! not by those fist , and 
 knife , and bludgeon . all your pigeon breast club together have not 
 manhood for that . but to palm off upon I some dastardly deed of your 
 own ; by snare and scrap of false evidence - false oath , too , no 
 doubt - to smuggle I off to the hangman . that be your precious 
 contrivance . once again I be here ; but this once only . what for ? - why , 
 to laugh at , and spit at , and spurn you . and if one man amongst you have 
 in he an ounce of man 's blood , let he show I the traitor who plan 
 that pitiful project , and be they a dozen , they shall carry the mark of 
 this hand till their carcass go to the surgeon 's scalpel . " 

 he cease . though each be now hustle the other towards he , and the 
 whole pack of miscreant be close up , like hound round a wild boar

 life call Cutts ! 

 " look you , General Jas . , it be all a mistake your ever come here . 
 you be a fine fellow once , particularly in the french way of do 
 business - large prize and lot of row . that do not   suit we ; we be quiet 
 Englishmen . you brag of beat and bully the gentleman who admit you 
 amongst they , and of not share their plan or risk ; but that sort of 
 thing be quite out of order - no precedent for it . how do we know that 
 you be not a spy , or could not be make one , since you say you owe we 
 nothing , and hold we in such scorn ? truth be , we be all sick of you . 
 you say you only come this once : very well , you have spin your yarn - now 
 go . that be all we want ; go in peace , and never trouble we again . 
 gentleman , I move that General Jas . be expel this club , and request 
 to withdraw . " 

 " I second it , " say the man whom Jasper have fling on the table . 

 " those who be in favour of the resolution , hold up their 
 hand ; - all - carry unanimously . General Jas . be expel . " 

 " expel I ! " say Jasper , who in the mean while , sway to and fro his 
 brawny bulk , have clear the space round he , and stand rest his 
 hand on the heavy armchair from which he have rise . 

 a hostile and simultaneous movement of the group bring four or five of 
 the foremost on he . up rise the chair on which Jasper have lean - up it

 the butcher 's blow . with his left hand he wrench a knife from a third 
 of the foe , and thus armed with blade and buckler , he spring on the 
 table , tower over all . before he be the man with the revolver , 
 a genteeler outlaw than the rest - ticket - of - leave man , who have be 
 transport for forgery . " shall I shoot he ? " whisper this knave to 
 Cutts . Cutts draw back the hesitate arm . " no ; the noise ! bludgeon 
 safe . " pounce , as Cutts whisper - pounce as a hawk on its quarry , 
 dart Jasper 's swoop on the Forger , and the next moment , fling the 
 chair in the face of those who be now swarm up the table , Jasper 
 be arm with the revolver , which he have clutch from its startled 
 owner , and its six barrel threaten death , right and left , beside and 
 before and around he , as he turn from face to face . instantly there 
 fall a hush - instantly the assault pause . every one feel that there 
 no faltering would make the hand tremble or the ball swerve . whereever 
 Jasper turn the foe recoil . he laugh with audacious mockery as he 
 survey the recreant . 

 " down with your arm , each of you - down that knife , down that bludgeon . 
 that be well . down your - there ; your - yours . what , all down ! pile they 
 here on the table at my foot . dog , what do you fear?-death . the first 
 who refuse die . " 

 Mute and servile as a repentant Legion to a Caesar 's order , the knave 
 pile their weapon . 

 " Unbar the door , you two . you , orator Cutts , go in front ; light a 
 candle - open the street - door . so - so - so . who will treat I with a parting 
 cup - to your health ? thank you , sir . fall back there ; stand back - along 
 the wall - each of you . line my way . Ho , ho ! - you harm I - you daunt 
 I - you - you ! stop - I have a resolution to propose . hear it , and cheer . 
 ' that this meeting rescind the resolution for the expulsion of General 
 Jasper , and entreat he humbly to remain , the pride and ornament of the 
 club ! ' those who be for that resolution , hold up their hand - as many 
 as be against it , theirs . carry unanimously . gentleman , I thank 
 you - proud day of my life - but I will see you hang first ; and till 
 that sight divert I , - gentleman , your health . " 

 descend from his eminence , he pass slowly down the room unscathed , 
 unmenaced , and , with a low mocking bow at the threshold , stride along 
 the passage to the streetdoor . there , see Cutts with the light in his 
 hand , he uncocke the pistol , strike off the cap , and give it 
 to his quondam associate , say : " return that to its owner , with my 
 compliment . one word - speak truth , and fear nothing . do you send help 
 to Darrell ? " " no ; I swear it . " 

 " I be sorry for it . I should like to have owe so trusty a friend that 
 one favour . go back to your pal . understand now why I scorn to work 
 with such rotten tool . " 

 " a wonderful fellow , indeed ! " mutter Cutts , as his eye follow the 
 recede form of the triumphant bravo . " all London might look to itself , 
 if he have more solid brain , and less liquid fire in they . " 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

  JASPER LOSELY sleep under the PORTICO from which falsehood be 
  BORNE by black horse . he forget a promise , REWEAVES a scheme , 
  visit a RIVER - SIDE , and a door close on the STRONG MAN and the 
  GRIM WOMAN . 

 Jasper , have satisfy the wild yearning of his wound vanity . he have

 she say , with much sullenness , but with no apparent surprise . " mistress

 mistress 's order . " 

 " oh , Mrs. Crane be out of town , " say Jasper , much relieved ; " where have 
 she go ? " 

 " I do not   know . " 

 " when will she be back ? " 

 " in a few day ; so she tell I . will you walk in , and have breakfast ? 
 Mistress say there be to be always plenty in the house - you might come 
 any moment . please scrape your foot . " 

 Jasper heavily mount into the drawing - room , and impatiently await 
 the substantial refreshment , which be soon place before he . the

 prim book - shelves - the empty birdcage - the break lute - the patent 
 easy - chair - the footstool - the sofa , which have be add to the

 first adopt as a son - nay , on the hearth - rug the very slipper , on the 
 back of the chair the very dressing - gown , graciously wear by he while 
 yet the fairness of his form justify his fond respect for it . 

 for that day he be content with the negative luxury of complete 
 repose ; the more so as , in every attempt to move , he feel the same

 great seat of intelligence form at the spine with the great mainspring 
 of force ; and , withal , a reluctance to stir , and a more than usual 
 inclination to doze . but the next day , though these unpleasant 
 sensation continue , his impatience of thought and hate of solitude 
 make he anxious to go forth and seek some distraction . no distraction 
 leave to he but the gaming - table - no companion but fellow - victim in 
 that suck whirlpool . well , he know a low gaming - house , open all day 
 as all night . wish to add somewhat to the miserable remain of the 
 l1 borrow on the horse , that make all his capital , he ask Bridgett , 
 indifferently , to oblige he with two or three sovereign ; if she have 
 they not , she might borrow they in the neighbourhood till her mistress 
 return . Bridgett answer , with ill - simulate glee , that her mistress 
 have give positive order that Mr. Losely be to have everything he 
 call for , except - money . jasper colour with wrath and shame ; but 
 he say no more - whistle - take his hat - go out - repair to the 
 gaming - house - lose his last shilling , and return moodily to dine in 
 Podden Place . the austerity of the room , the loneliness of the evening , 
 begin now to inspire he with unmitigated disgust , which be add in 
 fresh account to his old score of repugnance for the absent Arabella . 
 the affront put upon he in the order which Bridgett have so faithfully 
 repeat make he yet more distastefully contemplate the dire necessity

 like go back to a preparatory school , to be mulct of pocket - money ,

 Darrell - still more intolerable ; except - he pause in his cogitation ,

 return ! - except to forget his father 's prayer and his own

 might much more easily work on the delicate nerve of a woman , shrink 
 from all noise and scandal , than he could on the stubborn pride of his 
 resolute father - in - law . perhaps it be on account of Sophy - perhaps to

 his altered feature than Rugge have do . these thought gather on he 
 strong and strong all the evening , and grow into resolve with the 
 next morning . he sally out after breakfast - the same numbness ; but 
 he walk it off . easy enough to find the address of the Marchioness of 
 Montfort . he ask it boldly of the porter at the well - know house of

 foot , and thence to the small , scatter hamlet immediately contiguous

 lounge near the river - side ; and enter into conversation with they 
 about their craft , which be sufficiently familiar to he , for he have 
 ply the strong oar on that tide in the holiday of his youth , he 
 proceed to inquiry , which be readily and unsuspectingly answer . 
 " yes , there be a young lady with Lady Montfort ; they do not know her 
 name . they have see she often in the lawn - see she too , at church . she 
 be very pretty ; yes , she have blue eye and fair hair . " of his father

 describe - lame , and with one eye - who have live some month ago in a

 have make basket - they do not know if for sale ; if so , perhaps for a

 relation to the young lady . but Lady Montfort 's head coachman live 
 in the village , and could , no doubt , give he all the information he 
 require . " Jasper be too wary to call on the coachman ; he have learn

 might only have expose himself to question , and to the chance of his

 thus set she on her guard ; for no doubt his father have caution 
 she against he . it never occur to he that the old man could already 
 have return ; and those to whom he confine his interrogatory be 
 quite ignorant of the fact . Jasper have no intention to intrude himself 
 that day on Lady Montfort . his self - love shrank from present himself 
 to a lady of such rank , and to whom he have be once present on equal 
 term , as the bridegroom of her friend and the confidential visitor to 
 her mother , in habiliment that bespoke so utter a fall . well , too , on 
 all account , to appear something of a gentleman ; more likely to excite 
 pity for suffering - less likely to suggest excuse for rebut his 
 claim , and show he to the door . Nay , indeed , so dressed , in that 
 villanous pea - jacket , and with all other habiliment to match , would any 
 servant admit he ? - could he get into Lady Montfort 's presence ? he must 
 go back - wait for Mrs. Crane 's return . doubtless she would hail his 
 wish - half a reform in itself - to castoff the outward sign of an 
 accept degradation . 

 accordingly he go back to town in much well spirit , and so absorb 
 in his hope , that , when he arrive at Podden Place , he do not 
 observe that , from some obliquity of vision , or want of the normal 
 correspondence between will and muscle , his hand twice miss the 
 knocker - wander first above , then below it ; and that , when actually 
 in his clasp , he do not feel the solid iron : the sense of touch seem 
 suspend . Bridgett appear . " mistress be come back , and will see you . " 

 Jasper do not look charm ; he wince , but screw up his courage , and 
 mount the stair - slowly - heavily . form the landing - place above glare 
 down the dark shin eye that have almost quail his bold spirit 
 nearly six year before ; and almost in the same word as then , a voice 
 as exulting , but less stern , say : " so you come at last to I , Jasper 
 losely - you be come . " rapidly - flittingly , with a step noiseless as a 
 spectre 's , Arabella Crane descend the stair ; but she do not , as when 
 he first seek that house in the year before , grasp his hand or 
 gaze into his face . rather , it be with a shrink avoidance of his 
 touch - with something like a shudder - that she glide by he into the 
 open drawing - room , beckon he to follow . he halt a moment ; he feel 
 a longing to retreat - to fly the house ; his superstitious awe of she 
 very benefit come back to he more strongly than ever . but her help at 
 the moment be necessary to his very hope to escape all future need of 
 she , and , though with a vague foreboding of unconjecturable evil , he 
 step into the room , and the door close on both . 




 BOOK XI . 




 CHAPTER I. 

  " the course of true love never do RUN SMOOTH ! " may it not be 
  because where there be no obstacles , there be no test to the

  for long voyage . 

 I pass over the joyous meeting between Waife and Sophy . I pass over

 the old man fear at first that she might , by some mean or other ,

 CHAPTER II . 

 an offering to the MANES . 

 three side of Waife 's cottage be within Lady Montfort 's ground ; the

 Haughton - "

 " you do ? good heaven ! and that lawyer will be barbarous enough

 return ! and all methodically compute - tersely - arithmetically - down 
 to fraction . so that every farthing seem , and indeed be , his lawful 
 due . and that sum invest in an annuity of L500 a year - income which ,

 " it be quite a business - like computation , I tell you , sir ; all do by

 table , and that sort of thing . William Losely , you can not leave an

 go out of the Guards into the line - I mean to be a soldier in good

 " Sir , " say Lionel , who be now on one knee by Waife 's chair - ' sir , I 
 have never yet ask man for his blessing - not even Guy Darrell . will

 solemnly , but with profound humility - one hand on the Bible beside he , 
 one on the young soldier 's bended head - William Losely bless Charles 
 Haughton 's son - and ; have do so , involuntarily his arm open , and

 CHAPTER III . 

  nothing SO OBSTINATE as a young MAN 's HOPE ; nothing so eloquent as A 
  LOVER 's TONGUE . 

 Hitherto there have be no reference to Sophy . not Sophy 's lover , but

 Waife look down . 

 " before I ask the servant to announce I I take the precaution to 
 learn that you be alone . but a few word more - hear they patiently .

 " he will ! " cry Waife , joyously . " and if he should inquire and if Sophy 
 should prove to be , as I have ever believe , his daughter 's child , would

 affection and ask her hand . " 

 " what ! Mr. Darrell would consent to your union with the child of 
 peasant , and not with his own grandchild ? " 

 " Sir , sir , you rack I to the heart ; but if you know all , you would not

 step , 

 " but , " resume Lionel , " go to Fawley yourself . seek Darrell ; compare the

 Waife stand motionless and voiceless . this passionate appeal strike he

 " and , sir , " add Lionel , in a low , sad tone - " can I ask you , whose

 in a few minute he reopen the room - door , beckon silently to

 CHAPTER IV . 

  GUY DARRELL 's views in the invitation to WAIFE . 

 Lionel have but inadequately represent , for he could but imperfectly 
 comprehend , the profound impression make upon Guy Darrell by George 
 Morley 's disclosure . himself so capable of self - sacrifice , Darrell be 
 the man above all other to regard with an admiring reverence , which 
 partook of awe , a self - immolation that seem almost above humanity - to 
 he who set so lofty an estimate on good name and fair repute . he have

 " I apologise , " - slow to say , " I repent " ; very - very - very slow indeed to 
 say , " I forgive " ; yet let he once say , " I repent , " " I apologise , " or " I

 but it must not be suppose that , in authorise Lionel to undertake

 the winter evening have close , George and Darrell be converse in

 have lead to a permanent coldness between man so disposed to esteem each 
 other , they hear the sound of wheel on the frosty ground - the shrill 
 bell at the porch - door . 




 CHAPTER V. 

  the VAGABOND receive in THE MANOR - HOUSE AT FAWLEY .

 CHAPTER VII . 

  SOPHY , DARRELL , and the FLUTE - PLAYER . DARRELL . PREPARES a surprise 
  for WAIFE . 

 Sophy be come . she have cross that inexorable threshold . she be a guest

 it have be say before that Fairthorn know his secret . Fairthorn have

 but at this moment Fairthorn be a support to he under other

 bride spring from the loin of Lion king ! darrell , then , do full

 on the other hand , Sophy 's feeling towards her host be almost equally

 grey wall ? where be the evidence of the relationship of which she have

 kinsman , and that for Lionel 's sake that kinsman bid she welcome ? 
 and Lionel too - go the very day before she arrive ! that she learn

 not address a line to herself , though but to condole with she on her

 Waife 's sojourn under that roof baffle she at tempt at conjecture . the

 neither George nor his wife give she any clue to the inquiry that

 her very heart be shut up , except where it could gush forth - nor even

 thus pass day in which Sophy 's time be spend chiefly in Waife 's

 Fairthorn and Sophy thus make acquaintance , distant and shy at first on

 the musician 's covert meaning in these heraldic discourse , Sophy , with

 and these recital make she conceive a more soft and tender interest

 while Fairthorn and Sophy thus mature acquaintance , Darrell and Waife 
 be draw close and close to each other . certainly no one would be

 have pass over the ploughshare of self - sacrifice . Darrell have offer
