 Dead - Waife to a Son whose life have become his terror . to one man , name 
 have be an idol ; to the other , NAME have be a weed cast away into the 
 mire . to the one man , unjoyous , evanescent glory - to the other , a shame 
 that have be bear with a sportive cheerfulness , dash into sorrow 
 only when the world 's contumely threaten to despoil Affection of its 
 food . but there be something akin in their joint experience of earthly 
 vanity ; - so little solace in worldly honour to the triumphant 
 orator - so little of misery to the vagrant Mime while his conscience 
 mutely appeal to Heaven from the verdict of his kind . and as beneath 
 all the levity and whim of the man rear and nurture , and fit by 
 his characteristic tendency , to view life through its humour , not 
 through its passion , there still run a deep undercurrent of grave and 
 earnest intellect and feeling - so too , amidst the severer and stately 
 texture of the once ambitious , laborious mind , which have conduct 
 Darrell to renown - amidst all that gather - up intensity of passion , 
 which admit no comedy into Sorrow , and see in Love but the aspect of 
 fate - amidst all this lofty seriousness of soul , there be yet a vivid 
 capacity of enjoyment - those fine sensibility to the pleasurable 
 sun - ray of life , which be constitutional to all geniu , no matter how 
 grave its vocation . true , affliction at last may dull they , as it dull 
 all else that we take from Nature when she equip we for life . yet , 
 in the mind of Darrell , affliction have shatter the thing most gravely 
 coveted , even more than it have mar its perceptive acknowledgment of 
 the sympathy between fancy that move to smile , and thought that 
 bequeath solemn lesson , or melt to no idle tear . have Darrell be 
 place amidst the circumstance that make happy the home of earnest 
 man , Darrell would have be mirthful ; have Waife be place amongst the 
 circumstance that concentrate talent , and hedge round life with train 
 thickset and belt laurel , Waife would have be grave . 

 it be not in the early conference that take place in Waife 's 
 apartment that the subject which have lead the old man to Fawley be 
 bring into discussion . when Waife have seek to introduce it - when , 
 after Sophy 's arrival , he have look wistfully into Darrell 's face , 
 strive to read there the impression she have create , and , unable to 
 discover , have begin , with tremulous accent , to reopen the cause that 
 weigh on he - Darrell stop he at once . " Hush - not yet ; remember 
 that it be in the very moment you first broach this sorrowful topic , 
 on arrive here , and perceive how different the point of view from 
 which we two must regard it , that your nerve give way - your illness 
 rush on you . wait , not only till you be strong , but till we know 
 each other well . this subject be one that it become we to treat 
 with all the strength of our reason - with all the calm which either can 
 impose upon the feeling that ruffle judgment . at present , talk we of 
 all matter except that , which I promise you shall be fairly discuss 
 at last . " 

 Darrell find , however , that his most effective diversion from the 
 subject connect with Sophy be through another channel in the old 
 man 's affection , hope , and fear . George Morley , in repeat the 
 conversation he have overhear between Waife and Jasper , have naturally , 
 while clear the father , somewhat soften the bravado and cynicism 
 of the son 's language , and more than somewhat brighten the touch of 
 natural feeling by which the bravado and cynicism have be alternate . 
 and Darrell have sufficient magnanimity to conquer the repugnance with 
 which he approach a name associate with so many dark and hateful 
 memory , and , avoid as much as possible distinct reference to 
 Jasper 's past life , to court a consultation on the chance of save 
 from the bad the life that yet remain . with whom else , indeed , than 
 Jasper 's father could Darrell so properly and so unreservedly discuss a 
 matter in which their interest and their fear be in common ? - as though 
 he be render some compensation to Waife for the disappointment he 
 would experience when Sophy 's claim come to be discuss - if he could 
 assist in relieve the old man 's mind as to the ultimate fate of the 
 son for whom he have make so grand a sacrifice , Darrell speak to Waife 
 somewhat in detail of the view with which he have instruct Colonel 
 Morley to find out and to treat with Jasper . he hear from the Colonel 
 almost daily . Alban have not yet discover Jasper , nor even succeed 
 in trace Mrs. Crane ! but an account of Jasper 's farewell visit to 
 that den of thief , from which he have issue safe and triumphant , 
 have reach the ear of a detective employ by the Colonel , and on 
 tolerably good term with Cutts ; and it be no small comfort to know 
 that Jasper have finally break with those miscreant comrade , and 
 have never again be see in their haunt . as Arabella have introduce 
 herself to Alban by her former name , and neither he nor Darrell be 
 acquaint with that she now bear , and as no question on the subject 
 could be put to Waife during the early stage of his illness , so it 
 be several day before the Colonel have succeed in trace she out as 
 Mrs. Crane of Podden Place - a discovery effect by a distant relation 
 to whom he have be refer at the famous school of which Arabella 
 have be the pride , and who be no doubt the owner of those sheepskin 
 account - book by which the poor grim woman have once vainly seek to 
 bribe Jasper into honest work . but the house in Podden Place be shut 
 up - not a soul in charge of it . the house immediately adjoin it be 
 tenantless . the Colonel learn , however , from a female servant in 
 an opposite house , that several day ago she have see a tall , 
 powerful - look man enter Mrs. Crane 's street - door ; that she have not 
 see he quit it ; and that some evening afterwards , as this servant 
 be close up the house in which she serve , she have remark a large 
 private carriage drive away from Mrs. Crane 's door ; that it be too 
 dark to see who be in the carriage , but she have notice a woman whom 
 she feel fully sure be Mrs. Crane 's servant , Bridgett Greggs , on the 
 box beside the coachman . 

 Alban have be to the agent employ by Mrs. Crane in the letting of her 
 house , but have not there gain any information . the Colonel believe 
 that Mrs. Crane have succeed in remove Jasper from London - have , 
 perhaps , accompany he abroad . if with she , at all event for the 
 present he be safe from the sting of want , and with one who have swear 
 to save he from his own guilty self . if , however , still in England , 
 Alban have no doubt , soon or later , to hunt he up . 

 upon the whole , this conjectural information , though unsatisfactory , 
 allay much anxiety . Darrell make the most of it in his representation 
 to Waife . and the old man , as we know , be one not hard to comfort , 
 never quarrel irrevocably with Hope . 

 and now Waife be rapidly recover . Darrell , after spend the great 
 part of several day , intent upon a kind of study from which he have be 
 estranged for many year , take to frequent absence for the whole 
 day ; go up to London by the early train , come back by the 
 late . George Morley also go to London for a few hour . darrell , 
 on return , do not allude to the business which take he to the 
 metropolis ; neither do George , but the latter seem unusually animate 
 and excited . at length , after one of these excursion , so foreign to his 
 habit , he and George enter together the old man 's apartment not long 
 before the early hour at which the convalescent retire to rest . Sophy 
 be seat on the footstool at Waife 's knee , read the Bible to he , 
 his hand rest lightly on her bended head . the sight touch both 
 George and Darrell ; but darrell of the two be the more affected . what 
 young , pure voice shall read to HIM the Book of Hope in the evening of 
 lonely age ? Sophy start in some confusion , and as , in quit the 
 room , she pass by Darrell , he take her hand gently , and scan she 
 feature more deliberately , more earnestly than he have ever yet seem 
 to do ; then he sigh , and drop the hand , murmur , " pardon I . " 
 be he seek to read in that fair face some likeness to the Darrell 
 lineament ? if he have find it , what then ? but when Sophy be go , 
 Darrell come straight to Waife with a cheerful brow - with a kindling 
 eye . 

 " William Losely , " say he . 

 " Waife , if you please , sir , " interrupt the old man . " William Losely , " 
 repeat Darrell , " justice seek to repair , so far as , alas ! it now 
 can , the wrong inflict on the name of William Losely . your old friend 
 Alban Morley supply I with the note he have make in the matter of 
 your trial , I arrange the evidence they furnish . the Secretary for 
 the Home Department be one of my most intimate political friend - a man 
 of humanity - of sense . I place that evidence before he . I , George , and 
 Mr. Hartopp , see he after he have peruse it - " 

 " my - son - Lizzy 's son ! " 

 " his secret will be keep . the question be not who commit the act 
 for which you suffer , but whether you be clearly , incontestably , 
 innocent of the act , and , in plead guilty , do but sublimely bear the 
 penalty of another . there will be no new trial - there be none who would 
 prosecute . I bring back to you the Queen 's free pardon under the Great 
 Seal . I should explain to you that this form of the royal grace be so 
 rarely give that it need all the strength and affect circumstance 
 of your peculiar case to justify the Home Secretary in listening , not 
 only to the interest I could bring to bear in your favour , but to his 
 own humane inclination . the pardon under the Great Seal differ from an 
 ordinary pardon . it purge the blood from the taint of felony - it remit 
 all the civil disability which the mere expiry of a penal sentence 
 do not remove . in short , as applicable to your case , it become 
 virtually a complete and formal attestation of your innocence . Alban 
 Morley will take care to apprise those of your old friend who may yet 
 survive , of that revocation of unjust obloquy , which this royal deed 
 imply - Alban Morley , who would turn his back on the high noble in 
 Britain if but guilty of some jockey trick on the turf ! live henceforth 
 openly , and in broad daylight if you please ; and trust to we three - the 
 Soldier , the Lawyer , the Churchman - to give to this paper that value 
 which your Sovereign 's adviser intend it to receive . " 

 " your hand now , dear old friend ! " cry George . " you remember I 
 command you once to take mine as man and gentleman - as man and 
 gentleman , now honour I with your . " 

 " be it possible ? " falter Waife , one hand in George 's , the 
 other extend in implore appeal to Darrell - " be it possible ? I 
 vindicate - I clear - and yet no felon 's dock for Jasper ! - the son not 
 criminate by the father 's acquittal ! tell I that ! again - again ! " 

 " it be so , believe I . all that rest be to force on that son , if 
 he have a human heart , the conviction that he will be bad than a 
 parricide if he will not save himself . " 

 " and he will - he shall . oh , that I could but get at he ! " exclaim the 
 preacher . 

 " and now , " say Darrell - " now , George , leave we ; for now , upon equal 
 term , we two father can discuss family difference . " 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

  SOPHY 's claim EXAMINED and CANVASSED . 

 " I take this moment , " say Darrell , when leave alone with Waife -   ah , 
 reader , let we keep to that familiar name to the last !   - " I take 
 this moment , " say Darrell , " the first moment in which you can feel 
 thoroughly assure that no prejudice against yourself cloud my judgment 
 in reference to she whom you believe to be your grandchild , to commence , 
 and I trust to conclude forever , the subject which twice bring you 
 within these wall . on the night of your recent arrival here , you 
 give this copy of a french woman 's declaration , to the effect that two 
 infant have be place out with she to nurse ; that one of they be my 
 poor daughter 's infant , who be about to be take away from she ; that 
 the other be confide to she by its parent , a french lady , whom she 
 speak of as a very liberal and distinguished person , but whose name be 
 not state in the paper . " 

 WAIFE . - " the confession describe that lady as an artiste ; distinguish 
 artiste be the expression - viz . , a professional person - a painter - an 
 actress - a singer - or - " 

 DARRELL   drily . - " an opera - dancer ! I understand the french word 
 perfectly . and I presume the name be not mention in the document , from 
 motif of delicacy ; the child of a distinguished french artiste be 
 not necessarily bear in wedlock . but this lady be very grateful to 
 the nurse for the care show to her infant , who be very sickly ; and 
 promise to take the nurse , and the nurse 's husband also , into her

 lady 's offer appear to she like a permanent provision ; that the life 
 of this artiste 's infant be of the utmost value to she - the life of my 
 poor daughter 's child of comparative insignificance . but the infant of 
 the artiste die , and the nurse 's husband put it into his wife 's head to 
 tell your son   then a widower , and who have see so little of his child 
 as to be easily deceive , that it be his infant who die . the nurse 
 shortly afterwards remove to Paris , take with she to the artiste 's 
 house the child who in reality be my daughter 's . " 

 " it seem very probable , do it not - do it not ? " say the ex - comedian 
 eagerly . 

 " it seem to I , " reply the ex - lawyer , " very probable that a witness , 
 enter into court with the confession of one villanous falsehood , 
 would have little scruple to tell another . but I proceed . this rich and 
 liberal artiste die ; the nurse 's conscience then suddenly awaken - she 
 see Mr. Hammond - she inform he of the fraud she have practise . a lady 
 of rank , who have know Matilda , and have see both the infant when both 
 be live under the nurse 's charge , and observe they more attentively 
 than your son have do - corroborate the woman 's story , state that the 
 artiste 's child have dark eye instead of blue ; that the artiste herself 
 be never deceive - but , have take a great fancy to the spurious 
 infant , be willing to receive and cherish it as her own ; and that she 
 know several person who will depose that they hear the artiste say 
 that the child be not her own . on this evidence your son take to 
 himself this child - and this child be your Sophy - and you wish I to 
 acknowledge she as my daughter 's offspring . do not look I so earnestly 
 in the face , my dear and respected guest . it be when you read in my 
 face what my lip shrink from utter that your emotion overcome your 
 strength , and your very mind desert you . now , be firm . your Sophy 
 have no need of I - she be under your charge , and your name be clear . 
 she have find a friend - a protectress - in her own sex . Lady Montfort 's 
 rank give to she a position in the world as high as I could offer ; and 
 as to mere pecuniary provision for she , make your mind easy - it shall be 
 secure . but bear with I when I add , resolutely and calmly , that this 
 nurse 's attestation be to I a gross and poor attempt at imposture 
 than I have anticipate ; and I be amazed that a man of your ability 
 should have be content to accept it . " 

 " oh , Mr. Darrell , do not   say so ! it be such a blessing to think , when 
 my son be lose to I , that I might fill up the void in my heart with 
 an innocent , love child . do not   talk of my ability . if you , whose 
 ability none can question - if you have long and yearn for such a 
 comforter - if you have wish - if you wish now this tale to be true , 
 you would have believe it too ; you would believe it now - you would 
 indeed . two man look so differently at the same story - one deeply 
 interested that it should be true - one determine , if possible , to find 
 it false . be it not so ? " 

 Darrell smile slightly , but could not be induce to assent even to so 
 general a proposition . he feel as if he be pit against a counsel 
 who would take advantage of every concession . 

 Waife continue . " and whatever seem most improbable in this confession , 
 be render probable at once - if - if - we may assume that my unhappy son , 
 tempt by the desire to - to - " 

 " spare yourself - I understand - if your son wish to obtain his wife 's 
 fortune , and therefore connive at the exchange of the infant , and be 
 therefore , too , enable always to corroborate the story of the exchange 
 whenever it suit he to reclaim the infant , I grant this - and I 
 grant that the conjecture be sufficiently plausible to justify you in 
 attach to it much weight . we will allow that it be his interest at 
 one time to represent his child , though live , as no more ; but you must 
 allow also that he would have deem it his interest later , to fasten 
 upon I , as my daughter 's , a child to whom she never give birth . here we 
 entangle ourselves in a controversy without datum , without fact . let we 
 close it . believe what you please . why should I shake conviction that 
 render you happy ? be equally forbear with I . I do full justice to 
 your Sophy 's charming quality . in herself , the proud parent might 
 rejoice to own she ; but I can not acknowledge she to be the daughter of 
 Matilda Darrell . and the story that assure you she be your grandchild , 
 still more convince I that she be not mine ! " 

 " but be not thus inflexible , I implore you ; - you can be so kind , so 
 gentle ; - she would be such a blessing to you - later - perhaps - when I 
 be dead . I be plead for your sake - I owe you so much ! I should repay 
 you , if I could but induce you to inquire - and if inquiry should prove 
 that I be right . " 

 " I have inquire sufficiently . " 

 " ' then I will go and find out the nurse . I will question she . I will - " 

 " hold . be persuade ! Hug your belief ! inquire no far ! " 

 " why - why ? " 

 Darrell be mute . 

 Waife pass and repasse his hand over his brow , and then cry 
 suddenly : " but if I could prove she not to be my grandchild , then she 
 might be happy ! - then - then - ah , sir , young Haughton tell I that if 
 she be but the daughter of honest parent - no child of Jasper 's , no 
 grandchild of mine - then you might not be too proud to bless she at 
 least as his bride ! and , sir , the poor child love the young man . how 
 could she help it ? and , at her age , life without hope be either very 
 short , or very , very long ! let I inquire ! I should be happy even to 
 know that she be not my grandchild . I should not love she less ; and 
 then she would have other to love she when I be go to Lizzy ! " 

 Darrell be deeply move . to he there be something in this 
 old man - ever forget himself , ever so hurried on by his 
 heart - something , I say , in this old man , before which Darrell feel his 
 intellect subdue and his pride silence and abash . 

 " yes , sir , " say Waife , musingly , ' so let it be . I be well now . I will 
 go to France to - morrow . " 

 Darrell nerve his courage . he have wish to spare Waife the pain which 
 his own persuasion cause to himself . well now to be frank . he 
 lay his hand on Waife 's shoulder , and look he in the face , say 
 solemnly : " I entreat you not ! do you suppose that I would not resume 
 inquiry in person , nor pause till the truth be make amply clear , if I 
 have not strong reason to prefer doubt to certainty ? " 

 " what do you mean , sir ? " 

 " there be a woman whose career be , I believe , at this moment revive 
 into fresh notoriety as the heroine of some drama on the stage of 
 Paris - a woman who , when year pal her fame and reft she spoil , as a 
 courtesan renowne for the fool she have beggar , for the young heart 
 she have corrupt , seek plunder still by crime , to which law be less 
 lenient ; charge with swindling , with fraud , with forgery , and at last 
 more than suspect as a practised poisoner , she escape by suicide the 
 judgment of human tribunal . " 

 " I know of whom you speak - that dreadful Gabrielle Desmarets , but for 
 whom my sacrifice to Jasper 's future might not have be in vain ! it be 
 to save Sophy from the chance of Jasper 's ever place she within reach 
 of that woman 's example that I take she away . " 

 " be it not , then , well to forbear ask who be your Sophy 's 
 parent , than to learn from inquiry that she be indeed your grandchild , 
 and that her mother be Gabrielle Desmarets ? " 

 Waife utter a cry like a shriek , and then sate voiceless and aghast . 
 at last he exclaim : " I be certain it be not so ! do you ever see that 
 woman ? " 

 " never that I know of ; but George tell I that he hear your son state 
 to you that she have make acquaintance with I under another name , and if 
 there be a design to employ she in confirmation of his tale - if he be 
 then speak truth to you , doubtless this be the lady of rank refer 
 to in the nurse 's confession - doubtless this be the woman once palm 
 upon I as Matilda 's confidante . in that case I have see she . what 
 then ? " 

 " Mother be not write on her face ! she could never have be a mother . 
 oh , you may smile , sir ; but all my life I have be a reader of the 
 human face ; and there be in the aspect of some woman the barrenness as 
 of stone - no mother 's throb in their bosom - no mother 's kiss on their 
 lip . " 

 " I be a poor reader of woman 's face , " say Darrell ; " but she must be 
 very unlike woman in general , who allow you to know she a bit well if 
 you stand read her face till doomsday . besides , at the time you see 
 Gabrielle Desmarets , her mode of life have perhaps give to she an aspect 
 not originally in her countenance . and I can only answer your poetic 
 conceit by a poetic illustration - Niobe turn to stone ; but she have a 
 great many daughter before she petrify . pardon I , if I would turn 
 off by a jest a thought that I see would shock you , as myself , if 
 gravely encourage . encourage it not . let we suppose it only a chance 
 that inquiry might confirm this conjecture ; but let we shun that chance . 
 meanwhile , if inquiry be to be make , one more likely than either of we 
 to get at the truth have promise to make it , and soon or later we may 
 learn from she the result - I mean that ill - fat Arabella Fossett , whom 
 you know as Crane . " 

 Waife be silent ; but he keep turn in his hand , almost 
 disconsolately , the document which assoil he from the felon 's taint , 
 and say at length , as Darrell be about to leave , " and this thing be of 
 no use to she , then ? " 

 Darrell come back to the old man 's chair , and say softly : " friend , do 
 not fancy that the young have only one path to happiness . you grieve 
 that I can not consent to Lionel 's marriage with your Sophy . dismiss from 
 your mind the desire for the impossible . gently wean from hers what be 
 but a girl 's first fancy . " 

 " it be a girl 's first love . " 

 " and if it be , " say Darrell , calmly , " no complaint more sure to yield 
 to change of air . I have know a girl as affectionate , as pure , as full 
 of all womanly virtue , as your Sophy   and I can give she no high 
 praise   - love more deeply than Lionel can love ; profess , doubtless 
 at the time believe , that she also love for life ; betroth too ; 
 faith solemnise by promise ; yet in less than a year she be another 's 
 wife . change of air , change of heart ! I do not underrate the effect 
 which a young man , so win as Lionel , would naturally produce on the 
 fancy or the feeling of a girl , who as yet , too , have see no other ; 
 but impression in youth be character in the sand . grave they ever 
 so deeply , the tide roll over they ; and when the ebb show the surface 
 again , the character be go , for the sand be shift . courage ! Lady 
 Montfort will present to her other with form as fair as Lionel 's , and 
 as elegantly dress . with so much in her own favour , there be young 
 patricians enough who will care not a rush what her birth ; - young 
 lords - Lady Montfort know well how fascinating young lord can be ! 
 Courage ! before a year be out , you will find new character write on 
 the sand . " 

 " you do not   know Sophy , sir , " say Waife , simply ; " and I see you be 
 resolve not to know she . but you say Arabella Crane be to inquire ; 
 and should the inquiry prove that she be no child of Gabrielle 
 Desmarets - that she be either your own grandchild or not mine - that - " 

 " let I interrupt you . if there be a thing in the world that be cruel 
 and treacherous , it be a false hope ! crush out of every longing think 
 the belief that this poor girl can prove to be one whom , with my 
 consent , my kinsman can woo to be his wife . Lionel Haughton be the sole 
 kinsman leave to whom I can bequeath this roof - tree - these acre hallow 
 to I because associate with my early lesson in honour and with the 
 dream which direct my life . he must take with the heritage the name 
 it represent . in his child , that name of Darrell can alone live 
 still in the land . I say to you , that even be my daughter now in 
 existence , she would not succeed I - she would not inherit nor 
 transmit that name . why ? - not because I be incapable of a Christian 's 
 forgiveness , but because I be not capable of a gentleman 's treason 
 to his ancestor and himself ; - because Matilda Darrell be false and 
 perfidious ; because she be dead to honour , and therefore her birthright 
 to a heritage of honour be irrevocably forfeit . and since you compel 
 I to speak rudely , while in you I revere a man above the power of law 
 to degrade - while , could we pass a generation , and Sophy be your child 
 by your Lizzy , I should proudly welcome an alliance that make you and I 
 as brother - yet I can not contemplate - it be beyond my power - I can not 
 contemplate the picture of Jasper Losely 's daughter , even by my own 
 child , the Mistress in my father 's home - the bearer of my father 's name . 
 ' tis in vain to argue . grant I the slave of a prejudice - grant these 
 idea to be antiquate bigotry - I be too old to change . I ask from 
 other no sacrifice which I have not bear . and whatever be Lionel 's 
 grief at my resolve , grief will be my companion long after he have 
 forget that he mourn . " 




 CHAPTER IX . 

  POOR SOPHY ! 

 the next morning Mills , in give Sophy a letter from Lady Montfort , 
 give she also one for Waife , and she recognise Lionel Haughton 's 
 handwriting on the address . she go straight to Waife 's sitting - room , 
 for the old man have now resume his early habit , and be up and 
 dress . she place the letter in his hand without a word , and stand 
 by his side while he open it , with a certain still firmness in the 
 expression of her face , as if she be make up her mind to some great 
 effort . the letter be ostensibly one of congratulation . Lionel have see 
 Darrell the day before , after the latter have leave the Home Secretary 's 
 office , and have learn that all which Justice could do to repair the 
 wrong inflict have be do . here Lionel 's word , though brief , be 
 cordial , and almost joyous ; but then come a few sentence steep in 
 gloom . there be an allusion , vague and delicate in itself , to the 
 eventful conversation with Waife in reference to Sophy - a sombre , 
 solemn farewell convey to she and to hope - a passionate prayer for her 
 happiness - and then an abrupt wrench , as it be , away from a subject 
 too intolerably painful to prolong - an intimation that he have succeed 
 in exchange into a regiment very shortly to be send into active 
 service ; that he should set out the next day to join that regiment in 
 a distant part of the country ; and that he trust , should his life 
 be spar by war , that it would be many year before he should revisit 
 England . the sense of the letter be the more affecting in what be 
 conceal than in what be express . evidently Lionel desire to convey 
 to Waife , and leave it to he to inform Sophy , that she be henceforth 
 to regard the writer as vanish out of her existence - depart , as 
 irrevocably as depart the Dead . 

 while Waife be read , he have turn himself aside from Sophy ; he have 
 rise - he have go to the deep recess of the old mullioned window , half 
 screen himself beside the curtain . noiselessly , Sophy follow ; and 
 when he have close the letter , she lay her hand on his arm , and say 
 very quietly : " Grandfather , may I read that letter ? " 

 Waife be startle , and reply on the instant , " no , my dear . " 

 " it be well that I should , " say she , with the same quiet firmness ; 
 and then see the distress in his face , she add , with her more 
 accustomed sweet docility , yet with a forlorn droop of the head : " but as 
 you please , grandfather . " 

 Waife hesitate an instant . be she not right ? - would it not be well 
 to show the letter ? after all , she must confront the fact that Lionel 
 could be nothing to her henceforth ; and would not Lionel 's own word 
 wound she less than all Waife could say ? so he put the letter into her 
 hand , and sate down , watch her countenance . 

 at the open sentence of congratulation , she look up inquiringly . 
 poor man , he have not speak to she of what at another time it would have 
 be such joy to speak ; and he now , in answer to her look , say almost 
 sadly : " only about I , Sophy ; what do that matter ? " but before the 
 girl read , a line far , she smile on he , and tenderly kiss his 
 furrow brow . 

 " do not   read on , Sophy , " say he quickly . she shake her head and resume . 
 his eye still upon her face , he mark it change as the sense of the 
 letter grow upon she , till , as , without a word , with scarce a visible 
 heave of the bosom , she lay the letter on his knee , the change have 
 become so complete , that it seem as if another stand in her place . in 
 very young and sensitive person , especially female   though I have 
 see it even in our hard sex , a great and sudden shock or revulsion of 
 feeling reveal itself thus in the almost preternatural alteration of 
 the countenance . it be not a mere paleness - a skin - deep loss of colour : 
 it be as if the whole bloom of youth have rush away ; hollow never 
 discernible before appear in the cheek that be so round and smooth ; the 
 muscle fall as in mortal illness ; a havoc , as of year , seem to have 
 be work in a moment ; flame itself do not so suddenly ravage - so 
 suddenly alter - leave behind it so ineffable an air of desolation and 
 ruin . Waife spring forward and clasp she to his breast . 

 " you will bear it , Sophy ! the bad be over now . Fortitude , my 
 child ! - fortitude ! the human heart be wonderfully sustain when it 
 be not the conscience that weigh it down - grief , that we think at the 
 moment must kill we , wear themselves away . I speak the truth , for I too 
 have suffer ! " 

 " poor grandfather ! " say Sophy , gently ; and she say no more . but when 
 he would have continue to speak comfort , or exhort to patience , she 
 press his hand tightly , and lay her finger on her lip . he be hush 
 in an instant . 

 presently she begin to move about the room , busy herself , as usual , 
 in those slight , scarce perceptible arrangement by which she love to 
 think that she minister to the old man 's simple comfort . she place 
 the armchair in his favourite nook by the window , and before it the 
 footstool for the poor lame foot ; and draw the table near the chair , 
 and look over the book that George have select for his perusal from 
 Darrell 's library ; and choose the volume in which she see his mark , 
 to place near to his hand , and tenderly clear the mist from his 
 reading - glass ; and remove one or two withered or ailing snowdrop from 
 the little winter nosegay she have gather for he the day before - he 
 watch she all the time , silent as herself , not dare , indeed , to 
 speak , lest his heart should overflow . 

 these little task of love over , she come towards he a few pace , and 
 say : " please , dear grandfather , tell I all about what have happen to 
 yourself , which should make we glad - that be , by - and - by ; but nothing 
 as to the rest of that letter . I will just think over it by myself ; but 
 never let we talk of it , grandy dear , never more - never more . " 




 CHAPTER X. 

  TREES that , like the POPLAR , LIFT UPWARD all their boughs , give no 
  shade and no SHELTER , whatever their height . TREES the most 
  LOVINGLY SHELTER and shade US , when , like the willow , the high 
  SOAR their summit , the lowlier DROOP their BOUGHS . 

 usually when Sophy leave Waife in the morning , she would wander out into 
 the ground , and he could see her pass before his window ; or she would 
 look into the library , which be almost exclusively give up to the 
 Morleys , and he could hear her tread on the old creaking stair . but 
 now she have steal into her own room , which communicate with his 
 sitting - room - a small lobby alone intervene - and there she remain so 
 long that he grow uneasy . he creep softly to her door and listen . he 
 have a fineness of hear almost equal to his son 's ; but he could not 
 hear a sob - not a breath . at length he softly open the door and look 
 in with caution . 

 the girl be seat at the foot of the bed , quite still - her eye fix 
 on the ground , and her finger to her lip , just as she have place it 
 there when implore silence ; so still , it might be even slumber . all 
 who have grieve respect grief . Waife do not like to approach she ; but 
 he say , from his stand at the threshold : " the sun be quite bright now , 
 Sophy ; go out for a little while , darling . " 

 she do not look round , she do not stir ; but she answer with 
 readiness , " yes , presently . " 

 so he close the door and leave she . an hour pass away ; he look in 
 again ; there she be still - in the same place , in the same attitude . 

 " Sophy , dear , it be time to take your walk ; go - Mrs. Morley be in front , 
 before my window . I have call to she to wait for you . " 

 " yes - presently , " answer Sophy , and she do not move . 

 Waife be seriously alarmed . he pause a moment - then go back to his 
 room - take his hat and his staff - come back . 

 " Sophy , I should like to hobble out and breathe the air ; it will do I 
 good . will you give I your arm ? I be still very weak . " 

 Sophy now start - shake back her fair curl - rose - put on her bonnet , and 
 in less than a minute be by the old man 's side . draw his arm fondly 
 into her , they descend the stair ; they be in the garden ; Mrs. Morley 
 come to meet they - then George . Wife exert himself to talk - to be 
 gay - to protect Sophy 's abstract silence by his own active , desultory , 
 erratic humour . twice or thrice , as he lean on Sophy 's arm , she draw 
 it still near to she , and press it tenderly . she understand - she 
 thank he . Hark ! from some undiscovered hiding - place near the 
 water - Fairthorn 's flute ! the music fill the landscape as with a living 
 presence ; the swan pause upon the still lake - the tame doe steal 
 through yonder leafless tree ; and now , musing and slow , from the same 
 desolate covert , come the doe 's master . the music spell they all . Guy 
 Darrell see his guest where they have halt by the stone sun - dial . 
 he advance - join they - congratulate Waife on his first walk as a 
 convalescent . he quote Gray 's well - know verse applicable to that 
 event , and when , in that voice sweet as the flute itself , he come to 
 the line :   " see the wretch who long have tost , "   c. - GRAY . 

  " the common sun , the air , the sky , 
  to he be open paradise " 

 Sophy , as if suddenly strike with remorse at the thought that she , 
 and she alone , be mar that open paradise to the old man in his 
 escape from the sick - room to " the sun , the air , the sky , " abruptly 
 raise her look from the ground , and turn they full upon her 
 guardian 's face , with an attempt at gladness in her quivering smile , 
 which , whatever its effect on Waife , go straight to the innermost 
 heart of Guy Darrell . on the instant he recognise , as by intuitive 
 sympathy , the anguish from which that smile struggle forth - know that 
 Sophy have now learn that grief which lie deep within himself - that 
 grief which make a sick chamber of the whole external world , and which 
 greet no more , in the common boon of Nature , the opening Paradise of 
 recover Hope ! his eye linger on her face as its smile wan , and 
 perceive that change which have so startled Waife . involuntarily he 
 move to her side - involuntarily draw her arm within his own - she thus 
 support the one who cherish - support by the one who disown she . 
 Guy Darrell might be stern in resolve which afflict other , as he be 
 stern in afflict himself ; but for other he have at least compassion . 

 Poor Waife , with nature so different , mark Darrell 's movement , and , 
 ever ready to seize on comfort , say inly : " he relent . I will not go 
 to - morrow as I have intend . Sophy must win her way ; who can resist 
 she ? " 

 talk languish - the wintry sun begin to slope - the air grow keen - Waife 
 be lead in - the Morleys go up into his room to keep he company - Sophy 
 escape back to her own . Darrell continue his walk , plunge deep into 
 his maze of beechwood , follow by the doe . the swan dip their neck 
 amongst the water - weed ; the flute have cease , and drearily still be the 
 grey horizon , see through the skeleton boughs - see behind the ragged 
 sky - line of shaft and parapet in the skeleton palace . 

 Darrell do not visit Waife 's room that day ; he conclude that Waife 
 and Sophy would wish to be much alone ; he dread renewal of the only 
 subject on which he have no cheer word to say . Sophy 's smile , Sophy 's 
 face haunt he . in vain he repeat to himself : " tut , it will soon 
 pass - only a girl 's first fancy . " 

 but Sophy do not come back to Waife 's room when the Morleys have leave 
 it : Waife creep into her room as before , and , as before , there she sit 
 still as if in slumber . she come in , however , of her own accord , to 
 assist , as usual , in the meal which he take apart in his room help 
 he - help herself , but eat nothing . she talk , however , almost gaily ; 
 hop he will be well enough to leave the next day ; wonder whether 
 Sir Isaac have miss they very much ; read to he Lady Montfort 's 
 affectionate letter to herself ; and when dinner be over , and Waife 's 
 chair draw to the fireside , she take her old habitual place on 
 the stool beside he , and say : " now , dear grandfather - all about 
 yourself - what happy thing have chance to you ? " 

 alas ! poor Waife have but little heart to speak ; but he force himself ; 
 what he have to say may do good to she . 

 " you know that , on my own account , I have reason for secresy - change of 
 name . I shun all those whom I have ever know in former day ; could 
 take no calling in life by which I might be recognise ; deem it a 
 bless mercy of Providence that when , not able to resist offer that 
 would have enable I to provide for you as I never otherwise could , I 
 assent to hazard an engagement at a London theatre - trust for my 
 incognito to an actor 's art of disguise - come the accident which , of 
 itself , annihilate the temptation into which I have suffer myself to 
 be lead . for , ah , child ! have it be know who and what be the William 
 Waife whose stage - mime trick move harmless mirth , or tear as 
 pleasant , the audience would have rise , not to applaud , but hoot , 
 ' Off , off , ' from both world - the Mimic as the real ! well , have I be 
 dishonest , you - you alone feel that I could not have dare to take 
 you , guiltless infant , by the hand . you remember that , on my return 
 to Rugge 's wandering theatre , bring you with I , I exaggerate the 
 effect of my accident - affect to have lose voice - stipulate to be 
 spar appear on his stage . that be not the mere pride of manhood 
 shrink from the display of physical affliction . no . in the first 
 village that we arrive at , I recognise an old friend , and I see that , 
 in spite of time , and the accident that have disfigure I , he recognise 
 I , and turn away his face , as if in loathing . an old friend , 
 Sophy - an old friend ! oh , it pierce I to the heart ; and I resolve , 
 from that day , to escape from Rugge 's stage ; and I consent till the 
 mean of escape , and some less dependent mode of livelihood , be find , 
 to live on thy earning , child ; for if I be discover by other old 
 friend , and they speak out , my disgrace would reflect on you ; and 
 well to accept support , from you than that ! alas ! appearance be so 
 strong against I , I never deem they could be clear away , even from 
 the sight of my near friend . but Providence , you know , have be so 
 kind to we hitherto ; and so Providence will be kind to we again , Sophy . 
 and now , the very man I think most hard to I - this very Guy Darrell , 
 under whose roof we be - have be the man to make those whose opinion 
 I most value know that I be not dishonest ; and Providence have raise a 
 witness on my behalf in that very Mr. Hartopp , who judge I   and any 
 one else might have do the same   too bad to be fit company for you ! 
 and that be why I be congratulate ; and , oh , Sophy , though I have bear 
 it as Heaven do enable we to bear what of ourselves we could not , and 
 though one learn to shrug a patient shoulder under the obloquy which 
 may be heap on we by that crowd of mere stranger to we and to each 
 other , which be call ' the world , ' yet to slink out of sight from a 
 friend , as one more to be shun than a foe - to take like a coward the 
 lashing of Scorn - to wince , one raw sore , from the kindness of Pity - to 
 feel that in life the sole end of each shift and contrivance be to slip 
 the view - hallo , into a grave without epitaph , by path as stealthy and 
 sly as the poor hunted fox , when his last chance - and sole one - be , by 
 winding and doubling , to run under the earth ; to know that it would be 
 an ungrateful imposture to take chair at the board - at the hearth , of 
 the man who , unknowe your secret , say , ' friend , be social ' ; accept 
 not a crust that one do not pay for , lest one feel a swindler to the 
 kind fellow - creature whose equal we must not be ! - all this - all this , 
 Sophy , do at time chafe and gall more than I ought to have let it do , 
 consider that there be one who see it all , and would - do not   cry , 
 Sophy ; it be all over now . " 

 " not cry ! oh , it do I so much good . " 

 " all over now ! I be under this roof - without shame or scruple ; and if 
 Guy Darrell , know all my past , have prove my innocence in the eye 
 of those whom alone I care for , I feel as if I have the right to stand 
 before any crowd of man erect and shameless - a man once more with Men ! 
 oh , darling ! let I but see thy old happy smile again ! the happy 
 smile of the young be the sunshine of the old . be patient - be firm ; 
 Providence be so very kind , Sophy . " 




 CHAPTER XI . 

  WAIFE EXACTS from GEORGE MORLEY the FULFILMENT of one of those 
  promise which MEAN nothing or everything . 

 the next day George Morley visit Waife 's room early than usual . 
 Waife have send for he . Sophy be seat by her grandfather - his hand in 
 her . she have be exert herself to the utmost to talk cheerfully - to 
 shake from her aspect every cloud of sorrow . but still that CHANGE be 
 there - more marked than even on the previous day . a few hour of intense 
 struggle , a single night wholly without sleep , will tell on the face 
 of early youth . not till we , hard veteran , have go through such 
 struggle as life permit not to the slight responsibility of new 
 recruit - not till sleepless night have grow to we familiar will 
 Thought seem to take , as it be , strength , not exhaustion , from 
 unrelaxe exercise - nourish the brain , sustain the form by its own 
 untiring , fleshless , spiritual immortality ; not till many a winter have 
 strip the leaf ; not till deep , and far out of sight , spread the 
 root that support the stem - will the beat of the east wind leave no 
 sign on the rind . 

 George have not , indeed , so notice , the day before , the kind of 
 wither blight that have pass over the girl 's countenance ; but he do 
 now - when she meet his eye more steadfastly , and have resume something of 
 the open genial infantine grace of manner which constitute her peculiar 
 charm , and which it be difficult to associate with deep grief than 
 those of childhood . 

 " you must scold my grandfather , " she say . " he choose to fancy that 
 he be not well enough yet to leave ; and I be sure that he be , and will 
 recover more quickly at home than here . " 

 " Pooh ! " say Waife ; " you young thing suppose we old folk can be as 
 brisk as yourself ; but if I be to be scold , leave Mr. George unawe 
 by your presence , and go out , my dear , while the sun last : I know by 
 the way of that blackbird that the day will be overcast by noon . " 

 as soon as they be alone , George say abruptly : " your Sophy be look 
 very ill , and if you be well enough to leave , it might be well 
 for she to move from this gloomy house . Movement itself be a great 
 restorative , " add George , with emphasis . 

 " you see , then , that she look ill - very ill , " say Waife deliberately ; 
 " and there be that in your manner which tell I you guess the cause . " 

 " I do guess it from the glimpse which I catch of Lionel 's face after he 
 have be closet a short time with Mr. Darrell at my uncle 's house two 
 day ago . I guess it also from a letter I have receive from my uncle . " 

 " you guess right - very right , " say Waife , still with the same serious , 
 tranquil manner . " I show she this letter from young Haughton . read 
 it . " George hurry his eye over the letter , and return it silently . 
 Waife proceed : 

 " I be frightened yesterday by the strange composure she show . in 
 her face alone could be read what she suffer . we talk last night . 
 I speak of myself - of my old sorrow - in order to give her strength to 
 support her ; and the girl have a heroic nature , Mr. George - and she be 
 resolve to conquer or to die . but she will not conquer . " George begin 
 the usual strain of a console in such trial . Waife stop he . " all 
 that you can say , Mr. George , I know beforehand ; and she will need no 
 exhortation to prayer and to fortitude . I steal from my room when it be 
 almost dawn . I see a light under the door of her chamber . I just look 
 in - softly - unperceived . she have not go to bed . she be by the open 
 window - star die out of the sky - kneeling on the floor , her face 
 bury in her hand . she have pray . in her soul , at this moment , be 
 sure that she be pray now . she will devote herself to I - she will be 
 cheerful - you will hear her laugh , Mr. George ; but she will not conquer 
 in this world ; long before the new year be out , she will be look 
 down upon our grief with her bright smile ; but we shall not see she , Mr. 
 George . do not think this be an old man 's foolish terror ; I know sorrow 
 as physicians know disease ; it have its mortal symptom . hush ! hear I 
 out . I have one hope - it be in you . " 

 " in I ? " 

 " yes . do you remember that you say , if I could succeed in opening to 
 your intellect its fair career , you would be the good friend to I man 
 ever have ? and I say , ' agree , but change the party in the contract ; 
 befriend my Sophy instead of I , and if ever I ask you , help I in aught 
 for her welfare and happiness ; ' and you say , ' with heart and soul . ' 
 that be the bargain , Mr. George . now you have all that you then 
 despair of ; you have the dignity of your sacred calling - you have the 
 eloquence of the preacher . I can not cope with Mr. Darrell - you can . he 
 have a heart - it can be soften ; he have a soul - it can be free from the 
 wither that tether it down ; he have the virtue you can appeal to ; and 
 he have the pride which you , as a christian minister , have the right to 
 prove to be a sin . I can not argue with he ; I can not reprove the man to 
 whom I owe so much . all rank of man and of mind should be equal to you , 
 the pastor , the divine . you minister of the gospel address yourself 
 unabashed to the poor , the humble , the uninstructed . do Heaven give you 
 power and commandment over these alone ? go , preacher ! go ! speak with the 
 same authority to the great , to the haughty , to the wise ! " the old man 's 
 look and gesture be sublime . 

 the Preacher feel a thrill vibrate from his ear to his heart ; but his 
 reason be less affected than his heart . he shake his head mournfully . 
 the task thus assign to he be beyond the limit which custom 
 prescribe to the priest of the English Church ; - dictation to a man not 
 even of his own flock , upon the close affair of that man 's private 
 hearth and home ! our society allow no such privilege ; and our society 
 be right . 

 Waife , watch his countenance , see at once what be pass in his 
 mind , and resume , as if answer George 's own thought : 

 " ay , if you be but the commonplace priest ! but , you be something 
 more ; you be the priest specially endow for all special purpose of 
 good . you have the mind to reason - the tongue to persuade - the majestic 
 earnestness of impassioned zeal . nor be you here the priest alone ; you 
 be here the friend , the confidant , of all for whom you may exert your 
 power . oh , George Morley , I be a poor ignorant blunderer when presume 
 to exhort you as Christian minister ; but in your own word - I address 
 you as man and gentleman , you declare that ' think and zeal should not 
 stammer whenever I say , keep your promise . ' I say it now - keep faith to 
 the child you swear to I to befriend ! " 

 " I will go - and at once , " say George , rise . " but be not sanguine . 
 I see not a chance of success . a man so superior to myself in year , 
 station , ability , repute ! " 

 " where would be Christianity , " say Waife , " if the early preacher 
 have raise such question ? there be a soldier 's courage - be there not a 
 priest 's ? " 

 George make no answer , but , with abstracted eye , gather brow , and 
 slow , meditative step , quit the room , and seek Guy Darrell . 




 BOOK XII . 




 CHAPTER I. 

  the MAN of the world shows MORE indifference to the thing and 
  DOCTRINES of the world than might be suppose . - but he vindicate 
  his character , which might otherwise be jeopardised , by THE 
  ADROITNESS with which , have resolve to ROAST CHESTNUTS in the 
  ASHES of another MAN 'S HEARTH , he handle they when hottest by the 
  PROXY of a - CAT 'S PAW . 

 in the letter which George tell Waife he have receive from his uncle , 
 George have an excuse for the delicate and arduous mission he undertake , 
 which he do not confide to the old man , lest it should convey more 
 hope than its nature justify . in this letter , Alban relate , with a 
 degree of feeling that he rarely manifest , his farewell conversation 
 with Lionel , who have just depart to join his new regiment . the poor 
 young man have buoy himself up with delighted expectation of the 
 result of Sophy 's prolonged residence under Darrell 's roof ; he have 
 persuade his reason that when Darrell have be thus enable to see and 
 judge of she for himself , he would be irresistibly attract towards 
 she ; that Innocence , like truth , would be mighty and prevail ; Darrell 
 be engage in the attempt to clear William Losely 's name and blood from 
 the taint of felony ; - Alban be commission to negotiate with Jasper 
 Losely on any term that would remove all chance of future disgrace 
 from that quarter . oh yes ! to poor Lionel 's eye obstacle vanish - the 
 future become clear . and thus , when , after tell he of his final 
 interview with the Minister , Darrell say , " I trust that , in bring 
 to William Losely this intelligence , I shall at least soften his 
 disappointment , when I make it thoroughly clear to he how impossible it 
 be that his Sophy can ever be more to I - to we - than a stranger whose 
 virtue create an interest in her welfare " - Lionel be stun as by a 
 blow . scarcely could he murmur : 

  " you have see she - and your resolve remain the same . " 

 " can you doubt it ? " answer Darrell , as if in surprise . " the resolve 
 may now give I pain on my account , as before it give I pain on your . 
 but if not move by your pain , can I be move by mine ? that would be a 
 baseness . " the Colonel , in depict Lionel 's state of mind after 
 the young soldier have write his farewell to Waife , and previous to 
 quit London , express very gloomy foreboding . " I do not say , " 
 write he , " that Lionel will guiltily seek death in the field , nor do 
 death there come more to those who seek than to those who shun it ; 
 but he will go upon a service expose to more than ordinary suffering , 
 privation , and disease - without that rallying power of hope - that will , 
 and Desire to Live , which constitute the true stamina of Youth . and 
 I have always set a black mark upon those who go into war joyless and 
 despondent . send a young fellow to the camp with his spirit break , his 
 heart heavy as a lump of lead , and the first of those epidemic , which 
 thin rank more than the cannon , say to itself , ' there be a man 
 for I ! ' any doctor will tell you that , even at home , the gay and 
 light - hearted walk safe through the pestilence , which settle on the 
 moping as malaria settle on a marsh . Confound Guy Darrell 's ancestor , 
 they have spoil Queen Victoria as good a young soldier as ever wear 
 a sword by his side ! six month ago , and how blithely Lionel Haughton 
 look forth to the future ! - all laurel ! - no cypress ! and now I feel as 
 if I have shake hand with a victim sacrifice by Superstition to 
 the tomb of the dead . I can not blame darrell : I dare say in the same 
 position I might do the same . but no ; on second thought , I should not . 
 if Darrell do not choose to marry and have son of his own , he have no 
 right to load a poor boy with benefit , and say : ' there be but one way 
 to prove your gratitude ; remember my ancestor , and be miserable for the 
 rest of your day ! ' Darrell , forsooth , intend to leave to Lionel the 
 transmission of the old Darrell name ; and the old Darrell name must 
 not be tarnish by the marriage on which Lionel have unluckily set his 
 heart ! I respect the old name ; but it be not like the House of Vipont - a 
 British Institution . and if some democratical cholera , which do not 
 care a rush for old name , carry off Lionel , what become of the old 
 name then ? Lionel be not Darrell 's son ; Lionel need not perforce take 
 the old name . let the young man live as Lionel Haughton , and the old 
 name die with Guy Darrell ! 

 " as to the poor girl 's birth and parentage , I believe we shall never 
 know they . I quite agree with Darrell that it will be wise never to 
 inquire . but I dismiss , as farfetched and improbable , his supposition 
 that she be Gabrielle Desmaret 's daughter . to I it be infinitely more 
 likely , either that the deposition of the nurse , which poor Willy give 
 to Darrell , and which Darrell show to I , be true   only that Jasper 
 be connive at the temporary suspension of his child 's existence while 
 it suit his purpose   - or that , at the bad , this mysterious young 
 lady be the daughter of the artiste . in the former supposition , as I 
 have say over and over again , a marriage between Lionel and Sophy 
 be precisely that which darrell should desire ; in the latter case , of 
 course , if Lionel be the head of the House of Vipont , the idea of such 
 an union would be inadmissible . but Lionel , entre nous , be the son of 
 a ruin spendthrift by a linen - draper 's daughter . and Darrell have but 
 to give the handsome young couple five or six thousand a year , and I 
 know the world well enough to know that the world will trouble itself 
 very little about their pedigree . and really Lionel should be leave 
 wholly free to choose whether he prefer a girl whom he love with his 
 whole heart , five or six thousand a year , happiness , and the chance of 
 honour in a glorious profession to which he will then look with 
 glad spirit - or a life - long misery , with the right , after Darrell 's 
 death - that I hope will not be these thirty year - to bear the name of 
 Darrell instead of Haughton ; which , if I be the last of the Haughtons , 
 and have any family pride - as , thank Heaven I have not - would be a 
 painful exchange to I ; and dearly buy by the addition of some 
 additional thousand a year , when I have grow perhaps as little dispose 
 to spend they as Guy Darrell himself be . but , after all , there be one 
 I compassionate even more than young Haughton . my morning rid of late 
 have be much in the direction of Twickenham , visit our fair cousin 
 Lady Montfort . I go first to lecture she for let these young 
 people see so much of each other . but my anger melt into admiration 
 and sympathy when I find with what tender , exquisite , matchless 
 friendship she have be all the while scheme for Darrell 's happiness ; 
 and with what remorse she now contemplate the sorrow which a friendship 
 so grateful , and a belief so natural , have innocently occasion . that 
 remorse be wear she to death . Dr. F - - - , who attend poor dear 
 Willy , be also attend she ; and he tell I privately that his 
 skill be in vain - that her case baffle he ; and he have very serious 
 apprehension . darrell owe some consideration to such a friend . and to 
 think that here be life permanently embitter , if not risk , by the 
 ruthless obstinacy of the well - hearted man I ever meet ! now , though I 
 have already intimate my opinion to Darrell with a candour due to the 
 old and dearest of my friend , yet I have never , of course , in the 
 letter I have write to he or the talk we have have together , speak 
 out so plainly as I do in writing to you . and have thus write , 
 without awe of his grey eye and dark brow , I have half as mind to add 
 ' seize he in a happy moment and show he this letter . ' yes , I give you 
 full leave ; show it to he if you think it would avail . if not , throw 
 it into the fire , and - pray Heaven for those whom we poor mortal can not 
 serve . " 

 on the envelope Alban have add these word : " but of course , before 
 show the enclosed , you will prepare Darrell 's mind to weigh its 
 content . " and probably it be in that curt and simple injunction that 
 the subtle man of the world evince the astuteness of which not a trace 
 be apparent in the body of his letter . 

 Though Alban 's communication have much excite his nephew , yet George have 
 not judge it discreet to avail himself of the permission to show it 
 to Darrell . it seem to he that the pride of his host would take much 
 more offence at its transmission through the hand of a third person 
 than at the frank tone of its reasoning and suggestion . and George 
 have determine to re - enclose it to the Colonel , urge he to forward it 
 himself to Darrell just as it be , with but a brief line to say , " that , 
 on reflection , Alban submit direct to his old school - fellow the 
 reasoning and apprehension which he have so unreservedly pour forth 
 in a letter commence without the intention at which the writer arrive 
 at the close . " but now that the preacher have undertake the duty of an 
 advocate , the letter become his brief . 

 George pass through the library , through the study , up the narrow 
 stair that finally conduct to the same lofty cell in which Darrell 
 have confront the midnight robber who claim a child in Sophy . with a 
 nervous hand George knock at the door . 

 unaccustomed to any intrusion on the part of guest or household in that 
 solitary retreat , somewhat sharply , as if in anger , Darrell 's voice 
 answer the knock . 

 " who be there ? " 

 " George Morley . " 

 Darrell open the door . 




 CHAPTER II . 

  " a good ARCHER be not know by his arrows , but his aim . " " a good 
  MAN be no MORE to be feared than a sheep . " " a good surgeon must 
  have an EAGLE 's EYE , a LION 's heart , and a lady 's hand . " " a good 
  tongue be a good weapon . " and despite those SUGGESTIVE or 
  ENCOURAGING PROVERBS , GEORGE MORLEY have UNDERTAKEN something so 
  opposed to all proverbial philosophy that it become a grave 
  question what he will do with it . 

 " I come , " say George , " to ask you one of the great favour a man can 
 confer upon another ; it will take some little time to explain . be you 
 at leisure ? " 

 Darrell 's brow relaxed . 

 " seat yourself in comfort , my dear George . if it be in my power to serve 
 or to gratify Alban Morley 's nephew , it be I who receive a favour . " 
 Darrell think to himself - " the young man be ambitious - I may aid in 
 his path towards a see ! " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " first let I say that I would consult your intellect 
 on a matter which habitually attract and engage mine - that old vexed 
 question of the origin and use of Evil , not only in the physical , but 
 the moral world ; it involve problem over which I would ponder for 
 hour as a boy - on which I write essay as a schoolman - on which I 
 perpetually collect illustration to fortify my view as a theologian . " 

 " he be write a book , " think Darrell , enviously ; " and a book on such 
 a subject will last he all his life . happy man ! " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " the Pastor , you know , be frequently consult by the 
 suffering and oppress ; frequently call upon to answer that question 
 in which the scepticism of the humble and the ignorant ordinarily 
 begin : ' why be I suffer ? why be I oppress ? be this the justice of 
 Providence ? have the Great Father that benign pity , that watchful care 
 for his child , which you preacher tell we ? ' ever intent on deduce 
 example from the life to which the clue have become apparent , must 
 be the Priest who have to reason with affliction cause by no apparent 
 fault ; and where , judge by the Canons of Human justice , cloud and 
 darkness obscure the Divine - still to ' vindicate the way of God to 
 man . ' " 

 DARRELL . - " a philosophy that precede , and will outlive , all other 
 school . it be twin - bear with the world itself . go on ; though the theme 
 be inexhaustible , its interest never flag . " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " have it strike you , Mr. Darrell , that few life have 
 ever pass under your survey ; in which the inexpressible tenderness of 
 the Omniscient have be more visibly clear than in that of your guest , 
 William Losely ? " 

 DARRELL   surprise . - " clear ? to I , I confess that if ever there be 
 an instance in which the divine tenderness , the Divine justice , which I 
 can never presume to doubt , be yet undiscernible to my bounded vision , 
 it be in the instance of the very life you refer to . I see a man of 
 admirable virtue - of a childlike simplicity of character , which make 
 he almost unconscious of the grandeur of his own soul - involve by 
 a sublime self - sacrifice - by a virtue , not by a fault - in the most 
 dreadful of human calamity - ignominious degradation ; - hurl in the 
 midday of life from the sphere of honest man - a felon 's brand on his 
 name - a vagrant in his age ; justice at last , but tardy and niggard , and 
 give he but little joy when it arrive ; because , ever think only 
 of other , his heart be wrap in a child whom he can not make happy in 
 the way in which his hope have be set ! - George - no , your illustration 
 might be turn by a sceptic into an argument against you . " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " not unless the sceptic refuse the elementary 
 starting - ground from which you and I may reason ; not if it be grant 
 that man have a soul , which it be the object of this life to enrich 
 and develop for another . we know from my uncle what William Losely be 
 before this calamity befell he - a genial boon - companion - a careless , 
 frank , ' good fellow ' - all the virtue you now praise in he dormant , 
 unguesse even by himself . suddenly come CALAMITY ! - suddenly arise the 
 SOUL ! degradation of name , and with it dignity of nature ! how poor , how 
 slight , how insignificant William Losely the hanger - on of rural Thanes 
 compare with that William Waife whose entrance into this house , 
 you - despite that felon 's brand when you know it be the martyr 's 
 glory , - greet with noble reverence ; whom , when the mind itself be 
 stricken down - only the soul leave to the wreck of the body - you tend 
 with such pious care as he lie on - your father 's bed ! and do you , who 
 hold Nobleness in such honour - do you , of all man , tell I that you 
 can not recognise that celestial tenderness which ennoble a Spirit for 
 all eternity ? " 

 " George , you be right , " cry Darrell ; " and I be a blockhead and 
 blunderer , as man always be when he mistake a speck in his telescope 
 for a blotch in the sun of a system . " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " but more difficult it be to recognise the mysterious 
 agency of Heavenly Love when no great worldly adversity force we 
 to pause and question . let Fortune strike down a victim , and even the 
 heathen cry , ' this be the hand of God ! ' but where Fortune bring no 
 vicissitude ; where her wheel run smooth , drop wealth or honour 
 as it roll - where affliction centre its work within the secret , 
 unreveale heart - there , even the wise man may not readily perceive 
 by what mean Heaven be admonish , force , or woo he near to 
 itself . I take the case of a man in whom Heaven acknowledge a favour 
 son . I assume his outward life crown with success , his mind store 
 with opulent gift , his nature endow with lofty virtue ; what an heir 
 to train through the brief school of earth for due place in the age 
 that roll on for ever ! but this man have a parasite weed in each bed of 
 a soul rich in flower ; - weed and flower intertwine , stem with 
 stem - their fibre unite even deep down to the root . 

 " can you not conceive with what untiring vigilant care Heaven will seek 
 to disentangle the flower from the weed ? - how   let I drop inadequate 
 metaphor   - how Heaven will select for its warning chastisement that 
 very error which the man have so blent with his virtue that he hold it 
 a virtue itself ? - how , gradually , slowly , pertinaciously , it will gather 
 this beautiful nature all to itself - insist on a sacrifice it will 
 ask from no other ? to complete the true nature of poor William Losely , 
 Heaven ordain the sacrifice of worldly repute ; to complete the true 
 nature of Guy Darrell , God ordain he the sacrifice of PRIDE ! " 

 Darrell start - half rise ; his eye flash - his cheek pal ; but he 
 remain silent . 

 " I have approach the favour I supplicate , " resume George , draw a 
 deep breath , as of relief . " great favour man can scarcely bestow 
 upon his fellow . I entreat you to believe that I respect , and love ,

 friendship that I may claim the privilege , without which friendship 
 be but a form ; - just as no freedom be more obnoxious than intrusion 
 on confidence withhold , so no favour , I repeat , more precious than the 
 confidence which a man of worth vouchsafe to he who invite it with no 
 claim but the loyalty of his motif . " 

 say Darrell , soften , but with stateliness : " all human life be as 
 separate circle ; they may touch at one point in friendly approach , but , 
 even where they touch , each round itself from off the other . with this 
 hint I be content to ask at what point in my circle you would touch ? " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " I thank you gratefully ; I accept your illustration . 
 the point be touch ; I need no other . " he pause a moment , as if 
 concentrate all his thought , and then say , with musing accent : 
 " yes , I accept your illustration ; I will even strengthen the force of 
 the truth imply in it by a more homely illustration of my own . there 
 be small skeleton abridgment of history which we give to child . 
 in such a year a king be crown - a battle be fight ; there be 
 some great disaster , or some great triumph . of the true progress and 
 development of the nation whose record be thus epitomise - of the 
 complicated cause which lead to these salient event - of the animate , 
 varied multitudinous life which have be hurry on from epoch to 
 epoch , the abridgment tell nothing . it be so with the life of each 
 individual man : the life as it stand before we be but a sterile 
 epitome - hide from our sight the emotion which be the People of the 
 Heart . in such a year occur a visible something - a gain - a loss - a 
 success - a disappointment ; the People of the heart crown or depose 
 a king . this be all we know ; and the most voluminous biography 
 ever write must still be a meagre abridgment of all that really 
 individualised and form a man . I ask not your confidence in a single 
 detail or fact in your existence which lie beyond my sight . far from I 
 so curious an insolence ; but I do ask you this : reflect on your past 
 life as a whole , have not your chief sorrow have a common idiosyncrasy ? 
 have they not be strangely direct towards the frustration of some 
 one single object - cherish by your early hope , and , as if in 
 defiance of fate , resolutely clung to even now ? " 

 " it be true , " mutter Darrell . " you do not offend I ; go on ! " 

 " and have not these sorrow , in frustrate your object , often assume ,

 harass or invade , almost as if it be a strategic policy that guide 
 they where they could most pain , or humble , or eject a FOE that they 
 be order to storm ? degrade you they could not ; such be not their 
 mission . Heaven leave you intact a kingliness of nature - a loftiness of 
 spirit , unabase by assault level not against yourself , but your 
 pride ; your personal dignity , though singularly sensitive , though 
 bitterly gall , stand proof . what might lower less man , lower 
 not you ; Heaven leave you that dignity , for it belong alike to your 
 intellect and your virtue - but suffer it to be a source of your

 be cover the fault against which be direct the sorrow . you 
 frown - forgive I . " 

 " you do not transgress , unless it be as a flatterer ! if I frown , it 
 be unconsciously - the sign of thought , not anger . pause ! - my mind have 
 leave you for a moment ; it be look into the past . " 

 the past ! - be it not true ? that home to whose porch come in time the 
 Black Horses , in time just to save from the last bad dishonour , but 
 not save from year rack by each pang that can harrow man 's dignity 
 in each daily assault on the fort of man 's pride ; the sly treacherous 
 daughter - her terrible marriage - the man whose disgrace she have link

 true , what a war upon Pride ! and even in that secret and fatal love 
 which have be of all his grief the most influential and enduring , have 
 his pride be less bitterly wound , and that pride less enthroned in 
 his being , would his grief have be so relentless , his attempt at 
 its conquest so vain ? and then , even now - what be it say , " I can 
 bless ? " - holy LOVE ! what be it say , " but not pardon " ? - stern PRIDE ! 
 and so onto these last revolution of sterile life . be he not miserable 
 in Lionel 's and Sophy 's misery ? forlorn in that Citadel of Pride - close 
 round and invest with sorrow - and the last hope that have flee to the 
 fortress , slay in defence of its outwork . with hand shade his face , 
 Darrell remain some minute silent . at last he raise his head , and 
 his eye be steadfast , his lip firm . 

 " George Morley , " say he , " I acknowledge much justice in the censure you 
 have convey , with so artful a delicacy that , if it fail to reform , it 
 can not displease , and leave much to be seriously revolve in solitary 
 self - commune . but though I may own that pride be not make for man , and 
 that in the blindness of human judgment I may often have confound 
 pride with duty , and suffer for the mistake , yet that one prevail 
 object of my life , which with so startling a truth you say it have 
 please Heaven to frustrate , I can not hold an error in itself . you have 
 learn enough from your uncle , see enough of I yourself , to know what 
 that object have be . you be scholar enough to concede to I that it 
 be no ignoble homage which either nation or person render to the 
 ancestral Dead - that homage be an instinct in all but vulgar and sordid 
 nature . have a man no ancestry of his own - rightly and justly , if 
 himself of worth , he appropriate to his lineage all the hero , and 
 bard , and patriot of his fatherland ! a free citizen have ancestor 
 in all the glorious chief that have adorn the State , on the sole 
 condition that he shall revere their tomb and guard their memory as 
 a son ! and thus , whenever they who speak trumpet - tongue to grand 
 democracy would rouse some quail generation to heroic deed or 
 sacrifice , they appeal in the name of Ancestors , and call upon the 
 living to be worthy of the dead ! that which be so laudable - nay , so 
 necessary a sentiment in the mass , can not be a fault that anger Heaven 
 in the man . like all high sentiment , it may compel harsh and rugged 
 duty ; it may need the stern suppression of many a gentle impulse - of 
 many a pleasing wish . but we must regard it in its merit and consistency

 Lionel 's generous love and yon fair girl 's ingenuous and touching grace , 
 let we break up the court ; the judge have no choice but the law which 
 imperiously govern his judgment . " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " I have not hitherto presume to apply to particular 
 case the general argument you so indulgently allow I to urge in 
 favour of my theory , that in the world of the human heart , when closely 
 examine , there be the same harmony of design as in the external

 postulate of a science . bear with I a little long if I still pursue

 special instance - to say , ' this you should do , this you should not do . ' 
 all I would ask be , leave to proffer a few more suggestion to your own 
 large and candid experience . " 

 say Darrell , irresistibly allure on , but with a tinge of his grave 
 irony : " you have the true genius of the pulpit , and I concede to you its 
 right . I will listen with the wish to profit - the more susceptible of 
 conviction because free from the necessity to reply . " 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " you vindicate the object which have be the main

 bold - hearted boy - in this ancient hall , amidst these primitive

 and Knighthood , and all the poetical colour of Old , to unite the homage 
 due to the ancestral dead with the future ambition of life ; - image full 
 of interest and of pathos - a friendless child of a race more beloved

 George pause , and tear stand in Darrell 's eye . 

 " yes , " resume the scholar - " yes , for the child , for the youth , for 
 the man in his first dare stride into the Action of Life , that object 
 command our respectful sympathy . 

 " but wait a few year . have that object expand ? have it lead on into 
 object embrace humanity ? remain it alone and sterile in the bosom of 
 successful genius ? or be it prolific and fruitful of grander design - of 
 more widespreading us ? make genius successful , and all man have the 
 right to say , ' brother , help we ! ' what ! no other object still but to 
 build up a house ! - to recover a line ! what be grand at one stage of an 
 onward career , be narrow and small at another ! ambition limit to the 
 rise of a family ! 

 " can our sympathy still hallow that ? no ! in Guy Darrell 
 successful - that ambition be treason to earth ! mankind be his family 
 now ! THEREFORE Heaven thwart the object which oppose its own end 
 in create you ! THERFORE childles you stand on your desolate hearth ! 
 THEREFORE , lo ! side by side - yon uncompleted pile - your own uncompleted 
 life ! " 

 Darrell sit dumb . - he be appal ! 

 GEORGE MORLEY . - " have not that object stint your very intellect ? have it 
 not , while baffle in its own centre aim - have it not rob you of the 
 glory which youth crave , and which manhood might have win ? Idolater

 grateful respect of the Future , which man ever give to that genius whose 
 object be knit with mankind ? suddenly , in the zenith of life , amidst

 genius be give . you muse . Heaven permit these rude word to strike 
 home ! Guy Darrell , it be not too late ! Heaven 's warning be always in

 master failing of Pride . to we as Christians , or as reasoner , it be not 
 in this world that every duty be to find its special meed ; yet by that

 say ' stubborn child , yield at last to the warning vouchsafe to thee

 law ; sacrifice now to I the thing thou most clingest to - Pride . I make

 duty to thee , because it entail a triumphant self - conquest , and pay

 will follow its noiseless path up to the smile of God ! " 

 the preacher cease . 

 Darrell breathe a long sigh , rise slowly , take George 's hand , press 
 it warmly in both his own , and turn quickly and silently away . he 
 pause in the deep recess where the gleam of the wintry sun shoot through 
 the small casement , aslant and pale on the massive wall : open the

 church - tower - on the dark evergreen that belt his father 's tomb .

 abrupt impatience ; and George feel that word write must remain to

 Darrell turn and look towards George , as if in surprise to see he 
 still linger there . 

 " I have now but to place before you this letter from my uncle to myself ; 
 it enter into those detail which it would have ill become I specially

 by your old friend - by a man who have no dull discrimination in the 
 perplexity of life or the nicety of honour . "

 on George 's pale and powerful countenance , say : " how be it that , with 
 an experience of mankind which you will pardon I for assume to be 
 limited , you yet read so wondrously the complicated human heart ? " 

 " if I really have that gift , " say George , " I will answer your question 
 by another : be it through experience that we learn to read the human 
 heart - or be it through sympathy ? if it be experience , what become of 
 the Poet ? if the Poet be bear , not make , be it not because he be bear to 
 sympathise with what he have never experience ? " 

 " I see ! there be bear preacher ! " 

 Darrell reseat himself , and begin Alban 's letter . he be evidently 
 move by the Colonel 's account of Lionel 's grief , mutter to himself , 
 " poor boy ! - but he be brave - he be young . " when he come to Alban 's 
 foreboding on the effect of dejection upon the stamina of life , he 
 press his hand quickly against his breast as if he have receive a 
 shock ! he muse a while before he resume his task ; then he read rapidly
